| 8 | $\eta_{comb} = \frac{LHV_{out}}{HHV_{in}} \times 100$ | Combustion efficiency | % |
| 9 | $E_{bio} = M \times CV \times \eta$ | Bioenergy potential | MJ |
| 10 | $P_{biogas} = V \times Y_m$ | Biogas production ($Y_m$ = yield) | m³/day |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoid the `pipe` symbol trap:** Anywhere you see a numerical *absolute value* or a *conditional*, KTU formula sheets often write it as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$. Use $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX, **never a raw `|x|`**, so that markdown tables don't break.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power-plant sizing:** Kerala's 7.5 MW *Sakthinagar* and 6 MW *Pallipuram* bagasse-based power plants require precise moisture and CV data of bagasse to design boilers and steam cycles.
- **Biogas digester design:** The volatile solids (VS) content determines digester volume via:

$$V_{d} = \frac{M_{waste} \times VS \times Y_m}{\rho_{CH_4} \times t_{HRT}}$$

- **Material selection for storage:** High-moisture biomass (>$M=60\,\%$) needs drying to prevent microbial decay and spontaneous combustion.
- **Tar formation in gasification:** Proximate and ultimate data drive choice between air-blown, steam-blown, or oxygen-blown gasifiers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Dulong–Berthelot HHV Formula

The Dulong–Berthelot equation is built on **stoichiometric** combustion chemistry. Let us derive it step by step.

**Step 1 — Combustion stoichiometry.** For 100 kg of dry fuel containing C kg carbon, H kg hydrogen, O kg oxygen, and S kg sulphur, the combustion reactions are:

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$

$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$$

$$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$$

**Step 2 — Oxygen required (kg of O₂ per 100 kg fuel).**

Each kg of C needs $\tfrac{32}{12}$ kg of O₂; each kg of H needs $\tfrac{32}{4}$ kg; each kg of S needs $\tfrac{32}{32}$ kg.

$$\text{External O}_2 = \tfrac{32}{12}C + \tfrac{32}{4}H + \tfrac{32}{32}S$$

But part of this oxygen is **already present** in the fuel — internal oxygen, $O$ kg per 100 kg of fuel — and that is treated as already combined with the fuel.

**Step 3 — Heat released per kg of each element (Mendeleev / Dulong constants).**

- Carbon to CO₂: $\approx 33{,}830$ kJ/kg of C, **i.e., $0.338\,\text{MJ/kg C} \times 100 = 33.8$ MJ per 100 kg C** ⇒ coefficient of $C$ is **0.338 MJ/(kg fuel)** if C is in %.
- Hydrogen to liquid H₂O: ≈ 142,800 kJ/kg of H. But the oxygen already present in the fuel must first be satisfied. Each kg of oxygen already bound internally consumes 8 kg of hydrogen (because $H_2O$ has H:O = 2:16 = 1:8). So **net effective hydrogen** is $(H - O/8)$ kg per 100 kg fuel.
- Sulphur to SO₂: ≈ 9,500 kJ/kg of S, i.e., **0.095 MJ/kg × 100** ⇒ coefficient **0.095**.

**Step 4 — Combine into a single expression.**

$$HHV = 0.338\,C + 1.428\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095\,S \;\;\;\;[\text{MJ/kg, dry basis}]$$

This is the Dulong–Berthelot formula. It is empirical but matches experimental bomb-calorimeter values to within **±3 %** for most lignocellulosic biomass.

**Sanity check:** Pure cellulose (C₆H₁₀O₅) has $C = 44.44\,\%$, $H = 6.17\,\%$, $O = 49.38\,\%$, $S = 0$. Substituting:

$$HHV = 0.338 \times 44.44 + 1.428 \left(6.17 - \frac{49.38}{8}\right)$$

$$= 15.02 + 1.428 \times (6.17 - 6.17) = 15.02\,\text{MJ/kg}$$

Literature value ≈ 17.5 MJ/kg — the discrepancy is because Dulong–Berthelot neglects the heating contribution of any cellulose-bound oxygen that is released without combustion. The formula works best for $O/C < 1$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Conversion of HHV → LHV

When biomass burns, the water formed appears as **vapour** at flame temperature. The latent heat lost is $h_{fg} = 2{,}442$ kJ/kg at 25 °C.

**Step 1 — Water produced per kg of fuel.**

- From hydrogen: each kg of H produces 9 kg of H₂O ($H_2 + ½O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$, 2 + 16 = 18; H atomic weight 2 ⇒ 9 kg H₂O per kg H).
- From moisture: $M$ kg water per kg fuel (when M is in %).

Total water = $\tfrac{9H}{100} + \tfrac{M}{100}$ kg water per kg of fuel (with H and M in %).

**Step 2 — Latent heat lost.**

$$L_{lost} = 2.442 \times \left(\frac{9H}{100} + \frac{M}{100}\right) \;\;\;\;[\text{MJ/kg}]$$

**Step 3 — Final relation.**

$$LHV = HHV - L_{lost} = HHV - 2.442 \left(\frac{9H + M}{100}\right)$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Biomass Feedstocks

| Feedstock | $M$ (%) | $VM$ (%, db) | $FC$ (%, db) | $A$ (%, db) | C (%, daf) | H (%, daf) | O (%, daf) | HHV (MJ/kg, db) |
|-----------|---------|--------------|--------------|-------------|------------|------------|------------|------------------|
| Wood (pine) | 30 | 82.0 | 16.5 | 1.5 | 50.2 | 6.1 | 43.5 | 19.8 |
| Bagasse | 50 | 85.0 | 12.5 | 2.5 | 48.0 | 6.4 | 44.7 | 18.9 |
| Rice husk | 9 | 65.0 | 16.0 | 19.0 | 47.0 | 5.7 | 46.0 | 15.0 |
| Rice straw | 12 | 70.0 | 16.5 | 13.5 | 45.0 | 5.4 | 48.0 | 14.6 |
| Coconut coir pith | 11 | 78.0 | 19.0 | 3.0 | 49.5 | 5.9 | 44.0 | 19.1 |
| Cattle dung (dried) | 10 | 60.0 | 18.0 | 22.0 | 40.0 | 5.5 | 51.0 | 16.0 |
| Switchgrass | 15 | 75.0 | 17.0 | 8.0 | 47.5 | 5.8 | 45.0 | 17.6 |
| Microalgae (dried) | 5 | 80.0 | 8.0 | 12.0 | 52.0 | 7.5 | 30.0 | 21.0 |
| Bituminous coal (for contrast) | 5 | 30.0 | 60.0 | 10.0 | 80.0 | 5.0 | 8.0 | 32.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the table (KTU examiner's trick):**
> Notice the **inverse correlation** between *Oxygen (O)* and *HHV*. Biomass has high O, so it has low CV. Coal has low O, so it has high CV. KTU exam questions often ask: *"Why does biomass have a lower calorific value than coal?"* — the answer is *high oxygen + low fixed carbon + high volatiles*.

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Estimating Total Biomass Energy in a Region

**Step 1 — Gather regional data.**
- Agricultural production: $P$ tonnes/yr of main product
- Residue-to-Product Ratio: $RPR$
- Moisture content: $M$
- Dry-basis HHV: $HHV_{db}$
- Collection efficiency: $\eta_c$ (typically 0.5 – 0.8)
- Combustion plant efficiency: $\eta_p$ (typically 0.25 – 0.35 for Rankine cycle)

**Step 2 — Dry residue mass.**

$$M_{res, db} = P \times RPR \times (1 - M) \times 10^3 \;\;\;\;[\text{kg/yr}]$$

(factor $10^3$ converts tonnes to kg)

**Step 3 — Gross energy content.**

$$E_{gross} = M_{res, db} \times HHV_{db} \;\;\;\;[\text{MJ/yr}]$$

**Step 4 — Recoverable & useful energy.**

$$E_{recoverable} = E_{gross} \times \eta_c$$

$$E_{useful} = E_{recoverable} \times \eta_p \;\;\;\;[\text{MJ/yr}]$$

**Step 5 — Convert to MW continuous.**

$$P_{MW} = \frac{E_{useful}}{3.6 \times 8760} \;\;\;\;[\text{MW}]$$

(1 kWh = 3.6 MJ; 1 year = 8760 h)

### 3.4.1 Worked Numerical Example

A Kerala district produces 200,000 tonnes/yr of paddy. RPR for rice straw = 1.5, moisture = 12 %, HHV = 14.6 MJ/kg (db), $\eta_c = 0.6$, $\eta_p = 0.25$. Estimate the continuous power output from a straw-fired plant.

**Solution:**

$$M_{res, db} = 200{,}000 \times 1.5 \times (1 - 0.12) \times 10^3 = 2.64 \times 10^8 \text{ kg/yr}$$

$$E_{gross} = 2.64 \times 10^8 \times 14.6 = 3.854 \times 10^9 \text{ MJ/yr}$$

$$E_{useful} = 3.854 \times 10^9 \times 0.6 \times 0.25 = 5.78 \times 10^8 \text{ MJ/yr}$$

$$P_{MW} = \frac{5.78 \times 10^8}{3.6 \times 8760} = \frac{5.78 \times 10^8}{31{,}536} = 1.83 \times 10^4 \text{ kW} \approx 18.3 \text{ MW}$$

So a 200,000-tonne paddy district can support a continuous ~**18 MW** straw power plant at these typical efficiency levels.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Bioenergy Resource Flow

The diagram below maps the full pathway from raw biomass to end-use bioenergy, highlighting the role of *availability* and *characteristics* in routing each feedstock to its optimal conversion route.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sunlight + CO2 + H2O] --> B[Photosynthesis]
    B --> C1[Agricultural Residues]
    B --> C2[Forestry Residues]
    B --> C3[Energy Crops]
    B --> C4[Municipal Solid Waste]
    B --> C5[Animal Wastes]
    B --> C6[Aquatic Biomass]

    C1 --> D1[Characteristics Analysis]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D1
    C6 --> D1

    D1 --> D2[Moisture M]
    D1 --> D3[Volatiles VM]
    D1 --> D4[Fixed Carbon FC]
    D1 --> D5[Ash A]
    D1 --> D6[Ultimate C H O N S]
    D1 --> D7[HHV LHV]

    D2 --> R1{Routing Decision}
    D3 --> R1
    D4 --> R1
    D5 --> R1
    D6 --> R1
    D7 --> R1

    R1 -->|Low M High FC| P1[Combustion to Steam Power]
    R1 -->|High VM Low Ash| P2[Gasification to Syngas]
    R1 -->|High Moisture Organic| P3[Anaerobic Digestion to Biogas]
    R1 -->|Sugar or Starch| P4[Fermentation to Bioethanol]
    R1 -->|Oil Bearing Seeds| P5[Transesterification to Biodiesel]
    R1 -->|Lignocellulosic| P6[2G Pretreatment and Hydrolysis]

    P1 --> E1[Electricity and Process Heat]
    P2 --> E2[Fischer Tropsch Fuels]
    P3 --> E3[CNG Substitute and Digestate Fertilizer]
    P4 --> E4[Gasoline Blending E5 E10]
    P5 --> E5[Diesel Substitute B100]
    P6 --> E6[Cellulosic Ethanol]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Hierarchy of Bioenergy Resource Availability

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0[Primary Energy] --> L1[Solar]
    L0 --> L2[Wind]
    L0 --> L3[Hydro]
    L0 --> L4[Biomass]

    L1 --> S1[Photovoltaic PV]
    L1 --> S2[Concentrated Solar Power CSP]

    L2 --> W1[Onshore Wind]
    L2 --> W2[Offshore Wind]

    L3 --> H1[Large Hydro]
    L3 --> H2[Small and Micro Hydro]

    L4 --> B1[Forestry Biomass]
    L4 --> B2[Agricultural Residues]
    L4 --> B3[Energy Crops]
    L4 --> B4[Organic Waste]
    L4 --> B5[Aquatic Biomass]

    B1 --> X1[Firewood Charcoal Pellets]
    B2 --> X2[Bagasse Straw Husk Briquettes]
    B3 --> X3[Bioethanol Biodiesel]
    B4 --> X4[Biogas Compost]
    B5 --> X5[Biodiesel from Microalgae]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: How Characteristics Influence Conversion Choice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Biomass Sample] --> T1[Sample Preparation and Drying]
    T1 --> T2[Proximate Analysis]
    T1 --> T3[Ultimate Analysis]
    T1 --> T4[Bomb Calorimeter CV Test]

    T2 --> U1[VM FC A M]
    T3 --> U2[C H O N S]
    T4 --> U3[HHV MJ per kg]

    U1 --> V1{Decision Matrix}
    U2 --> V1
    U3 --> V1

    V1 -->|M greater than 60 percent| W1[Drying Required]
    V1 -->|A greater than 10 percent| W2[Leaching to Reduce Ash]
    V1 -->|VM greater than 70 percent| W3[Gasification or Pyrolysis]
    V1 -->|FC greater than 25 percent| W4[Direct Combustion]
    V1 -->|S greater than 0.5 percent| W5[Desulphurisation Needed]

    W1 --> Y1[Energy Output]
    W2 --> Y1
    W3 --> Y1
    W4 --> Y1
    W5 --> Y1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualisation Tip:** Examiners often award 2 marks for *clearly labelled* conversion flow diagrams. Include arrows, feed names, and final products. The above block diagrams are KTU-board-friendly because they show both **availability** (left branches) and **characteristics-driven routing** (decision diamonds on the right).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. Define biomass and list any four categories of biomass with one example each. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Model Answer:**
Biomass is the biodegradable fraction of products, residues, and wastes of biological origin from agriculture, forestry, and related industries, as well as the biodegradable municipal and industrial waste.
- *Agricultural residues* — rice straw
- *Forestry residues* — sawdust
- *Energy crops* — *Miscanthus*
- *Municipal solid waste* — food waste
- *Animal wastes* — cattle dung

*(Any four, with one example each — 3 marks)*

### Q2. Differentiate between HHV and LHV of a fuel. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**Model Answer:**
| HHV (Higher Heating Value) | LHV (Lower Heating Value) |
|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Heat released assuming water vapour from combustion is condensed and latent heat is recovered. | Heat released when water vapour escapes without condensation. |
| Greater numerical value. | Always less than HHV. |
| Measured in a bomb calorimeter. | Calculated by subtracting the latent-heat term from HHV. |

Numerically, $LHV = HHV - 2.442 \times \left(\frac{9H + M}{100}\right)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** Classify bioenergy resources in detail. Discuss the global and Indian availability of agricultural and forestry biomass. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define proximate and ultimate analysis of biomass. A biomass sample has C = 49 %, H = 6.0 %, O = 44 %, S = 0.1 %, M = 10 %, VM = 76 %, FC = 14 % on as-received basis. Calculate the HHV using the Dulong–Berthelot equation and then the LHV. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a)

Bioenergy resources are classified based on **physical state** and **source origin**:

- **Solid biomass:** wood, agricultural residues, energy crops, MSW.
- **Liquid biofuels:** bioethanol, biodiesel, bio-oil from pyrolysis.
- **Gaseous biofuels:** biogas (anaerobic digestion), syngas (gasification), producer gas.

**Global availability (FAO / World Bank estimates):**

- Global crop residue generation: ≈ **3 billion tonnes/yr**.
- Forestry residue potential: ≈ **1.5 billion tonnes/yr**.
- MSW generation: ≈ **2.01 billion tonnes/yr** (organic ≈ 45 %).
- Energy crop potential (set-aside land): ≈ **5 – 10 Gha × 10 t/ha** = up to 50 Gt/yr.

**Indian availability (MNRE estimates):**

- Agricultural residues: ≈ **500 million tonnes/yr**; surplus ≈ 140 Mt.
- Forestry residues: ≈ **67 million tonnes/yr**.
- MSW: ≈ **62 million tonnes/yr** (organic ≈ 33 Mt).
- Cogeneration potential from sugar mills: ≈ **5,000 MW** (installed: ~2,500 MW).

**Kerala's special position:**

- Coconut coir pith: **0.5 – 0.7 Mt/yr** → potential feedstock for pyrolysis and briquetting.
- Rice straw (Kuttanad, Kole lands): ≈ **0.6 Mt/yr**.
- Cashew apple waste: ≈ **0.05 Mt/yr** → potential bioethanol.

*(Classification 3 marks, Global 2 marks, India 2 marks = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (b)

**Proximate analysis** quantifies moisture (M), volatile matter (VM), fixed carbon (FC), and ash (A) — the four operational parameters of any solid fuel.
**Ultimate analysis** quantifies the elemental composition C, H, O, N, S — required for stoichiometric and CV calculations.

**Given (as-received):** C = 49 %, H = 6.0 %, O = 44 %, S = 0.1 %, M = 10 %, VM = 76 %, FC = 14 %.

**Step 1 — Convert to dry basis.** Since ash is not given, we use:

$$C_{db} = \frac{C_{ar}}{1 - M/100} = \frac{49}{0.9} = 54.44\,\%$$

$$H_{db} = \frac{6.0}{0.9} = 6.67\,\%$$

$$O_{db} = \frac{44}{0.9} = 48.89\,\%$$

$$S_{db} = \frac{0.1}{0.9} = 0.11\,\%$$

**Step 2 — Apply Dulong–Berthelot.**

$$HHV = 0.338 \times 54.44 + 1.428 \times \left(6.67 - \frac{48.89}{8}\right) + 0.095 \times 0.11$$

$$= 18.40 + 1.428 \times (6.67 - 6.11) + 0.0105$$

$$= 18.40 + 1.428 \times 0.56 + 0.0105$$

$$= 18.40 + 0.7997 + 0.0105 = 19.21\,\text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Convert HHV to LHV.** Use H on dry basis = 6.67 %, M (as received) = 10 %.

$$LHV = HHV - 2.442 \times \left(\frac{9 \times 6.67 + 10}{100}\right)$$

$$= 19.21 - 2.442 \times \left(\frac{60.03 + 10}{100}\right) = 19.21 - 2.442 \times 0.7003$$

$$= 19.21 - 1.710 = 17.50\,\text{MJ/kg}$$

**Answer:** $HHV \approx 19.2$ MJ/kg, $LHV \approx 17.5$ MJ/kg.

*(Formula 2 marks, Conversion 2 marks, Substitution 2 marks, Final 1 mark = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**(a)** Discuss the characteristics of biomass that make it suitable as a renewable energy source. Compare the properties of biomass with coal. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A sugar mill produces 250,000 tonnes/yr of bagasse at 50 % moisture. RPR for bagasse is 0.30, HHV (db) = 18.9 MJ/kg, collection efficiency = 0.85, plant thermal efficiency = 0.30. Estimate (i) the gross energy in the residue, (ii) the useful electrical energy, and (iii) the continuous power in kW the plant can deliver. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a)

Biomass is characterised by:

1. **Renewability:** Refilled by photosynthesis on seasonal timescales.
2. **Carbon neutrality (in principle):** CO₂ released on combustion is recaptured by next crop.
3. **Versatility:** Can be converted to solid, liquid, and gaseous fuels.
4. **Indigenous availability:** Reduces import dependence — vital for India's energy security.
5. **Rural employment generation:** Decentralised bioenergy creates farm-gate income.
6. **Low sulphur & nitrogen:** Lower SO₂, NOₓ emissions per MJ than coal.
7. **Biodegradability:** Wastes become resources.

**Comparison with coal:**

| Property | Biomass | Coal |
|----------|---------|------|
| Carbon (daf) | 45 – 50 % | 70 – 90 % |
| Oxygen (daf) | 35 – 45 % | 3 – 10 % |
| Volatile matter | 70 – 85 % | 25 – 35 % |
| Fixed carbon | 14 – 25 % | 50 – 70 % |
| HHV (MJ/kg, db) | 15 – 21 | 25 – 35 |
| Sulphur | < 0.1 % | 1 – 3 % |
| Moisture | 5 – 60 % | 5 – 10 % |
| Ash | 0.5 – 15 % | 5 – 20 % |
| Renewability | Yes (decades) | No (millions of years) |
| CO₂ per MJ | Low | High |

**Inference:** Biomass has lower CV but is renewable, with cleaner emissions and distributed availability. Coal has higher CV but is polluting and finite.

*(Characteristics 3 marks, Comparison 3 marks, Inference 1 mark = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (b)

**Given:** Main product (sugar) output: 250,000 t/yr bagasse, M = 50 %, HHV(db) = 18.9 MJ/kg, $\eta_c = 0.85$, $\eta_p = 0.30$.

**Step 1 — Dry mass of bagasse.**

$$M_{dry} = 250{,}000 \times (1 - 0.50) \times 10^3 = 1.25 \times 10^8 \text{ kg/yr}$$

(Note: RPR = 0.30 is for a different residue; here bagasse is the *main* residue itself, so it is used directly.)

**Step 2 — Gross energy in residue.**

$$E_{gross} = M_{dry} \times HHV_{db} = 1.25 \times 10^8 \times 18.9 = 2.3625 \times 10^9 \text{ MJ/yr}$$

**Step 3 — Useful electrical energy.**

$$E_{useful} = E_{gross} \times \eta_c \times \eta_p = 2.3625 \times 10^9 \times 0.85 \times 0.30$$

$$= 2.3625 \times 10^9 \times 0.255 = 6.024 \times 10^8 \text{ MJ/yr}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kWh.**

$$E_{kWh} = \frac{6.024 \times 10^8}{3.6} = 1.6733 \times 10^8 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 5 — Continuous power (kW).**

$$P_{kW} = \frac{1.6733 \times 10^8}{8760} = 19{,}100 \text{ kW} \approx 19.1 \text{ MW}$$

**Answer:**
- Gross energy ≈ **2.36 × 10⁹ MJ/yr** (≈ 6.56 × 10⁵ MWh/yr)
- Useful energy ≈ **6.02 × 10⁸ MJ/yr** (≈ 1.67 × 10⁵ MWh/yr)
- Continuous power ≈ **19.1 MW**

*(Formula selection 1 mark, Dry mass 1 mark, Gross energy 1 mark, Useful energy 2 marks, kW conversion 2 marks = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert *as-received* to *dry basis* before using the Dulong–Berthelot equation.** This error alone can shift HHV by **5 – 15 %** and is the single biggest marking penalty.
> 2. **Mixing units** — using kJ/kg in some steps and MJ/kg in others. Keep MJ/kg throughout.
> 3. **Neglecting moisture in the LHV term** — students often write $LHV = HHV - 2.442 \times 9H/100$ and forget the $M$ term, losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing FC + VM with 100 %** — those two sum to 100 % *only* on a moisture- and ash-free basis; on a dry basis, $VM + FC + A = 100$.
> 5. **Not labelling diagrams** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for clear, labelled conversion flow diagrams in Module 1 questions.
> 6. **Ignoring the **RPR** vs **Cᵤ** distinction** — they are *not* the same; RPR is a mass ratio, $C_u$ is a usability-adjusted mass.
> 7. **Overlooking Kerala's local feedstocks** — Kerala-specific examples (coconut coir pith, cashew apple, rice straw) fetch bonus marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Biomass** = recent (≤ 100 yr) photosynthetic organic matter; **renewable** in human timescales.
> - **Three classes of biofuels:** solid (wood, pellets), liquid (ethanol, biodiesel), gas (biogas, syngas).
> - **Six availability categories:** agricultural residues, forestry residues, energy crops, MSW, animal wastes, aquatic biomass.
> - **Proximate analysis** = $M + VM + FC + A = 100$ % (on as-received or dry basis).
> - **Ultimate analysis** = $C + H + O + N + S = 100$ % (on dry-ash-free basis).
> - **Dulong–Berthelot:** $HHV = 0.338C + 1.428(H - O/8) + 0.095S$ (dry basis, MJ/kg).
> - **LHV correction:** $LHV = HHV - 2.442 \times (9H + M)/100$.
> - **Biomass vs Coal:** Biomass has **higher O, higher VM, lower FC, lower CV, lower S**, and is **renewable**.
> - **RPR** = residue-to-product mass ratio; **Cᵤ** = usable coefficient.
> - **Kerala priority feedstocks:** coconut coir pith, rice straw (Kuttanad), cashew apple, MSW (Kochi, Trivandrum), animal dung.
> - **Availability arithmetic mantra:** $E_{useful} = M \times (1 - M) \times HHV \times \eta_c \times \eta_p$; $P_{kW} = E_{useful} / (3.6 \times 8760)$.
> - **Characteristic-driven routing:** high M → dry first; high VM → gasify; high FC → combust; high S → desulphurise; high A → wash/leach.
> - **Photosynthesis efficiency** globally: 0.1 – 1 %; **biomass is the largest renewable energy source** (≈ 10 % of global primary energy).
> - **Biomass is a *current-account* deposit** in the Sun's bank, while **fossil fuels are *fixed deposits*** — remember the analogy!

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy yields from conversion of energy crops to biofuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy yields from conversion of energy crops to biofuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Yield (KTU 2024 Definition):** *Energy yield* from the conversion of energy crops to biofuels is defined as the **net useful energy recovered per unit land area (MJ/ha) or per unit mass of feedstock (MJ/kg)**, accounting for the heating value of the produced biofuel and the efficiency of the biological, thermochemical, or chemical conversion pathway used.

An **energy crop** is a crop cultivated primarily for the purpose of producing bioenergy (heat, electricity, or liquid/gaseous transport fuels) rather than for food or feed. Conversion to biofuel can be classified under three principal reaction families:

1. **Biochemical Conversion** – enzymatic/fermentative routes (sugars → ethanol; biomass → biogas via anaerobic digestion).
2. **Thermochemical Conversion** – heat-driven routes (combustion, pyrolysis, gasification, hydrothermal liquefaction).
3. **Chemical Conversion** – catalytic/transesterification routes (triglycerides → Fatty Acid Methyl Esters / FAME).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Energy Piggy Bank" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of an energy crop as a *solar piggy bank*. Sunlight (solar energy) is deposited daily into plant biomass through photosynthesis. A biofuel conversion plant acts like a *bank teller* who withdraws this stored energy in a usable currency — liquid ethanol, biodiesel, or methane. The **energy yield** is essentially *how much currency you walk out with*, minus the *fees* (energy spent on fertilizers, harvesting, distillation). A high-yield crop + efficient conversion = a rich withdrawal; a thirsty, low-yield crop with an inefficient refinery = a depleted account.

### Standard Energy Density Metrics (Benchmarks for KTU Problems)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Higher Heating Value | **HHV** | **MJ/kg** |
| Lower Heating Value | **LHV** | **MJ/kg** |
| Volumetric Energy Density | $\rho_E$ | **MJ/L** |
| Mass Energy Yield | $Y_m$ | **GJ/ha/yr** |
| Energy Return on Investment | **EROI** | **dimensionless** |
| Crop Productivity | $P_b$ | **tonnes dry matter/ha/yr** |

### Classification of Energy Crops (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Generational Tiers:**
> - **1st Generation** – Edible sugar/starch/oil crops (Sugarcane, Maize, Soybean, Rapeseed, Palm).
> - **2nd Generation** – Lignocellulosic non-food crops (Switchgrass, *Miscanthus*, Jatropha, Poplar).
> - **3rd Generation** – Aquatic/microbial biomass (Microalgae, Macroalgae, Cyanobacteria).
> - **4th Generation** – Genetically engineered high-lipid/low-photorespiration strains (emerging).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The "Why–How" Logic of Energy Yield Computation

The energy yield chain follows a strict **multiplicative cascade**. Any weakness in one link collapses the entire chain:

> **Step 1 — Photosynthetic Fixation:** Solar radiation $I$ (MJ/m²/day) is intercepted by canopy with efficiency $\eta_p \approx 1\text{–}3\%$, producing dry biomass $M_b$ (kg/ha).
>
> **Step 2 — Pretreatment/Extraction:** The biomass is conditioned (milling, hydrolysis, oil pressing) with an energy penalty $E_{in,1}$.
>
> **Step 3 — Conversion Reaction:** A stoichiometric reaction (e.g., fermentation, transesterification) converts the intermediate with conversion efficiency $\eta_c$.
>
> **Step 4 — Upgrading/Purification:** Distillation, washing, drying consume energy $E_{in,2}$ but raise fuel quality.
>
> **Step 5 — Net Energy:** $E_{net} = E_{out} - (E_{in,1} + E_{in,2} + E_{agri})$.

The **net energy balance** determines whether a biofuel pathway is genuinely renewable or a net energy *consumer*.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **examination-grade** and frequently appear in KTU Part A and Part B questions. Memorize units as well as symbols.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{out} = m_f \cdot \text{HHV}_f$ | Energy content of fuel produced | MJ |
| 2 | $Y_v = \dfrac{m_f}{A \cdot t}$ | Volumetric yield per hectare per year | L/ha/yr |
| 3 | $E_y = Y_v \cdot \rho_E$ | Energy yield per hectare | MJ/ha/yr |
| 4 | $E_{net} = E_{out} - E_{in,total}$ | Net energy balance | MJ |
| 5 | $\text{NEB} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in,total}}$ | Net Energy Balance (ratio) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{EROI} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in,total}}$ | Energy Return on Investment | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_p \cdot \eta_h \cdot \eta_c$ | Cumulative pathway efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{T_0}{T_1}$ | Carnot thermal ceiling | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - h_{fg}\!\left(\dfrac{9H}{100}\right)$ | LHV from HHV | MJ/kg |
| 10 | $M_{biofuel} = Y_{biofuel} \cdot M_{biomass}$ | Stoichiometric mass yield | kg |
| 11 | $\eta_{conv} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}}\times 100\%$ | Reaction conversion efficiency | % |
| 12 | $\text{SER} = \dfrac{E_{biomass}}{E_{fossil-input}}$ | Solar Energy Recovery ratio | dimensionless |

**Where:**
- $m_f$ = mass of fuel produced (kg)
- $\rho_E$ = volumetric energy density of fuel (MJ/L)
- $A$ = land area (ha)
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization of water $\approx \mathbf{2.26\ MJ/kg}$ at 100 °C
- $H$ = hydrogen mass fraction in fuel
- $\eta_p$ = photosynthetic efficiency
- $\eta_h$ = harvest/collection efficiency
- $\eta_c$ = conversion efficiency

### Reference Heating Values of Common Biofuels (Memorize for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard HHV Reference Table:**

| Biofuel | HHV (MJ/kg) | LHV (MJ/kg) | Density (kg/L) | $\rho_E$ (MJ/L) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bioethanol (E100)** | 29.7 | 26.9 | 0.789 | 21.2 |
| **Biodiesel (FAME)** | 40.1 | 37.2 | 0.880 | 32.7 |
| **Biogas (60% CH₄)** | 22–27 | 20–25 | $\approx 1.15$ kg/m³ | 22.7 MJ/m³ |
| **Pure Methane (CH₄)** | 55.5 | 50.0 | 0.656 (g/L) | 32.8 |
| **Bio-oil (pyrolysis)** | 17–22 | 16–20 | 1.20 | 20–26 |
| **Methanol (CH₃OH)** | 22.7 | 20.0 | 0.792 | 15.8 |
| **Gasoline (reference)** | 46.0 | 43.0 | 0.745 | 32.0 |
| **Diesel (reference)** | 45.5 | 42.5 | 0.832 | 35.3 |

### Energy Yields of Major Energy Crops (Real Engineering Data)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Land-Based Energy Productivity — Memorize the Order of Magnitude:**

| Crop | Type | Biofuel | Yield (L/ha) | GJ/ha/yr (approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sugarcane** | 1st Gen | Ethanol | 6,000 – 8,000 | 130 – 170 |
| **Sugar beet** | 1st Gen | Ethanol | 5,000 – 7,000 | 110 – 150 |
| **Maize (corn)** | 1st Gen | Ethanol | 3,000 – 4,000 | 65 – 85 |
| **Cassava** | 1st Gen | Ethanol | 4,000 – 6,000 | 85 – 125 |
| **Oil palm** | 1st Gen | Biodiesel | 4,000 – 6,000 | 130 – 195 |
| **Rapeseed** | 1st Gen | Biodiesel | 1,100 – 1,500 | 38 – 50 |
| **Soybean** | 1st Gen | Biodiesel | 500 – 700 | 18 – 24 |
| **Jatropha** | 2nd Gen | Biodiesel | 1,500 – 2,500 | 50 – 85 |
| **Switchgrass** | 2nd Gen | Cellulosic EtOH | 2,800 – 3,800 | 60 – 85 |
| ***Miscanthus*** | 2nd Gen | Combustion | — (heat basis) | 200 – 300 |
| **Microalgae** | 3rd Gen | Biodiesel | 5,000 – 15,000 | 175 – 500 |
| **Woody biomass** | 2nd Gen | Pellets/chips | — | 100 – 200 |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — Ethanol Yield from Sugarcane via Fermentation

> [!NOTE]
> **Reaction:** $C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{yeast}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$

**Step 1 — Molecular Weights:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{glucose} &= 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 180\ \text{g/mol} \\
M_{ethanol} &= 2(12) + 6(1) + 1(16) = 46\ \text{g/mol} \\
M_{CO_2} &= 1(12) + 2(16) = 44\ \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric Mass Yield:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{m_{EtOH,theoretical}}{m_{glucose}} &= \frac{2 \times 46}{180} = \frac{92}{180} = 0.5111 \\
\Rightarrow m_{EtOH,theoretical} &= 0.5111 \times m_{glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **theoretical Gay-Lussac yield** = **51.1% wt/wt**.

**Step 3 — Realistic Industrial Yield (with $\eta_{fermentation} \approx 90\%$, distillation losses):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{overall} &= 0.5111 \times 0.90 \times 0.98 \approx 0.450 \\
m_{EtOH,actual} &\approx 0.450 \times m_{glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Key Insight:** Only about **45% of the glucose mass** is recoverable as ethanol. The remaining ~45% becomes CO₂ and ~10% is lost to byproducts (glycerol, yeast biomass, fusel alcohols).

**Step 4 — From Cane to Ethanol — Full Mass Balance:**

Assume **1 tonne (1000 kg) of sugarcane** contains ~14% sucrose = 140 kg sucrose = 140 kg equivalent glucose (approx., since sucrose hydrolyses to glucose + fructose).

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{EtOH} &= 0.450 \times 140 = 63\ \text{kg} \\
V_{EtOH} &= \frac{63}{0.789} \approx 79.8\ \text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

> If sugarcane productivity = 80 tonnes/ha, then:
> $$V_{EtOH/ha} = 79.8 \times 80 = 6{,}387\ \text{L/ha/yr}$$

---

### Derivation 2 — Biodiesel Yield via Transesterification

> [!NOTE]
> **Reaction:** Triglyceride + 3 Methanol → 3 FAME + Glycerol

**Step 1 — Generic Triglyceride Molar Mass:**

For a generic oil with average fatty acid chain of $\text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{33}\text{COO}$-:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{triglyceride} &\approx 3(12 \times 17 + 33 + 2 \times 16) + \text{glycerol core} \\
&\approx 3 \times 281 + 38 = 881\ \text{g/mol (approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Molar Mass of FAME (Methyloleate):**

$$
M_{FAME} = 12 \times 19 + 36 + 2 \times 16 = 296\ \text{g/mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric Mass Yield:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{m_{FAME,theoretical}}{m_{oil}} &= \frac{3 \times 296}{881} = \frac{888}{881} = 1.008 \\
&\approx 1.00\ (\text{essentially 1:1 mass yield})
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Insight:** Transesterification is **mass-additive** — the methanol "stitch" replaces the glycerol "backbone" almost mass-for-mass. A 100 kg oil input yields **~100 kg FAME** (theoretically).

**Step 4 — Real-World Industrial Yield:**

With catalyst losses, soap formation, washing losses ($\eta \approx 95\%$):

$$
m_{FAME,actual} = 0.95 \times 1000 = 950\ \text{kg per tonne of oil}
$$

---

### Derivation 3 — Biogas Yield via Anaerobic Digestion (Buswell Equation)

> [!NOTE]
> **General Buswell Reaction:**
> $$C_n H_a O_b N_c + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} + \dfrac{3c}{4}\right)H_2O \longrightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8}\right)CH_4 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8}\right)CO_2 + c\,NH_3$$

**Step 1 — Apply to Glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$):**

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2
$$

> **Theoretical biogas:** **0.75 m³ CH₄ per kg glucose** (at STP, ideal gas).

**Step 2 — Volumetric Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{CH_4} &= \frac{m_{glucose}}{180} \times 3 = 0.01667\, m_{glucose}\ \text{kmol} \\
V_{CH_4} &= 0.01667 \times 22.4 = 0.373\ m^3\ CH_4\ \text{per kg glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy Content:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{CH_4} &= 0.373 \times 35.8\ \text{MJ/m}^3 = 13.4\ \text{MJ/kg glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Comparison to Ethanol (10.8 MJ/kg glucose theoretical):** Anaerobic digestion to methane is **thermodynamically more energy-efficient** than fermentation to ethanol.

---

### Derivation 4 — EROI Computation (Energy Return on Investment)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula:** $\text{EROI} = \dfrac{E_{output}}{E_{input}}$

**Step 5 — Typical EROI Ranges for Biofuel Pathways:**

| Pathway | EROI Range | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| Sugarcane ethanol (Brazil) | **8 – 10** | Best-in-class |
| Maize ethanol (USA) | 1.3 – 1.8 | Marginal |
| Soybean biodiesel | 1.9 – 3.0 | Moderate |
| Rapeseed biodiesel (EU) | 1.5 – 2.5 | Moderate |
| Palm oil biodiesel | 3.0 – 9.0 | High, deforestation concerns |
| Cellulosic ethanol (2G) | 2.0 – 6.0 | Improving |
| Algae biodiesel | 1.0 – 3.0 | R&D stage |
| Biogas (AD of manure) | 5 – 30 | High if waste credited |

> **Critical Threshold:** For a fuel to be a *net energy provider*, **EROI must be > 1**. For societal sustainability, **EROI > 3** is generally required to fund refining, transport, and distribution.

---

### Derivation 5 — Mass-to-Energy Conversion Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** 1 hectare of oil palm yields 5 tonnes of crude palm oil (CPO) per year. The transesterification process produces biodiesel with 95% mass efficiency. Calculate (a) the mass of biodiesel, (b) the energy content in GJ, and (c) the energy yield in GJ/ha.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{biodiesel} &= 5{,}000 \times 0.95 = 4{,}750\ \text{kg/ha/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{out} &= m_{biodiesel} \times \text{HHV}_{FAME} \\
&= 4{,}750 \times 40.1 \\
&= 190{,}475\ \text{MJ/ha/yr} \\
&= 190.5\ \text{GJ/ha/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Energy yield ≈ 190 GJ/ha/yr from oil palm biodiesel** — among the highest land-based biofuel energy yields.

---

### Analytical Matrix — Cross-Comparison of Energy Yields

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exam framing — choose the highest-yield pathway per unit land:**

| Crop → Fuel | Yield (L/ha) | LHV (MJ/L) | Energy (GJ/ha) | EROI |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sugarcane → Ethanol | 7,000 | 21.2 | 148.4 | 8.5 |
| Oil palm → Biodiesel | 5,000 | 32.7 | 163.5 | 5.5 |
| Rapeseed → Biodiesel | 1,300 | 32.7 | 42.5 | 2.0 |
| Maize → Ethanol | 3,500 | 21.2 | 74.2 | 1.5 |
| Microalgae → Biodiesel | 10,000 | 32.7 | 327.0 | 2.0 |
| *Miscanthus* → Heat | — | — | 250.0 | 15+ |

> **Conclusion:** *Miscanthus* for heat and oil palm for biodiesel are the land-productivity champions. Algae have the highest theoretical ceiling but lowest current EROI.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — Energy Crop-to-Biofuel Conversion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sunlight MJ per m2 per day] --> B[Energy Crops 1st 2nd 3rd Gen]
    B --> B1[Sugarcane Maize Sugarbeet]
    B --> B2[Oil palm Rapeseed Soybean Jatropha]
    B --> B3[Switchgrass Miscanthus Algae]

    B1 --> C1[Biochemical Pathway]
    B2 --> C2[Chemical Transesterification]
    B3 --> C3[Thermochemical Lignocellulosic Route]

    C1 --> D1[Fermentation Distillation]
    C2 --> D2[Glycerol Separation FAME Washing]
    C3 --> D3[Pretreatment Hydrolysis SSF]

    D1 --> E1[Bioethanol E100]
    D2 --> E2[Biodiesel FAME]
    D3 --> E4[Cellulosic Ethanol]

    B3 --> F1[Anaerobic Digestion]
    F1 --> F2[Biogas CH4 and CO2]
    B3 --> F3[Gasification Pyrolysis]
    F3 --> F4[Syngas Bio oil Char]

    E1 --> G[Energy Output MJ per ha]
    E2 --> G
    E4 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F4 --> G

    H[Agricultural Input Energy] --> I[Net Energy Balance]
    J[Conversion Energy Penalty] --> I
    G --> I

    I --> K{EROI Greater than 1}
    K -- Yes --> L[Sustainable Fuel]
    K -- No --> M[Net Energy Loser]
```

### Diagram 2 — Biofuel Conversion Routes (Detailed Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Biochemical Route
        B1[Sugar or Starch] --> B2[Hydrolysis if starch]
        B2 --> B3[Fermentation with yeast]
        B3 --> B4[Distillation and Dehydration]
        B4 --> B5[Anhydrous Ethanol]
    end

    subgraph Chemical Route
        C1[Triglyceride Oil] --> C2[Methanol plus NaOH Catalyst]
        C2 --> C3[Transesterification at 60 C]
        C3 --> C4[Phase Separation]
        C4 --> C5[FAME Layer]
        C4 --> C6[Glycerol Byproduct]
    end

    subgraph Thermochemical Route
        T1[Lignocellulosic Biomass] --> T2[Size Reduction and Drying]
        T2 --> T3[Pyrolysis 500 C]
        T3 --> T4[Bio oil]
        T3 --> T5[Bio char]
        T2 --> T6[Gasification 800 to 1000 C]
        T6 --> T7[Producer Gas syngas]
    end

    subgraph Anaerobic Digestion
        A1[Wet Biomass] --> A2[Anaerobic Digester 35 to 55 C]
        A2 --> A3[Biogas CH4 plus CO2]
        A2 --> A4[Digestate Fertilizer]
    end

    B5 --> OUT[Final Biofuel Product]
    C5 --> OUT
    T4 --> OUT
    T7 --> OUT
    A3 --> OUT
```

### Diagram 3 — Mass and Energy Flow in a Sugarcane Ethanol Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[1000 kg Sugarcane] --> MILL[Juice Extraction]
    MILL --> J[140 kg Sucrose Juice]
    MILL --> BAG[Bagaasse 500 kg dry fiber]
    J --> FER[Fermentation]
    FER --> BE[63 kg Ethanol]
    FER --> CO2[61 kg CO2]
    BAG --> BOIL[Boiler Combustion Heat]
    BOIL --> STEAM[Process Steam]
    STEAM --> FER
    BE --> DIST[Distillation]
    DIST --> FUEL[Anhydrous Ethanol 79.8 L]
    FUEL --> EOUT[Energy Out 1692 MJ]

    INPUT --> EN1[Energy In Agriculture 250 MJ]
    EN1 --> NEB[Net Energy Balance EROI approx 6.7]
    EOUT --> NEB
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper, May 2024]**

**Q1. Define "energy yield" in the context of biofuel production. State its SI unit and two factors on which it depends.**

**Model Answer:**

> Energy yield is the **net useful energy recovered per unit area of land (MJ/ha) or per unit mass of feedstock (MJ/kg)** when an energy crop is converted to a biofuel. It is the product of **mass/volumetric yield of the biofuel** and its **lower (or higher) heating value**.
>
> **SI Unit:** MJ/ha (preferred for agricultural energetics) or MJ/kg.
>
> **Two governing factors:**
> 1. **Crop productivity** (tonnes dry matter/ha/yr) — controlled by climate, soil, agronomy, photosynthesis.
> 2. **Conversion pathway efficiency** ($\eta_{conv}$) — determined by reaction stoichiometry, losses, energy penalties in refining.
>
> Mathematical form: $E_y = P_b \cdot X_c \cdot \eta_{conv} \cdot \text{HHV}_f$, where $X_c$ is the mass fraction of convertible substrate in biomass.

---

**Q2. Differentiate between Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV) of a biofuel. Why is LHV preferred in engine applications?**

**Model Answer:**

> | Aspect | HHV | LHV |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Definition** | Heat released with water product **condensed** to liquid | Heat released with water product **as vapour** |
> | **Value** | Higher | Lower by $h_{fg} \cdot (9H/100)$ |
> | **Includes** | Latent heat of condensation of water | Excludes it |
>
> **HHV − LHV** = $\dfrac{9H}{100} \times 2.26$ MJ/kg (for H = mass fraction of hydrogen).
>
> **LHV is preferred in engine applications** because the exhaust gases leave the engine at high temperature (typically 200–400 °C) — the water in the exhaust is **never condensed**; its latent heat cannot be recovered. Hence, the *usable* energy is the LHV. Manufacturers always quote LHV for fuels like diesel, petrol, biodiesel, and ethanol.

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – University Past Paper Style, 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Energy Yields from Sugarcane-Ethanol Pathway*

**(a)** *Derive the theoretical and actual ethanol yield per hectare from sugarcane, given:*  
*Sugarcane yield = 80 tonnes/ha; Sucrose content = 14% w/w; Fermentation efficiency = 90%; Distillation recovery = 98%; HHV of ethanol = 29.7 MJ/kg; Density of ethanol = 0.789 kg/L.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Calculate the Net Energy Balance (NEB) and EROI, if the total energy input (agriculture + conversion) is 28 GJ/ha. Comment on the sustainability.* **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Step 1 — Sucrose per hectare:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{sucrose} &= 80{,}000 \times 0.14 = 11{,}200\ \text{kg/ha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent glucose** (sucrose hydrolyses to glucose + fructose, both fermentable):

$$
m_{glucose} \approx 11{,}200 \times \frac{180}{342} = 11{,}200 \times 0.5263 = 5{,}895\ \text{kg/ha}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical ethanol (Gay-Lussac):**

$$
m_{EtOH,theo} = 0.5111 \times 5{,}895 = 3{,}012\ \text{kg/ha}
$$

**Step 4 — With fermentation efficiency 90% and distillation 98%:**

$$
m_{EtOH,actual} = 3{,}012 \times 0.90 \times 0.98 = 2{,}657\ \text{kg/ha}
$$

**Step 5 — Volume of ethanol:**

$$
V_{EtOH} = \frac{2{,}657}{0.789} = 3{,}367\ \text{L/ha}
$$

**Step 6 — Energy content:**

$$
E_{out} = 2{,}657 \times 29.7 = 78{,}913\ \text{MJ/ha} = 78.9\ \text{GJ/ha}
$$

> **Final Answer (a):** *Ethanol yield = 3,367 L/ha; Energy content = 78.9 GJ/ha.*

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NEB} = \text{EROI} &= \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = \frac{78.9}{28} = 2.82
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Sustainability Comment:** With EROI ≈ 2.82, the pathway is **moderately sustainable** — the energy recovered is nearly 3× the energy invested. For societal viability (funding distribution, refining, transport), the pathway is acceptable but below the Brazilian benchmark of 8–10. To improve, one may exploit **bagasse** as process heat (it has heating value ~19 MJ/kg) and use **vinasse** as fertilizer to reduce the agricultural energy input.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Comparative Energy Yields: Biodiesel vs Bioethanol*

**(a)** *A hectare of oil palm produces 5,000 kg of crude palm oil (CPO). CPO is transesterified with 95% efficiency to FAME biodiesel. Calculate the biodiesel mass, volume, and energy yield (in GJ/ha). Density of FAME = 0.88 kg/L; HHV of FAME = 40.1 MJ/kg.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Compare the energy yield of this oil-palm biodiesel with a sugarcane-ethanol pathway producing 3,000 L ethanol/ha. LHV ethanol = 21.2 MJ/L. Discuss which pathway is more land-efficient and why.* **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

**Step 1 — Biodiesel mass:**

$$
m_{FAME} = 5{,}000 \times 0.95 = 4{,}750\ \text{kg/ha}
$$

**Step 2 — Biodiesel volume:**

$$
V_{FAME} = \frac{4{,}750}{0.88} = 5{,}398\ \text{L/ha}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy content (using HHV):**

$$
E_{out} = 4{,}750 \times 40.1 = 190{,}475\ \text{MJ/ha} = 190.5\ \text{GJ/ha}
$$

> **Final Answer (a):** *Biodiesel = 4,750 kg/ha = 5,398 L/ha = 190.5 GJ/ha.*

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Step 1 — Sugarcane ethanol energy:**

$$
E_{ethanol} = 3{,}000\ \text{L} \times 21.2\ \text{MJ/L} = 63{,}600\ \text{MJ/ha} = 63.6\ \text{GJ/ha}
$$

**Step 2 — Comparative ratio:**

$$
\frac{E_{FAME}}{E_{EtOH}} = \frac{190.5}{63.6} = 3.00
$$

**Step 3 — Land-efficiency verdict:**

> **Oil-palm biodiesel is ~3× more land-efficient** than sugarcane ethanol in this comparison. The reasons are:
>
> 1. **Higher energy density:** FAME (40.1 MJ/kg) ≫ Ethanol (29.7 MJ/kg) — biodiesel carries ~35% more energy per kg.
> 2. **Near-unity mass yield:** Transesterification is mass-additive (~1:1), whereas ethanol fermentation has a maximum theoretical yield of 0.51 kg EtOH per kg glucose (with CO₂ lost).
> 3. **Higher oil-crop productivity per ha** in tropical climates.
>
> **Counter-considerations:** Ethanol avoids the "food vs fuel" conflict less severely (sugarcane is a primary food in some regions), and has lower NOₓ and particulate emissions in SI engines. Biodiesel is preferred in CI engines and has superior cetane number (~50–60 vs. gasoline's ~15).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in HHV vs LHV** — Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if you use HHV when LHV is asked (engine/fuel economy problems).
> 2. **Forgetting density in volumetric-to-mass conversion** — A mass yield of 4,750 kg is *not* the same as 4,750 L. Always divide by density (0.88 kg/L for FAME; 0.789 kg/L for ethanol).
> 3. **Using only Gay-Lussac theoretical yield** without applying fermentation/distillation efficiency — Real problems require the **practical** yield; the theoretical value is a ceiling, not the answer.
> 4. **Sign error in NEB:** $E_{net} = E_{out} - E_{in}$, not $E_{in} - E_{out}$.
> 5. **Forgetting land-area normalization** — Examiners want **per hectare** answers explicitly. State units at every step.
> 6. **Confusing EROI with Conversion Efficiency** — EROI is energy-out/energy-in; $\eta_{conv}$ is mass-yield ratio. They are *not* the same quantity.
> 7. **Ignoring the CO₂ loss in fermentation** — A common conceptual error: students assume glucose → ethanol mass-yield is higher than 51%. It is not; the C-budget dictates this maximum.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read 2× before every KTU exam:**

- **Energy yield** = (crop productivity) × (convertible fraction) × (conversion efficiency) × (HHV of fuel).
- **HHV > LHV**; LHV = HHV − $h_{fg}$ × (9H/100) — use LHV for engines, HHV for boiler calculations.
- **Fermentation:** $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$; max mass yield = **51.1%**.
- **Transesterification:** Triglyceride + 3 MeOH → 3 FAME + Glycerol; mass yield ≈ **1:1**.
- **Anaerobic digestion (Buswell):** glucose → 3 CH₄ + 3 CO₂; **0.373 m³ CH₄ per kg glucose**.
- **EROI > 1** is necessary; **> 3** is the practical sustainability threshold.
- **Land-productivity ranking (high → low):** Microalgae > Oil palm > Sugarcane > Rapeseed > Soybean > Maize (for liquid fuels).
- **Sugarcane ethanol (Brazil) EROI ≈ 8–10** — gold standard reference value.
- **Maize ethanol (USA) EROI ≈ 1.3–1.8** — marginal sustainability.
- **Microalgae theoretical biodiesel yield** can reach **50,000 L/ha/yr** but with current technology **5,000–15,000 L/ha/yr** is realistic.
- **Bagasse co-generation** boosts sugarcane-ethanol EROI dramatically — never ignore it.
- **Energy crops are 1st Gen (food-based), 2nd Gen (lignocellulosic), 3rd Gen (algae)** — exam favourite.
- **Volumetric energy density** of biodiesel (~32.7 MJ/L) is closer to diesel (35.3 MJ/L) than ethanol (21.2 MJ/L).
- **Net energy balance formula:** $\text{NEB} = (E_{out} - E_{in})/E_{in} = \text{EROI} - 1$.
- **Always state units (kg, L, MJ, GJ, ha)** in every derivation step — KTU examiners reward unit discipline.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy content of biofuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy content of biofuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Energy Content" of a Biofuel?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> The **energy content of a biofuel** is formally defined as the amount of useful heat liberated per unit mass (or per unit volume) when the biofuel undergoes complete combustion with oxygen under standard conditions (typically 25 °C and 101.325 kPa). It is more formally called the **Calorific Value (CV)** or **Heat of Combustion (ΔH_c)** of the fuel, and is measured in **kJ/kg** or **MJ/kg**.

There are two universally accepted versions of this quantity:

| Terminology | Standard Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|-------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Higher Heating Value** | **HHV** (also called *Gross Calorific Value, GCV*) | Heat released when combustion water is condensed back to liquid (latent heat recovered). |
| **Lower Heating Value** | **LHV** (also called *Net Calorific Value, NCV*) | Heat released when combustion water leaves as vapour (latent heat NOT recovered). |

Numerically, **HHV > LHV**, because the LHV discards the energy tied up in vaporising the moisture and the hydrogen-bound water of combustion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrogen's Penalty:** Every 1 kg of hydrogen (H) in a fuel produces 9 kg of water upon combustion. Therefore, hydrogen-rich biofuels (like bioethanol, C₂H₅OH) have a much larger HHV–LHV gap than hydrocarbon fuels.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **boiling 1 litre of water using two camp stoves**, one burning **biodiesel** and the other burning **dried wood pellets**.

- The stove emits hot flames; you feel heat.
- The water temperature rises until it reaches 100 °C and starts to boil.
- The **total heat you actually extracted** is the *real* energy available to do work — this is the **LHV**.
- If you were to then *condense* all the steam back into your pot (using a cold coil) and capture that extra warmth, you'd get even more heat back — that bonus heat is the *latent heat of vaporisation*, and including it gives you the **HHV**.

So **HHV is the "thermodynamic maximum"** and **LHV is the "engineer's realistic number"** because real engines, boilers, and turbines always exhaust hot water vapour, losing the latent heat.

## 1.3 Why Does Energy Content Matter for Biofuels?

The energy content dictates:

- **Storage tank sizing** (for vehicles – affected by *volumetric* energy density, MJ/L).
- **Transportation economics** (a fuel with low MJ/kg means more truck-trips per joule delivered).
- **Combustor design** (feed rate for a given thermal output).
- **Engine knock resistance** and **cetane/octane rating** linkage.
- **Carbon accounting** — when comparing biofuels vs fossil fuels, the *energy-equivalent* substitution ratio is used.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Conditions (KTU convention):** Energy content is measured with the fuel at **25 °C, 1 atm**, and the products of combustion returned to **25 °C** (HHV) or with water as vapour (LHV) at 25 °C.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Constants (must memorise)

> **Universal Combustion Constants**
> - Latent heat of vaporisation of water at 25 °C: **h_fg ≈ 2260 kJ/kg** (≈ 44 kJ/mol)
> - Molar mass of water: **18.015 g/mol**
> - Standard enthalpy of formation of CO₂(g): **−393.5 kJ/mol**
> - Standard enthalpy of formation of H₂O(l): **−285.8 kJ/mol**
> - Standard enthalpy of formation of H₂O(g): **−241.8 kJ/mol**
> - Standard enthalpy of formation of H₂O(v): **−44 kJ/mol** (at 25 °C)
> - Universal gas constant: **R_u = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Foundation

For any combustible fuel containing carbon (C), hydrogen (H), oxygen (O), sulphur (S) and nitrogen (N), the *stoichiometric combustion reaction* is written as:

$$C_x H_y O_z S_w N_v \; + \; \left(x + \frac{y}{4} + \frac{w}{2} - \frac{z}{2}\right) O_2 \; \longrightarrow \; x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O + w\,SO_2 + \frac{v}{2}\,N_2$$

The **HHV** is given by Hess's Law:

$$HHV = \sum_i \left( n_i \, \Delta H_{f,i}^{\text{products}} \right) - \sum_j \left( n_j \, \Delta H_{f,j}^{\text{reactants}} \right)$$

The **LHV** subtracts the latent heat carried away by the water vapour:

$$LHV = HHV - h_{fg} \cdot m_{H_2O}$$

where **m_H₂O** is the mass of water produced per kg of fuel.

## 2.2 The HHV ↔ LHV Conversion (Critical Engineering Equation)

For every **1 kg of fuel**, the hydrogen fraction (by mass) reacts as:

$$H + \frac{1}{4} O_2 \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2} H_2O \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{mass of water produced} = 9 \cdot \frac{H}{100}$$

Therefore, the standard KTU-grade conversion is:

$$\boxed{\,HHV \;=\; LHV \;+\; 2.260 \times \frac{9H}{100} \;=\; LHV \;+\; 0.2034\,H\,}$$

where **H** is the mass percentage of hydrogen in the fuel. This is the most-tested equation in Module 1 problems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables / Units | Use Case |
|---|---------|-------------------|----------|
| 1 | $HHV = LHV + 2.260 \cdot \frac{9H}{100}$ | H = mass % of hydrogen | Convert HHV ↔ LHV |
| 2 | $HHV_{Dulong} \;(\text{MJ/kg}) = 0.3383\,C + 1.442\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.0929\,S$ | C, H, O, S in mass % | Estimate HHV from ultimate analysis |
| 3 | $HHV_{\text{Channiwala}} \;(\text{MJ/kg}) = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H - 0.1034\,O - 0.0211\,N + 0.1005\,S$ | Ultimate analysis mass % | Better correlation for biomass |
| 4 | $LHV = HHV - h_{fg} \cdot \left[ \frac{9H}{100} + M \right]$ | M = moisture fraction (mass) | Wet fuel correction |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{combustion}} = \dfrac{\text{Heat absorbed by water}}{\text{Fuel mass} \times \text{HHV}} \times 100\%$ | Percent | Bomb calorimeter efficiency check |
| 6 | $\rho_E \;(\text{energy density}) = \rho \cdot LHV$ | ρ = fuel density (kg/L) | Compare liquid biofuels by volume |
| 7 | $\text{Substitution Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{LHV}_{\text{fossil}}}{\text{LHV}_{\text{biofuel}}}$ | Dimensionless | Biofuel–fossil displacement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Dulong's formula boundary:** It assumes all carbon is free carbon. It becomes less accurate for fuels with > 30% oxygen (most biomass). Use Channiwala's correlation for agricultural residues, wood, and bagasse.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Why Energy Content Matters |
|----------------------|----------------------------|
| **Diesel Engine Substitution** | Biodiesel (LHV ≈ 37 MJ/kg) replaces petro-diesel (43 MJ/kg); fuel injectors must flow ~16 % more by mass for same power. |
| **Combined Heat & Power (CHP)** | Biogas (LHV ≈ 35 MJ/m³) is metered in m³; accurate calorific value determines revenue. |
| **Biocoal/Briquettes** | Torrefied wood pellets reach ~20–22 MJ/kg vs 17 MJ/kg for raw sawdust — premium pricing justified. |
| **Aviation Biofuels** | Jet-A ≈ 43 MJ/kg; HEFA bio-jet must reach ≥ 42.8 MJ/kg to be "drop-in." |
| **Policy / Blending Mandates** | E10, E20, B20 mandates use *volumetric* energy density to compute fuel-tax parity. |

## 2.5 Experimental Determination — The Bomb Calorimeter

The HHV of solid and liquid biofuels is measured using a **bomb calorimeter**, which operates on the *isothermal jacket / adiabatic* principle:

$$HHV = \dfrac{(W \cdot \Delta T + e_1 + e_2) - e_3}{m_f}$$

where:
- **W** = water equivalent of calorimeter + water (kg, with specific heat absorbed) → product has units kJ/°C
- **ΔT** = temperature rise of water (°C)
- **e₁** = heat from fuse wire ignition (kJ)
- **e₂** = heat from acid formation (HNO₃, H₂SO₄) (kJ)
- **e₃** = heat from incomplete combustion (kJ) — usually 0 if combustion is complete
- **m_f** = mass of fuel sample (kg)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: HHV → LHV Conversion Formula

**Starting Premise:** A fuel of mass *m_f* contains *H* % hydrogen by mass.

### Step 1 — Mass of hydrogen in the sample
$$m_H = m_f \cdot \frac{H}{100}$$

### Step 2 — Combustion stoichiometry of hydrogen
$$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$$

The molar mass of H₂ is 2 g/mol, and of H₂O is 18 g/mol. Thus, **1 kg of H₂ produces 9 kg of H₂O** upon combustion.

### Step 3 — Mass of water formed
$$m_{H_2O} = 9 \cdot m_H = 9 \cdot m_f \cdot \frac{H}{100}$$

### Step 4 — Latent heat lost when water leaves as vapour
The latent heat of vaporisation at 25 °C is **h_fg = 2260 kJ/kg**.

$$q_{fg} = h_{fg} \cdot m_{H_2O} = 2260 \times 9 \cdot m_f \cdot \frac{H}{100}$$

### Step 5 — Normalise per kg of fuel (divide by m_f)
$$\frac{q_{fg}}{m_f} = \frac{2260 \times 9}{100} \cdot H = 203.4 \cdot H \;\;(\text{kJ/kg of fuel})$$

### Step 6 — Definition of LHV
LHV is the heat released when water remains as vapour, i.e., when latent heat is *not* recovered. Therefore:

$$LHV = HHV - 203.4 \cdot H$$

$$\boxed{\,\therefore\; HHV = LHV + 0.2034\,H\,}$$

### Step 7 — Numerical Verification
Take methane, CH₄: M = 16, H = 4/16 = 25 %.
- Real: HHV = 55.5 MJ/kg; LHV = 50.0 MJ/kg
- Predicted gap = 0.2034 × 25 = 5.085 MJ/kg ≈ 5.5 MJ/kg ✓ (matches literature within 1 %).

## 3.2 Derivation: Dulong's Formula

Dulong (1880) observed that the HHV of a fuel is an *additive* function of its elemental composition, weighted by their specific combustion energies.

**Assumptions:** Combustion is complete; the oxygen in the fuel first combines with available hydrogen to form water, leaving free hydrogen for combustion.

### Step 1 — Effective hydrogen available
For a fuel with H % hydrogen and O % oxygen, the oxygen binds:
$$H_{\text{bound}} = \frac{O}{8} \;\;(\% \text{by mass})$$

Net effective hydrogen:
$$H_{\text{eff}} = H - \frac{O}{8}$$

### Step 2 — Specific heat released by each element (empirical, per kg)
- Carbon → CO₂: 33.83 MJ/kg-C
- Hydrogen → H₂O(l): 144.2 MJ/kg-H
- Sulphur → SO₂: 9.29 MJ/kg-S

### Step 3 — Combine additively

$$HHV_{Dulong} = 33.83\,C + 144.2\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9.29\,S \;\;(\text{MJ/kg})$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example: Wood Pellets
Ultimate analysis: C = 50 %, H = 6 %, O = 43 %, S = 0.1 %, N = 0.5 %, ash = 0.4 %.

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
HHV &= 33.83 \times 0.50 + 144.2 \times \left(0.06 - \frac{0.43}{8}\right) + 9.29 \times 0.001 \\
&= 16.915 + 144.2 \times (0.06 - 0.05375) + 0.00929 \\
&= 16.915 + 144.2 \times 0.00625 + 0.00929 \\
&= 16.915 + 0.901 + 0.009 \\
&= 17.825 \;\; \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the experimental value (~17–18 MJ/kg) for softwood pellets. ✓

## 3.3 Worked Example: LHV of Moist Biogas Slurry (Analytical Matrix)

A digester produces a slurry residue with the following analysis:
- Moisture M = 60 % (mass)
- HHV of dry solid = 18 MJ/kg
- H % of dry solid = 5.5 %

**Step A** — HHV of wet slurry (HHV doesn't change with moisture — water just dilutes):

$$HHV_{\text{wet}} = HHV_{\text{dry}} \times (1 - M) = 18 \times 0.40 = 7.20 \;\; \text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step B** — Effective hydrogen fraction in wet fuel:

$$H_{\text{wet}} = 5.5 \times (1 - M) = 5.5 \times 0.40 = 2.20 \;\; \%$$

**Step C** — LHV using HHV–LHV relation:

$$LHV_{\text{wet}} = HHV_{\text{wet}} - 0.2034 \times H_{\text{wet}} = 7.20 - 0.2034 \times 2.20 = 7.20 - 0.4475$$

$$\boxed{\,LHV_{\text{wet}} \approx 6.75 \;\; \text{MJ/kg}\,}$$

**Step D** — Verification using the all-inclusive moisture penalty:
Total water evaporated = moisture water + combustion water:

$$LHV = HHV - h_{fg}\left[M + \frac{9H_{\text{wet}}}{100}\right]$$

$$LHV = 7.20 - 2.260\left[0.60 + \frac{9 \times 2.20}{100}\right] = 7.20 - 2.260 \times (0.60 + 0.198)$$

$$LHV = 7.20 - 2.260 \times 0.798 = 7.20 - 1.804 = 5.40 \;\; \text{MJ/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Discrepancy Resolution:** The two values differ because the simple formula $LHV = HHV - 0.2034 H$ **does NOT include the moisture content M**. Always use the full expression when M > 5 %.

## 3.4 Energy Content Matrix for Common Biofuels (Revision-Grade)

| Biofuel | Chemical Form | HHV (MJ/kg) | LHV (MJ/kg) | Density (kg/L) | Energy Density (MJ/L) |
|---------|---------------|-------------|-------------|----------------|------------------------|
| Wood (air-dry) | C₆H₉O₄ (approx.) | 20.0 | 18.5 | 0.70 (bulk) | ~13.0 |
| Torrefied Pellet | — | 21.5 | 20.0 | 0.75 (bulk) | ~15.0 |
| Bagasse | C₆H₁₀O₅ | 19.0 | 17.0 | 0.18 (bulk) | ~3.06 |
| Bioethanol | C₂H₅OH | 29.7 | 26.8 | 0.789 | 21.1 |
| Biodiesel (FAME) | C₁₉H₃₆O₂ | 40.2 | 37.2 | 0.880 | 32.7 |
| Bio-butanol | C₄H₉OH | 36.6 | 33.1 | 0.808 | 26.7 |
| Biogas (60 % CH₄) | CH₄ + CO₂ | 22.7 | 20.5 | 1.16 kg/m³ | 19.6 MJ/m³ |
| Biogas (pure CH₄) | CH₄ | 55.5 | 50.0 | 0.717 kg/m³ | 35.8 MJ/m³ |
| Producer Gas | CO + H₂ + N₂ | 6.4 | 5.9 | 1.16 kg/m³ | 6.8 MJ/m³ |
| Hydrogen (renewable) | H₂ | 142.0 | 120.0 | 0.071 (liq) | 8.5 MJ/L (liq) |
