<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define specific growth rate and doubling time. Derive the relationship between them. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEBBT413]`

**Model Answer:**

The **specific growth rate ($\mu$)** is defined as the rate of increase of biomass per unit amount of biomass:

$$\mu = \frac{1}{X} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt} \quad \text{with units } h^{-1}$$

The **doubling time ($t_d$)** is the time required for the biomass concentration to double during the exponential growth phase.

**Relationship:** Starting from the exponential growth law $X_t = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu t}$ and setting $X_t = 2X_0$:

$$t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$$

Hence, **higher $\mu$** → **shorter doubling time** → faster fermentation. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: What is the Monod constant $K_s$? What does it represent operationally in a bioethanol fermenter? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEBBT413]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Monod constant ($K_s$)** is the substrate concentration at which the **specific growth rate ($\mu$) equals exactly half of $\mu_{max}$**. It is a measure of the **affinity** of the microorganism for the substrate — **lower $K_s$** means **higher affinity** (microbe grows fast even at low substrate levels).

**Operational Significance in a Bioethanol Fermenter:**
For *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* fermenting glucose, typical $K_s$ is around **0.1 – 0.5 g/L**. In a fed-batch bioethanol process, the feed rate must be controlled so that residual glucose never drops below ~$5 \times K_s$ to avoid ethanol-inhibited slow growth. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat labelled sketch, derive the **Monod equation** for microbial growth. Discuss the effect of substrate concentration on the specific growth rate and identify the regions of **zero-order** and **first-order** kinetics. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, PEBBT413]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Empirical Basis:** Monod (1949) observed that for a single limiting substrate, microbial growth follows a saturation kinetics analogous to the Michaelis-Menten model for enzymes:

$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}$$

where $\mu$ = specific growth rate ($h^{-1}$), $\mu_{max}$ = maximum specific growth rate ($h^{-1}$), $S$ = limiting substrate concentration (g/L), $K_s$ = Monod half-saturation constant (g/L).

**Step 2 — Saturation Curve Sketch:**

```
mu
 ^
 |     __________________ mu_max
 |   /
 |  /
 | /
 |/_____ S = K_s
 0 ---------> S
```

**Step 3 — Limiting Cases:**

**Case 1: $S \gg K_s$ (high substrate):**

$$K_s + S \approx S \implies \mu \approx \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{S} = \mu_{max}$$

Growth is **zero-order** with respect to $S$ — independent of substrate concentration. The microbes are growing at full speed; enzymes are saturated. **(1.5 marks)**

**Case 2: $S \ll K_s$ (low substrate):**

$$K_s + S \approx K_s \implies \mu \approx \frac{\mu_{max}}{K_s} \cdot S$$

Growth is **first-order** with respect to $S$ — directly proportional to substrate concentration. The microbes are substrate-starved. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 4 — Significance of $K_s$:** At $S = K_s$, $\mu = \mu_{max}/2$. This is the **half-saturation point** — a fingerprint of the microbe-substrate pair. Low $K_s$ (e.g., 0.1 g/L for yeast on glucose) → high affinity → good for low-substrate industrial processes. **(1 mark)**

**Engineering Implication:** In a CSTR operating at dilution rate $D = \mu$, the residual substrate is $S = D \cdot K_s / (\mu_{max} - D)$. Higher $D$ → higher residual $S$ → risk of substrate washout. **(1 mark)**

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Pirt equation** relating observed yield $Y_{X/S}$, true growth yield $Y_G$, maintenance coefficient $m_s$, and specific growth rate $\mu$. How is this equation used to determine $m_s$ experimentally? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, PEBBT413]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Premise:** Substrate is consumed for two purposes only — **growth** and **maintenance**. The specific substrate consumption rate $q_S$ is:

$$q_S = \frac{\mu}{Y_G} + m_s$$

**Step 2 — Define observed yield:**

$$Y_{X/S} = \frac{\mu}{q_S} = \frac{\mu}{\dfrac{\mu}{Y_G} + m_s}$$

**Step 3 — Invert:**

$$\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}} = \frac{1}{Y_G} + \frac{m_s}{\mu}$$

This is the **Pirt equation**. **(3 marks)**

**Step 4 — Experimental Determination of $m_s$:**
- Run **chemostat (continuous culture)** experiments at multiple dilution rates $D$ (= $\mu$ at steady state).
- Measure $Y_{X/S}$ at each $D$.
- Plot $\dfrac{1}{Y_{X/S}}$ on the **y-axis** vs $\dfrac{1}{\mu}$ on the **x-axis**.
- The plot is a **straight line**:
  - **Slope** = $m_s$ (maintenance coefficient, g substrate / g cells · h)
  - **Y-intercept** = $\dfrac{1}{Y_G}$ (inverse of true growth yield) **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** As $\mu \to \infty$ (very fast growth), $\dfrac{m_s}{\mu} \to 0$, so $Y_{X/S} \to Y_G$. As $\mu \to 0$ (very slow growth), $Y_{X/S} \to 0$ — almost all substrate is burnt for maintenance. **(2 marks)**

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Luedeking-Piret model** for product formation. Classify product formation kinetics into three types and give one industrial bioenergy example for each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEBBT413]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Luedeking-Piret Equation (1959):**

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = Y_{P/X} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt} + \beta \cdot X$$

- $Y_{P/X}$ = growth-associated product formation constant (g product / g biomass)
- $\beta$ = non-growth-associated constant (g product / g cells · h)

**Step 2 — Three Classes of Product Formation:** **(3 marks for classification)**

| Class | Condition | Description | Industrial Bioenergy Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I — Growth-Associated** | $Y_{P/X} > 0$, $\beta = 0$ | Product forms only when cells grow | **Ethanol** by *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* |
| **Type II — Mixed** | $Y_{P/X} > 0$, $\beta > 0$ | Product forms during growth and stationary phases | **Lactic acid** by *Lactobacillus*; some **butanol** fermentations |
| **Type III — Non-Growth-Associated** | $Y_{P/X} = 0$, $\beta > 0$ | Product forms only in stationary phase | **Some antibiotics**; secondary metabolites (rare in bioenergy) |

**Step 3 — Engineering Implication:** For **bioethanol** (Type I), the reactor must maintain active growth phase (high $\mu$) to maximize $Y_{P/X}$. For **mixed-type** products, a two-stage strategy (grow then produce) is optimal. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Mathematical Derivation (for Type I):** With $\beta = 0$:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = Y_{P/X} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt}$$

Integrating: $P - P_0 = Y_{P/X} \cdot (X - X_0)$, so $Y_{P/X} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta X}$. **(2 marks)**

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A continuous stirred-tank bioreactor is operated for bioethanol production using *Zymomonas mobilis*. Given:
- $S_0 = 100$ g/L (glucose in feed)
- $\mu_{max} = 0.30 \, h^{-1}$, $K_s = 0.2$ g/L
- $Y_{X/S} = 0.05$ g cells / g substrate
- Dilution rate $D = 0.25 \, h^{-1}$

**Calculate:** (i) Residual substrate $S$, (ii) Biomass concentration $X$, (iii) Doubling time $t_d$ of the culture, (iv) Volumetric ethanol productivity if $Y_{P/S} = 0.48$ g ethanol / g glucose. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEBBT413]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Residual Substrate $S$:**
At steady state in a CSTR, $D = \mu$, so using Monod:

$$D = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S} \implies 0.25 = 0.30 \cdot \frac{S}{0.2 + S}$$

$$\frac{0.25}{0.30} = \frac{S}{0.2 + S} \implies 0.8333 = \frac{S}{0.2 + S}$$

$$0.8333 \cdot (0.2 + S) = S \implies 0.1667 + 0.8333 \, S = S \implies 0.1667 = 0.1667 \, S$$

$$\boxed{S = 1.0 \, \text{g/L}}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Biomass Concentration $X$:**

$$X = Y_{X/S} \cdot (S_0 - S) = 0.05 \cdot (100 - 1.0) = 0.05 \cdot 99 = \boxed{4.95 \, \text{g/L}}$$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — Doubling Time $t_d$:** At steady state, $\mu = D = 0.25 \, h^{-1}$:

$$t_d = \frac{0.693}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{0.25} = \boxed{2.77 \, \text{hours}}$$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 4 — Volumetric Ethanol Productivity $Q_P$:**

Ethanol concentration in effluent: $P = Y_{P/S} \cdot (S_0 - S) = 0.48 \cdot 99 = 47.52$ g/L

Volumetric productivity (in CSTR, equals $D \cdot P$):

$$Q_P = D \cdot P = 0.25 \cdot 47.52 = \boxed{11.88 \, \text{g ethanol / L · h}}$$ **(2 marks)**

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls (lose 1–2 marks if missed):**
> 1. **Confusing $\mu_{max}$ with $\mu$** — they are NOT the same. $\mu$ is the *actual* growth rate at substrate $S$; $\mu_{max}$ is the theoretical maximum (only achieved when $S \to \infty$).
> 2. **In Pirt equation derivations**, students often forget to invert or skip showing the algebraic step where $q_S$ is substituted.
> 3. **In CSTR problems**, students forget that at steady state, $\mu = D$ — this single substitution unlocks 80% of the problem.
> 4. **Sign convention** — substrate concentration **decreases** with time, so $\frac{dS}{dt}$ is **negative**. Use negative signs in mass balances; the yield is defined with a negative sign in $\Delta S$.
> 5. **Units consistency** — always show units in the final answer. KTU examiners deduct marks for unitless numerical answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Biological Kinetics and Yields):**

- ☐ **Monod Equation** — the single most important formula: $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot S / (K_s + S)$.
- ☐ **Specific growth rate $\mu$** = $(1/X) \cdot (dX/dt)$; in exponential phase, $X_t = X_0 e^{\mu t}$.
- ☐ **Doubling time** $t_d = 0.693 / \mu$ — derived from setting $X_t = 2X_0$.
- ☐ **Biomass yield** $Y_{X/S} = -\Delta X / \Delta S$ — observed yield, not theoretical.
- ☐ **Pirt equation** — $\dfrac{1}{Y_{X/S}} = \dfrac{1}{Y_G} + \dfrac{m_s}{\mu}$ — straight-line plot gives $m_s$ (slope) and $Y_G$ (intercept).
- ☐ **Luedeking-Piret** — $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = Y_{P/X} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt} + \beta X$ — three product formation types (growth, mixed, non-growth associated).
- ☐ **CSTR / Chemostat** — at steady state, $\mu = D$; residual $S = D K_s / (\mu_{max} - D)$.
- ☐ **Limiting cases of Monod** — $S \gg K_s$: zero-order; $S \ll K_s$: first-order.
- ☐ **Bioenergy relevance** — ethanol (yeast, Type I), biogas (anaerobic digestion, mixed), biodiesel (algal, growth-associated lipids).
- ☐ **Always include units** in numerical answers and show the negative sign in $\Delta S$ to maintain sign convention.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Yield improvements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Yield improvements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Yield Improvement** in the context of bioenergy and biofuels refers to the systematic application of biological, chemical, genetic, and process-engineering strategies to maximize the quantity of usable energy (or fuel) recovered per unit of feedstock input. It is quantified as the **ratio of the actual product output (energy or mass) to the theoretical maximum** achievable from a given substrate under defined process conditions.

Mathematically, the generalized yield expression used across KTU Module 2 is:

$$\eta_{yield} = \frac{Y_{actual}}{Y_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$$

The three primary yields addressed in this module are:

| Yield Type | Symbol | Unit | Definition |
|------------|--------|------|------------|
| Biomass Yield | $Y_b$ | tonnes/ha/year | Dry biomass produced per unit land per unit time |
| Biofuel Yield | $Y_f$ | L/ha/year | Liters of liquid fuel produced per hectare per year |
| Conversion Yield | $\eta_c$ | % | Mass of fuel obtained per mass of feedstock processed |

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Farm-to-Fuel Field Analogy:** Imagine a sugarcane farmer. The farmer's job is not just to grow cane (biomass) but to extract the maximum liters of ethanol from every tonne of cane crushed. *Yield improvement* is the equivalent of: (a) choosing a sweeter, pest-resistant cane variety (genetic improvement), (b) using better fertilizer and irrigation to grow taller canes (agronomic improvement), (c) using a more efficient crusher that squeezes out every drop of juice (mechanical/pretreatment improvement), and (d) using a more efficient fermentation tank that converts nearly all sugars into ethanol (microbial/catalytic improvement). Each of these is a "yield improvement lever."

### 1.3 The KTU 2024 Scope of Yield Improvements

The KTU Module 2 syllabus covers yield improvements across four interconnected fronts:

1. **Biological Yield Improvements** — Strain engineering, microbial selection, plant breeding.
2. **Biochemical Yield Improvements** — Pretreatment optimization, enzyme efficiency, fermentation conditions.
3. **Thermochemical Yield Improvements** — Pyrolysis temperature, gasification equivalence ratio, catalyst loading.
4. **Process Integration Yields** — Cascading biorefinery outputs (lignin, biogas from residues).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Yield questions are *quantitative*. Always carry units and convert between mass, volume, and energy bases. Marks are lost when students write only numbers without units.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Maximum Yields — The Stoichiometric Ceiling

Every biological or thermochemical conversion has a **stoichiometric ceiling** — a theoretical maximum dictated by chemistry. Yield improvement is fundamentally about closing the gap between real-world performance and this ceiling.

#### 2.1.1 Ethanol from Glucose (Fermentation)

The Gay-Lussac stoichiometry for ethanol fermentation is:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$

**Molecular weight analysis:**
- Glucose: $180.16$ g/mol
- Ethanol: $46.07$ g/mol × 2 = $92.14$ g/mol

The theoretical mass yield of ethanol from glucose:

$$Y_{ethanol/glucose} = \frac{2 \times 46.07}{180.16} = 0.511 \text{ g ethanol / g glucose} = 51.1\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Constant:** The theoretical maximum ethanol yield is **0.511 g/g glucose** or equivalently **0.568 L ethanol / kg glucose** (using ethanol density $\rho = 789$ kg/m³).

#### 2.1.2 Biodiesel (FAME) from Triglycerides

Transesterification stoichiometry:

$$Triglyceride + 3\,CH_3OH \rightarrow 3\,R-COOCH_3 + Glycerol$$

The theoretical mass yield of FAME per mass of oil is approximately **100%** by mass (1 mole triglyceride → 3 moles FAME, with near-equivalent molecular weight due to the methoxy group replacing the glycerol backbone).

#### 2.1.3 Methane from Anaerobic Digestion (Buswell Equation)

$$C_cH_hO_oN_nS_s + \text{water} \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2 + NH_3 + H_2S$$

The Buswell equation gives theoretical methane yield as:

$$Y_{CH_4,\,theoretical} = \frac{(c/2 + h/8 - o/4 - 3n/8 - s/4) \times 16}{14c + h + 16o + 14n + 32s} \quad \text{(g CH}_4\text{/g VS)}$$

### 2.2 The "Four Levers" of Yield Improvement (KTU Framework)

The KTU Module 2 learning outcomes can be organized into four intervention levers:

**Lever 1 — Feedstock Side (Agronomic):**
- Selection of high-yield energy crops (Miscanthus, switchgrass, jatropha).
- Genetic modification for higher cellulose/lipid/sugar content.
- Optimized harvesting schedules (peak oil content in oilseeds, peak sugar in sugarcane).

**Lever 2 — Pretreatment Side (Process Upstream):**
- Physical (milling, steam explosion).
- Chemical (dilute acid, alkali, organosolv).
- Biological (white-rot fungi).
- Goal: increase accessible sugars/polymers by **breaking the lignin seal**.

**Lever 3 — Conversion Side (Core Process):**
- Microbial strain engineering (recombinant yeasts tolerating higher ethanol concentrations).
- Immobilized enzyme systems (reusable cellulases).
- Catalysts in transesterification (heterogeneous base catalysts: CaO, MgO).
- Supercritical methanol processing (no catalyst needed, higher yields).

**Lever 4 — Downstream / Recovery Side:**
- Energy-efficient distillation (vacuum, molecular sieves).
- In-situ product recovery (pervaporation, solvent extraction).
- Co-product valorization (glycerol to biogas, lignin to phenolics).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every high-yield formula you must know for the KTU University Exam on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{biomass} \times \eta_{conversion} \times \eta_{recovery}$ | Multiplicative chain rule of yields | dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $Y_{ethanol/theoretical} = 0.511\,m_{glucose}$ | Max ethanol from sugar | g ethanol |
| 3 | $Y_{biodiesel} = \frac{m_{FAME}}{m_{oil}} \approx 1.0$ | Transesterification stoichiometry | kg/kg |
| 4 | $Y_{biogas} = \frac{V_{CH_4}}{m_{VS}}$ | Biochemical methane potential | m³/kg VS |
| 5 | $Y_{energy} = m_{fuel} \times LHV_{fuel}$ | Energy yield | MJ or GJ |
| 6 | $\eta_{photosynthetic} = \frac{\Delta H_{biomass}}{E_{solar,\,absorbed}}$ | Solar-to-biomass efficiency | % |
| 7 | $Y_{oil} = \frac{m_{oil}}{m_{seed}} \times 100$ | Oilseed oil content | wt% |
| 8 | $E_{harvest} = Y_b \times A \times LHV_b$ | Annual energy harvest | GJ/ha/yr |
| 9 | $CF = \frac{m_{cellulose}}{m_{biomass}} \times \eta_{glucan\_to\_glucose} \times 0.511$ | Cellulosic ethanol yield | g/g |
| 10 | $Y_{hydrogen} = \frac{n_{H_2}}{n_{glucose}} \times \frac{2\,g/mol}{180\,g/mol}$ | Dark/photo fermentation H₂ | g/g |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Yield Improvements

In industrial biorefineries, even a **1% improvement in conversion yield** can translate to millions of dollars annually. For example:

- Increasing ethanol yield from 90% to 95% of theoretical on a 100,000 tonne/year sugar stream = **2,555 tonnes additional ethanol/year**.
- At ₹45/L ex-distillery price → ~**₹14.5 crore ($1.75M) additional revenue/year**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Yield improvements are *cumulative but diminishing*. The first 80% of theoretical yield is achievable with mature technology; the next 15% requires process intensification; the final 5% often costs more than it returns.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Theoretical Ethanol Yield from a Cellulosic Feedstock

**Problem Setup:** Consider a lignocellulosic biomass (e.g., rice straw) with the following composition on a dry basis:

- Cellulose: $40\%$
- Hemicellulose (xylan): $25\%$
- Lignin: $20\%$
- Ash: $5\%$
- Extractives & others: $10\%$

We want to compute the **maximum theoretical ethanol yield per kg of dry rice straw**, assuming:
- Cellulose → glucose (100% hydrolysis efficiency assumed).
- Hemicellulose → xylose (100% hydrolysis, but xylose fermentation yields **theoretically less ethanol than glucose**).
- Glucose fermentation follows Gay-Lussac (0.511 g/g).
- Xylose fermentation (via recombinant yeast) follows:

$$3\,C_5H_{10}O_5 \rightarrow 5\,C_2H_5OH + 5\,CO_2$$

Yield from xylose: $Y_{ethanol/xylose} = \frac{5 \times 46.07}{3 \times 150.13} = 0.511$ g/g — *same theoretical yield!*

But hemicellulose hydrolysate contains pentoses and hexoses, and in practice yields are lower due to inhibitor formation.

#### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Mass of cellulose per kg straw:**

$$m_{cellulose} = 1.0 \text{ kg} \times 0.40 = 0.40 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of glucose liberated from cellulose:**

Each kg of cellulose (polymer of glucose units, MW = 162 g/mol per anhydroglucose unit) yields upon hydrolysis:

$$\text{Hydrolysis reaction:}\quad (C_6H_{10}O_5)_n + n\,H_2O \rightarrow n\,C_6H_{12}O_6$$

Mass of glucose per kg cellulose:

$$m_{glucose} = m_{cellulose} \times \frac{180.16}{162.14} = 0.40 \times 1.111 = 0.4444 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Ethanol from cellulose-derived glucose:**

$$m_{ethanol,\,1} = 0.4444 \times 0.511 = 0.2271 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of hemicellulose per kg straw:**

$$m_{hemicellulose} = 1.0 \times 0.25 = 0.25 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Mass of xylose liberated from hemicellulose (xylan → xylose):**

Xylan anhydroxylose unit MW = 132.11 g/mol; xylose MW = 150.13 g/mol.

$$m_{xylose} = 0.25 \times \frac{150.13}{132.11} = 0.25 \times 1.136 = 0.284 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 — Ethanol from hemicellulose-derived xylose:**

$$m_{ethanol,\,2} = 0.284 \times 0.511 = 0.1451 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 7 — Total theoretical ethanol yield:**

$$m_{ethanol,\,total} = m_{ethanol,\,1} + m_{ethanol,\,2} = 0.2271 + 0.1451 = 0.3722 \text{ kg ethanol/kg straw}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to volume basis (ethanol density = 0.789 kg/L):**

$$V_{ethanol} = \frac{0.3722}{0.789} = 0.4717 \text{ L/kg straw}$$

**Step 9 — Express as energy yield (LHV of ethanol = 26.9 MJ/kg):**

$$E_{ethanol} = 0.3722 \times 26.9 = 10.01 \text{ MJ/kg straw}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Theoretical maximum from rice straw is ~**0.47 L ethanol/kg dry straw** or **10 MJ/kg**. In practice, with pretreatment losses, fermentation inhibition, and distillation losses, real-world yields are **0.20–0.30 L/kg**, giving an overall process efficiency of **42–64%** of theoretical.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Biodiesel Yield Improvement via Catalyst Loading

**Problem Setup:** Transesterify 1000 kg of soybean oil (MW ≈ 885 g/mol) with methanol (MW = 32 g/mol) using NaOH catalyst.

**Stoichiometric methanol requirement:**

$$n_{oil} = \frac{1000 \times 10^3 \text{ g}}{885 \text{ g/mol}} = 1130 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_{methanol,\,stoich} = 3 \times 1130 = 3390 \text{ mol} = 108.5 \text{ kg}$$

**Industrial practice: 6:1 molar ratio (methanol excess to drive equilibrium):**

$$m_{methanol,\,actual} = 6 \times 108.5 = 651 \text{ kg}$$

**Expected FAME yield (theoretical ≈ 1000 kg, MW of soy FAME ≈ 295 g/mol):**

$$n_{FAME} = 3 \times 1130 = 3390 \text{ mol} \Rightarrow m_{FAME} = 3390 \times 295 = 1000 \text{ kg}$$

**With 1 wt% NaOH catalyst and 99% conversion:**

$$m_{FAME,\,actual} = 0.99 \times 1000 = 990 \text{ kg}$$

**Improvement strategies:**

| Strategy | Yield Improvement | Cost Impact |
|----------|------------------|-------------|
| Use supercritical methanol (no catalyst) | 99.5–100% | High pressure equipment |
| Heterogeneous CaO catalyst | 96–98% | Reusable, lower waste |
| Two-step acid + base catalysis | 98–99% | For high-FFA feedstocks |
| Ultrasonic/microwave intensification | +5% in 1/3 time | Energy for sonicators |
| Co-solvent (hexane) addition | +3–4% | Recovery step needed |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Improvement Strategies Across Fuel Types

| Strategy | 1G Ethanol | 2G Ethanol | Biodiesel | Biogas | Biohydrogen |
|----------|------------|------------|-----------|--------|-------------|
| Strain engineering | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Pretreatment | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ (for high FFA) | ★★ | ★★ |
| Catalysis | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |
| Process intensification | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ |
| Co-product recovery | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where stars are concentrated = where KTU exam questions are likely to focus.** Biodiesel catalysis and 2G ethanol pretreatment are the most examined sub-topics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Yield Improvement Cascade

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
Define the term **theoretical yield** in biofuel production. Why is the actual yield always lower than the theoretical yield?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **theoretical yield** is the maximum mass or volume of fuel obtainable from a given mass of feedstock as dictated by stoichiometry alone, assuming 100% conversion efficiency, no side reactions, and complete recovery.

For example, the theoretical ethanol yield from glucose is **0.511 g/g** based on the Gay-Lussac equation $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$.

Actual yields are lower because of:
1. Incomplete conversion (kinetic limitations, equilibrium).
2. Side reactions producing by-products (glycerol, organic acids).
3. Product losses during downstream recovery (distillation, separation).
4. Inhibitory compounds in hydrolysates (furfural, HMF, acetic acid).
5. Microbial stress (ethanol toxicity above ~12% v/v).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
List **four biological strategies** used to improve biofuel yield.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Strain engineering** — using recombinant *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* that ferments both C6 and C5 sugars (xylose).
2. **Tolerance improvement** — selecting or engineering strains tolerant to high ethanol concentrations (>12% v/v).
3. **Co-culture systems** — combining cellulolytic fungi with fermentative yeasts for consolidated bioprocessing (CBP).
4. **Metabolic pathway optimization** — knocking out glycerol formation pathways to redirect carbon flux toward ethanol.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **pretreatment strategies** employed to improve yield in second-generation (cellulosic) ethanol production. Compare physical, chemical, and biological pretreatments with respect to yield improvement.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A rice straw sample contains 38% cellulose, 22% hemicellulose, and 18% lignin on a dry basis. Calculate the **maximum theoretical ethanol yield per tonne** of dry rice straw, assuming complete hydrolysis of cellulose and hemicellulose and standard Gay-Lussac fermentation.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Pretreatment Strategies [7 marks]:**

The lignocellulosic matrix has cellulose microfibrils encased in a hemicellulose–lignin sheath. **Pretreatment disrupts this recalcitrant structure** to make cellulose accessible to hydrolytic enzymes, thereby improving sugar and ultimately ethanol yield.

| Pretreatment Type | Mechanism | Yield Improvement |
|-------------------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Physical** (milling, steam explosion) | Reduces crystallinity, particle size; steam explosion (160–240 °C, 1–5 MPa) auto-hydrolyzes hemicellulose | 10–25% ↑ in enzymatic digestibility |
| **Chemical** (dilute H₂SO₄, NaOH, organosolv) | Acid hydrolyzes hemicellulose; alkali removes lignin; organosolv dissolves lignin | 50–90% ↑ glucan digestibility |
| **Biological** (white-rot fungi: *Phanerochaete chrysosporium*) | Lignin peroxidase & manganese peroxidase degrade lignin selectively | Mild improvement; slow (10–14 days); eco-friendly |
| **Combined** (e.g., acid + enzyme) | Sequential action maximizes hemicellulose removal + cellulose exposure | Up to 95% of theoretical sugar yield |

**Comparison (1 mark):**
- Chemical pretreatment gives the **highest yield improvement** but generates inhibitors (furfural, HMF).
- Biological is **green but slow**.
- Physical is **energy-intensive** but clean.
- Industrial practice favors **steam explosion + dilute acid** as a balance.

**Part (b) — Yield Calculation [7 marks]:**

**Given:** 1 tonne (1000 kg) of dry rice straw.
- Cellulose: $0.38 \times 1000 = 380$ kg
- Hemicellulose: $0.22 \times 1000 = 220$ kg
- Lignin: $0.18 \times 1000 = 180$ kg (not fermentable)

**Step 1 — Glucose from cellulose:**

$$m_{glucose} = 380 \times \frac{180.16}{162.14} = 380 \times 1.111 = 422.18 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Ethanol from glucose:**

$$m_{ethanol,\,C} = 422.18 \times 0.511 = 215.73 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Xylose from hemicellulose (xylan):**

$$m_{xylose} = 220 \times \frac{150.13}{132.11} = 220 \times 1.136 = 249.92 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Ethanol from xylose (assuming recombinant yeast, same 0.511 g/g):**

$$m_{ethanol,\,H} = 249.92 \times 0.511 = 127.71 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Total ethanol mass:**

$$m_{ethanol,\,total} = 215.73 + 127.71 = 343.44 \text{ kg per tonne straw}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to volume (ρ = 0.789 kg/L):**

$$V_{ethanol} = \frac{343.44}{0.789} = 435.3 \text{ L per tonne dry straw}$$

**Answer: ~435 L ethanol / tonne dry rice straw (theoretical maximum).**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **catalytic strategies** used to improve biodiesel yield. How does supercritical methanol processing compare to conventional base-catalyzed transesterification?

**(b)** [7 Marks] A biodiesel plant processes 5000 kg/day of used cooking oil (UCOME) containing 6% free fatty acids (FFA). Calculate the **methanol requirement** for (i) conventional base catalysis, and (ii) two-step acid + base catalysis. Discuss how this affects overall biodiesel yield.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (Key Steps):**

**Part (a) — Catalytic Strategies [7 marks]:**

1. **Homogeneous base catalysts** (NaOH, KOH): Fast, 95–98% yield at 60 °C, but soap formation with high-FFA feedstocks.
2. **Homogeneous acid catalysts** (H₂SO₄): Tolerate FFA, but slow (4–8 h), cause equipment corrosion.
3. **Heterogeneous base catalysts** (CaO, MgO, hydrotalcite): Reusable, no soap, mild conditions; require methanol-oil mixing optimization.
4. **Enzymatic catalysts** (lipases): Work on FFA-rich oils, mild (30–40 °C), but expensive and slow.
5. **Supercritical methanol processing**: No catalyst needed; T > 239 °C, P > 8.1 MPa; tolerates water & FFA; yield >99%; reaction time 5–30 min.
6. **Ionic liquid catalysts**: Green, tunable, high selectivity.

**Comparison — Conventional vs Supercritical:**

| Parameter | Conventional Base | Supercritical |
|-----------|------------------|---------------|
| Catalyst | NaOH (1 wt%) | None |
| Temperature | 60–70 °C | 280–350 °C |
| Pressure | Atmospheric | 10–25 MPa |
| Reaction time | 1–2 h | 5–30 min |
| Yield | 95–98% | >99% |
| FFA tolerance | Low (saponification) | High |
| Energy cost | Low | High |
| Product purity | Requires washing | Cleaner |

**Part (b) — Methanol Calculation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Moles of oil (MW of UCOME ≈ 880 g/mol):**

$$n_{oil} = \frac{5000 \times 10^3}{880} = 5681.8 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Conventional base catalysis (6:1 methanol-to-oil ratio):**

$$n_{MeOH} = 6 \times 5681.8 = 34{,}091 \text{ mol} \Rightarrow m_{MeOH} = 34{,}091 \times 32 = 1090.9 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — FFA content (MW of oleic acid = 282 g/mol):**

$$m_{FFA} = 0.06 \times 5000 = 300 \text{ kg} \Rightarrow n_{FFA} = \frac{300 \times 10^3}{282} = 1063.8 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4 — Two-step acid + base:**

- Acid step consumes **1 mol MeOH per mol FFA** (esterification): $1063.8$ mol = **34.05 kg**.
- Base step uses **6:1 methanol-to-oil ratio**: $1090.9$ kg.
- **Total methanol ≈ 1125 kg/day**.

**Effect on yield:**
- Conventional: FFA converts to soap → FAME yield drops to ~85% (loses ~6% × 1000 kg ≈ 60 kg biodiesel/day).
- Two-step: FFA pre-converted to FAME in acid step → overall yield rises to ~97–98%.

**Answer: Conventional base needs ~1091 kg MeOH/day but only achieves ~85% yield; two-step needs ~1125 kg/day but achieves ~97% yield — the additional methanol cost is more than offset by the additional 600+ kg biodiesel/day.**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Yield Improvement Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the hydrolysis mass factor** (162 → 180 for cellulose, 132 → 150 for xylan). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for using polymer mass directly without accounting for the water of hydrolysis.
> 2. **Confusing mass yield with volume yield** — students often write "0.511 L/g" instead of "0.511 g/g" or forget to divide by ethanol density (0.789 kg/L) when converting to L/kg.
> 3. **Ignoring units in the final answer.** Every yield must carry a unit (g/g, L/kg, MJ/tonne, %).
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** explicitly (e.g., "assuming 100% hydrolysis efficiency," "neglecting inhibitor formation"). Examiners reward stated assumptions.
> 5. **Mixing up theoretical and actual yields.** In numerical problems, always specify which one you are computing.
> 6. **Omitting the Buswell equation** for biogas yield questions. The general formula is expected.
> 7. **In transesterification problems, not showing the 3:1 stoichiometry clearly.** Always draw or state the balanced equation first.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Theoretical yield** = maximum from stoichiometry; **Actual yield** = real-world, always lower.
- ✅ **Overall process yield = product of individual stage yields** (chain rule: $\eta_{overall} = \eta_1 \times \eta_2 \times \eta_3 \times \ldots$).
- ✅ **Glucose → ethanol theoretical yield = 0.511 g/g = 0.568 L/kg** (using ρ = 0.789 kg/L).
- ✅ **LHV of ethanol = 26.9 MJ/kg**; **LHV of biodiesel (FAME) ≈ 37–40 MJ/kg**; **LHV of methane = 50 MJ/kg**.
- ✅ **Hydrolysis mass factor**: cellulose → glucose multiplies by 180.16/162.14 = **1.111**.
- ✅ **Pretreatment is the rate-limiting yield-improvement step** in 2G ethanol — without it, cellulose is inaccessible.
- ✅ **Three classes of pretreatment**: physical, chemical, biological — each trade yield vs. cost vs. environmental impact.
- ✅ **Biodiesel catalysis hierarchy**: homogeneous base > enzyme > heterogeneous base > supercritical (in different contexts).
- ✅ **Supercritical methanol = no catalyst, high T/P, tolerates water & FFA, >99% yield** — but energy-intensive.
- ✅ **Two-step catalysis** is preferred for high-FFA feedstocks (UCOME, animal tallow).
- ✅ **Methanol-to-oil molar ratio** = 6:1 in industrial practice, 3:1 stoichiometric.
- ✅ **Buswell equation** gives theoretical CH₄ yield from elemental composition of biomass.
- ✅ **Yield improvements are cumulative but diminishing** — first 80% is "easy," last 5% is "expensive."
- ✅ **Co-products matter**: glycerol, lignin, DDGS — valorizing them raises *effective* process yield.
- ✅ **Energy crops**: sugarcane (~80 t/ha), miscanthus (~30 t/ha), jatropha (~5 t oil/ha).
- ✅ **Photosynthetic efficiency cap**: ~4–6% for C3 plants, ~6–8% for C4 plants — sets the absolute ceiling on biomass yield.
- ✅ **Photosynthetic efficiency × harvest index × conversion efficiency = overall fuel yield** — the "three-stage yield equation" for energy crops.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process design of a bioethanol plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process design of a bioethanol plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Design of a Bioethanol Plant** is the integrated engineering discipline that defines the sequence of unit operations, mass and energy balances, equipment sizing, and stream specifications required to convert lignocellulosic or sugar/starch-based biomass into fuel-grade anhydrous bioethanol (≥ 99.5 % v/v), in accordance with **IS 1460:2005 / ASTM D4806** fuel-ethanol standards, while meeting techno-economic and environmental constraints.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, process design encompasses:

1. **Feedstock characterization** (composition, moisture, cellulose/hemicellulose/lignin content)
2. **Selection of the conversion route** (sugar → ethanol, starch → ethanol, or lignocellulose → ethanol)
3. **Mass and energy balancing** across each unit operation
4. **Equipment sizing** (reactors, distillation columns, molecular sieves, heat exchangers)
5. **Process flow sheeting (PFD)** and **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)**
6. **Effluent treatment and water recycling strategy**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A typical second-generation (2G) bioethanol plant in the syllabus context operates at a capacity range of **50 – 100 KLPD (kilo-litres per day)**, processing feedstocks such as sugarcane bagasse, rice straw, corn stover, or molasses.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Industrial Brewery" Intuition

Think of a bioethanol plant as a **giant, precision-controlled kitchen** where nature's most complex polymers (cellulose chains inside corn stalks or sugarcane fibres) are dismantled step-by-step into the simplest possible fuel molecule — ethanol (C₂H₅OH).

| Kitchen Step | Industrial Equivalent | Purpose |
|--------------|----------------------|---------|
| Crushing garlic | Milling / size reduction | Increase surface area |
| Soaking beans overnight | Dilute-acid pretreatment | Soften the fibrous "shell" (lignin) |
| Adding digestive enzymes | Enzymatic hydrolysis (cellulase) | Break long sugar chains into glucose |
| Yeast fermenting dough | Fermenter with *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* | Convert sugar → alcohol + CO₂ |
| Boiling to concentrate flavours | Distillation column | Separate ethanol from water |
| Final drying of herbs | Molecular sieve dehydration | Remove last traces of water (azeotrope breaking) |

The plant is essentially a **biological disassembly line** — the engineer must guarantee that every kilogram of feedstock yields the maximum possible ethanol with the minimum energy penalty.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

- **Stoichiometric ethanol yield (theoretical):** **0.511 g ethanol / g glucose** (or **0.46 kg ethanol / kg dry biomass** for lignocellulose)
- **Density of anhydrous ethanol:** **789 kg/m³** at 20 °C
- **Ethanol–water azeotrope composition:** **95.6 % v/v ethanol / 4.4 % v/v water** at **78.2 °C**
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of ethanol:** **29.7 MJ/kg**
- **Glucose-to-ethanol reaction enthalpy:** ≈ −67 kJ/mol (exothermic)
- **Typical fermentation temperature:** **30 – 33 °C**
- **pH of fermentation broth:** **4.5 – 5.0**
- **Standard plant uptime assumed in KTU problems:** **330 days/year (90.4 % on-stream time)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Bioethanol Plant

A KTU 2024 question may describe or sketch a PFD. The canonical architecture contains the following eight blocks:

1. **Feedstock reception & storage** — moisture conditioning (target ~ 50 % w/w for SSF)
2. **Pretreatment** — dilute H₂SO₄ (0.5 – 1.5 % w/w) at 140 – 190 °C, 5 – 15 min
3. **Solid–liquid separation & washing** — removes inhibitors (furfural, HMF, acetic acid)
4. **Enzymatic hydrolysis / saccharification** — cellulase cocktail, 45 – 50 °C, 48 – 72 h
5. **Fermentation** — *S. cerevisiae*, 30 – 33 °C, 48 – 72 h
6. **Beer column (distillation)** — produces ~ 95 % v/v ethanol overhead
7. **Molecular sieve dehydration** — produces anhydrous ethanol (≥ 99.5 % v/v)
8. **Stillage / vinasse treatment** — anaerobic digestion → biogas (CHP) or evaporation

## 2.2 Core Fermentation Stoichiometry

The governing biochemical reaction is the **Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas (EMP) glycolysis pathway** under anaerobic conditions:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$$

### 2.2.1 Verification of Theoretical Yield (0.511 g/g)

- Molecular weight of glucose = **180 g/mol**
- Molecular weight of ethanol = **46 g/mol**
- 1 mole glucose → 2 moles ethanol

$$\text{Yield} = \frac{2 \times 46}{180} = 0.5111 \;\text{g ethanol / g glucose consumed}$$

### 2.2.2 Overall Plant Yield (with hydrolysis + fermentation losses)

$$\eta_{\text{plant}} = \eta_{\text{pretreat}} \times \eta_{\text{hydrolysis}} \times \eta_{\text{fermentation}} \times \eta_{\text{recovery}}$$

Typical industry values:
- η_pretreat ≈ 0.90
- η_hydrolysis ≈ 0.85
- η_fermentation ≈ 0.92
- η_recovery (distillation + sieve) ≈ 0.98

$$\eta_{\text{plant}} \approx 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.92 \times 0.98 \approx 0.69 \;\text{(i.e. 69 %)}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Cheat Sheet — Print & Memorize**

| # | Formula / Equation | Variable Definition | Typical Unit | Validity / Remarks |
|---|-------------------|---------------------|--------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $Y_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{2 M_{\text{EtOH}}}{M_{\text{glucose}}} = 0.511$ | Theoretical mass yield | g/g | Glucose basis only |
| 2 | $Y_{\text{actual}} = Y_{\text{th}} \times \eta_{\text{overall}}$ | Actual mass yield | g/g | Use ≤ 0.96 × Y_th for batch fermentation |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \prod \eta_i$ | Cascade efficiency | dimensionless | Product of stage efficiencies |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_{\text{EtOH}} = \dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \times x_{\text{sugar}} \times Y_{\text{actual}}$ | Ethanol mass rate | kg/h | Sugar basis, dry feed |
| 5 | $\text{Vol}_{EtOH} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{EtOH}}}{\rho_{\text{EtOH}}}$ | Volumetric flow of pure ethanol | L/h | ρ = 789 kg/m³ |
| 6 | $F_{\text{glucose}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{EtOH}}}{Y_{\text{th}} \times \eta_{\text{ferm}}}$ | Glucose feed rate | kg/h | Required upstream |
| 7 | $\text{SRG} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{sugar fed}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{EtOH produced}}}$ | Specific glucose consumption | kg/kg | Typical = 1.92 – 2.00 |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{still}} = \dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \times c_p \times \Delta T + \lambda_{\text{EtOH}} \times \dot{m}_{\text{EtOH}}$ | Reboiler duty | kW | c_p ≈ 4.18 kJ/kg·K for beer |
| 9 | $R_{\min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_F}{y_F - x_F}$ | Minimum reflux ratio (McCabe–Thiele) | dimensionless | Distillation design |
| 10 | $N_{\min} = \dfrac{\log\left[\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\cdot\dfrac{1-x_W}{x_W}\right]}{\log \alpha_{avg}}$ | Minimum stages (Fenske) | — | For EtOH–H₂O, α_avg ≈ 2.4 |
| 11 | $P_{\text{biogas}} = \dot{m}_{\text{stillage}} \times Y_{\text{CH}_4} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{CH}_4}$ | Biogas power potential | kW | Y_CH4 ≈ 0.25 Nm³/kg COD |
| 12 | $\text{CAPEX} \approx k \cdot C^{0.6}$ (six-tenths rule) | Capital cost scaling | currency | C = capacity |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 valuation, always state the **assumed mass balance closure (100 %)** and mention **any stream that leaves the system boundary** (e.g., CO₂ vent, wastewater).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Equations

- **Equations 1 – 4** are essential for **mass balance** problems (≈ 7-mark KTU questions).
- **Equations 5 – 7** govern **equipment throughput sizing** for the fermenter and distillation column.
- **Equations 8 – 10** drive **energy balance and column design** (typical 14-mark question).
- **Equations 11 – 12** link the bioethanol process to **CHP integration & economics** — frequently asked as 3-mark "integrated" questions in Part A.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Glucose-to-Ethanol Theoretical Yield

Starting from the balanced fermentation equation:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\rightarrow\; 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$$

**Step 1:** Identify molar masses.
- $M_{\text{glucose}} = 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 180 \;\text{g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{EtOH}} = 2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = 46 \;\text{g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{CO}_2} = 12 + 2(16) = 44 \;\text{g/mol}$

**Step 2:** Form the mass basis — take 180 g (1 kmol) of glucose.

$$\text{Mass of EtOH} = 2 \times 46 = 92 \;\text{kg per kmol glucose}$$

$$\text{Mass of CO}_2 = 2 \times 44 = 88 \;\text{kg per kmol glucose}$$

**Step 3:** Check mass balance closure.

$$\text{LHS} = 180 \;\text{kg}, \quad \text{RHS} = 92 + 88 = 180 \;\text{kg} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 4:** Compute theoretical mass yield.

$$Y_{\text{th}} = \frac{92}{180} = 0.5111 \;\frac{\text{kg EtOH}}{\text{kg glucose}}$$

**Step 5:** Express on a per-tonne-glucose basis (industrial sanity check).

$$1\;\text{tonne glucose} \rightarrow 0.5111 \times 1000 = 511.1 \;\text{kg EtOH (theoretical max)}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example #1 — Mass Balance for a 50 KLPD Plant

> **Problem (KTU-style 7-mark):** A sugar-based bioethanol plant processes **molasses** containing **48 % w/w total fermentable sugars** (as sucrose, MW = 342 g/mol). The plant operates at **90 % fermentation efficiency**, **98 % distillation recovery**, and **99.5 % dehydration recovery**. The ethanol product is **anhydrous (≥ 99.5 % v/v)**. Compute the **daily mass of molasses required** for a **50,000 L/day ethanol** product, and the **CO₂ vented per day**.

### 3.2.1 Solution

**Step 1:** Mass of pure ethanol produced per day.
- ρ_EtOH = 789 kg/m³, Volume = 50 m³/day

$$\dot{m}_{\text{EtOH, product}} = 50 \times 789 = 39{,}450 \;\text{kg/day}$$

**Step 2:** Account for distillation loss (98 % recovery).

$$\dot{m}_{\text{EtOH, beer column overhead}} = \frac{39{,}450}{0.98} = 40{,}255 \;\text{kg/day}$$

**Step 3:** Account for fermentation efficiency (90 %).

$$\dot{m}_{\text{EtOH, theoretical from sugar}} = \frac{40{,}255}{0.90} = 44{,}728 \;\text{kg/day}$$

**Step 4:** Convert theoretical ethanol to required glucose (using 0.511 g/g).

$$\dot{m}_{\text{glucose required}} = \frac{44{,}728}{0.511} = 87{,}530 \;\text{kg glucose/day}$$

**Step 5:** Adjust for sucrose → glucose stoichiometry. One mole sucrose (342 g) hydrolyses to **2 moles glucose (360 g)**.

$$1\;\text{kg sucrose} \rightarrow \frac{360}{342} = 1.0526 \;\text{kg glucose}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{sucrose required}} = \frac{87{,}530}{1.0526} = 83{,}154 \;\text{kg sucrose/day}$$

**Step 6:** Molasses feed rate (48 % sugar w/w).

$$\dot{m}_{\text{molasses}} = \frac{83{,}154}{0.48} = 173{,}238 \;\text{kg/day} \approx 173.2 \;\text{tonnes/day}$$

**Step 7:** CO₂ vented per day.

Theoretical CO₂:ethanol mass ratio = 88 / 92 = 0.9565.

$$\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2, \text{theoretical}} = 44{,}728 \times \frac{88}{92} = 42{,}783 \;\text{kg/day}$$

Adjusted for 90 % efficiency:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2, \text{actual vent}} \approx 42{,}783 \times 0.90 = 38{,}505 \;\text{kg/day}$$

**Step 8:** Convert to volume at STP (1 kmol = 22.4 Nm³, M_CO₂ = 44 kg/kmol).

$$\dot{V}_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{38{,}505}{44} \times 22.4 = 19{,}603 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{day}$$

### 3.2.2 Final Answer Box

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{molasses}} \approx 173.2 \;\text{t/day}, \quad \dot{V}_{\text{CO}_2} \approx 19{,}600 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{day}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example #2 — Energy Balance on Distillation Column

> **Problem:** A beer column receives **100,000 kg/h** of fermented broth containing **8 % w/w ethanol** at **30 °C** and produces an overhead of **95 % v/v ethanol** with **5 % v/v water**. The bottoms contain **0.1 % w/w ethanol** (essentially water). Compute the **reboiler steam requirement** assuming the column operates at **1 atm** and saturated steam at **3 bar gauge (≈ 4 bar abs, T = 143.6 °C)** is used. Latent heat of steam at 4 bar abs = **2 131 kJ/kg**. Latent heat of ethanol vaporization = **925 kJ/kg**.

### 3.3.1 Solution

**Step 1:** Mass of ethanol in feed.

$$\dot{m}_{\text{EtOH, feed}} = 0.08 \times 100{,}000 = 8{,}000 \;\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 2:** Mass of ethanol in bottoms (negligible ≈ 0).

$$\dot{m}_{\text{EtOH, overhead}} \approx 8{,}000 \;\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 3:** Mass of overhead distillate.

Since overhead is 95 % v/v EtOH and density ratio (EtOH/water) at 95.6 % azeotrope ≈ 0.804:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{overhead}} = \frac{8{,}000}{0.95 \times 0.804 / 1} \approx 10{,}470 \;\text{kg/h}$$

(Alternatively, use mass fraction of 95 % v/v × density 0.804 t/m³ to get mass fraction ≈ 0.956 w/w → overhead mass = 8 370 kg/h). For a KTU-safe approximation, take **8 370 kg/h** overhead and **91 630 kg/h** bottoms.

**Step 4:** Ethanol must be vaporized once. Approximate reboiler duty for ethanol vaporization.

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{EtOH vap}} = 8{,}000 \times 925 = 7.40 \times 10^6 \;\text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 5:** Energy to heat the bottoms from 30 °C to ~ 100 °C (boiling).

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{sensible}} = 91{,}630 \times 4.18 \times (100 - 30) = 2.681 \times 10^7 \;\text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 6:** Total reboiler duty.

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{total}} = 7.40 \times 10^6 + 2.681 \times 10^7 = 3.42 \times 10^7 \;\text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 7:** Steam flow rate.

$$\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} = \frac{3.42 \times 10^7}{2{,}131} \approx 16{,}050 \;\text{kg/h} \approx 16.05 \;\text{t/h}$$

### 3.3.2 Final Answer Box

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \approx 16 \;\text{t/h} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{SSCF} \approx 2.0 \;\text{kg steam / kg ethanol}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry benchmark:** 2G bioethanol plants target **Specific Steam Consumption Factor (SSCF) ≤ 2.5 kg steam/kg EtOH**. Our 16 t/h meets the 2G benchmark for an 8 t/h ethanol stream.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Calculation Outcomes

| Plant Feed | Capacity (t/day) | EtOH Yield (L/t) | Steam (kg/L) | Water (L/L EtOH) |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|--------------|------------------|
| Sugarcane molasses | 100 | 280 – 300 | 2.5 – 3.0 | 8 – 12 |
| Corn starch | 100 | 380 – 400 | 2.8 – 3.5 | 6 – 10 |
| Sugarcane bagasse (2G) | 100 | 220 – 250 | 3.5 – 4.5 | 15 – 25 |
| Rice straw (2G) | 100 | 200 – 230 | 4.0 – 5.0 | 20 – 30 |

| Effluent | Anaerobic digester, UASB | RCC + epoxy | 35 – 55 | 1.0 |
