<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
**Define a triglyceride. With the help of a neat sketch, explain its structure and indicate the products formed when it undergoes transesterification with methanol. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **triglyceride (triacylglycerol, TAG)** is a triester of glycerol (propane-1,2,3-triol) with three fatty acid molecules. Structurally:

- Central **glycerol backbone** (C₃H₈O₃) bearing three hydroxyl groups.
- Each –OH is esterified with a long-chain **fatty acid (R-COOH)**, releasing one molecule of water per ester bond.

$$\text{TAG} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{catalyst}} 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol}$$

Products: **3 molecules of Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME = biodiesel)** + **1 molecule of glycerol** (by-product, useful in pharma/cosmetics).

**Sketch expected (1 mark):** Glycerol drawn vertically with three fatty acid chains as zig-zag lines attached via –O–C(=O)– ester linkages.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
**Differentiate between saturated, monounsaturated, and polyunsaturated fatty acids with two examples each. Why is the degree of unsaturation critical for biodiesel quality? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Saturated | No C=C double bonds | Palmitic (C16:0), Stearic (C18:0) |
| Monounsaturated | Exactly one C=C | Oleic (C18:1), Palmitoleic (C16:1) |
| Polyunsaturated | Two or more C=C | Linoleic (C18:2), Linolenic (C18:3) |

**Why critical:** Degree of unsaturation governs **cetane number (ignition quality), oxidative stability, iodine value, and cold-flow behavior** of the resulting biodiesel. Highly unsaturated feedstocks (e.g., soybean) need antioxidant additives; highly saturated ones (e.g., coconut) suffer high pour points in temperate climates.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Explain the composition of vegetable oils with reference to fatty acid profile. Discuss in detail how the fatty acid composition influences the major physico-chemical properties of biodiesel. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Vegetable oils are predominantly mixtures of **triacylglycerols (95–98 %)** built from one glycerol molecule and three fatty acids. Minor components (phospholipids, tocopherols, sterols, FFAs) account for 2–5 %. The *fatty acid profile* — i.e., the identity and proportion of individual FAs — is the single most important descriptor of a feedstock.

For any vegetable oil, the FA profile can be categorized into:
1. **Chain length distribution** — short (C8–C12), medium (C14–C16), long (C18+).
2. **Saturation class** — saturated (SFAs), monounsaturated (MUFAs), polyunsaturated (PUFAs).

**Influence on biodiesel properties:**

- **Cetane Number (CN):** Increases with chain length, decreases with unsaturation. Long saturated FAs (C18:0) → high CN ≈ 65–80; PUFAs (C18:3) → low CN ≈ 35–45.
- **Kinematic Viscosity:** Higher MW / longer chain → higher viscosity. Coconut-derived FAME has lower viscosity than palm-derived FAME due to shorter dominant C12 chains.
- **Oxidative Stability:** PUFAs (especially linolenic, C18:3) oxidize rapidly due to **bis-allylic methylene groups**. EN 14214 caps linolenic at 12 %.
- **Cold Flow Properties:** Saturated FAs crystallize at higher temperatures → high cloud/pour point. Palm FAME performs poorly in winter.
- **Iodine Value (IV):** Direct measure of unsaturation. EN 14214 caps IV at **120 g I₂/100 g**.
- **Saponification Value (SV):** Higher SV indicates shorter average chain length; coconut oil SV ≈ 255 vs. soybean SV ≈ 190.

Thus, **FA composition is the master variable** that determines both processing conditions (catalyst, temperature, alcohol ratio) and final fuel specifications.

---

**(b) A vegetable oil has the following fatty acid composition (mass %): Palmitic 10 %, Stearic 4 %, Oleic 25 %, Linoleic 52 %, Linolenic 9 %. Calculate (i) average molecular weight of the oil, (ii) saponification value, and (iii) iodine value. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| FA | $w_i$ | $M_i$ (g/mol) | $D_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Palmitic (C16:0) | 0.10 | 256.42 | 0 |
| Stearic (C18:0) | 0.04 | 284.48 | 0 |
| Oleic (C18:1) | 0.25 | 282.46 | 1 |
| Linoleic (C18:2) | 0.52 | 280.45 | 2 |
| Linolenic (C18:3) | 0.09 | 278.43 | 3 |

**(i) Average MW of FA mixture:**

$$M_{\text{FA,avg}} = \frac{(0.10)(256.42)+(0.04)(284.48)+(0.25)(282.46)+(0.52)(280.45)+(0.09)(278.43)}{1.00}$$

$$M_{\text{FA,avg}} = \frac{25.642+11.379+70.615+145.834+25.059}{1} = 278.53 \; \text{g/mol}$$

**MW of oil (TAG):**

$$M_{\text{TAG}} = 3 \times 278.53 + 38.05 = 835.59 + 38.05 = 873.64 \; \text{g/mol}$$

**(ii) Saponification Value:**

$$SV = \frac{56.1 \times 1000 \times 3}{873.64} = \frac{168300}{873.64} \approx 192.7 \; \text{mg KOH/g}$$

**(iii) Iodine Value:**

$$IV = (0.10)(0)+(0.04)(0)+(0.25)(89.86)+(0.52)(181.00)+(0.09)(273.49)$$

$$IV = 0 + 0 + 22.47 + 94.12 + 24.61 = 141.2 \; \text{g I}_2/100\text{g}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) 873.6 g/mol (ii) 192.7 mg KOH/g (iii) 141.2 g I₂/100 g.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) List and explain the major physical and chemical properties of vegetable oils relevant to biodiesel production. Discuss how these properties are determined by fatty acid composition. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Major properties:**

1. **Density (kg/m³):** ~ 910–925 for most vegetable oils; increases slightly with chain length and saturation. Important for energy content calculation.

2. **Kinematic Viscosity (mm²/s):** Typically 30–50 cSt at 40 °C for raw oils; biodiesel target 3.5–5.0 cSt. Decreases with unsaturation, increases with chain length.

3. **Flash Point (°C):** > 200 °C for most oils → safer storage than petrodiesel (~55 °C).

4. **Acid Value (mg KOH/g):** Measures free fatty acids; oils with AV > 2 need pretreatment via esterification before transesterification.

5. **Saponification Value:** Indicator of average chain length; affects soap formation.

6. **Iodine Value:** Indicator of unsaturation; affects oxidative stability.

7. **Cetane Number:** Ignition quality; depends on chain length and saturation.

8. **Calorific Value (MJ/kg):** ~ 36–40 MJ/kg; slightly less than petrodiesel (~ 42–45 MJ/kg) due to bound oxygen.

**Compositional dependence:** A longer saturated chain (e.g., C18:0) raises viscosity, cetane number, and calorific value but worsens cold flow. A shorter unsaturated chain (e.g., C16:1) lowers viscosity but reduces cetane number.

---

**(b) Compare the fatty acid composition of soybean oil, palm oil, rapeseed oil, and coconut oil. Explain why coconut oil is generally not preferred for biodiesel production despite being abundantly available in Kerala. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| FA | Soybean | Palm | Rapeseed | Coconut |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| C12:0 (Lauric) | 0 % | 0 % | 0 % | **48 %** |
| C14:0 (Myristic) | 0 % | 1 % | 0 % | 17 % |
| C16:0 (Palmitic) | 10 % | **44 %** | 4 % | 9 % |
| C18:0 (Stearic) | 4 % | 5 % | 2 % | 2 % |
| C18:1 (Oleic) | 23 % | 39 % | **61 %** | 6 % |
| C18:2 (Linoleic) | **51 %** | 10 % | 21 % | 2 % |
| C18:3 (Linolenic) | 7 % | 0 % | 11 % | 0 % |
| C20+ / others | 5 % | 1 % | 1 % | 16 % |

**Key observations:**
- Soybean: dominated by **PUFAs** → low oxidative stability.
- Palm: balanced SFAs + MUFAs → ideal oxidation/viscosity compromise.
- Rapeseed/Canola: dominated by oleic acid → high oxidative stability, good cold flow.
- Coconut: dominated by **short-chain saturated lauric (C12:0)** → unique profile.

**Why coconut oil is not preferred for biodiesel:**
1. **High cloud/pour point** (~ 22–25 °C): crystallizes in moderate climates, causing fuel-line blockage in engines.
2. **Excessive saponification:** high SV (~255) consumes more catalyst, increasing soap formation and reducing FAME yield.
3. **Poor cold-flow properties** even in Kerala's mild winter; performance degrades in North Indian conditions.
4. **Higher acidity:** FFAs cause soap during alkali-catalyzed transesterification.
5. **Economic priority:** coconut oil has higher value as edible/cooking oil and in oleochemical industries (soaps, surfactants, MCTs) than as biodiesel feedstock.

Hence, despite Kerala's abundant coconut production, biodiesel programs prefer **used cooking oil, jatropha, or waste fish oil**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add the glycerol backbone** (38.05) when going from $M_{\text{FA,avg}}$ to $M_{\text{TAG}}$ — this alone costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up the IV formula:** students sometimes use $D_i \times 100 / M_i$ instead of $2 \times 126.9 \times D_i \times 100 / M_i$. The factor 253.8 (= 2 × MW of I) **MUST** appear.
> 3. **Wrong unit of IV:** the correct unit is g I₂ / 100 g oil, *not* g/mol.
> 4. **Forgetting that SV is per gram of oil** and that 1 mol of TAG consumes 3 mol KOH — losing the factor of 3.
> 5. **No proper sketch** in 3-mark answers — examiners explicitly award 1 mark for a labeled triglyceride structure.
> 6. **Citing IV without interpreting** — always conclude whether the value falls in drying / semi-drying / non-drying oil categories.
> 7. **Not comparing with biodiesel specs (EN 14214/ASTM D6751)** in 14-mark answers — examiners reward contextual linkage.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Vegetable oil = mixture of triacylglycerols (95–98 %)** + minor lipids.
- **Triglyceride structure:** glycerol + 3 fatty acids – 3 H₂O.
- **Fatty acid shorthand:** C:N (carbon count : double-bond count).
- **Major FAs in vegetable oils:** Palmitic (C16:0), Stearic (C18:0), Oleic (C18:1), Linoleic (C18:2), Linolenic (C18:3), Lauric (C12:0 — coconut/palm kernel).
- **Key derived formulas to memorize:**
  - $M_{\text{TAG}} = 3\,M_{\text{FA,avg}} + 38.05$
  - $SV = (56.1 \times 1000 \times 3)/M_{\text{TAG}}$
  - $IV_i = (253.8 \times D_i)/M_i$ for pure FA; weighted-sum for oil.
- **Property–composition linkages (high-yield):**
  - ↑ unsaturation ⇒ ↓ CN, ↓ oxidative stability, ↑ IV, ↑ cold-flow
  - ↑ chain length ⇒ ↑ viscosity, ↑ CN, ↑ calorific value
  - ↑ saturation ⇒ ↑ cloud/pour point (worse cold flow)
- **Feedstock preference rule of thumb:**
  - Tropical → palm/jatropha (good oxidation stability)
  - Temperate → soybean/canola (good cold flow)
  - Coconut → edible/oleochemical use, **not** biodiesel
- **Biodiesel specs to quote:** EN 14214 (IV ≤ 120, CN ≥ 51), ASTM D6751, BIS 15607.
- **Indian/KTU-relevant feedstocks:** jatropha, karanj, mahua, waste cooking oil, rice bran oil.
- **Cetane number (Knothe):** $CN = 46.3 + 5458/M_{\text{TAG}} - 0.225 \times IV$
- **Degree of unsaturation:** $DU = \%\text{MUFA} + 2 \times \%\text{PUFA}$
- **Always** present answers in tabular form for composition questions; examiners reward visual organization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Extraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extraction** in the context of bioenergy and biofuels refers to the **separation of a desired bio-component** (such as vegetable oil from oilseeds, lipids from microalgae, or fermentable sugars from lignocellulosic biomass) **from a solid or semi-solid matrix** by employing physical, chemical, mechanical, or enzymatic means. It is a critical **upstream unit operation** that dictates the yield, purity, and downstream viability of the resulting biofuel (biodiesel, bioethanol, or biogas precursor).

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, extraction is the *gateway process* that converts raw biomass into a processable intermediate. The choice of extraction technique fundamentally governs the **overall energy return on investment (EROI)** of the entire biofuel life-cycle.

## 1.2 Real-World Intuition — The "Squeezing a Wet Sponge" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a kitchen sponge soaked in oil.** To recover the oil you can:
> 1. **Squeeze it mechanically** → Mechanical Expeller Press
> 2. **Wash it with a detergent (solvent) that dissolves the oil** → Solvent Extraction
> 3. **Use repeated cycles of fresh solvent in a closed system** → Soxhlet Extraction
> 4. **Use pressurised CO₂ (like a power-washer)** → Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SCFE)
>
> Each method has a different **recovery efficiency**, **energy cost**, and **oil quality**. This is exactly the trade-off matrix engineers evaluate in biofuel extraction.

## 1.3 Standard Classification of Extraction Technologies

| Family | Driving Force | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Pressure / Shear | Oilseeds (mustard, soybean, jatropha) |
| **Solvent-based** | Solubility gradient | High oil-content biomass, microalgae |
| **Supercritical** | Pressure above critical point | Nutraceutical-grade algal oil |
| **Enzymatic** | Biochemical hydrolysis | Aqueous enzymatic process (AEP) |
| **Assisted** | Ultrasound / Microwave energy | Pretreatment intensification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Constants for Reference:**
> - Density of *n*-hexane: **0.6548 g/cm³** at 20 °C
> - Critical point of CO₂: **T_c = 31.1 °C**, **P_c = 73.8 bar**
> - Standard Soxhlet reflux ratio: **4 to 6 cycles per hour**
> - Boiling point of *n*-hexane: **68.7 °C** (ideal for thermolabile oils)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Extraction

The extraction process — regardless of method — follows a **three-stage kinetic profile**:

1. **Stage 1 — Rapid Wash Phase (Convective Transfer):**
   The surface oil/wax/solute is readily accessible. Solvent (or pressure) removes it almost instantly. This contributes **~70–80 %** of total yield in well-cracked oilseeds.

2. **Stage 2 — Slow Diffusion Phase (Fickian Transfer):**
   Solute must migrate from the **interior cellular matrix** to the surface. Governed by **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion**; this is the rate-limiting step.

3. **Stage 3 — Desorption & Final Cleanup:**
   The bound oil (chemisorbed to protein/lignin matrices) is released by solvent action or enzyme lysis. Last **5–10 %** of yield — often uneconomical to recover.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Increasing **temperature**, **solvent-to-solid ratio (S/S)**, or **particle size reduction (greater surface area)** all accelerate Stage 2. The trade-off: excessive heat degrades thermosensitive bio-oils; excessive S/S inflates solvent-recovery cost.

## 2.2 Comparison of Principal Extraction Methods

| Method | Working Principle | Oil Recovery (%) | Energy Intensity | Oil Quality |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical (Expeller)** | Pressure through screw-press | 70 – 85 | Moderate | High (no solvent residue) |
| **Solvent (Batch)** | Solubility in *n*-hexane | 90 – 98 | High (recovery still) | Excellent |
| **Soxhlet** | Refluxing solvent in cycles | 95 – 99 | High | Excellent (pure extract) |
| **Supercritical CO₂ (SCFE)** | Tunable solvating power of SC-CO₂ | 90 – 97 | Very High | Premium (food-grade) |
| **Ultrasound-Assisted (UAE)** | Cavitation-induced cell disruption | 85 – 95 | Low–Moderate | High |
| **Aqueous Enzymatic (AEP)** | Hydrolytic enzymes + water | 80 – 90 | Moderate | Excellent (phospholipid-free) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Extraction Engineering

| # | Parameter | Equation | Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Extraction Yield (Y)** | $Y = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | % (w/w) | $0 \le Y \le 100$ |
| 2 | **Solvent-to-Solid Ratio (R)** | $R = \dfrac{V_{\text{solvent}}}{m_{\text{feed}}}$ | L/kg | Typical: $4 \le R \le 10$ |
| 3 | **Mass Balance** | $m_{\text{feed}} = m_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{spent\_solid}} + m_{\text{losses}}$ | kg | Closed system: $\sum m_{\text{in}} = \sum m_{\text{out}}$ |
| 4 | **Fick's First Law** | $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | kg/(m²·s) | Steady-state diffusion |
| 5 | **Fick's Second Law** | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | kg/(m³·s) | Transient, planar geometry |
| 6 | **Solvent Recovery Ratio** | $\eta_{\text{rec}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{recovered}}}{V_{\text{fed}}} \times 100$ | % | KTU benchmark: $\ge 95\%$ |
| 7 | **Overall Recovery Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = Y \times \eta_{\text{rec}}$ | % | Engineering optimisation target |
| 8 | **Power Number (Expeller)** | $N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5}$ | dimensionless | Standard scale-up parameter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These equations feed directly into **Aspen Plus / SuperPro Designer** simulations for commercial biodiesel plants, where yield, solvent recovery, and energy integration determine the **minimum selling price (MSP)** of the biofuel.

## 2.4 Why Mechanical → Solvent Cascade?

In KTU-pattern industrial problems, the **expeller + solvent** hybrid configuration is the gold standard. The expeller (mechanical) de-oils the cake to ~6–8 % residual oil, and the **solvent extractor** recovers the remainder. The combined yield exceeds **99 %** — which is virtually unattainable by either method alone.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Oil Extraction Yield from Mass Balance

**Problem Statement (KTU model):** *A 1,000 kg batch of jatropha seeds is processed in an expeller followed by solvent extraction. The expeller yields 280 kg of crude oil, and the solvent stage recovers an additional 18 kg. Losses total 4 kg. Determine the overall extraction yield and identify the limiting mass balance closure error.*

**Step 1 — Define the system boundaries**

We define the extraction plant as a black box with one inlet (jatropha seeds) and three outlets (oil, spent solid, miscellaneous losses).

$$
m_{\text{feed}} = m_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{spent}} + m_{\text{losses}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{feed}} &= 1{,}000 \text{ kg} \\
m_{\text{oil}} &= m_{\text{expeller}} + m_{\text{solvent}} = 280 + 18 = 298 \text{ kg} \\
m_{\text{losses}} &= 4 \text{ kg} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mass of spent solid (cake)**

$$
m_{\text{spent}} = m_{\text{feed}} - m_{\text{oil}} - m_{\text{losses}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{spent}} &= 1{,}000 - 298 - 4 \\
m_{\text{spent}} &= 698 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the overall extraction yield**

$$
Y = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{feed}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= \dfrac{298}{1{,}000} \times 100 \\
Y &= 29.8 \text{ \% (w/w)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Closure check (KTU examiners expect this)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum m_{\text{out}} &= 298 + 698 + 4 = 1{,}000 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Error} &= \dfrac{\vert \sum m_{\text{in}} - \sum m_{\text{out}} \vert}{m_{\text{in}}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{\vert 1{,}000 - 1{,}000 \vert}{1{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 0.0 \text{ \%} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The mass balance **must close to within ±0.5 %** to receive full marks. If not, the student is expected to **explicitly flag** the discrepancy and recommend a re-measurement strategy.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Solvent-to-Solid Ratio for Target Recovery

**Problem:** *Design a batch solvent extractor to treat 500 kg of soybean flakes (6 % residual oil) with *n*-hexane. If the target residual oil in spent flakes is 0.5 % and the miscella concentration must be held at 25 % w/w, compute the required solvent volume.*

**Step 1 — Oil mass to be removed**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{oil, in}} &= 0.06 \times 500 = 30 \text{ kg} \\
m_{\text{oil, out}} &= 0.005 \times 500 = 2.5 \text{ kg} \\
m_{\text{oil, removed}} &= 30 - 2.5 = 27.5 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Miscella mass from 25 % w/w oil concentration**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Miscella}_{\text{total}} &= \dfrac{m_{\text{oil, removed}}}{0.25} \\
&= \dfrac{27.5}{0.5} \cdot 2 \\
&= 110 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Wait — recompute carefully:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Miscella}_{\text{total}} &= \dfrac{27.5}{0.25} = 110 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Pure solvent mass required**

$$
m_{\text{solvent}} = \text{Miscella}_{\text{total}} - m_{\text{oil, removed}} = 110 - 27.5 = 82.5 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to volume using hexane density (0.6548 g/cm³)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{solvent}} &= \dfrac{m_{\text{solvent}}}{\rho_{\text{hexane}}} \\
&= \dfrac{82.5 \text{ kg}}{0.6548 \text{ kg/L}} \\
V_{\text{solvent}} &\approx 126.0 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solvent-to-Solid Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{V_{\text{solvent}}}{m_{\text{feed}}} = \dfrac{126.0}{500} \\
R &\approx 0.252 \text{ L/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering verdict:** A ratio of 0.252 L/kg is **too lean** for industrial operation (typical: 4–10 L/kg). The student should therefore **recommend a multi-stage counter-current extractor** rather than a single batch pass. This is the kind of *design optimisation comment* that distinguishes a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark one.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fickian Diffusion Kinetics (Slab Geometry)

For a planar oilseed slab of half-thickness $L$, initial uniform oil concentration $C_0$, and surface concentration maintained at $C_s$, the transient concentration profile is:

$$
\dfrac{C(x,t) - C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = \dfrac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^n}{2n+1} \exp\!\left[-D(2n+1)^2\pi^2 t / 4L^2\right] \cos\!\left[\dfrac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2L}\right]
$$

**Average slab concentration** (the experimentally measurable quantity) simplifies to:

$$
\bar{C}(t) = C_s + \dfrac{8(C_0 - C_s)}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{(2n+1)^2} \exp\!\left[-D(2n+1)^2\pi^2 t / 4L^2\right]
$$

**At long times (Stage 2 dominant)**, the first term suffices:

$$
\bar{C}(t) \approx C_s + \dfrac{8(C_0 - C_s)}{\pi^2} \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{D\pi^2 t}{4L^2}\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Application:** Experimentalists fit $\ln(\bar{C}(t) - C_s)$ vs. $t$ to obtain the **effective diffusion coefficient $D$** — a KTU-typical exam question. Typical *D* for oil in soybean flakes (in *n*-hexane, 60 °C): **$1.2 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Universal Extraction Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Biomass Feedstock] --> A1[Pre-Treatment Stage]
    A1 --> A2[Drying and Size Reduction]
    A2 --> A3[Flaking or Cracking]
    A3 --> B1{Extraction Mode Selection}
    B1 -->|Mechanical| C1[Expeller Press]
    B1 -->|Solvent| C2[Solvent Extractor]
    B1 -->|Soxhlet| C3[Soxhlet Apparatus]
    B1 -->|Supercritical| C4[SCFE Reactor]
    B1 -->|Enzymatic| C5[AEP Reactor]
    C1 --> D1[Crude Oil Phase 1]
    C2 --> D2[Crude Oil Phase 2]
    C3 --> D3[Crude Oil Phase 3]
    C4 --> D4[Refined Oil Phase 4]
    C5 --> D5[Hydrolysed Oil Phase 5]
    D1 --> E1[Oil Recovery and Purification]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Solvent Recovery Column]
    E1 --> F2[Degumming Section]
    F1 --> G1[Recycled Solvent Tank]
    F2 --> G2[Refined Bio-Oil Storage]
    C1 --> H1[Spent Cake]
    C2 --> H1
    C3 --> H1
    C4 --> H1
    C5 --> H1
    H1 --> H2[Downstream Conversion Biodiesel or Bioethanol]
    G1 --> C2
    G1 --> C3
    G1 --> C4
    G1 --> C5
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Soxhlet Extraction Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Boiling Flask with Solvent] -->|Vapour Rise| S2[Reflux Condenser]
    S2 -->|Condensate Drip| S3[Thimble Holding Biomass]
    S3 -->|Siphon Cycle| S4[Syphon Arm]
    S4 -->|Recycle of Miscella| S1
    S1 -->|Continuous Reflux| S2
    S3 -->|Concentration Build-up| S5[Extract-rich Miscella]
    S5 -->|End of Run| S6[Evaporation and Solvent Recovery]
    S6 --> S7[Pure Extract Product]
    S6 --> S8[Recovered Solvent]
```

## 4.3 Functional Hierarchy — Hybrid Expeller + Solvent Plant

| Block ID | Stage | Inlet | Outlet | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| BLK1 | Seed Cleaning | Raw seeds | Cleaned seeds | $m_{\text{out}} = m_{\text{in}} \times (1 - x_{\text{impurity}})$ |
| BLK2 | Expeller Press | Cleaned seeds | Crude oil + cake | $Y_1 = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil, expeller}}}{m_{\text{seed}}}$ |
| BLK3 | Cake Conditioning | Spent cake | Flaked cake | $T_{\text{cook}} \approx 60-80$ °C |
| BLK4 | Solvent Extractor | Flaked cake | Miscella + marc | $Y_2 = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil, solvent}}}{m_{\text{cake}}}$ |
| BLK5 | Distillation | Miscella | Pure oil + recovered solvent | $\eta_{\text{rec}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{recovered}}}{V_{\text{fed}}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why block diagrams?** Mermaid cannot faithfully render a *physical* expeller screw geometry, so the **block-level functional architecture** is the KTU-accepted alternative — it captures mass and energy flow without ambiguity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Differentiate between mechanical extraction and solvent extraction of oil from oilseeds.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Mechanical extraction uses **physical pressure** (expeller/screw press) to squeeze oil out of seed cells, typically recovering **70–85 %** of available oil without chemical solvents. It is **energy-moderate** and produces solvent-free crude oil. Solvent extraction, in contrast, exploits the **solubility differential** between oil and a low-boiling solvent (usually *n*-hexane) to dissolve the oil from ground flakes, achieving **90–98 %** recovery but requiring an **additional solvent-recovery step** (distillation). It is **energy-intensive** due to evaporation but offers far higher yield. (3 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2. State Fick's second law of diffusion and explain its relevance to solvent extraction.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Fick's second law states that the **rate of change of solute concentration** at a point is proportional to the **second spatial derivative of concentration**, expressed as:
> $$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$
> In solvent extraction, the **slow diffusion stage** (Stage 2 kinetics) is rate-limited by the **migration of oil from the interior of seed cells to the surface**. Hence, Fick's law directly governs extraction time, particle size, and effective diffusivity *D*. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Extraction Problems:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** between mass (kg) and volume (L) without converting via density is the **#1 mark-loss reason** — always insert $\rho$ explicitly.
> 2. Forgetting to **subtract residual oil** in the spent solid when computing solvent requirement — yields grossly oversized extractors.
> 3. **Ignoring closure check** ($\sum m_{\text{in}} = \sum m_{\text{out}}$) loses at least **1 mark** in any mass-balance question.
> 4. Citing *Fick's first law* when the problem is transient — should always be *Fick's second law* unless explicitly steady-state.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
> **Q A.** (a) Explain the working principle of a **Soxhlet extractor** with a neat sketch. Discuss its advantages and limitations over batch solvent extraction. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Solution:**
> **Working Principle:** A Soxhlet extractor is a **closed-loop reflux apparatus** that repeatedly washes a solid sample with a *fresh portion of condensed solvent*. The solvent in the boiling flask is vaporised by an electric heating mantle, rises through a vapour tube, and is condensed by a reflux condenser. The condensate drips onto the **thimble** holding the biomass. When the solvent level in the thimble chamber reaches the **siphon-arm crest**, the entire miscella is siphoned back to the boiling flask. The cycle repeats **4–6 times per hour** for 6–24 hours, accumulating extract in the flask.
>
> **Advantages over batch solvent extraction:**
> - Continuous **fresh solvent** contact, raising recovery to **95–99 %**.
> - **Thermolabile** oils are protected because the bulk solvent pool is cooler than the boiling flask.
> - **No mechanical agitation** required, simplifying operation.
> - Smaller solvent volume needed per unit mass of solute recovered.
>
> **Limitations:**
> - **Long extraction time** (often 12–24 h).
> - **Energy-intensive** reflux over many hours.
> - Not suited for **thermolabile compounds** that degrade in the boiling flask.
> - **Scale-up is non-trivial** — laboratory apparatus is hard to industrialise.
>
> **Conclusion:** Soxhlet remains the gold-standard analytical method; industrial-scale plants favour **continuous counter-current extractors**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b) A 250 kg batch of oilseeds is processed in a single-stage solvent extractor. The seeds contain 38 % oil (w/w) and the post-extraction spent cake has 1.2 % residual oil. The miscella concentration exiting the extractor is 22 % (w/w). If 250 L of *n*-hexane is fed, calculate the extraction yield, the mass of oil recovered, and the miscella purity deviation. Comment on whether a multi-stage extractor is justified. (7 marks)**
>
> **Model Solution:**
> **Given:** $m_{\text{feed}} = 250$ kg; $C_{\text{feed}} = 0.38$; $C_{\text{cake}} = 0.012$; $C_{\text{miscella}} = 0.22$; $V_{\text{hexane}} = 250$ L.
>
> **Step 1 — Mass of oil initially present**
> $$m_{\text{oil, in}} = 0.38 \times 250 = 95 \text{ kg}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Mass of oil remaining in spent cake**
> $$m_{\text{oil, cake}} = 0.012 \times 250 = 3.0 \text{ kg}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Mass of oil recovered in miscella**
> $$m_{\text{oil, recovered}} = 95 - 3.0 = 92 \text{ kg}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Extraction yield**
> $$Y = \dfrac{92}{95} \times 100 = 96.84 \text{ %}$$
>
> **Step 5 — Mass of miscella (from 22 % w/w oil)**
> $$m_{\text{miscella}} = \dfrac{92}{0.22} = 418.18 \text{ kg}$$
>
> **Step 6 — Mass of hexane in miscella**
> $$m_{\text{hexane, miscella}} = 418.18 - 92 = 326.18 \text{ kg}$$
>
> **Step 7 — Convert 250 L hexane to mass (ρ = 0.6548 kg/L)**
> $$m_{\text{hexane, fed}} = 250 \times 0.6548 = 163.7 \text{ kg}$$
>
> **Step 8 — Miscella purity deviation**
> $$\Delta C = \dfrac{C_{\text{actual}} - C_{\text{target}}}{C_{\text{target}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{22 - 22}{22} \times 100 = 0.0 \text{ % (consistent)}$$
>
> > **Wait** — check if the 250 L feed matches the 22 % miscella requirement:
> > If 92 kg oil requires $R$ kg of solvent for 22 % miscella:
> > $$0.22 = \dfrac{92}{92 + R} \implies R = \dfrac{92 \times 0.78}{0.22} = 326.18 \text{ kg}$$
> > This corresponds to $326.18 / 0.6548 \approx 498.2$ L of hexane — almost **2× the available 250 L**.
> > $$\text{Deviation} = \dfrac{250 - 498.2}{498.2} \times 100 \approx -49.8 \text{ % (severely under-supplied)}$$
>
> **Engineering Verdict:** The **single-stage extractor is grossly under-designed** — solvent deficit is ~50 %. A **multi-stage counter-current extractor** (typically 4–6 stages) is strongly justified. In such a configuration, the **same 250 L of hexane can be re-used** across stages, achieving the 22 % miscella concentration with effective solvent economy. (7 marks)

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 (Alternative)]**
> **Q B.** (a) Describe the **supercritical fluid extraction (SCFE)** of bio-oil using CO₂. Mention its critical parameters and compare it with conventional solvent extraction. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Solution:**
> **Principle:** SCFE exploits the **tunable solvating power** of a fluid held above its **critical temperature (T_c)** and **critical pressure (P_c)**. For CO₂, the critical point is **T_c = 31.1 °C, P_c = 73.8 bar**. Above this point, CO₂ possesses **both gas-like diffusivity and liquid-like density**, enabling rapid penetration of biomass pores and efficient dissolution of non-polar bio-oils.
>
> **Typical SCFE Operating Window for Biodiesel Feedstocks:**
> - Pressure: **250 – 400 bar**
> - Temperature: **40 – 60 °C**
> - CO₂ flow rate: **1 – 5 kg CO₂ / kg feed**
> - Extraction time: **1 – 4 hours**
>
> **Comparison with Solvent Extraction (e.g., n-hexane):**
> - **No solvent residue** in extract (CO₂ evaporates on depressurisation) — food/pharma-grade.
> - **Low operating temperature** preserves thermolabile antioxidants.
> - **Selectivity** tunable via pressure modulation.
> - **Disadvantage:** High capital cost and compressor load.
>
> **Conclusion:** SCFE is preferred for **high-value, low-volume extracts** (algal nutraceuticals, specialty oils). For bulk biodiesel feedstock, *n*-hexane remains the industrial workhorse. (7 marks)
>
> **(b) The extraction yield of oil from a microalgal biomass follows Fickian diffusion. In a slab of half-thickness L = 0.5 mm, the effective diffusivity of lipid in supercritical CO₂ is D = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ m²/s. Estimate the time required to reach 90 % extraction completion. (7 marks)**
>
> **Model Solution:**
> **Given:** $L = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m; $D = 4.0 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s; $E = 0.90$ (extraction fraction).
>
> **Step 1 — Residual fraction after extraction**
> $$\text{Residual fraction} = 1 - E = 0.10$$
>
> **Step 2 — Long-time Fickian solution for the average concentration** (first-term approximation):
> $$\dfrac{\bar{C}(t) - C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{D \pi^2 t}{4L^2}\right]$$
>
> For negligible surface concentration ($C_s \approx 0$) and large $E$, the residual fraction simplifies to:
> $$\dfrac{\bar{C}(t)}{C_0} \approx \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{D \pi^2 t}{4L^2}\right]$$
>
> **Step 3 — Set residual fraction = 0.10 and solve for t**
> $$0.10 = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{D \pi^2 t}{4L^2}\right]$$
> $$\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{D \pi^2 t}{4L^2}\right] = 0.10 \times \dfrac{\pi^2}{8} = 0.1234$$
> $$-\dfrac{D \pi^2 t}{4L^2} = \ln(0.1234) = -2.0923$$
> $$t = \dfrac{2.0923 \times 4L^2}{D \pi^2}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Substitute numerical values**
> $$L^2 = (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$
> $$t = \dfrac{2.0923 \times 4 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{4.0 \times 10^{-10} \times 9.8696}$$
> $$t = \dfrac{2.0923 \times 10^{-6}}{3.948 \times 10^{-9}}$$
> $$t \approx 530.0 \text{ s} \approx 8.83 \text{ min}$$
>
> **Step 5 — Engineering interpretation**
> The extraction is **90 % complete in less than 10 minutes** for thin algal slabs — this justifies the **high throughput of SCFE** in microalgal biodiesel production. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Extraction (Module 2):**
> - Extraction is the **upstream mass-transfer operation** that converts biomass into a processable bio-oil/sugar stream.
> - **Three kinetic stages:** (1) fast wash, (2) Fickian diffusion, (3) desorption cleanup.
> - **Methods:** Mechanical (expeller), Solvent (*n*-hexane), Soxhlet, SCFE, UAE, AEP.
> - **Yield equation:** $Y = (m_{\text{oil}} / m_{\text{feed}}) \times 100$.
> - **Mass balance must close** to within ±0.5 % — always verify.
> - **Solvent-to-solid ratio (R):** typical $4 - 10$ L/kg for industrial extractors.
> - **Soxhlet** is the analytical gold standard; **expeller + solvent** is industrial scale.
> - **SC-CO₂** uses $T > 31.1$ °C and $P > 73.8$ bar — premium extracts, high capex.
> - **Fick's second law** governs Stage 2 kinetics; effective diffusivity $D$ for oil in seed flakes $\sim 10^{-10}$ m²/s.
> - **Hybrid configuration (expeller → solvent)** is the KTU-favoured industrial solution.
> - **Engineering optimisation rule:** maximise $Y \times \eta_{\text{rec}}$ subject to $\eta_{\text{energy}} \ge \text{threshold}$.
> - **Common pitfall:** unit mismatches (mass vs volume without $\rho$ conversion).
> - **Bonus tip:** Always recommend **counter-current multi-stage** over single-stage when $R$ falls below 0.5 L/kg.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production and use
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Production and use

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Biofuels** are gaseous, liquid, or solid fuels produced from **biomass feedstocks** through biochemical, thermochemical, or chemical conversion routes. The topic "Production and Use" within Module 2 of the KTU PEBBT413 syllabus specifically addresses the **industrial-scale manufacturing processes** of the three principal transport-grade biofuels — **bioethanol (C₂H₅OH)**, **biodiesel (Fatty Acid Methyl Esters – FAME)**, and **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)** — together with their downstream end-use applications in transportation, power generation, and heating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 2 – Production and Use):**
> The student must demonstrate competency in: (i) feedstock selection, (ii) process chemistry & stoichiometry, (iii) unit operations (pre-treatment, reaction, separation, purification), (iv) yield & efficiency calculations, and (v) end-use blending ratios, engine compatibility, and storage.

### 1.2 Classification of Biofuels by Generation

| Generation | Feedstock Type | Conversion Route | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1st Gen** | Sugar, starch, vegetable oil, animal fat | Biochemical / Chemical | Bioethanol from sugarcane, Biodiesel from soybean oil |
| **2nd Gen** | Lignocellulosic biomass, non-edible oils, waste cooking oil | Advanced biochemical / Thermochemical | Cellulosic ethanol, biodiesel from Jatropha |
| **3rd Gen** | Algae | Lipid extraction + transesterification | Algal biodiesel, biohydrogen |
| **4th Gen** | Genetically engineered microorganisms | Electrofuels, synthetic biology | Iso-butanol, farnesane |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Kitchen to Fuel Tank" Idea
Imagine your kitchen. **Sugar** from sugarcane is what your body ferments into energy. Yeast does exactly the same thing — it "eats" glucose and "exhales" ethanol and CO₂. **Vegetable oil** in your frying pan is a triglyceride — a fat molecule. If you react it with methanol using a catalyst (NaOH/KOH), you get a thinner liquid that diesel engines love — this is biodiesel. **Wet organic waste** in a sealed landfill naturally breaks down to release methane — replicate this in a sealed digester and you get biogas. Each biofuel is essentially **stored sunlight captured by photosynthesis**, released back as a usable fuel.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Density of biodiesel (FAME)**: ≈ **0.88 g/cm³** at 15 °C
> - **Density of bioethanol**: ≈ **0.789 g/cm³** at 20 °C
> - **Energy content (LHV)** of bioethanol: ≈ **29.7 MJ/kg**
> - **Energy content (LHV)** of biodiesel: ≈ **37.2 MJ/kg**
> - **Energy content (LHV)** of biogas (60% CH₄): ≈ **20 MJ/m³**
> - **Universal gas constant (R)**: **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **Molar mass of methanol (CH₃OH)**: **32.04 g/mol**
> - **Molar mass of glycerol (C₃H₈O₃)**: **92.09 g/mol**
> - **Molar mass of a typical triglyceride (triolein, C₅₇H₁₀₄O₆)**: **885.4 g/mol**
> - **Molar mass of methyl oleate (FAME)**: **296.49 g/mol**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Bioethanol — Production Chemistry
Bioethanol is produced by **fermentation** of fermentable sugars using the yeast *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (or *Zymomonas mobilis*).

**Step 1 – Hydrolysis of disaccharides:**
$$\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{invertase}} 2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$$

**Step 2 – Glycolysis + Alcoholic Fermentation (Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas pathway):**
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{zymase}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$$

- **Theoretical yield**: 1 mol glucose (180 g) → 2 mol ethanol (92 g) + 2 mol CO₂ (88 g).
- **Mass yield (theoretical)** = (92/180) × 100 = **51.1 % w/w**.
- **Practical industrial yield** ≈ **90–95 % of theoretical** → **~46–48 % w/w**.

**Step 3 – Distillation & Dehydration:**
- Simple distillation yields only ~95 % v/v ethanol (azeotrope with water).
- **Molecular sieves (3 Å zeolite)** or **azeotropic distillation (benzene/cyclohexane)** are required to reach **anhydrous ethanol (99.5–99.9 % v/v)** suitable for engine blending.

### 2.2 Lignocellulosic (2G) Ethanol — Pretreatment
Because *S. cerevisiae* cannot directly ferment cellulose/hemicellulose, **pretreatment + enzymatic hydrolysis** is required:
- **Pretreatment methods**: dilute acid (H₂SO₄), steam explosion, ammonia fiber explosion (AFEX), organosolv.
- **Enzymatic hydrolysis**: cellulase cocktail (endoglucanase + exoglucanase + β-glucosidase) converts cellulose to glucose.
- **Fermentation inhibitor removal**: detoxification to remove furfural, HMF, acetic acid.

### 2.3 Biodiesel — Transesterification Mechanism
Biodiesel (FAME) is produced by reacting a **triglyceride** with a **short-chain alcohol** (methanol preferred) in the presence of an **alkaline catalyst** (NaOH or KOH).

**Overall stoichiometric reaction:**
$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{NaOH/KOH, 60 °C}} 3\,\text{R-COO-CH}_3 + \text{Glycerol}$$

The reaction proceeds in **three consecutive reversible steps** (mono-, di-, triglyceride intermediates).

**Why methanol?**
- Lowest molecular weight alcohol → highest FAME yield per kg oil.
- Cheap and readily available.
- Forms a separate glycerol phase easily (favours forward reaction).
- Low boiling point (64.7 °C) → easy recovery.

### 2.4 Biogas — Anaerobic Digestion (AD)
Biogas is produced by **microbial decomposition of organic matter in the absence of oxygen**. The process has four sequential stages:

| Stage | Reaction | Microorganism | Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydrolysis** | Polymers → Monomers | Clostridium, Bacillus | Amino acids, sugars, fatty acids |
| **Acidogenesis** | Monomers → VFAs | Lactobacillus | Acetate, propionate, butyrate, H₂, CO₂ |
| **Acetogenesis** | VFAs → Acetate | Syntrophobacter | CH₃COOH, H₂, CO₂ |
| **Methanogenesis** | Acetate / H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄ | Methanobacterium, Methanosarcina | CH₄ + CO₂ |

**Overall stoichiometry (Buswell equation):**
$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2$$

For glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆):
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 3\,\text{CH}_4 + 3\,\text{CO}_2$$

**Typical biogas composition (by volume)**: **CH₄ 50–70 %**, **CO₂ 30–40 %**, trace H₂S, NH₃, water vapour.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are recurring KTU exam favourites.**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical ethanol yield from glucose | $Y_E = \dfrac{2 \times 46}{180} \times 100$ | % w/w |
| 2 | Theoretical FAME yield (per mole triglyceride) | 3 mol FAME per 1 mol TG | mol/mol |
| 3 | Mass yield of FAME (using triolein) | $\dfrac{3 \times 296.49}{885.4} \times 100$ | % w/w |
| 4 | Required methanol mass (stoichiometric) | $m_{MeOH} = m_{oil} \times \dfrac{3 \times 32.04}{885.4} \times \dfrac{1}{MW_{oil}}$ | kg |
| 5 | Excess methanol factor | $\alpha = \dfrac{n_{MeOH,act}}{n_{MeOH,stoich}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Volumetric methane yield | $V_{CH_4} = \dfrac{n_{CH_4} \times R \times T}{P}$ | m³ |
| 7 | Energy content of biogas | $E = V \times \rho_{CH_4} \times LHV_{CH_4} \times x_{CH_4}$ | MJ |
| 8 | Higher heating value (HHV, Dulong) of biomass | $HHV = 0.338\,C + 1.428\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095\,S$ | MJ/kg |
| 9 | Carbon balance | $C_{in} = C_{out}$ (closed system) | g C |
| 10 | Reaction conversion | $X = \dfrac{n_{TG,0} - n_{TG,f}}{n_{TG,0}}$ | dimensionless |

### 2.6 End-Use Applications & Blending Ratios

| Biofuel | Engine / Use | Common Blends |
|---|---|---|
| **Bioethanol** | Spark-ignition (Otto) engines | **E5, E10, E85, E100** |
| **Biodiesel** | Compression-ignition (Diesel) engines | **B2, B5, B20, B100** |
| **Biogas** | Gas engines, turbines, boilers, CNG vehicles | **Upgraded to ~96 % CH₄ (biomethane)** for grid injection |
| **Bioethanol → ETBE** | Gasoline oxygenate | Ethyl *tert*-butyl ether (15 % v/v) |
| **Biodiesel → HEFA** | Aviation (renewable diesel) | Hydroprocessed Esters and Fatty Acids |

### 2.7 Engineering Utility in the Real World
- **Transportation decarbonization**: India launched **E20 (20 % ethanol blending)** in petrol by **2025** under the Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme.
- **National Biodiesel Mission (NBM)** targets **B5 blending** in diesel.
- **SATAT (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation)** initiative sets up **5,000 compressed biogas (CBG) plants** by 2025.
- **Biogas plants** are mandatory in Kerala for **schools, hostels, and dairy farms** with > 50 cattle (Panchayat-level regulations).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Mass Yield of FAME from Triolein (Triglyceride of Oleic Acid)

**Given reaction:**
$$\text{C}_{57}\text{H}_{104}\text{O}_6 + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow 3\,\text{C}_{19}\text{H}_{36}\text{O}_2 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_3$$

**Step 1 — Verify stoichiometric balance:**

| Element | LHS | RHS |
|---|---|---|
| C | 57 + 3 = 60 | 3(19) + 3 = 60 ✓ |
| H | 104 + 12 = 116 | 3(36) + 8 = 116 ✓ |
| O | 6 + 3 = 9 | 3(2) + 3 = 9 ✓ |

**Step 2 — Molecular weights:**
- Triolein (C₅₇H₁₀₄O₆) → 12(57) + 1(104) + 16(6) = 684 + 104 + 96 = **885.4 g/mol**
- Methanol (CH₃OH) → 12 + 4 + 16 = **32.04 g/mol**
- Methyl oleate (C₁₉H₃₆O₂) → 12(19) + 1(36) + 16(2) = 228 + 36 + 32 = **296.49 g/mol**
- Glycerol (C₃H₈O₃) → 36 + 8 + 48 = **92.09 g/mol**

**Step 3 — Mass of methanol required per 1000 kg oil:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{MeOH,stoich} &= 1000 \times \frac{3 \times 32.04}{885.4} \\
&= 1000 \times \frac{96.12}{885.4} \\
&= 1000 \times 0.10857 \\
&= 108.57\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of FAME produced from 1000 kg oil (theoretical):**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{FAME,th} &= 1000 \times \frac{3 \times 296.49}{885.4} \\
&= 1000 \times \frac{889.47}{885.4} \\
&= 1004.6\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Mass yield of FAME:**

$$Y_{FAME} = \frac{m_{FAME,th}}{m_{oil}} \times 100 = \frac{1004.6}{1000} \times 100 \approx 100.46\ \%\,w/w$$

> [!NOTE]
> The FAME yield is **slightly > 100 %** because the three methyl groups added to the glycerol backbone (replacing the glycerol moiety) have greater total mass than the glycerol split off. This is **not** a violation of mass conservation — it includes the methanol mass that becomes chemically bound.

**Step 6 — Mass of glycerol by-product:**

$$m_{glycerol} = 1000 \times \frac{92.09}{885.4} = 104.0\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 7 — Mass balance check:**

$$m_{oil} + m_{MeOH} = 1000 + 108.57 = 1108.57\ \text{kg}$$
$$m_{FAME} + m_{glycerol} = 1004.6 + 104.0 = 1108.6\ \text{kg} \quad\checkmark$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose

**Reaction:** C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂

**Step 1 — Molecular weights:**
- Glucose → 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 72 + 12 + 96 = **180.16 g/mol**
- Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) → 2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = **46.07 g/mol**
- CO₂ → 12 + 32 = **44.01 g/mol**

**Step 2 — Theoretical ethanol mass from 1000 kg glucose:**

$$m_{EtOH,th} = 1000 \times \frac{2 \times 46.07}{180.16} = 1000 \times 0.5114 = 511.4\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Practical yield (90 % of theoretical):**

$$m_{EtOH,prac} = 0.90 \times 511.4 = 460.3\ \text{kg per 1000 kg glucose}$$

**Step 4 — CO₂ produced:**

$$m_{CO_2} = 1000 \times \frac{2 \times 44.01}{180.16} = 488.6\ \text{kg}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Methane Volume from Anaerobic Digestion of 1 kg Glucose

**Reaction:** C₆H₁₂O₆ + 0 H₂O → 3 CH₄ + 3 CO₂ (using Buswell's equation with b = 6, a = 12, n = 6; coefficient of water = 6 − 12/4 − 6/2 = 6 − 3 − 3 = 0).

**Step 1 — Moles of glucose in 1 kg:**

$$n_{glucose} = \frac{1000}{180.16} = 5.551\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of CH₄ produced:**

$$n_{CH_4} = 3 \times 5.551 = 16.65\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 3 — Volume of CH₄ at STP (T = 273.15 K, P = 101.325 kPa):**

$$V_{CH_4,STP} = n_{CH_4} \times 22.414\ \text{L/mol} = 16.65 \times 22.414 = 373.2\ \text{L} \approx 0.373\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Volume at mesophilic AD conditions (T = 35 °C = 308.15 K):**

$$V_{CH_4,35°C} = V_{STP} \times \frac{308.15}{273.15} = 0.373 \times 1.1284 = 0.421\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Total biogas volume (assuming 60 % CH₄):**

$$V_{biogas} = \frac{V_{CH_4}}{0.60} = \frac{0.421}{0.60} = 0.702\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Energy content (LHV of CH₄ = 50 MJ/kg; density of CH₄ at STP = 0.716 kg/m³):**

$$E = 0.373 \times 0.716 \times 50 = 13.35\ \text{MJ per kg glucose}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative End-Use

| Property | Bioethanol | Biodiesel | Biogas |
|---|---|---|---|
| Physical state | Liquid | Liquid | Gaseous |
| Energy density (LHV, MJ/kg) | **29.7** | **37.2** | **20–55** (varies with CH₄) |
| Cetane number | 8 | **45–55** | — |
| Octane number (RON) | **108–110** | — | 130 (CH₄) |
| Flash point (°C) | 13 | **120–180** | −188 (CH₄) |
| Sulfur content | < 10 ppm | < 10 ppm | Trace H₂S |
| O₂ content (% mass) | 34.7 | 11 | — |
| Compatibility with engine | Spark-ignition | Compression-ignition | Dual-fuel / gas engines |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical — KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem:** 500 kg of waste cooking oil (assumed pure triolein, MW = 885.4) is transesterified with **150 % stoichiometric excess methanol** in the presence of 1 % NaOH catalyst at 60 °C. Assuming 95 % conversion, calculate:
> (a) Actual mass of methanol charged, (b) Mass of FAME produced, (c) Mass of glycerol produced.

**Solution:**

**(a) Stoichiometric methanol = 500 × (96.12/885.4) = 54.28 kg.**
With 150 % excess → total = 2.5 × 54.28 = **135.7 kg methanol charged**.

**(b) FAME produced = η × m_oil × (3 × 296.49 / 885.4) = 0.95 × 500 × 1.0046 = 477.2 kg.**

**(c) Glycerol produced = 0.95 × 500 × (92.09/885.4) = 49.4 kg.**

> [!NOTE]
> The **unreacted methanol (135.7 − 54.28 = 81.42 kg)** is recovered by distillation and recycled in industrial practice.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Flow Diagram — Biodiesel Production (Batch / Continuous Transesterification)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[feedVegOilFeedstock] --> B[feedPreTreatment]
    A2[feedMethanolFeedstock] --> C[feedCatalystMixing]
    A3[feedNaOHorKOH] --> C
    B --> C
    C --> D[feedReactor60C1hStirred]
    D --> E[feedGravityDecanter]
    E --> F[feedFAMERichPhase]
    E --> G[feedGlycerolRichPhase]
    F --> H[feedWaterWash]
    H --> I[feedDryingVacuum]
    I --> J[feedBiodieselFAMEStorage]
    G --> K[feedGlycerolPurification]
    K --> L[feedPharmaceuticalGlycerol]
    J --> M[feedBlendingB5B20]
    M --> N[feedDieselEngineUse]
```

**Process description (in words):**
1. **Feedstock prep**: Degumming (H₃PO₄) → Neutralization (NaOH) → Water wash → Drying → oil with < 0.1 % free fatty acids (FFA).
2. **Catalyst mixing**: Methoxide solution prepared by dissolving NaOH/KOH pellets in methanol (forms CH₃ONa⁺ + H₂O).
3. **Transesterification reactor**: Stirred tank, 60 °C, 1 h, atmospheric pressure, methanol-to-oil molar ratio = 6:1.
4. **Phase separation**: Gravity decanter — FAME (top, density 0.88 g/cm³) + glycerol (bottom, density 1.26 g/cm³).
5. **FAME purification**: Water wash → vacuum drying → product storage.
6. **Glycerol purification**: Acidification → ion exchange → distillation.

### 4.2 Block Flow Diagram — Biogas Production (Anaerobic Digestion)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[feedBiomassFeedstock] --> B[feedFeedPreparationSizeReduction]
    B --> C[feedMixingWithWater1to3]
    C --> D[feedAnaerobicDigester35C25Days]
    D --> E[feedBiogasCollection]
    D --> F[feedDigestateSlurry]
    E --> G[feedH2SScrubber]
    G --> H[feedCHPEngine]
    G --> I[feedUpgradedBiomethaneGrid]
    F --> J[feedSolidLiquidSeparator]
    J --> K[feedLiquidFractionFertigation]
    J --> L[feedSolidFractionCompost]
```

**Process description (in words):**
- **Feedstock**: Cattle dung, kitchen waste, agricultural residue, sewage sludge.
- **Mixing**: TS (total solids) adjusted to 8–12 % w/w for efficient hydrolysis.
- **AD reactor**: Continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) or plug-flow; mesophilic (35 °C) or thermophilic (55 °C); HRT 20–30 days.
- **Biogas utilization**: CHP (combined heat and power) engines for electricity, or upgraded to biomethane (CO₂ removal via water scrubbing / PSA / membrane) for grid injection / CNG vehicles.
- **Digestate management**: Liquid → biofertilizer; Solid → compost.

### 4.3 Block Flow Diagram — Bioethanol Production (1G and 2G)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[feedSugarcane] --> B1[feedMillingJuiceExtraction]
    A2[feedLignocellulosicBiomass] --> B2[feedPretreatmentDiluteAcid]
    A3[feedCornStarch] --> B3[feedLiquefactionSaccharification]
    B1 --> C[feedFermentation30C48h]
    B2 --> C2[feedEnzymaticHydrolysis]
    C2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[feedDistillation95v]
    D --> E[feedMolecularSieveDehydration]
    E --> F[feedAnhydrousEthanol99.5v]
    F --> G[feedBlendingE5E10E20]
    D --> H[feedSpentWashDDGS]
```

### 4.4 End-Use Application Flow — Engine Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[feedBioethanol] --> Q1[feedSparkIgnitionEngine]
    P2[feedBiodiesel] --> Q2[feedDieselEngine]
    P3[feedBiogas] --> Q3[feedGasEngineDualFuel]
    P3 --> Q4[feedUpgradedCBGCNG]
    P4[feedBioethanol] --> Q5[feedETBEGasolineOxygenate]
    P2 --> Q6[feedRenewableDieselHEFA]
    Q1 --> R[feedTransportFuelPool]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    Q4 --> R
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
List the three generations of biofuels with one example each. Justify why 2G biofuels are termed "second generation."

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **1st Generation**: Biofuels from edible food crops — *e.g.,* bioethanol from sugarcane juice, biodiesel from soybean oil.
> - **2nd Generation**: Biofuels from lignocellulosic biomass, non-edible oils, and waste — *e.g.,* cellulosic ethanol from rice straw, biodiesel from Jatropha.
> - **3rd Generation**: Biofuels from algae — *e.g.,* algal biodiesel, biohydrogen from microalgae.
> - **Justification**: 2G biofuels are termed "second generation" because they use **non-food, abundantly available lignocellulosic feedstocks**, eliminating the "food vs. fuel" conflict inherent to 1G fuels and offering greater sustainability and lower land-use impact. (1 mark)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Write the stoichiometric equation for the transesterification of a triglyceride with methanol. Why is methanol preferred over ethanol for biodiesel production?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> $$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{cat.}} 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol}$$
> Methanol is preferred because: (i) it is the **cheapest** short-chain alcohol, (ii) it has the **lowest molecular weight (32.04 g/mol)**, giving highest FAME yield per kg of oil, (iii) it is **easily recovered** due to low boiling point (64.7 °C), (iv) it forms a **clean two-phase separation** with glycerol, and (v) ethanol forms **stable emulsions** with the reaction mixture, complicating product separation. (2 marks for any four points)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Set B]**
> (a) With a neat block diagram, describe the **batch transesterification process** for biodiesel production. State the typical process conditions and the role of each unit operation. **(7 marks)**
> (b) Starting from triolein (MW = 885.4 g/mol), derive the **stoichiometric methanol requirement** and the **theoretical FAME yield** for 1 tonne (1000 kg) of oil. Comment on why industrial practice uses **6:1 to 9:1 methanol-to-oil molar ratio** rather than the stoichiometric 3:1. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Block diagram** (as in Section 4.1 above) — **3 marks** for correct flow and labelled units.

**Process conditions & role of each unit (4 marks):**
- **Degumming & neutralization**: Removes phospholipids and FFA that would otherwise form soap with the catalyst (saponification side reaction).
- **Catalyst (NaOH/KOH, 1 % w/w of oil)**: Dissolved in methanol to form sodium methoxide (CH₃ONa⁺), the actual active catalyst.
- **Reactor conditions**: 60 °C (just below methanol boiling point to prevent loss), 1 hour, atmospheric pressure, 350–400 rpm stirring for mass-transfer-limited reaction.
- **Decanter**: Gravity settling exploits the density difference — FAME (top, ρ ≈ 0.88 g/cm³) and glycerol phase (bottom, ρ ≈ 1.26 g/cm³).
- **Water wash**: Removes residual catalyst, glycerol traces, and soap.
- **Vacuum drying**: Reduces water content below 0.05 % to prevent hydrolysis during storage.
- **Methanol recovery**: Unreacted methanol distilled off and recycled.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

(1) **Reaction stoichiometry:**
$$\text{C}_{57}\text{H}_{104}\text{O}_6 + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow 3\,\text{C}_{19}\text{H}_{36}\text{O}_2 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_3$$

(2) **Stoichiometric methanol mass for 1000 kg triolein:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{MeOH,stoich} &= 1000 \times \frac{3 \times 32.04}{885.4} \\
&= 1000 \times 0.10857 = 108.57\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

(3) **Theoretical FAME yield:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{FAME,th} &= 1000 \times \frac{3 \times 296.49}{885.4} = 1004.6\ \text{kg} \\
Y_{FAME} &= 100.46\ \%\,w/w
\end{aligned}$$

(4) **Why excess methanol (6:1 to 9:1) is used industrially (3 marks):**
- The transesterification is a **reversible (equilibrium-limited) reaction**. Le Chatelier's principle dictates that an **excess of methanol drives the equilibrium forward**, increasing triglyceride conversion.
- A **6:1 molar ratio** (i.e., 2× stoichiometric) typically gives > 95 % conversion in 1 h.
- Beyond **9:1**, the marginal gain in conversion does not justify the **added cost of methanol recovery, larger reactor volume, and energy penalty for distillation**.
- Excess methanol also **reduces viscosity** of the reaction mixture, improving mass transfer.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Set A]**
> (a) With a labelled block diagram, explain the **four stages of anaerobic digestion** for biogas production. Write the **Buswell equation** and apply it to glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) to find the CH₄:CO₂ molar ratio. **(7 marks)**
> (b) Compare **bioethanol, biodiesel, and biogas** in terms of physical state, energy density, typical blending ratio with conventional fuel, and the engine type they suit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Block diagram** (as in Section 4.2) — **3 marks** for the four AD stages and biogas utilization.

**Description of four stages (3 marks):**
1. **Hydrolysis** — Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are broken down into monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids) by extracellular enzymes.
2. **Acidogenesis** — Monomers are converted to volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂.
3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs are oxidized to acetate, H₂, and CO₂ by syntrophic bacteria.
4. **Methanogenesis** — Acetate is split into CH₄ + CO₂, and H₂ + CO₂ is reduced to CH₄ by methanogens.

**Buswell equation derivation for glucose:**

General Buswell equation:
$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2$$

For C₆H₁₂O₆ (n = 6, a = 12, b = 6):
- Coefficient of H₂O = 6 − 12/4 − 6/2 = 6 − 3 − 3 = **0**
- Coefficient of CH₄ = 6/2 − 12/8 + 6/4 = 3 − 1.5 + 1.5 = **3**
- Coefficient of CO₂ = 6/2 + 12/8 − 6/4 = 3 + 1.5 − 1.5 = **3**

$$\boxed{\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 3\,\text{CH}_4 + 3\,\text{CO}_2}$$

**CH₄:CO₂ molar ratio = 1:1 (50:50 by volume) — 1 mark.**

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

| Property | Bioethanol | Biodiesel | Biogas |
|---|---|---|---|
| Physical state | Liquid | Liquid | Gas |
| Energy density (MJ/kg) | 29.7 | 37.2 | 20–55 (CH₄ = 50) |
| Blending ratio | E5, E10, E20, E85 | B2, B5, B20, B100 | 100 % (after upgrading to biomethane) |
| Engine type | Spark-ignition (SI) | Compression-ignition (CI) | Gas engine / dual-fuel / CNG |

(7 marks = 1 mark per correct row × 4 properties + 3 marks for the table presentation and concluding sentence identifying India-specific programmes E20, B5, SATAT.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Production & Use Module):**
> 1. **Failing to balance the transesterification equation** — at least 1 mark is deducted for an unbalanced reaction.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of catalyst in transesterification** — many students write "NaOH reacts with oil" instead of "NaOH reacts with methanol to form CH₃ONa, which is the true catalyst."
> 3. **Confusing hydrolysis and acidogenesis** — these are two distinct stages, not synonyms.
> 4. **Not stating the AD temperature (mesophilic 35 °C vs. thermophilic 55 °C)** — this is a 1-mark question in Part A.
> 5. **Forgetting the azeotrope issue** in ethanol distillation (95 % v/v) — the need for molecular sieves is a common Part A question.
> 6. **Confusing LHV and HHV** — for engine fuel calculations, LHV is appropriate; for heating value in combustion, HHV is used.

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things you MUST remember for the KTU 2024 ESE on "Production and Use":**

- ☐ **Transesterification** = 1 TG + 3 MeOH → 3 FAME + Glycerol (NaOH/KOH cat., 60 °C, 1 h).
- ☐ **Theoretical FAME yield** ≈ 100.46 % w/w (slightly > 100 % because of methanol incorporation).
- ☐ **Methanol-to-oil molar ratio** = 6:1 to 9:1 industrially (stoichiometric = 3:1).
- ☐ **Bioethanol reaction**: C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂. Theoretical yield = **51.1 % w/w**; practical = **~46–48 % w/w**.
- ☐ **Ethanol distillation azeotrope** at 95.6 % v/v — molecular sieves (3 Å) needed for anhydrous.
- ☐ **Anaerobic digestion**: 4 stages — **H**ydrolysis → **A**cidogenesis → **A**cetogenesis → **M**ethanogenesis.
- ☐ **Buswell equation** gives CH₄ and CO₂ stoichiometry; for glucose, **CH₄:CO₂ = 1:1**.
- ☐ **Biogas composition** ≈ 60 % CH₄ + 40 % CO₂ + trace H₂S; energy content ≈ **20 MJ/m³**.
- ☐ **First-generation biofuels** → food vs. fuel conflict → leads to **2G (lignocellulosic)**.
- ☐ **End-use blending ratios**: E5/E10/E20/E85 (petrol), B5/B20/B100 (diesel), upgraded biomethane (CBG).
- ☐ **India-specific programmes**: EBP (E20 by 2025), NBM (B5), SATAT (5,000 CBG plants).
- ☐ **Bioethanol** suits **SI (Otto) engines**; **Biodiesel** suits **CI (Diesel) engines**; **Biogas** suits **gas/dual-fuel engines**.
- ☐ Key constants: MW of methanol = **32.04**, glycerol = **92.09**, triolein = **885.4**, FAME = **296.49**.
- ☐ **Octane rating** of ethanol ≈ 108–110 RON; **Cetane number** of biodiesel ≈ 45–55.
- ☐ **Practical yields** (memory anchor): Biodiesel ~95 %, Ethanol ~90 %, Biogas (CH₄) ~60 % (of theoretical).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of vegetable oil as an alternative diesel fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of vegetable oil as an alternative diesel fuel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Vegetable oils are triglyceride-based bio-liquids extracted from oilseeds, fruits, or nuts of plants, which can be used — either directly or after chemical modification — as a renewable, alternative fuel for Compression Ignition (CI / Diesel) engines. Their molecular structure, derived from glycerol and long-chain fatty acids, makes them chemically analogous to petroleum diesel in terms of carbon–hydrogen content, while their renewability addresses the sustainability gap of fossil fuels.*

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cooking Oil in a Tractor" Mental Model

Imagine you have leftover sunflower oil after frying food. You notice it is **thicker, oily, and slippery** — almost like a slow-pouring syrup. Now imagine tipping that into the fuel tank of a diesel tractor. Would it work? **Mechanically, yes — the engine will run.** But will it run *well*? **Not really**, for the same reason honey doesn't pour cleanly out of a bottle: it is **too viscous**.

This "syrup-thickness" of vegetable oil is the central engineering challenge covered in this topic. The KTU 2024 module explores how we can either (a) thin the oil, (b) blend it, (c) crack it thermally, or (d) chemically convert it into **biodiesel (FAME — Fatty Acid Methyl Esters)** so it behaves *exactly* like petroleum diesel.

### 1.2 The Why — Motivation for Vegetable Oil as Diesel Alternative

| Driver | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Energy Security** | India imports ~85% of crude oil; vegetable oil is a domestically grown feedstock. |
| **Renewability** | Vegetable oils are part of the **short carbon cycle** — CO₂ released on combustion is reabsorbed by next season's crop. |
| **Lubricity** | Vegetable oils have superior lubricity, reducing engine wear (ASTM D6079 HFRR value). |
| **Biodegradability** | Rapidly biodegradable (≥ 90% in 21 days, OECD 301B) — safer for soil and water. |
| **Sulfur-Free** | Negligible sulfur (< 10 ppm) → lower SOₓ emissions compared to diesel (~ 350–500 ppm). |

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Board Point:** The KTU 2024 examiner consistently tests the **properties comparison table** between vegetable oil and diesel. Memorize density, viscosity, flash point, calorific value, and cetane number.

### 1.3 Classification of Vegetable Oils Used as Fuels

Vegetable oils are broadly classified into three feedstock generations:

1. **First-Generation (Edible Oils):** Soybean, Sunflower, Rapeseed, Palm, Groundnut, Coconut.
2. **Second-Generation (Non-Edible Oils):** Jatropha (*Jatropha curcas*), Karanja / Pongamia (*Pongamia pinnata*), Neem (*Azadirachta indica*), Mahua, Castor.
3. **Third-Generation (Waste / Used Cooking Oils):** Yellow grease, brown grease, spent frying oil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU emphasizes *non-edible* oils (Jatropha, Karanja):** They do **not** compete with the food chain, can grow on **marginal/wasteland**, and align with India's **National Biodiesel Mission (NBM)** targeting **5% blending by 2030**.

### 1.4 The Four Utilization Pathways (Roadmap of the Module)

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes **four** engineering routes for deploying vegetable oil in a diesel engine:

$$\text{Vegetable Oil} \;\longrightarrow\; \begin{cases} \textbf{Route 1:} & \text{Direct Use / Blending (20–30%)} \\ \textbf{Route 2:} & \text{Microemulsion Formation} \\ \textbf{Route 3:} & \text{Thermal Cracking (Pyrolysis)} \\ \textbf{Route 4:} & \text{Transesterification} \rightarrow \text{Biodiesel (FAME)} \end{cases}$$

Each route addresses the **single biggest drawback** of neat vegetable oil: **its high kinematic viscosity (≈ 30–50 mm²/s at 40 °C)**, which is **~10× higher** than that of petroleum diesel (≈ 2–4.5 mm²/s at 40 °C).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chemistry — Triglyceride Architecture

A vegetable oil molecule is a **triglyceride** (also called *triacylglycerol, TAG*), formed by the esterification of **one glycerol molecule** (C₃H₈O₃) with **three fatty acid molecules** (R–COOH), releasing three water molecules.

> [!NOTE]
> **General Reaction of Triglyceride Formation:**
> $$\text{Glycerol} + 3\,\text{Fatty Acid} \;\xrightleftharpoons[\;-\;3H_2O\;]{\;+\;3H_2O\;}\; \text{Triglyceride (TAG)}$$

The "R" in the fatty acid is a long hydrocarbon chain (typically **C12 to C22**). For example:
- **Oleic acid:** C₁₇H₃₃COOH (C18:1, one double bond)
- **Stearic acid:** C₁₇H₃₅COOH (C18:0, saturated)
- **Linoleic acid:** C₁₇H₃₁COOH (C18:2, two double bonds)

### 2.2 The "Why" — Engine Behavior of Each Property

Let us break down **why** each property matters, step by step, in a diesel engine:

**(a) Viscosity — The Atomization Story**
- A diesel engine relies on **fine atomization** of fuel through the injector nozzle (≈ 200–400 bar injection pressure).
- High viscosity → fuel **does not break into fine droplets** → poor air–fuel mixing → **incomplete combustion** → **smoke, carbon deposits, ring sticking**.
- The high viscosity arises from the **long triglyceride backbone** and **intermolecular van der Waals forces** in the C16–C18 fatty acid chains.

**(b) Cetane Number — The Ignition Delay Story**
- Cetane number measures **auto-ignition quality**. Higher = shorter ignition delay = smoother combustion.
- Vegetable oils: **CN ≈ 32–45**; Petroleum diesel: **CN ≈ 45–55**.
- Lower CN → longer delay → **diesel knock** and higher **NOₓ** (due to high premixed combustion fraction).

**(c) Calorific Value — The Energy Density Story**
- Vegetable oils: **36–40 MJ/kg**; Diesel: **42–46 MJ/kg**.
- Slightly lower because the **oxygen atoms in the ester linkage** "dilute" the combustible carbon–hydrogen content.

**(d) Flash Point & Fire Point — The Safety Story**
- Vegetable oils: **Flash point ≈ 220–300 °C** (very high).
- Diesel: **Flash point ≈ 50–70 °C**.
- **Advantage:** Safer to store and transport (lower fire hazard).
- **Disadvantage:** Difficult cold-starting in low-temperature environments.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for board exam questions on this topic. Every formula below has been used in previous KTU valuation key patterns.

| # | Quantity / Property | Formula | Units | Typical Vegetable Oil Value | Typical Diesel Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Kinematic Viscosity (ν)** | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | mm²/s (cSt) | **30 – 50** | 2 – 4.5 |
| 2 | **Dynamic Viscosity (μ)** | $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$ | mPa·s (cP) | 30 – 50 | 2 – 4.5 |
| 3 | **Density (ρ)** | Direct measurement (Pycnometer) | kg/m³ | **910 – 925** | 820 – 860 |
| 4 | **Specific Gravity (SG)** | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{fuel}}{\rho_{water}}$ | dimensionless | 0.91 – 0.925 | 0.82 – 0.86 |
| 5 | **API Gravity** | $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | °API | 21 – 24 | 33 – 37 |
| 6 | **Calorific Value (CV / LHV)** | Bomb calorimeter | MJ/kg | **36 – 40** | 42 – 46 |
| 7 | **Cetane Number (CN)** | ASTM D613 / IQT | — | 32 – 45 | 45 – 55 |
| 8 | **Flash Point** | Pensky-Martens (closed cup) | °C | 220 – 300 | 50 – 70 |
| 9 | **Fire Point** | Cleveland open cup | °C | 240 – 340 | 60 – 80 |
| 10 | **Pour Point** | ASTM D97 | °C | −10 to +10 | −20 to −35 |
| 11 | **Stoichiometric AFR** | $AFR_{st} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \dfrac{4.76(2C + H/2 - O)}{12C + H + 16O}$ | kg air / kg fuel | **~12.4** | ~14.5 |
| 12 | **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption** | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f}}{BP}$ | kg/kWh | 0.30 – 0.45 | 0.25 – 0.30 |
| 13 | **Brake Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{BTE} = \dfrac{BP \cdot 3600}{\dot{m}_{f} \cdot CV} \times 100$ | % | 25 – 32 | 30 – 38 |
| 14 | **Molecular Weight of TAG** | $M_{TAG} = M_{glycerol} + 3 \cdot M_{FA} - 3 \cdot M_{water}$ | g/mol | 850 – 900 | 170 – 200 |
| 15 | **Carbon Residue (Conradson)** | ASTM D189 | wt% | 0.20 – 0.50 | 0.01 – 0.10 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Board Hint:** When asked *"Why cannot vegetable oil be used directly in a diesel engine for long durations?"* — your answer must contain the keyword chain: **"high viscosity → poor atomization → incomplete combustion → carbon deposits → ring sticking → lubrication oil dilution → engine failure."** This 7-link chain is the gold-standard KTU valuation key.

### 2.4 Detailed Property Comparison: Vegetable Oil vs Diesel vs Biodiesel

| Property | Neat Vegetable Oil | Biodiesel (FAME) | Petroleum Diesel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | 918 | 880 | 840 |
| Viscosity @ 40 °C (mm²/s) | 38 | 4.5 | 3.0 |
| Flash Point (°C) | 250 | 160 | 65 |
| Calorific Value (MJ/kg) | 38.5 | 37.2 | 43.0 |
| Cetane Number | 40 | 52 | 50 |
| Oxygen (wt%) | ~11 | ~11 | 0 |
| Sulfur (ppm) | < 10 | < 10 | 350 – 500 |
| Stoichiometric AFR | 12.4 | 12.5 | 14.6 |

**Observation:** Biodiesel (FAME), produced by **transesterification**, has properties **closest to diesel** — this is why the KTU syllabus treats it as the *practical solution*.

### 2.5 Engine Operational Issues with Direct Use of Vegetable Oil

1. **Cold Start Failure** — high pour point and viscosity.
2. **Injector Coking** — incomplete combustion deposits carbon on injector tips.
3. **Combustion Chamber Deposits** — accumulation on piston crown and valves.
4. **Lubricating Oil Dilution** — unburnt oil seeps past piston rings → oil thinning → bearing failure.
5. **Increased Smoke Opacity** — due to larger droplet size and lower volatility.
6. **Increased NOₓ** — due to higher flame temperature from bound oxygen content.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rural Electrification:** Karanja oil-powered diesel generator sets in villages of Rajasthan and Tamil Nadu.
- **Agricultural Pumpsets:** Jatropha oil-fuelled CI engines in Karnataka's Deccan Plateau.
- **Defense Applications:** The Indian Army has trialed vegetable oil-fueled trucks in high-altitude warfare (Siachen) where fuel logistics are critical.
- **Railways:** Indian Railways' pioneer **"biodiesel train"** experiments (2003 onwards) used Jatropha methyl ester blends.
