## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Molecular Weight of Triolein (a Representative Triglyceride)

**Given:** Triolein is the triglyceride of **oleic acid** (C₁₇H₃₃COOH, C18:1).

**Step 1: Write the molecular formula of oleic acid.**

$$\text{Oleic Acid: } C_{18}H_{34}O_{2} \quad (M_{FA} = 18 \times 12 + 34 \times 1 + 2 \times 16 = 216 + 34 + 32 = 282 \text{ g/mol})$$

**Step 2: Write the molecular formula of glycerol.**

$$\text{Glycerol: } C_{3}H_{8}O_{3} \quad (M_{GLY} = 3 \times 12 + 8 \times 1 + 3 \times 16 = 36 + 8 + 48 = 92 \text{ g/mol})$$

**Step 3: Write the triglyceride formation reaction.**

$$C_3H_8O_3 \;+\; 3\,C_{18}H_{34}O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; C_{57}H_{104}O_6 \;+\; 3\,H_2O$$

**Step 4: Apply mass balance for molecular weight.**

$$M_{TAG} = M_{GLY} + 3 \cdot M_{FA} - 3 \cdot M_{H_2O}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$M_{TAG} = 92 + 3(282) - 3(18)$$

$$M_{TAG} = 92 + 846 - 54$$

$$\boxed{M_{TAG} = 884 \text{ g/mol}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by direct atom counting:**
> $$C: 3 + 3(18) = 57 \quad\checkmark \quad H: 8 + 3(34) - 6 = 104 \quad\checkmark \quad O: 3 + 3(2) - 3 = 6 \quad\checkmark$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) of Triolein

**Given:** Triolein combusts completely in air according to:

$$C_{57}H_{104}O_6 + a\,O_2 + b\,N_2 \longrightarrow 57\,CO_2 + 52\,H_2O + b\,N_2$$

**Step 1: Apply carbon balance.**

$$a \text{ is independent of C balance. The carbon in the fuel goes fully to } CO_2:$$
$$\text{Moles of } CO_2 = 57 \quad\text{(carbon balance satisfied)}$$

**Step 2: Apply hydrogen balance.**

$$104 \text{ H atoms on the left} = 2 \times (\text{moles of } H_2O) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{moles of } H_2O = 52 \quad\checkmark$$

**Step 3: Apply oxygen balance.**

$$\text{O atoms on the left} = 6 + 2a$$
$$\text{O atoms on the right} = 57 \times 2 + 52 \times 1 = 114 + 52 = 166$$

**Step 4: Solve for a.**

$$6 + 2a = 166$$

$$2a = 160$$

$$a = 80 \text{ moles of } O_2$$

**Step 5: Calculate moles of N₂ accompanying O₂ in air (using the standard atmospheric ratio N₂/O₂ = 3.76).**

$$b = 3.76 \times 80 = 300.8 \text{ moles of } N_2$$

**Step 6: Calculate the mass of air required.**

$$m_{O_2} = 80 \times 32 = 2560 \text{ g}$$
$$m_{N_2} = 300.8 \times 28 = 8422.4 \text{ g}$$
$$m_{air} = 2560 + 8422.4 = 10982.4 \text{ g}$$

**Step 7: Calculate the stoichiometric AFR (mass basis).**

$$AFR_{st} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \dfrac{10982.4}{884}$$

$$\boxed{AFR_{st} = 12.42 \text{ kg air / kg triolein}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with Diesel (C₁₂H₂₆, M = 170):**
> $$C_{12}H_{26} + 18.5\,O_2 \rightarrow 12\,CO_2 + 13\,H_2O$$
> $$AFR_{st,diesel} = \dfrac{18.5 \times 32 + 18.5 \times 3.76 \times 28}{170} = \dfrac{592 + 1947.7}{170} \approx 14.93$$
> **Conclusion:** Triolein requires **~17% less air** than diesel per kg of fuel because its bound oxygen participates in combustion.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_BTE) and BSFC Relationship

**Given:** A single-cylinder CI engine runs on Jatropha oil (CV = 38 MJ/kg) at:
- Fuel consumption: $\dot{m}_f = 4.0$ kg/h
- Brake Power: BP = 9.5 kW

**Step 1: Convert fuel flow to kg/s.**

$$\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{4.0}{3600} = 1.111 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the heat input rate (Q̇_in).**

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times CV = 1.111 \times 10^{-3} \times 38{,}000 = 42.22 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Brake Thermal Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{BTE} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{Q}_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{9.5}{42.22} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{BTE} = 22.5\%}$$

**Step 4: Calculate BSFC.**

$$BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} = \dfrac{4.0 \text{ kg/h}}{9.5 \text{ kW}}$$

$$\boxed{BSFC = 0.421 \text{ kg/kWh}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A diesel engine typically achieves η_BTE ≈ 30–35% on petrodiesel. The **drop to 22.5%** on neat vegetable oil arises from **incomplete combustion** and **higher heat losses** (due to deposits and elevated exhaust temperature).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Viscosity Reduction by Blending

**Problem:** A 20% by volume blend of soybean oil (ν₁ = 35 mm²/s) in diesel (ν₂ = 3.5 mm²/s) is to be prepared. Using the **Refutas equation** for kinematic viscosity blending:

$$\nu_{blend} = \exp\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot \ln \nu_i\right)$$

**Step 1: Identify the volume fractions.**

$$x_1 = 0.20 \text{ (soybean oil)}, \quad x_2 = 0.80 \text{ (diesel)}$$

**Step 2: Apply the equation.**

$$\nu_{blend} = \exp\!\left(0.20 \times \ln(35) + 0.80 \times \ln(3.5)\right)$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the logs.**

$$\ln(35) = 3.555, \quad \ln(3.5) = 1.253$$

**Step 4: Compute the weighted sum.**

$$0.20 \times 3.555 + 0.80 \times 1.253 = 0.711 + 1.002 = 1.713$$

**Step 5: Take the exponential.**

$$\nu_{blend} = e^{1.713} = 5.54 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\nu_{B20} \approx 5.5 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with linear (rule-of-mixtures) estimate:** $\nu_{linear} = 0.20(35) + 0.80(3.5) = 9.8$ mm²/s.
> The **logarithmic blending rule** is more accurate and gives a *lower* blend viscosity, which is closer to experimental data.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Engine Performance Comparison Table (Typical 4-Stroke DI Diesel Engine)

| Performance Parameter | Neat Vegetable Oil | B20 (20% Blend) | B100 (Biodiesel) | Petrodiesel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brake Power (kW) | ↓ 8–10% | ↓ 2–3% | ↓ 5–7% | Baseline (100%) |
| BSFC (kg/kWh) | ↑ 12–15% | ↑ 4–6% | ↑ 8–10% | Baseline |
| BTE (%) | ↓ 3–5 pts | ↓ 1 pt | ↓ 2–3 pts | Baseline |
| Smoke Opacity (BSU) | ↑ 25–40% | ↑ 5–10% | ↓ 20–30%* | Baseline |
| NOₓ (ppm) | ↑ 5–10% | ≈ Same | ↑ 10–15% | Baseline |
| CO (%) | ↑ 30% | ↑ 5% | ↓ 10–20% | Baseline |
| HC (ppm) | ↑ 15% | ≈ Same | ↓ 5–10% | Baseline |
| Engine Wear (ppm Fe in lube oil) | ↑ 60–80% | ↑ 10–15% | ↑ 5–10% | Baseline |

*Smoke reduction in B100 is due to **bound oxygen** promoting complete combustion.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** When asked to draw a comparison table, always use the symbols **↑ (increase) and ↓ (decrease)** with **quantitative ranges**. Examiners reward this over qualitative statements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transesterification Reaction (Word Equation):**
> $$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,CH_3OH \xrightarrow{KOH,\,60\,°C} 3\,R\text{-}COOCH_3 + Glycerol$$
> The methanol-to-oil **molar ratio is typically 6:1** (i.e., a 200% stoichiometric excess) to drive the equilibrium toward FAME.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**"List any three disadvantages of using neat vegetable oil as a fuel in a Compression Ignition (CI) engine."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **High Kinematic Viscosity (≈ 30–50 mm²/s at 40 °C):** Causes poor atomization through the injector nozzle, leading to large droplet size and incomplete combustion. *(1 mark)*

2. **Low Volatility and High Flash Point (≈ 250 °C):** Results in cold-start difficulties and the formation of carbon deposits on the injector tip, piston crown, and exhaust valves. *(1 mark)*

3. **Lubricating Oil Dilution and Polymerization:** Unburnt vegetable oil seeps past the piston rings into the crankcase, thinning the lubricating oil. The polyunsaturated fatty acids (linoleic, linolenic) also undergo **thermal polymerization**, forming engine-sludge and varnish. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**"What is meant by the cetane number of a diesel fuel? Why is the cetane number of vegetable oil generally lower than that of biodiesel?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The **cetane number (CN)** is a dimensionless indicator of the **auto-ignition quality** of a diesel fuel, measured by ASTM D613. It compares the ignition delay of the test fuel to a blend of *n*-cetane (CN = 100, short delay) and *α*-methylnaphthalene (CN = 0, long delay). A higher CN indicates a shorter ignition delay and smoother combustion.

- **Lower CN in Neat Vegetable Oil (1.5 marks):** Vegetable oils contain the bulky **triglyceride molecule (M ≈ 884 g/mol)** with three long fatty acid chains. The large molecular size and high viscosity cause **poor atomization and slow vaporization**, delaying the formation of a combustible air–fuel mixture. In contrast, **biodiesel (FAME)** consists of smaller, single-chain **methyl ester molecules (M ≈ 280–300 g/mol)** that vaporize and ignite more readily, giving CN ≈ 50–55.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *Compare the important physico-chemical properties of neat vegetable oil, biodiesel (FAME), and petroleum diesel. Explain how the differences in viscosity and cetane number affect engine performance. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **transesterification process** for converting vegetable oil into biodiesel. Write the balanced chemical reaction and state the role of the catalyst. **(7 marks)***

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) Solution — Property Comparison and Engine Performance Linkage**

**(i) Property Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property | Neat Vegetable Oil | Biodiesel (FAME) | Petroleum Diesel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | 918 | 880 | 840 |
| Viscosity @ 40 °C (mm²/s) | 38 | 4.5 | 3.0 |
| Flash Point (°C) | 250 | 160 | 65 |
| Calorific Value (MJ/kg) | 38.5 | 37.2 | 43.0 |
| Cetane Number | 40 | 52 | 50 |
| Stoichiometric AFR | 12.4 | 12.5 | 14.6 |

**(ii) Effect of Viscosity on Engine Performance (1.5 marks):**

- High viscosity of neat vegetable oil (≈ 38 mm²/s) → fuel **does not atomize** into fine droplets → **larger Sauter mean diameter (SMD)** → reduced air–fuel mixing rate → **incomplete combustion**.
- Results: ↑ BSFC, ↓ BTE, ↑ smoke, carbon deposits on injectors and piston rings.
- Biodiesel viscosity (≈ 4.5 mm²/s) is close to diesel (≈ 3 mm²/s), so atomization is comparable.

**(iii) Effect of Cetane Number on Engine Performance (1.5 marks):**

- Neat vegetable oil has CN ≈ 40, which gives a **longer ignition delay** in the CI engine.
- Longer delay → more fuel accumulates in the combustion chamber → **rapid premixed combustion** → **higher peak pressure and temperature** → **higher NOₓ** and **diesel knock**.
- Biodiesel (CN ≈ 52) has a shorter delay → combustion is more **diffusion-controlled and gradual** → smoother engine operation.

---

**Part (b) Solution — Transesterification Process**

**(i) Chemical Reaction (3 marks):**

$$C_3H_5(OCOR)_3 + 3\,CH_3OH \;\xrightleftharpoons[\text{KOH}]{60\,°C}\; 3\,R\,COOCH_3 + C_3H_5(OH)_3$$

- **Triglyceride** + **Methanol** → **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME / Biodiesel)** + **Glycerol**
- Molar ratio of methanol to oil = **6:1** (i.e., 200% excess) to drive equilibrium forward.

**(ii) Role of the Catalyst (1.5 marks):**

- **KOH (or NaOH)** acts as a **homogeneous base catalyst** (≈ 1% w/w of oil).
- It deprotonates methanol to form **methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻)**, which is the **active nucleophile** that attacks the carbonyl carbon of the triglyceride.

**(iii) Block Diagram Description (2.5 marks):**

1. **Feed Preparation:** Vegetable oil is filtered and heated to 50 °C; methanol is mixed with KOH in a separate tank.
2. **Reactor:** Both streams are fed into a **stirred batch reactor** maintained at **60 °C** for **1 hour** with continuous agitation (≈ 600 rpm).
3. **Decanter / Settling Tank:** The reacted mixture is allowed to stand; **crude glycerol (heavier, ~10 wt%) settles at the bottom**.
4. **Water Wash:** Biodiesel layer is washed with warm water to remove residual catalyst and soap.
5. **Drying:** Biodiesel is dried under **vacuum at 110 °C** to remove moisture.
6. **Product:** Pure biodiesel (FAME) with **> 96.5% ester content** (as per EN 14214 standard).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** *Derive the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio for the complete combustion of triolein (C₅₇H₁₀₄O₆). Compare your result with that of petroleum diesel (C₁₂H₂₆) and comment on the implications for engine design. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *Discuss the **problems associated with long-term use of neat vegetable oil** in a diesel engine. Explain how **blending with diesel** and **preheating** can mitigate these problems. **(7 marks)***

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) Solution — Stoichiometric AFR Derivation**

**(i) Triolein Combustion (4 marks):**

$$C_{57}H_{104}O_6 + 80\,O_2 + 300.8\,N_2 \rightarrow 57\,CO_2 + 52\,H_2O + 300.8\,N_2$$

- Carbon balance: 57 C → 57 CO₂ ✓
- Hydrogen balance: 104 H → 52 H₂O ✓
- Oxygen balance: 6 + 2(80) = 166; RHS: 57(2) + 52(1) = 166 ✓
- $M_{triolein} = 884$ g/mol
- $m_{air} = 80(32) + 300.8(28) = 2560 + 8422.4 = 10982.4$ g
- $AFR_{st} = 10982.4 / 884 = 12.42$

**(ii) Diesel Combustion (2 marks):**

$$C_{12}H_{26} + 18.5\,O_2 \rightarrow 12\,CO_2 + 13\,H_2O$$
- $M_{diesel} = 170$ g/mol
- $m_{air} = 18.5(32) + 18.5(3.76)(28) = 592 + 1947.7 = 2539.7$ g
- $AFR_{st,diesel} = 2539.7 / 170 = 14.94$

**(iii) Engine Design Implications (1 mark):**

- Triolein requires **17% less air** per unit mass than diesel, owing to its **bound oxygen** in the ester linkage.
- This alters the **volumetric air demand**, the **air filter sizing**, and the **EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) calibration** when an engine is re-tuned for vegetable oil fuels.

---

**Part (b) Solution — Long-Term Problems and Mitigation**

**(i) Problems of Long-Term Neat Vegetable Oil Use (4 marks):**

1. **Carbon Deposits:** Incomplete combustion forms a hard, varnish-like carbon layer on the piston crown, exhaust valves, and injector tips (a 200-hour endurance test on Jatropha oil typically shows 0.4–0.6 g carbon deposit per cylinder, compared to 0.05 g on diesel).
2. **Injector Coking:** The high viscosity leads to poor atomization; fuel thermally decomposes on the hot injector nozzle, forming coke that distorts the spray pattern.
3. **Lubricating Oil Dilution:** Unburnt oil seeps past the piston rings (especially at low loads), thinning the lube oil from SAE 30 to SAE 10 within ≈ 100 hours.
4. **Piston Ring Sticking:** Carbon deposits accumulate in the ring grooves, freezing the rings and causing **blow-by** (leakage of combustion gases into the crankcase).
5. **Polymerization:** Polyunsaturated fatty acids (C18:2, C18:3) undergo **thermal-oxidative polymerization** at 250–300 °C, forming gum and lacquer.

**(ii) Mitigation by Blending (1.5 marks):**

- Blending vegetable oil with diesel (typically **B20 = 20% oil + 80% diesel**) reduces the blend viscosity to ≈ 5–6 mm²/s — close to diesel.
- The Refutas equation gives $\nu_{B20} \approx 5.5$ mm²/s, allowing acceptable atomization in standard injectors.

**(iii) Mitigation by Preheating (1.5 marks):**

- Preheating the vegetable oil to **60–80 °C** before injection reduces its viscosity by **60–70%** (viscosity–temperature follows the **Andrade equation**: $\ln \mu = A + B/T$).
- A simple **heat exchanger** using engine coolant or exhaust gas is used; the preheated oil enters the injector at a viscosity close to diesel.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently make the following mistakes in transesterification questions and **lose 2–3 marks**:
> 1. **Forgetting to write the catalyst** (KOH / NaOH / H₂SO₄ for acid esterification) — the catalyst is *mandatory* in the reaction arrow.
> 2. **Omitting the temperature** (60 °C) and **time** (1 hour) in the reactor description.
> 3. **Writing the wrong molar ratio** — it is **6:1** (methanol to oil), not 3:1, because a stoichiometric excess is needed to drive the reversible reaction forward.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention glycerol as a by-product** — this is a 1-mark loss in almost every KTU paper.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2: Vegetable Oil as Diesel Fuel**

- ☐ **Vegetable oil = triglyceride** of glycerol + 3 long-chain fatty acids (C12–C22).
- ☐ **Key disadvantage: high viscosity** (30–50 mm²/s vs diesel's 2–4.5 mm²/s) → poor atomization.
- ☐ **Key disadvantage: low CN** (40 vs diesel 50) → long ignition delay.
- ☐ **Calorific value:** Vegetable oil ≈ 38 MJ/kg < Diesel ≈ 43 MJ/kg.
- ☐ **Flash point:** Vegetable oil ≈ 250 °C (much higher than diesel's 65 °C) → safer but harder cold start.
- ☐ **Bound oxygen content (~11%)** → lower AFR (~12.4 vs diesel's 14.9) → alters engine calibration.
- ☐ **Four utilization routes:** Direct Use / Blending / Microemulsion / Pyrolysis / Transesterification.
- ☐ **Transesterification reaction:** TAG + 3 CH₃OH → 3 FAME + Glycerol (6:1 molar ratio, KOH catalyst, 60 °C, 1 hr).
- ☐ **Molecular weight of triolein = 884 g/mol** (a favourite numerical in KTU papers).
- ☐ **B20 blend** is the most common field-deployment strategy (avoids engine modification).
- ☐ **Preheating to 60–80 °C** reduces viscosity by 60–70% via the Andrade equation.
- ☐ **Jatropha, Karanja, Neem** are the non-edible second-generation oils preferred in India.
- ☐ **Long-term problems:** Carbon deposits, ring sticking, lube oil dilution, polymerization, injector coking.
- ☐ **Renewable carbon cycle** — CO₂ released is recaptured by the next crop, so it is **carbon-neutral in principle**.
- ☐ **ASTM D6751 (USA) and EN 14214 (Europe)** are the two key biodiesel quality standards.
- ☐ **Biodiesel (FAME) viscosity ≈ 4.5 mm²/s** — the closest match to diesel among all routes.
- ☐ **Smoke reduces** with biodiesel (B100) due to bound oxygen; **NOₓ slightly increases** due to higher flame temperature.
- ☐ **Lubricity advantage** — FAME has an HFRR wear scar of ~200 μm vs diesel's ~400–500 μm, extending engine life.

> **End of KTU 2024 Module 2 Notes — Use of Vegetable Oil as an Alternative Diesel Fuel**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Growth
topics.content:
# GROWTH — Foundational Concept in Bioenergy & Bioprocess Engineering ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > **Growth** in the context of bioenergy and biofuels refers to the **orderly increase in cellular constituents** (biomass) of a microbial population, leading to a measurable rise in cell number, cell mass, or cell protein over time. It is quantified mathematically through **specific growth rate (μ)**, **doubling/generation time (t_d)**, **growth yield (Y)**, and **substrate utilization kinetics**. Mathematically, growth is expressed as the rate of biomass formation: $$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu X$$ where *X* is the biomass concentration (g/L) and *μ* is the **specific growth rate (h⁻¹)** — a kinetic parameter central to all bioprocess design. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The concept of microbial growth is the *cornerstone* of fermenter design for bioethanol, biogas, biodiesel (via oleaginous microbes), and biohydrogen production. Every bioreactor volume, feedstock loading rate, and productivity calculation traces back to growth kinetics. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of microbial growth like **a city population increasing over time**: - **Lag phase** = New settlers arriving in town; nobody is reproducing yet because they are unpacking, building infrastructure, and adapting to local conditions. - **Exponential phase** = Boom years! Every family has many children, and the population doubles rapidly — this is the *most productive* phase for industrial fermentations. - **Stationary phase** = The city is full. Space, food, and jobs become limited, so birth rate equals death rate — population stays constant. - **Death phase** = Resources run out, pollution builds up; population declines. Just like a city planner needs to know **how fast the population grows** to design roads, water supply, and housing, a bioprocess engineer needs growth kinetics to size bioreactors, plan feeding strategies, and estimate **biofuel yield**. > [!NOTE] > **Practical Biofuel Connection:** In *bioethanol fermentation*, yeast cells go through these exact phases. The fermenter is usually harvested in the **late exponential phase** to maximize both cell activity and ethanol productivity. Misjudging this phase can cost **millions of rupees** in industrial-scale biofuel plants. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following metrics are **must-know** for KTU examinations: | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value/Range | Significance | |-----------|--------|---------------------|--------------| | Specific growth rate | μ | 0.1 – 1.0 h⁻¹ | Speed of biomass production | | Doubling time | t_d | 0.5 – 5 h | Time for population to double | | Maximum specific growth rate | μ_max | 0.2 – 2.0 h⁻¹ | Genetic ceiling of organism | | Half-saturation constant | K_s | 1 – 100 mg/L | Substrate affinity indicator | | Biomass yield coefficient | Y_X/S | 0.3 – 0.6 g/g | Conversion efficiency | | Maintenance coefficient | m_s | 0.01 – 0.5 g/g·h | Cell upkeep energy cost | --- # DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Four Phases of Microbial Growth (Unbalanced/Closed System) When microbes are inoculated into a **batch culture** (a closed vessel with finite nutrients), the population follows a characteristic growth curve: ### Phase 1 — Lag Phase - **Duration:** Variable (minutes to hours). - **Physiology:** Cells synthesize enzymes, ribosomes, and metabolic machinery to utilize the new substrate. - **Mathematical implication:** dX/dt ≈ 0; μ ≈ 0. - **Why it matters for biofuels:** Longer lag = lower plant productivity. Engineers select robust strains or pre-adapt (acclimatize) inocula to minimize this phase. ### Phase 2 — Exponential (Log) Phase - **Duration:** Shortest but most metabolically active. - **Cellular behavior:** Cells divide at a constant rate through binary fission; **nutrients are in excess**, no toxic accumulation yet. - **Mathematical implication:** Growth is **first-order** with respect to biomass — dX/dt = μX. - **Why it matters for biofuels:** Ethanol, biogas, and biohydrogen are produced at **maximum volumetric rates** during this phase. ### Phase 3 — Stationary Phase - **Trigger:** Depletion of an essential nutrient OR accumulation of inhibitory product (e.g., ethanol toxicity at >12% v/v). - **Mathematical implication:** dX/dt = 0; growth rate = death rate. - **Why it matters for biofuels:** Determines the **maximum achievable biomass and product concentration** before nutrient depletion or product inhibition halts the process. ### Phase 4 — Death (Decline) Phase - **Mechanism:** Cells lose viability due to starvation, pH shifts, or toxic metabolite accumulation. - **Mathematical implication:** Exponential decline of viable cells. > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always draw the *standard growth curve* (4 phases on X-axis = time, Y-axis = log of cell number or biomass) — it earns you **easy marks** in any growth kinetics question. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **exam-essential** and have appeared in multiple KTU university exam papers. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | dX/dt = μX | Differential growth equation (exponential phase) | g/L·h | | 2 | μ = (1/X)(dX/dt) | Definition of specific growth rate | h⁻¹ | | 3 | t_d = ln(2) / μ | Doubling (generation) time | h | | 4 | X = X_0 · e^(μt) | Integrated exponential growth law | g/L | | 5 | μ = μ_max · S / (K_s + S) | **Monod Equation** (substrate-limited growth) | h⁻¹ | | 6 | K_s = substrate conc. at μ = μ_max/2 | Definition of half-saturation constant | g/L | | 7 | Y_X/S = -ΔX / ΔS | Biomass yield on substrate | g biomass/g substrate | | 8 | dS/dt = -(1/Y_X/S)(dX/dt) | Substrate consumption rate | g/L·h | | 9 | q_s = μ / Y_X/S | Specific substrate uptake rate | g/g·h | | 10 | μ_obs = μ_max - k_d | Net specific growth rate (with endogenous decay) | h⁻¹ | | 11 | t_d = 0.693 / μ | Doubling time (since ln2 ≈ 0.693) | h | | 12 | D = μ (at steady state, chemostat) | Dilution rate = specific growth rate | h⁻¹ | | 13 | D_crit = μ_max (1 - √K_s / (K_s + S_0)) | Critical dilution rate (washout threshold) | h⁻¹ | | 14 | Productivity = D · X | Volumetric productivity (chemostat) | g/L·h | --- ## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Core Concept **Why does exponential growth happen?** Because during log phase, every cell has access to abundant substrate and divides via binary fission at a fixed interval. After *n* generations starting from one cell, total cells = 2^n. **How is μ measured experimentally?** Plot ln(X) vs. t during log phase; the slope of the straight line = μ. **Why does growth eventually stop?** Because of **three simultaneous constraints**: 1. Substrate limitation (Monod effect) 2. Product inhibition (e.g., ethanol inhibits yeast at high concentrations) 3. Accumulation of toxic byproducts (pH drop, ammonia, etc.) **How do we keep cells growing continuously?** By using a **chemostat** — a continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) where fresh medium flows in at rate *F* and spent medium (with cells) flows out at the same rate, maintaining **steady-state** biomass. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Biofuel Application | Relevant Growth Concept Used | |---------------------|------------------------------| | **Bioethanol from sugarcane molasses** | Yeast growth kinetics + ethanol inhibition model (Aiba-Edwards) | | **Biogas (anaerobic digestion)** | Mixed-culture growth; methanogen kinetics; HRT = 1/D relationship | | **Biodiesel from oleaginous microbes** | Lipid accumulation triggered by **N-limitation in stationary phase** | | **Biohydrogen (dark fermentation)** | Clostridium growth under N₂ atmosphere; modified Monod | | **Microalgal biodiesel** | Photo-bioreactor design using light-limited growth (Steele model) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Specific:** The *Monod equation* is **THE most-tested formula** in this module. Memorize it, its derivation, and its limitations (it fails at very low S, doesn't account for multiple substrates or inhibition). --- # STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Exponential Growth Law (Batch Culture, Log Phase) ### Statement During exponential phase, the rate of biomass increase is proportional to the biomass concentration: $$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu X$$ **Goal:** Derive the explicit solution: X(t) = X_0 · e^(μt). ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Separation of variables** Since *μ* is approximately constant during log phase (constant substrate, no inhibition), we treat it as a constant. $$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu X$$ Rearrange by dividing both sides by *X*: $$\frac{dX}{X} = \mu \, dt$$ **Step 2: Integration from initial to final state** Integrate the left side from initial biomass X_0 (at t = 0) to biomass X (at time t), and the right side from 0 to t: $$\int_{X_0}^{X} \frac{dX}{X} = \mu \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ **Step 3: Evaluate both integrals** Left side: ∫dX/X = ln(X); Right side: μt. $$\ln(X) - \ln(X_0) = \mu t$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{X}{X_0}\right) = \mu t$$ **Step 4: Exponentiate both sides** $$\frac{X}{X_0} = e^{\mu t}$$ $$\boxed{X(t) = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu t}}$$ This is the **fundamental exponential growth law** in bioengineering. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Doubling Time Formula ### Statement The time t_d required for biomass to double (i.e., X = 2X_0) is related to μ. ### Derivation Starting from: $$X = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu t}$$ At t = t_d, X = 2X_0: $$2X_0 = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu t_d}$$ Divide both sides by X_0: $$2 = e^{\mu t_d}$$ Take natural logarithm of both sides: $$\ln(2) = \mu t_d$$ Since ln(2) ≈ 0.693: $$\boxed{t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A *E. coli* with μ = 1.0 h⁻¹ has t_d = 0.693 h ≈ **42 minutes**. This is why bacterial fermentations dominate industrial-scale biofuels — they grow extremely fast. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Monod Equation (Substrate-Limited Growth) ### Statement When substrate concentration *S* becomes limiting, μ is no longer constant but a function of S: $$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}$$ ### Derivation by Analogy (Michaelis-Menten Enzyme Kinetics) **Step 1: Define the growth-substrate relationship empirically** Jacques Monod (1942) observed that microbial growth resembles an *enzyme-substrate saturation* curve. As S → ∞, μ → μ_max (saturation); as S → 0, μ → 0 (first-order). **Step 2: Propose the hyperbolic form** $$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}$$ where K_s is the substrate concentration at which μ = μ_max / 2. **Step 3: Verify limiting cases** - When S ≫ K_s: μ ≈ μ_max · S/S = μ_max ✓ - When S ≪ K_s: μ ≈ μ_max · S/K_s ∝ S (first-order) ✓ **Step 4: Linearization for K_s determination (Lineweaver-Burk plot)** Invert the equation: $$\frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{\mu_{max}} \cdot \frac{K_s + S}{S} = \frac{1}{\mu_{max}} + \frac{K_s}{\mu_{max}} \cdot \frac{1}{S}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{K_s}{\mu_{max}} \cdot \frac{1}{S} + \frac{1}{\mu_{max}}}$$ A plot of 1/μ vs. 1/S gives intercept = 1/μ_max and slope = K_s/μ_max. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Biomass Yield Coefficient and Substrate Balance ### Statement Define Y_X/S and derive the substrate consumption rate. ### Derivation **Step 1: Definition of growth yield** Y_X/S is the ratio of biomass produced to substrate consumed: $$Y_{X/S} = \frac{-\Delta X}{\Delta S} = \frac{X - X_0}{S_0 - S}$$ The negative sign accounts for substrate *depletion* (ΔS < 0). **Step 2: Differential form** As dt → 0: $$Y_{X/S} = -\frac{dX/dt}{dS/dt}$$ **Step 3: Substitute dX/dt = μX** $$Y_{X/S} = -\frac{\mu X}{dS/dt}$$ **Step 4: Solve for substrate consumption rate** $$\boxed{\frac{dS}{dt} = -\frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}} = -\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt}}$$ This says: **the faster cells grow, the faster they consume substrate**, in direct proportion. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Doubling Time Calculation (Worked Numerical Example) **Problem:** A yeast culture in a bioethanol fermenter has X_0 = 0.5 g/L at t = 0. After 4 hours, X = 8.0 g/L. Calculate μ and t_d. ### Solution **Step 1: Use integrated exponential growth law** $$X = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu t}$$ $$8.0 = 0.5 \cdot e^{\mu \cdot 4}$$ **Step 2: Divide both sides by 0.5** $$\frac{8.0}{0.5} = e^{4\mu}$$ $$16 = e^{4\mu}$$ **Step 3: Take natural log of both sides** $$\ln(16) = 4\mu$$ $$\mu = \frac{\ln(16)}{4} = \frac{2.7726}{4} = 0.693 \text{ h}^{-1}$$ **Step 4: Calculate doubling time** $$t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{0.693} = 1.0 \text{ hour}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** This yeast doubles every hour — very fast. For industrial bioethanol production, this is desirable as it means rapid sugar-to-ethanol conversion. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Growth Phases (Mathematical Summary) | Phase | Governing Equation | μ value | t_d | Limiting Factor | |-------|--------------------|---------|-----|-----------------| | Lag | dX/dt ≈ 0 | ≈ 0 | ∞ | Enzyme induction time | | Exponential | dX/dt = μX | μ = μ_max (constant) | ln2/μ | None (substrate in excess) | | Stationary | dX/dt = 0 | μ_observed = 0 | ∞ | Nutrient depletion OR product inhibition | | Death | dX/dt = -k_d·X | Negative effective | — | Toxicity, starvation | --- # STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Microbial Growth Curve — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[TIME = 0
Inoculation] --> B[PHASE 1 LAG
Cell adapts
Enzyme synthesis
No division] B --> C[PHASE 2 EXPONENTIAL
Substrate in excess
Maximum growth rate
Biomass doubles regularly] C --> D[PHASE 3 STATIONARY
Nutrient depletes
Product accumulates
Growth equals death] D --> E[PHASE 4 DEATH
Toxic metabolites
Cell lysis
Biomass declines] E --> F[Fermenter harvested
or restarted] D --> G[Biomass harvested here
for biofuel extraction] C --> G ``` --- ## 4.2 Monod Kinetics — Substrate-to-Growth Relationship ```mermaid flowchart LR S[Substrate S
g per L] --> K[Monod Kinetics
mu equals mu max times S over Ks plus S] K --> M[Specific Growth Rate mu
h to power minus 1] M --> G[Biomass Increase dX by dt equals mu times X
g per L per h] G --> P[Biofuel Product
Ethanol OR Biogas OR Lipid] P -.feedback inhibition.-> K S -.limiting nutrient.-> K ``` --- ## 4.3 Continuous Culture (Chemostat) Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fresh Medium Reservoir
Contains substrate S0] --> B[Peristaltic Pump
Flow rate F] B --> C[Chemostat Bioreactor
Volume V working
Stirred aerobic OR anaerobic] C --> D[Biomass X
Substrate S
Product P
At steady state] C --> E[Outflow
Same rate F
D equals F by V] E --> F[Product Recovery
Centrifuge OR Distillation] F --> G[Biofuel Storage Tank] D -.feedback control of D.-> B ``` --- ## 4.4 Decision Flow — Batch vs Continuous vs Fed-Batch for Biofuels ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Choose Bioreactor Mode] --> B{High product stability required?} B -- YES --> C[Batch Culture
Simple, low contamination risk
Used for bioethanol] B -- NO --> D{Continuous long-term operation?} D -- YES --> E[Chemostat Continuous
High productivity
Used for biogas, microalgae] D -- NO --> F{High cell density needed?} F -- YES --> G[Fed-Batch Culture
Substrate fed gradually
Used for biodiesel via oleaginous yeast] F -- NO --> C ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Note:** These diagrams frequently appear as **"draw and explain"** questions worth **7–14 marks**. Always include **labeled axes, governing equations, and a real-world biofuel example**. --- # KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU2024-BBF-M2]** Define the following terms with respect to microbial growth kinetics: (a) Specific growth rate (μ) (b) Doubling time (t_d) (c) Half-saturation constant (K_s) ### Model Answer (3 marks) (a) **Specific growth rate (μ):** It is defined as the rate of increase of biomass per unit biomass concentration, expressed as μ = (1/X)(dX/dt). Its unit is **h⁻¹**. **[1 mark]** (b) **Doubling time (t_d):** The time required for the biomass concentration to double during the exponential growth phase. Mathematically, t_d = ln(2)/μ = 0.693/μ. Unit: hours. **[1 mark]** (c) **Half-saturation constant (K_s):** It is the substrate concentration at which the specific growth rate μ equals μ_max/2. It reflects the affinity of the microorganism for the substrate. Unit: g/L or mg/L. **[1 mark]** --- ### Question 2 (3 marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** Distinguish between **batch culture** and **continuous culture (chemostat)** in terms of growth kinetics. ### Model Answer (3 marks) | Feature | Batch Culture | Continuous Culture (Chemostat) | |---------|---------------|-------------------------------| | Nutrient supply | Finite, added once | Continuous inflow of fresh medium | | Growth phase observed | All 4 phases (lag, log, stationary, death) | Steady-state log phase only | | Operating equation | dX/dt = μX | At steady state: μ = D (dilution rate) | | Application | Bioethanol fermentation | Biogas, microalgal biodiesel | **[1 mark for batch definition, 1 mark for continuous definition, 1 mark for distinguishing equation/application]** --- ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question Choice A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question Paper, Module 2]** (a) Derive the **Monod equation** for substrate-limited microbial growth. Explain the physical significance of μ_max and K_s. **[7 marks]** (b) In a bioethanol fermenter, a yeast culture has μ_max = 0.45 h⁻¹ and K_s = 0.2 g/L. The fermenter operates with glucose concentration maintained at 1.5 g/L. Calculate: (i) The actual specific growth rate μ. (ii) The doubling time t_d. (iii) The biomass concentration after 6 hours, if X_0 = 0.1 g/L. **[7 marks]** ### Model Answer for Question Choice A #### Part (a) — Monod Equation Derivation [7 marks] Monod observed that microbial growth is analogous to enzyme-substrate kinetics. He proposed an empirical hyperbolic relationship: $$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S} \quad \text{...(1)}$$ **Step 1: Limiting case analysis** [1 mark] - When S ≫ K_s, μ ≈ μ_max · S/S = μ_max (zero-order in S; saturation). - When S ≪ K_s, μ ≈ μ_max · S/K_s ∝ S (first-order in S). **Step 2: Physical significance of μ_max** [2 marks] μ_max is the **maximum specific growth rate** achievable by the organism when substrate is in excess. It is determined by the **maximum rate of cellular metabolism** (protein synthesis, ribosome activity). It is a **genetic/intrinsic property** of the strain. In biofuel processes, a higher μ_max means faster fermentation cycles and higher plant productivity. **Step 3: Physical significance of K_s** [2 marks] K_s is the **half-saturation constant**, numerically equal to the substrate concentration at which μ = μ_max/2. It is an **inverse measure of substrate affinity**: - **Low K_s** → high substrate affinity → efficient scavenger → preferred for low-substrate feedstocks. - **High K_s** → low affinity → needs high substrate concentration to grow. In a chemostat, K_s determines the **washout threshold** (D_crit) and the residual substrate concentration. **Step 4: Plot and graphical interpretation** [2 marks] A plot of μ vs. S is a rectangular hyperbola passing through the origin, asymptotic to μ_max. The point (K_s, μ_max/2) is the inflection point. This curve forms the basis of all chemostat design. #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks] **Given:** - μ_max = 0.45 h⁻¹ - K_s = 0.2 g/L - S = 1.5 g/L (constant — maintained by fed-batch operation) - X_0 = 0.1 g/L, t = 6 h **(i) Calculate actual μ using Monod equation:** $$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}$$ $$\mu = 0.45 \cdot \frac{1.5}{0.2 + 1.5} = 0.45 \cdot \frac{1.5}{1.7}$$ $$\mu = 0.45 \cdot 0.8824 = 0.397 \text{ h}^{-1}$$ **[2 marks]** **(ii) Doubling time:** $$t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{0.397} = 1.745 \text{ h}$$ **[2 marks]** **(iii) Biomass at t = 6 h:** $$X = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu t} = 0.1 \cdot e^{0.397 \times 6}$$ $$X = 0.1 \cdot e^{2.382}$$ $$X = 0.1 \cdot 10.83 = 1.083 \text{ g/L}$$ **[3 marks]** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to use the *actual* μ (from Monod), not μ_max, in the doubling time formula. Since S > K_s, μ < μ_max, so t_d > ln(2)/μ_max. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing natural logarithm (ln) with log base 10 in t_d = 0.693/μ. The correct constant is ln(2) ≈ 0.693. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Using X_0 = 0 in exponential growth equation — division by zero error. Always verify X_0 > 0. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Reporting units wrongly — μ is in h⁻¹, t_d in h, X in g/L. --- ### Question Choice B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Alternate]** (a) Explain the **four phases of microbial growth** in a batch culture with neat sketches of the growth curve. Discuss the kinetic equations governing each phase. **[7 marks]** (b) A chemostat is operated at dilution rate D = 0.3 h⁻¹ with feed substrate concentration S_0 = 10 g/L. The organism has μ_max = 0.5 h⁻¹, K_s = 0.5 g/L, and Y_X/S = 0.4 g/g. Determine: (i) The steady-state substrate concentration S. (ii) The steady-state biomass concentration X. (iii) The volumetric productivity P = D · X. **[7 marks]** ### Model Answer for Question Choice B #### Part (a) — Four Phases of Microbial Growth [7 marks] Refer to the standard growth curve (semi-log plot of biomass vs. time): **Phase 1 — Lag Phase [1.5 marks]** - Cells do not divide but synthesize enzymes, ribosomes, and ATP. - dX/dt ≈ 0; μ_effective ≈ 0. - Equation: X ≈ X_0 (constant). - Industrial relevance: Strain pre-adaptation minimizes lag. **Phase 2 — Exponential (Log) Phase [2 marks]** - Cells divide at constant rate; nutrients in excess; no inhibition. - Equation: dX/dt = μ_max · X, integrated form X = X_0 · e^(μt). - Industrial relevance: **Maximum productivity zone** for bioethanol/hydrogen. **Phase 3 — Stationary Phase [1.5 marks]** - Growth rate = death rate due to substrate depletion OR product accumulation. - Equation: dX/dt = μX - k_d X = 0, so μ = k_d. - Industrial relevance: **Lipid accumulation in oleaginous yeasts** occurs here due to N-limitation → biodiesel production. **Phase 4 — Death Phase [1 mark]** - Viable cell count declines exponentially. - Equation: dX/dt = -k_d X. - Industrial relevance: Fermenter shut-down, sterilization before re-inoculation. **Sketch requirement [1 mark]:** Plot log(X) vs. t showing the four labeled phases clearly. #### Part (b) — Chemostat Steady-State Calculation [7 marks] **Given:** - D = 0.3 h⁻¹ - S_0 = 10 g/L - μ_max = 0.5 h⁻¹, K_s = 0.5 g/L - Y_X/S = 0.4 g/g **(i) Steady-state substrate concentration S:** At steady state in a chemostat, D = μ. Therefore: $$D = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}$$ $$0.3 = 0.5 \cdot \frac{S}{0.5 + S}$$ $$\frac{0.3}{0.5} = \frac{S}{0.5 + S}$$ $$0.6 \cdot (0.5 + S) = S$$ $$0.3 + 0.6 S = S$$ $$0.3 = S - 0.6 S = 0.4 S$$ $$\boxed{S = \frac{0.3}{0.4} = 0.75 \text{ g/L}}$$ **[3 marks]** **(ii) Steady-state biomass X:** Using the biomass balance at steady state, X = Y_X/S · (S_0 - S): $$X = Y_{X/S} \cdot (S_0 - S) = 0.4 \cdot (10 - 0.75)$$ $$X = 0.4 \cdot 9.25 = 3.7 \text{ g/L}$$ **[2 marks]** **(iii) Volumetric productivity P:** $$P = D \cdot X = 0.3 \cdot 3.7 = 1.11 \text{ g/L·h}$$ **[2 marks]** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Using μ = μ_max in a chemostat problem. Remember: at steady state, **μ = D ≠ μ_max** unless substrate is in huge excess. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing D (dilution rate, h⁻¹) with F (volumetric flow rate, L/h). D = F/V. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Using S_0 (inlet) instead of S (residual) in the Monod equation. The Monod equation uses the substrate concentration *in the reactor*, which is the *outlet* concentration at steady state. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting units in productivity (g/L·h, not g/h). --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Growth = orderly increase in cellular biomass**, quantified by dX/dt = μX. - [x] **Exponential phase** is mathematically described by **X = X_0 · e^(μt)** — the foundational growth law. - [x] **Doubling time:** t_d = ln(2)/μ = 0.693/μ — NEVER use log_10 here. - [x] **Monod equation:** μ = μ_max · S/(K_s + S) — the most tested formula in Module 2. - [x] **K_s** = substrate concentration at μ = μ_max/2; **low K_s = high affinity**. - [x] **Biomass yield Y_X/S** = (-ΔX)/(ΔS) → tells substrate-to-cell conversion efficiency. - [x] **Substrate consumption:** dS/dt = -(1/Y_X/S)(dX/dt) — coupled to growth rate. - [x] **Four phases:** Lag → Exponential → Stationary → Death (draw this curve whenever asked). - [x] **Chemostat steady state:** μ = D (specific growth rate = dilution rate). - [x] **Critical dilution rate D_crit** at washout = μ_max · (1 - √(K_s/(K_s+S_0))). - [x] **Productivity in chemostat** = D · X (g/L·h). - [x] **Biofuel link:** Ethanol productivity ∝ μ during log phase; oleaginous lipid accumulation occurs in **stationary phase** under N-limitation. - [x] **Limitation of Monod:** Fails at very low S, ignores multiple substrates, no product inhibition term — extended models exist (Andrews, Aiba-Edwards, Tessier). - [x] **Examination tip:** Always draw a **labeled growth curve** with axes and equations — earns free marks. --- > **END OF NOTES — MODULE 2, TOPIC: GROWTH** > **Course:** PEBBT413 — Bioenergy and Biofuels > **Scheme:** APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University, Kerala — 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned)

topics.title: Screening and harvesting of microalgae
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Screening and harvesting of microalgae

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Screening of Microalgae** refers to the systematic, multi-tiered process of **isolation, purification, identification, and selection** of microalgal strains from environmental samples (soil, water, air) or established culture collections, based on predefined morphological, physiological, biochemical, and molecular criteria that are relevant to the targeted end-application (e.g., biodiesel, biogas, bioethanol, or biohydrogen production).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Harvesting of Microalgae** is defined as the **downstream solid–liquid separation process** by which dilute microalgal biomass (typically suspended at concentrations of **0.1–1.0 g/L** in the culture broth) is concentrated into a dense slurry or paste (typically **50–250 g/L**), thereby removing the bulk of the aqueous phase to enable subsequent processing such as dewatering, drying, lipid extraction, or thermochemical conversion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Coffee Preparation):**
> Imagine you want to make a perfect cup of coffee. **Screening** is like hand-picking the highest quality coffee beans from a sack — you discard mouldy, broken, or sub-standard beans and keep only those with the right aroma, oil content, and size. **Harvesting** is the next step: once you have brewed the coffee (your microalgae culture), you need to separate the liquid (water) from the valuable solid (the extracted solids) using a filter, sieve, or press. The culture medium is the "water" and the microalgal cells are the "precious solid" — except in microalgae harvesting, the cells are microscopically small (3–30 µm), making the separation far more challenging than filtering coffee grounds.

### 1.3 Why These Two Steps are the *Bottleneck* of Microalgal Biorefining

> [!WARNING]
> Industry analyses consistently report that **harvesting alone accounts for 20–30% of the total production cost** of microalgal biofuels, while screening underpins the entire economics by ensuring the strain chosen is genuinely oleaginous, fast-growing, and robust. A poor screening decision leads to low lipid yields; a poor harvesting method makes the entire process economically non-viable.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Microalgal cell size:** typically **3–30 µm** (unicellular) to **100+ µm** (filamentous)
- **Culture biomass concentration (before harvest):** **0.1–1.0 g/L**
- **Target paste concentration (after harvest):** **50–250 g/L**
- **Concentration factor required:** **100× to 1000×**
- **Typical lipid content of screened oleaginous strains:** **20–70% of dry cell weight**
- **Gravitational acceleration (standard):** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Density of water at 25 °C:** **ρ_w = 997 kg/m³**

### 1.5 The Two-Stage Logic at a Glance

| Stage | Purpose | End Product | Key Challenge |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Screening** | Find the *right strain* | Pure, characterized isolate | Biodiversity, axenic culture |
| **Harvesting** | Recover *all the biomass* | Concentrated slurry | Small cell size, low density difference |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 SCREENING OF MICROALGAE — The Six-Tier Funnel

> [!NOTE]
> Screening is a **pyramidal funnel**: a vast environmental biodiversity at the top is progressively narrowed down to a single elite strain at the bottom. Each tier applies a more stringent and more expensive filter.

**Tier 1 — Sample Collection**
- Sources: freshwater, marine, hypersaline, soil, wastewater, symbiotic niches (lichens, corals).
- Tools: plankton nets (mesh size **20–80 µm**), Niskin bottles, sterile swabs.

**Tier 2 — Enrichment & Isolation**
- **Serial dilution** in liquid medium (e.g., BG-11, F/2, Bold's Basal Medium).
- **Streak plating** on agar-solidified medium to obtain axenic colonies.
- Alternative: **micropipette isolation** under an inverted microscope (Wiedeman's capillary method).

**Tier 3 — Morphological & Microscopic Identification**
- Light microscopy, fluorescence microscopy (autofluorescence of chlorophyll *a* and phycobiliproteins).
- Identification keys: *Botryococcus*, *Chlorella*, *Nannochloropsis*, *Scenedesmus*, *Spirulina* (now *Arthrospira*).

**Tier 4 — Molecular & Phylogenetic Confirmation**
- **18S rRNA gene sequencing**, **ITS regions**, **rbcL** marker.
- Confirms species identity and detects cryptic diversity.

**Tier 5 — Physiological & Biochemical Screening**
- Growth rate (µ, day⁻¹)
- Biomass productivity (g L⁻¹ d⁻¹)
- Lipid content (% DCW) — assessed by **Nile Red** or **BODIPY 505/515** fluorescence staining
- Carbohydrate/protein content
- Tolerance to pH, salinity, temperature, CO₂, heavy metals

**Tier 6 — Scale-Up & Pilot Validation**
- Lab photobioreactor → outdoor raceway pond → industrial scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The classical *Chlorella* and *Botryococcus* strains used in textbook questions are almost always screened using the **"rapid Nile Red fluorescence + flow cytometry"** route because it bypasses the slow gravimetric lipid estimation.

### 2.2 HARVESTING OF MICROALGAE — Classification of Methods

The harvesting methods fall into **three main classes** based on the physical principle exploited:

#### Class A — Mechanical (Physical) Methods
1. **Centrifugation**
2. **Filtration** (microfiltration, ultrafiltration, vacuum, pressure, tangential flow)
3. **Sedimentation / Gravity**

#### Class B — Chemical / Physico-Chemical Methods
1. **Flocculation** (chemical, electro-coagulation, bio-flocculation, auto-flocculation)
2. **Flotation** (Dissolved Air Flotation — DAF, Dispersed Air Flotation)

#### Class C — Biological Methods
1. **Bio-flocculation** using fungi, bacteria, or floc-forming microalgae
2. **Autoflocculation** triggered by high pH (>10.5)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every equation, boundary condition, and unit that a KTU 2024 board examiner can legitimately ask for in the question paper on this topic. Memorize this single block.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Description of Variables | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Biomass productivity** | $P \;=\; \dfrac{X_{f} \;-\; X_{i}}{t_{f} \;-\; t_{i}}$ | $X_{f}$ final, $X_{i}$ initial biomass; $t$ time | g L⁻¹ d⁻¹ | Strain selection metric |
| 2 | **Specific growth rate** | $\mu \;=\; \dfrac{\ln X_{f} \;-\; \ln X_{i}}{t_{f} \;-\; t_{i}}$ | Biomass concentrations and time | day⁻¹ | Selection for fast growers |
| 3 | **Doubling time** | $t_{d} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu} \;\approx\; \dfrac{0.693}{\mu}$ | $t_d$ doubling time; $\mu$ specific growth rate | day, h | Comparing strains |
| 4 | **Lipid productivity** | $P_{L} \;=\; P \times L_{c}$ | $P$ biomass productivity; $L_c$ lipid content (% DCW) | g L⁻¹ d⁻¹ | **Single most important** screening parameter |
| 5 | **Harvesting efficiency** | $\eta \;=\; \dfrac{C_{i} \;-\; C_{f}}{C_{i}} \times 100$ | $C_i$ initial, $C_f$ final cell concentration | % | Compare all methods |
| 6 | **Concentration factor** | $CF \;=\; \dfrac{V_{broth}}{V_{slurry}}$ | Volumes before/after harvesting | dimensionless | Volume reduction ratio |
| 7 | **Stokes' law (settling velocity)** | $v_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{r^{2}\,g\,(\rho_{p} - \rho_{f})}{18\,\mu}$ | $r$ cell radius; $g$ gravity; $\rho_p$ cell density; $\rho_f$ fluid density; $\mu$ viscosity | m s⁻¹ | Predicts gravity-sedimentation feasibility |
| 8 | **Modified Stokes for centrifuge** | $v_{c} \;=\; r_{p}^{2}\,\omega^{2}\,(R_{2}^{2} - R_{1}^{2})\,(\rho_{p} - \rho_{f})\,/\,(9\,\mu)$ | $\omega$ angular velocity; $R$ radii | m s⁻¹ | Centrifuge design |
| 9 | **Centrifugal acceleration ratio** | $Z \;=\; \dfrac{\omega^{2} R}{g} \;=\; \dfrac{RCF}{1}$ | $\omega$ rad/s; $R$ radius; $RCF$ relative centrifugal force (× g) | dimensionless | Centrifuge operating setting |
| 10 | **Capillary number (filtration)** | $Ca \;=\; \dfrac{\mu\, v}{\gamma_{LG}}$ | $v$ cross-flow velocity; $\gamma_{LG}$ liquid–gas interfacial tension | dimensionless | Membrane fouling control |
| 11 | **Recovery (flocculation)** | $R \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{OD_{f}}{OD_{i}}$ | Optical densities (proxy for cell conc.) | fraction | Flocculant screening |
| 12 | **Lipid-to-biomass ratio** | $L_{c} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{lipid}}{m_{biomass}} \times 100$ | Masses of lipid and total dry biomass | % w/w | Oleaginicity criterion |
| 13 | **Power required (centrifuge)** | $P_{w} \;=\; \dot{m}\,c_{p}\,\Delta T$ (thermal); or $P_{w} \;=\; \tau\,\omega$ | Mechanical work to separate cells | W, kW | Energy/cost analysis |
| 14 | **Reynolds number (settling)** | $Re_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_{f}\,v_{s}\,d_{p}}{\mu}$ | $d_p$ particle diameter | dimensionless | Stokes regime check |
| 15 | **Zeta potential threshold** | $\vert \zeta \vert \;<\; 20$ mV $\Rightarrow$ flocculates | Surface charge magnitude | mV | Chemical flocculation design |

### 2.4 Selection Decision Tree — When to Use What?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Mandated Logic:** The choice of harvesting method is dictated by **(i) cell size, (ii) cell density relative to water, (iii) culture value, and (iv) desired end-product quality.**

- **Large cells (>70 µm, e.g., *Spirulina*):** simple **filtration through cloth/screen** is sufficient.
- **Small cells (3–10 µm, e.g., *Chlorella*, *Nannochloropsis*):** **centrifugation** or **flocculation + flotation**.
- **High-value nutraceutical market:** **membrane filtration** (preserves cell integrity).
- **Lowest cost bulk recovery:** **auto-flocculation at high pH** combined with **DAF**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In commercial microalgal biorefineries (e.g., *Aurora Algae*, *Algenol*, *Sapphire Energy*), the integrated screening-to-harvesting chain determines whether the final biodiesel sells at **USD 1.20/L or USD 5/L**. Marine strains (e.g., *Nannochloropsis gaditana*) selected for high EPA and lipid content are typically harvested by **disk-stack centrifugation** at **3000–5000 × g**, achieving >95% recovery at 10–20% solids.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stokes' Settling Velocity and the *Why* Behind the Centrifuge Bottleneck

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**. It directly explains why gravity sedimentation of microalgae is impossible in practice, justifying the use of energy-intensive centrifuges.

We derive the terminal settling velocity of a single spherical microalgal cell in a viscous fluid under gravity.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram on a settling cell.**
Three forces act on a spherical cell of radius $r$ falling at terminal velocity $v_s$ in a fluid of viscosity $\mu$:

1. Gravitational (downward): $F_g = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\,\rho_{p}\,g$
2. Buoyant (upward): $F_b = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\,\rho_{f}\,g$
3. Viscous drag (Stokes' drag, upward): $F_d = 6\pi\mu r\,v_s$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's equilibrium at terminal velocity.**
At terminal velocity, the cell has zero acceleration, so the net force is zero:

$$
F_g \;-\; F_b \;-\; F_d \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\,\rho_{p}\,g \;-\; \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\,\rho_{f}\,g \;-\; 6\pi\mu r\,v_s \;=\; 0 \\[6pt]
\dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\,g\,(\rho_{p} - \rho_{f}) \;=\; 6\pi\mu r\,v_s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $v_s$.**

$$
v_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{\dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\,g\,(\rho_{p} - \rho_{f})}{6\pi\mu r}
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel terms ($\pi$ and one $r$).**

$$
\boxed{\,v_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{r^{2}\,g\,(\rho_{p} - \rho_{f})}{18\,\mu}\,}
$$

This is the famous **Stokes' Law** for terminal settling velocity.

**Step 6 — Apply numerical values to expose the bottleneck.**

Take a typical *Chlorella* cell:
- $r = 3\,\mu\text{m} = 3\times10^{-6}\,\text{m}$
- $\rho_p = 1080\,\text{kg/m}^3$ (cell density, slightly heavier than water)
- $\rho_f = 997\,\text{kg/m}^3$ (water at 25 °C)
- $\mu = 8.9\times10^{-4}\,\text{Pa\cdot s}$ (water at 25 °C)
- $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{(3\times10^{-6})^{2}\times 9.81 \times (1080-997)}{18 \times 8.9\times10^{-4}} \\[6pt]
v_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{9\times10^{-12} \times 9.81 \times 83}{1.602\times10^{-2}} \\[6pt]
v_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{7.327\times10^{-9}}{1.602\times10^{-2}} \\[6pt]
v_{s} \;\approx\; 4.57\times10^{-7}\,\text{m/s} \;\approx\; 0.0016\,\text{m/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion — KTU Exam Favourite:** A *Chlorella* cell falls at only **~1.6 mm per hour** under gravity. To settle a 1-metre culture column would take **>625 hours (~26 days)**. **This is the engineering justification for using centrifuges**, which multiply the effective $g$ by a factor of $RCF$ (typically 3000–10000 × g).

**Step 7 — Centrifuge version of the same equation.**

When the cell is in a centrifuge at angular velocity $\omega$ and distance $R$ from the axis, the radial acceleration is $\omega^{2} R$. Replacing $g$ in Stokes' equation gives:

$$
\boxed{\,v_{c} \;=\; \dfrac{r^{2}\,\omega^{2}\,R\,(\rho_{p} - \rho_{f})}{18\,\mu}\,}
$$

A centrifuge with $R = 0.2\,\text{m}$ and $\omega = 314\,\text{rad/s}$ (≈ 3000 rpm) produces $RCF = 20088 \times g$, increasing $v_c$ by **four orders of magnitude**, allowing complete separation in 5–10 minutes.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Harvesting Efficiency and the Mass-Balance View

**Step 1 — Define cell concentration** $C$ in the broth (cells mL⁻¹ or g L⁻¹) and the culture volume $V$.

**Step 2 — Initial cell mass.**

$$
M_{i} \;=\; C_{i} \times V_{i}
$$

**Step 3 — Final cell mass remaining in the supernatant (after harvest).**

$$
M_{f,sup} \;=\; C_{f} \times V_{f}
$$

**Step 4 — Harvesting efficiency definition.**

$$
\eta \;=\; \dfrac{M_{i} - M_{f,sup}}{M_{i}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify assuming $V_i \approx V_f$ (negligible volume change from removed water).**

$$
\boxed{\,\eta \;=\; \dfrac{C_{i} - C_{f}}{C_{i}} \times 100\%\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Example calculation.**

Suppose $C_i = 0.5\,\text{g/L}$ and after centrifugation $C_f = 0.02\,\text{g/L}$:

$$
\eta \;=\; \dfrac{0.5 - 0.02}{0.5} \times 100\% \;=\; \dfrac{0.48}{0.5}\times 100\% \;=\; 96\%
$$

This **96% harvesting efficiency** is typical of industrial disk-stack centrifuges.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of All Harvesting Methods (Exam-Ready Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a *high-frequency KTU 14-mark question format*. The following matrix must be memorized.

| Method | Cell-Size Suitability | Recovery (%) | Capital Cost | Operating Cost | Energy (kWh/ton) | Cell Damage | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gravity sedimentation** | >100 µm only | 10–30 | Very low | Very low | 0 | None | *Spirulina*-like large strains |
| **Microfiltration** | >5 µm | 70–90 | Medium | Medium | 2–5 | Low | *Chlorella* in lab/pilot |
| **Ultrafiltration** | <5 µm | 85–95 | High | Medium | 3–8 | Low | Value-added intracellular products |
| **Centrifugation** | All sizes | 90–99 | High | High | 8–15 | Moderate | *Nannochloropsis*, *Chlorella* |
| **Chemical flocculation** | All sizes | 70–95 | Low | Low–medium | 0.5–2 | None | Bulk biofuels |
| **Auto-flocculation (high pH)** | All sizes | 70–90 | Low | Very low | 0.2–1 | None | *Chlorella* with lime/NaOH |
| **Bio-flocculation (fungi)** | All sizes | 80–95 | Low | Very low | 0.1–0.5 | None | Pellet-forming strains |
| **Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** | 5–50 µm | 80–95 | Medium | Low | 1–3 | Low | Wastewater-grown algae |
| **Electrocoagulation** | All sizes | 95–99 | Medium | Medium | 2–4 | None | Saline marine strains |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Concentration Factor and Energy per Tonne of Biomass

**Step 1 — Definition of concentration factor.**

$$
CF \;=\; \dfrac{V_{broth}}{V_{slurry}}
$$

**Step 2 — Relation to solids fraction.**
If final solids = 15% w/w (typical paste), then $V_{slurry} = M_{biomass}/0.15$.

**Step 3 — Example.**
For 1 m³ of broth at 0.5 g/L → 500 g of biomass → at 15% solids → $V_{slurry} = 0.500/0.150 = 3.33$ L = 0.00333 m³.

$$
CF \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.00333} \;\approx\; 300\times
$$

**Step 4 — Energy calculation.**
Centrifuge energy ≈ 10 kWh per ton of broth:

$$
E_{specific} \;=\; \dfrac{10\,\text{kWh}}{1\,\text{ton broth}} \times 200\,\text{ton broth/ton biomass} \;=\; 2000\,\text{kWh/ton biomass}
$$

At USD 0.10/kWh → **USD 200/ton biomass** for harvesting alone — a major cost driver.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Screening Funnel (Tiered Selection Process)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Environmental Sample]:::start
    A1[Tier 1 Sample Collection]:::tier
    A2[Tier 2 Enrichment and Isolation]:::tier
    A3[Tier 3 Morphological Microscopy]:::tier
    A4[Tier 4 Molecular 18S rRNA ID]:::tier
    A5[Tier 5 Biochemical Screening]:::tier
    A6[Tier 6 Pilot Scale Up]:::tier
    A7[Elite Industrial Strain]:::final

    A0 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7

    classDef start fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef tier fill:#E0F8E0,stroke:#2E8B57,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Comprehensive Harvesting Method Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CULT[Cultivation Broth]:::input

    MECH[Mechanical Methods]:::cat1
    CENT[Centrifugation]:::meth
    FILT[Filtration]:::meth
    SED[Sedimentation]:::meth

    CHEM[Chemical Methods]:::cat2
    FLOC[Flocculation]:::meth
    FLOT[Flotation]:::meth
    ELE[Electrocoagulation]:::meth

    BIO[Biological Methods]:::cat3
    BF[Bacterial Fungal Flocculation]:::meth
    AF[Auto Flocculation High pH]:::meth

    DRY[Dewatering and Drying]:::post
    OUT[Concentrated Slurry]:::output
    EXT[Lipid Extraction]:::output

    CULT --> MECH
    CULT --> CHEM
    CULT --> BIO

    MECH --> CENT --> DRY
    MECH --> FILT --> DRY
    MECH --> SED --> DRY

    CHEM --> FLOC --> DRY
    CHEM --> FLOT --> DRY
    CHEM --> ELE --> DRY

    BIO --> BF --> DRY
    BIO --> AF --> DRY

    DRY --> OUT
    OUT --> EXT

    classDef input fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef cat1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000
    classDef cat2 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#D2691E,color:#000
    classDef cat3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef meth fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000
    classDef post fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#6A5ACD,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree for Selecting a Harvesting Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the cell size?}:::qst
    P1A[Over 70 micrometres]:::ans
    P1B[30 to 70 micrometres]:::ans
    P1C[Below 30 micrometres]:::ans

    Q2{Is the product high value?}:::qst
    Y1[Use membrane filtration]:::ans
    N1[Use chemical flocculation]:::ans

    Q3{Is the culture saline marine?}:::qst
    Y2[Use electrocoagulation]:::ans
    N2[Use centrifugation or DAF]:::ans

    Q1 -->|Large| P1A
    P1A --> X1[Use simple screening or cloth filtration]:::fin

    Q1 -->|Medium| P1B
    P1B --> Q2
```
