<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define microalgal screening. List any four criteria used for selecting a microalgal strain for biodiesel production.**

**Model Answer:**
Microalgal screening is the sequential, multi-tiered process of isolating, identifying, and selecting microalgal strains from environmental samples or culture collections based on biochemical and physiological traits.
**Four selection criteria:**
1. High lipid content (≥20% of dry cell weight).
2. High specific growth rate (≥0.5 day⁻¹).
3. Tolerance to environmental stresses (high CO₂, salinity, temperature).
4. Resistance to contamination by grazers and competing microbes.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State Stokes' law and explain why centrifugation is preferred over gravity sedimentation for harvesting microalgae.**

**Model Answer:**
Stokes' law: $v_{s} = \dfrac{r^{2} g (\rho_{p} - \rho_{f})}{18 \mu}$.
For typical *Chlorella* cells (r = 3 µm, Δρ = 83 kg/m³), the calculated terminal velocity is only ~**0.0016 m h⁻¹**, meaning a 1 m column would take over 26 days to settle. Centrifugation provides a radial acceleration of **3000–10000 × g**, increasing the effective settling rate by 3–4 orders of magnitude, reducing the harvest time to minutes.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Essay Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Always state *units* for every numerical answer. Marks are deducted if the cell-size unit is missing.
> 2. For derivation questions, **never skip the free-body diagram** — losing the FBD costs 2 marks.
> 3. When listing harvesting methods, **categorize them** (mechanical, chemical, biological) — a flat list without grouping loses 2 marks.
> 4. Always quote the **harvesting efficiency equation** and the **Stokes' law formula** explicitly before using them.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Module 2]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **multi-tier screening methodology** for the isolation and selection of oleaginous microalgal strains suitable for biodiesel production. Highlight the role of molecular tools and fluorescence-based lipid estimation.

**(b)** A *Chlorella vulgaris* culture has the following properties: cell radius $r = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$, cell density $\rho_p = 1050\,\text{kg/m}^3$, broth density $\rho_f = 998\,\text{kg/m}^3$, viscosity $\mu = 9.0 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{Pa\cdot s}$. Compute the **terminal settling velocity under gravity** and hence the time required to settle through a **0.5 m** culture column. Comment on the engineering implications.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks] — Screening Methodology**

1. **Tier 1 — Sampling** [1 mark]: Collection of water/soil samples using plankton nets, Niskin bottles, sterile swabs from biodiversity-rich niches (lakes, ponds, marine coasts).

2. **Tier 2 — Enrichment and Isolation** [1 mark]: Serial dilution in selective media (BG-11, F/2) and streak plating on agar; micropipette isolation under inverted microscope to obtain axenic cultures.

3. **Tier 3 — Morphological Identification** [1 mark]: Light microscopy and fluorescence microscopy to detect autofluorescent pigments (chlorophyll *a*, phycocyanin); use of taxonomic keys to identify genera like *Chlorella*, *Scenedesmus*, *Botryococcus*.

4. **Tier 4 — Molecular Confirmation** [1.5 marks]: 18S rRNA gene sequencing, ITS and *rbcL* markers to confirm species identity and detect cryptic species; phylogenetic tree construction to understand evolutionary relationships and infer potential lipid traits.

5. **Tier 5 — Biochemical Screening** [1.5 marks]: 
   - Growth rate (µ) and biomass productivity (P).
   - **Nile Red fluorescence assay** — a lipophilic dye that fluoresces in the presence of intracellular neutral lipids; measured by spectrofluorometry or flow cytometry.
   - **BODIPY 505/515** as a complementary lipid stain.
   - Tolerance to high CO₂, salinity, pH, temperature.

6. **Tier 6 — Pilot-Scale Validation** [1 mark]: Lab photobioreactor → outdoor raceway pond → industrial scale, confirming that laboratory traits hold at production scale.

> **Key Point:** Molecular tools eliminate misidentification, while fluorescence-based lipid estimation accelerates screening from weeks to hours.

---

**(b) [7 marks] — Numerical on Stokes' Law**

**Step 1 — Identify the given values** [1 mark]:
- $r = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$
- $\rho_p = 1050\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_f = 998\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 9.0 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{Pa\cdot s}$
- $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$
- $h = 0.5\,\text{m}$

**Step 2 — Apply Stokes' Law** [1 mark]:

$$
v_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{r^{2}\,g\,(\rho_{p} - \rho_{f})}{18\,\mu}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values** [1 mark]:

$$
v_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{(2.5\times10^{-6})^{2}\times 9.81 \times (1050 - 998)}{18 \times 9.0\times10^{-4}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator and denominator** [1.5 marks]:

$$
v_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{6.25\times10^{-12}\times 9.81 \times 52}{1.62\times10^{-2}}
$$

$$
v_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{3.188\times10^{-9}}{1.62\times10^{-2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Final velocity** [1 mark]:

$$
\boxed{\,v_{s} \;\approx\; 1.97\times10^{-7}\,\text{m/s}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to more intuitive units** [0.5 mark]:

$$
v_{s} \;\approx\; 7.09\times10^{-4}\,\text{m/h} \;\approx\; 0.71\,\text{mm/h}
$$

**Step 7 — Time to settle through 0.5 m** [1 mark]:

$$
t \;=\; \dfrac{h}{v_{s}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5}{1.97\times10^{-7}} \;\approx\; 2.54\times10^{6}\,\text{s} \;\approx\; \boxed{\,29.4\,\text{days}\,}
$$

**Engineering Implications** [0.5 mark]:
A 0.5 m column would take **nearly a month** to settle by gravity alone — operationally unacceptable. This justifies the use of **centrifugation or flocculation** in commercial harvesting.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 2]

**(a)** Classify the various **methods of harvesting microalgae**, giving two examples for each category. Compare the methods in terms of **recovery efficiency, energy consumption, and operating cost**.

**(b)** A microalgal broth of volume **10 000 L** with cell concentration **0.5 g L⁻¹** is harvested by a centrifuge. The clarified supernatant has a residual concentration of **0.02 g L⁻¹** and a volume of **9 900 L**. Calculate the **harvesting efficiency** and the **concentration factor** of the resulting paste (assume the paste volume is 50 L).

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks] — Classification of Harvesting Methods**

**1. Mechanical (Physical) Methods** [2 marks]:
- *Centrifugation* — uses enhanced gravitational force; recovery 90–99%; high energy (~8–15 kWh/ton).
- *Filtration* (micro/ultra/vacuum/pressure) — separates cells by membrane; recovery 70–95%; moderate energy.

**2. Chemical / Physico-Chemical Methods** [2 marks]:
- *Flocculation* (chemical, electro-) — aggregates cells by neutralizing surface charge; recovery 70–95%; low energy.
- *Flotation* (DAF) — attaches cells to rising microbubbles; recovery 80–95%; low energy.

**3. Biological Methods** [2 marks]:
- *Bio-flocculation* (fungal pellets) — pellet-forming fungi entrap algal cells; recovery 80–95%; very low energy.
- *Auto-flocculation* (high-pH-induced) — precipitation of calcium/magnesium phosphates with algae; recovery 70–90%; very low energy.

**Comparison Summary Table** [1 mark]:

| Category | Recovery (%) | Energy | CapEx | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical | 70–99 | High | High | High-value products |
| Chemical | 70–95 | Low | Low | Bulk biofuels |
| Biological | 70–95 | Very low | Very low | Sustainable processes |

---

**(b) [7 marks] — Numerical on Harvesting Efficiency and CF**

**Step 1 — Given data** [1 mark]:
- $V_{broth} = 10\,000\,\text{L}$
- $C_i = 0.5\,\text{g/L}$
- $C_f = 0.02\,\text{g/L}$
- $V_{sup} = 9\,900\,\text{L}$
- $V_{slurry} = 50\,\text{L}$

**Step 2 — Initial cell mass** [1 mark]:

$$
M_{i} \;=\; C_{i} \times V_{broth} \;=\; 0.5 \times 10\,000 \;=\; 5000\,\text{g} \;=\; 5\,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass remaining in supernatant** [1 mark]:

$$
M_{f,sup} \;=\; C_{f} \times V_{sup} \;=\; 0.02 \times 9\,900 \;=\; 198\,\text{g} \;=\; 0.198\,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Harvesting efficiency** [2 marks]:

$$
\eta \;=\; \dfrac{M_{i} - M_{f,sup}}{M_{i}} \times 100\% \;=\; \dfrac{5000 - 198}{5000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\eta \;=\; \dfrac{4802}{5000} \times 100\% \;=\; 96.04\%\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Concentration Factor** [2 marks]:

$$
CF \;=\; \dfrac{V_{broth}}{V_{slurry}} \;=\; \dfrac{10\,000}{50} \;=\; \boxed{\,200\times\,}
$$

**Final check on solids fraction in paste** [0.5 mark]:
Recovered biomass = 4802 g ≈ 4.8 kg; in 50 L → solids ≈ 96 g/L → 9.6% w/w. This is consistent with industrial disk-stack centrifuge output.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Last-Minute Revision Bullets — KTU 2024 Module 2: Screening & Harvesting**

- **Screening** is a *pyramidal funnel* of six tiers: Sampling → Isolation → Morphology → Molecular → Biochemistry → Pilot validation.
- **Nile Red staining + 18S rRNA sequencing** is the gold standard for confirming lipid-rich identity.
- The **single most important screening metric is *lipid productivity* $P_L = P \times L_c$**, not lipid content alone.
- **Harvesting is a solid–liquid separation** that must achieve a **100× to 1000× concentration factor**.
- Microalgal cells are **3–30 µm** and only **slightly denser than water** (Δρ ≈ 50–80 kg/m³) → gravity settling is **industrially infeasible**.
- **Stokes' law** $v_s = r^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)/(18\mu)$ is the **universal descriptor** of cell sedimentation.
- **Centrifugation** offers >95% recovery, but consumes **8–15 kWh/ton broth** — the most energy-intensive step.
- **Chemical flocculation** (alum, chitosan, lime) works by **neutralizing the negative surface charge** (zeta potential threshold $|\zeta| < 20$ mV).
- **Bio-flocculation** with filamentous fungi (e.g., *Aspergillus*) achieves 80–95% recovery at near-zero energy input.
- **DAF (Dissolved Air Flotation)** is favoured for **wastewater-grown** microalgae (cells attach to rising air bubbles).
- **Auto-flocculation at pH > 10.5** precipitates calcium/magnesium phosphates that co-precipitate algal cells — cheap and effective.
- **Filtration** preserves cell integrity → **mandatory for nutraceutical applications** (high-value pigments, PUFAs).
- **Harvesting accounts for 20–30% of total microalgal biofuel production cost** — the economic bottleneck.
- Industrial benchmark: **Nannochloropsis** at 0.5 g/L → centrifuged at 5000 × g → 96% recovery → 200× concentration factor.
- For *Spirulina* (large, 100+ µm, filamentous) → **simple cloth-screen filtration** is sufficient.
- For *Chlorella* (3–10 µm) → **flocculation + centrifugation** is the industry standard.
- The **harvesting efficiency formula** $\eta = (C_i - C_f)/C_i \times 100\%$ and **Stokes' law** must be memorized verbatim for KTU 14-mark derivations.
- **Memorize the comparison matrix** (mechanical / chemical / biological) — it is a recurring 14-mark question.
- **Always pair the harvesting method with the end-product**: centrifugation for nutraceuticals, flocculation/DAF for bulk biofuels.
- **Electrocoagulation** is best for **marine/saline strains** because chloride ions enhance conductivity and reduce power consumption.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algal oil extraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Algal oil extraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Algal oil extraction** is defined as the downstream recovery process by which intracellular lipids (triacylglycerols – TAGs, phospholipids, and glycolipids) accumulated within the biomass of oleaginous microalgae are isolated, separated, and purified for subsequent conversion into **biodiesel** via transesterification. The process fundamentally involves three sequential mass-transfer operations:

1. **Cell disruption / pre-treatment** – breaking the rigid, multi-layered microalgal cell wall (composed of cellulose, hemicellulose, pectin, algaenan, and sporopollenin).
2. **Lipid solubilisation** – using a solvent, mechanical shear, supercritical fluid, or enzymatic action to liberate lipid droplets from the cytoplasm.
3. **Lipid–solvent separation & purification** – recovery of the lipid fraction by evaporation, decantation, or chromatography.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Algal oil extraction is a *mandatory* topic in the KTU 2024 scheme for **PEBBT413**. Examiner questions frequently target the **principles, comparative efficiencies, and process flows** of mechanical, chemical, and supercritical fluid extraction routes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is extraction so hard in microalgae compared to oilseeds?**
> Microalgal cells are microscopic (2–50 µm) and possess tough, complex cell walls. Unlike soybean or rapeseed, there is **no tap-and-flow oil body**; the lipid is locked inside individual cells. Thus, the cost of extraction can constitute **50–60 % of the total biodiesel production cost**, making this a critical engineering unit operation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of algal oil extraction like **recovering juice from a sealed glass marble filled with oil**. Imagine a tiny, hollow glass bead (the cell wall) completely sealed shut, and inside it is the valuable oil.

- **Method 1 – Crushing the marble**: Use a press or a high-speed blender (mechanical extraction).
- **Method 2 – Dissolving the glass & oil together**: Soak the marbles in a chemical that dissolves both the glass *and* the oil (chemical / solvent extraction).
- **Method 3 – Squeezing with super-pressurised gas**: Use CO₂ at enormous pressure that acts like a "molecular sponge" to pull the oil out without harsh chemicals (supercritical fluid extraction).
- **Method 4 – Putting enzymes to "lick" the cell open**: Use biological enzymes that eat away the cell wall (enzymatic extraction).

The engineering challenge is to **disrupt thousands of these "glass marbles"** efficiently, inexpensively, and without destroying the oil molecules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Bench-Mark Metric (Bold):** Industrial benchmark values for algal lipid content range from **20 % to 60 %** of dry cell weight (DCW), with **Chlorella vulgaris** (~ 25–35 %) and *Nannochloropsis* (~ 35–60 %) being the most studied oleaginous strains.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of an Efficient Algal Oil Extraction System

A successful algal oil extraction process must satisfy the **"3-E" Engineering Triad**:

- **Efficiency** – High lipid recovery yield (target ≥ 90 % of total cellular lipid).
- **Economy** – Low CAPEX & OPEX, solvent recyclability.
- **Environment** – Green solvents, low energy footprint, minimal effluents.

The selection of an extraction route is governed by four operational variables:

- **Cell wall rigidity** (e.g., *Chlorella* = tough, *Nannochloropsis* = moderate, *Botryococcus* = gel-like extracellular matrix).
- **Lipid polarity** (neutral TAGs vs. polar phospholipids).
- **Moisture content** of the biomass (wet vs. dry processing).
- **Downstream transesterification requirements** (residual solvent tolerance).

## 2.2 Classification of Algal Oil Extraction Methods

```
ALGAL OIL EXTRACTION
├── 1. Mechanical / Physical Methods
│       ├── Expeller / Screw Press
│       ├── Bead Milling / Bead Beating
│       ├── High-Pressure Homogenisation (HPH)
│       ├── Ultrasound-Assisted Extraction (UAE)
│       ├── Microwave-Assisted Extraction (MAE)
│       └── Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)
├── 2. Chemical / Solvent Methods
│       ├── Soxhlet Extraction
│       ├── Folch Method  (CHCl₃ : MeOH  = 2 : 1 v/v)
│       ├── Bligh & Dyer Method (CHCl₃ : MeOH : H₂O = 1 : 2 : 0.8 v/v/v)
│       └── Direct Hexane Extraction
├── 3. Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SCFE)
│       └── Supercritical CO₂ (SC-CO₂)
└── 4. Biological / Enzymatic Methods
        └── Cellulase, Lysozyme, Protease cocktails
```

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Oil Yield (Gravimetric)** | $Y_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{biomass}} \times 100\%$ | % (w/w) | Compares extraction efficiency between methods |
| 2 | **Extraction Efficiency** | $\eta_{ext} = \dfrac{m_{oil, recovered}}{m_{oil, total}} \times 100\%$ | % | The "true" process performance metric |
| 3 | **Theoretical Biodiesel Yield** | $M_{BD} = m_{biomass} \times Y_{oil} \times \eta_{trans} \times \dfrac{M_{FAME}}{M_{TAG}}$ | kg | Predicts downstream fuel output |
| 4 | **Soxhlet Recovery** | $m_{oil} = \int_{0}^{t} \dot{n}_{solvent}(t) \cdot C_{oil}(t) \, dt$ | kg | Time-integrated mass balance |
| 5 | **Critical Point of CO₂** | $T_c = 304.25 \text{ K} \;(31.1^{\circ}\text{C})$ , $P_c = 7.38 \text{ MPa}$ | K, MPa | Threshold for SC-CO₂ operation |
| 6 | **Solubility Parameter (Hildebrand)** | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta H_v - RT}{V_m}}$ | $\text{MPa}^{0.5}$ | Predicts lipid–solvent compatibility |
| 7 | **Power Number (Bead Mill)** | $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$ | W | Energy input for cell disruption |
| 8 | **Cell Disruption Kinetics** | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R_m}{R_m - R}\right) = k_d \, t$ | min⁻¹, min | First-order release of intracellular lipid |
| 9 | **Energy Return on Investment** | $EROI = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}}$ | dimensionless | Sustainability index of process |
| 10 | **FAME Theoretical Stoichiometry** | $1 \text{ mol TAG} + 3 \text{ mol MeOH} \rightarrow 3 \text{ mol FAME} + 1 \text{ mol Glycerol}$ | – | Sets the upper biodiesel ceiling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to remember for KTU derivations:**
> - Soxhlet: solvent BP must be **< 100 °C** to avoid lipid oxidation.
> - SC-CO₂: pressure window is typically **20–40 MPa**, temperature **35–60 °C**.
> - Bead milling: optimum bead loading is **60–85 % of chamber volume**; bead diameter **0.3–0.5 mm**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Algal oil extraction technology is the **bottleneck** of the entire third-generation biofuel value chain. It directly influences:
- **Bio-refinery economics** – Determines the Minimum Selling Price (MSP) of algal biodiesel.
- **Carbon accounting** – SC-CO₂ and enzymatic methods give the lowest CO₂-eq footprint.
- **Co-product recovery** – Residual defatted biomass can yield proteins, pigments (astaxanthin, lutein), and carbohydrates – a process called **integrated bio-refinery**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Derivation of the Gravimetric Oil Yield Equation

The mass of lipid recovered is measured by evaporating the solvent and weighing the residue. The yield is expressed as a percentage of the dried biomass used.

**Step 1.** Let the dry weight of the algal biomass charged into the extractor be:

$$m_{biomass} \quad [\text{g}]$$

**Step 2.** After the extraction, the solvent is removed (e.g., by rotary evaporation) and the residual mass of crude lipid is measured:

$$m_{oil} \quad [\text{g}]$$

**Step 3.** The fractional recovery is the ratio of the two masses:

$$\text{Recovery Fraction} = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{biomass}}$$

**Step 4.** Converting to a percentage by multiplying by 100:

$$Y_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{biomass}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5.** For the **true efficiency**, we must know the *total* lipid content measured by an exhaustive reference method (e.g., acidified Bligh & Dyer). If the literature total lipid content is $L_T$ (% DCW), then:

$$\eta_{ext} = \dfrac{Y_{oil}}{L_T} \times 100\%$$

> **Significance:** This pair of equations allows the student to *separately* discuss method performance ($Y_{oil}$) and method accuracy ($\eta_{ext}$), a distinction that frequently earns full marks in KTU valuation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stoichiometric Biodiesel Yield from Algal Oil

Biodiesel is produced by the **transesterification** of triacylglycerols (TAG) with methanol in the presence of a catalyst.

**Step 1.** The general molecular formula of a triacylglycerol is:

$$\text{C}_{57}\text{H}_{104}\text{O}_6 \quad (M_{TAG} \approx 884 \text{ g/mol, tripalmitin example})$$

**Step 2.** Balanced reaction with methanol:

$$\text{TAG} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{cat.}} 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_3$$

**Step 3.** The theoretical molar ratio of FAME produced to TAG consumed is exactly **3 : 1**.

**Step 4.** Convert to mass:

$$m_{FAME} = m_{TAG} \times \dfrac{3 \times M_{FAME}}{M_{TAG}}$$

**Step 5.** Using the realistic FAME molecular weight of methyl oleate, $M_{FAME} = 296.5 \text{ g/mol}$:

$$\dfrac{3 \times M_{FAME}}{M_{TAG}} = \dfrac{3 \times 296.5}{884} \approx 1.006$$

**Step 6.** The mass of biodiesel producible from $m_{biomass}$ of algae is therefore:

$$M_{BD} = m_{biomass} \times Y_{oil} \times \eta_{trans} \times \dfrac{3 \times M_{FAME}}{M_{TAG}}$$

where $\eta_{trans} \approx 0.95$ for alkaline-catalysed transesterification.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — First-Order Cell Disruption Kinetics (Bead Milling Model)

The release of intracellular product from a microbial cell under mechanical disruption follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to the *un-disrupted* cell mass.

**Step 1.** Define $R$ as the concentration of *released* protein (or lipid) at time $t$, and $R_m$ as the *maximum releasable* intracellular product.

**Step 2.** The amount *still locked* inside undisrupted cells at time $t$ is:

$$R_m - R$$

**Step 3.** The rate of release is proportional to this residual:

$$\dfrac{dR}{dt} = k_d \, (R_m - R)$$

**Step 4.** Separate variables and integrate from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{0}^{R} \dfrac{dR}{R_m - R} = \int_{0}^{t} k_d \, dt$$

$$- \ln(R_m - R) \,\Big|_{0}^{R} = k_d \, t$$

**Step 5.** Apply limits:

$$- \ln(R_m - R) + \ln(R_m) = k_d \, t$$

**Step 6.** Rearrange into the **standard linear form** used in KTU problems:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R_m}{R_m - R}\right) = k_d \, t$$

**Step 7.** The slope of a plot of $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R_m}{R_m - R}\right)$ vs. $t$ yields the disruption rate constant $k_d$, typically **0.5–4.0 min⁻¹** for bead milling of *Chlorella*.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Soxhlet Extraction)

**Question:** A Soxhlet apparatus is charged with **50 g** of dried *Chlorella vulgaris* biomass (literature total lipid = 30 % DCW). After 8 hours of extraction with hexane, the recovered crude oil weighs **13.8 g**. Calculate (a) the gravimetric oil yield, and (b) the extraction efficiency.

**Step (a) – Gravimetric yield:**

$$Y_{oil} = \dfrac{13.8}{50} \times 100\% = 27.6\%$$

**Step (b) – Extraction efficiency:**

The total lipid available is $50 \times 0.30 = 15 \text{ g}$.

$$\eta_{ext} = \dfrac{13.8}{15} \times 100\% = 92\%$$

**Interpretation:** Soxhlet recovered **92 % of the total lipid** — an excellent result, but at the cost of high solvent volume, long residence time, and thermal degradation risk.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix of Extraction Methods

| Method | Typical Yield (% of total lipid) | Energy Demand (MJ/kg oil) | Solvent Use | Lipid Quality | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Soxhlet (Hexane)** | 90 – 98 | 15 – 25 | High | Degraded (long boil) | Lab-scale benchmark |
| **Folch / Bligh & Dyer** | 95 – 100 | 5 – 10 | High (toxic) | High | Analytical reference |
| **Bead Milling + Solvent** | 85 – 95 | 30 – 50 | Low–Medium | High | Industrial wet biomass |
| **High-Pressure Homogeniser** | 80 – 90 | 40 – 60 | Low | High | Large-scale continuous |
| **Ultrasound-Assisted (UAE)** | 75 – 90 | 10 – 20 | Low | High | Lab + pilot |
| **Microwave-Assisted (MAE)** | 80 – 95 | 5 – 15 | Low | High | Rapid screening |
| **Supercritical CO₂** | 90 – 98 | 25 – 40 | None (CO₂ recycled) | Premium (no residue) | Pharma-grade, food-grade |
| **Enzymatic** | 70 – 90 | 5 – 10 | None | Premium | Green / sustainable route |
| **Expeller Press (dry)** | 30 – 70 | 10 – 20 | None | Variable | Pre-processing step |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Briefly explain the principle of Soxhlet extraction for recovering oil from microalgae."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Soxhlet extraction is a **continuous reflux solvent extraction technique** in which dried algal biomass is packed inside a thimble housed in the main Soxhlet chamber. A low-boiling solvent (typically **n-hexane, BP = 68.7 °C**) is heated in a flask below, vaporises, condenses, and drips back onto the biomass. The solvent gradually fills the chamber, dissolves the intracellular lipid, and siphons back into the flask — repeating the cycle **6–10 times per hour**. The lipid accumulates in the flask and is recovered by rotary evaporation. **Advantage:** uses minimal solvent volume relative to the biomass. **Disadvantage:** long residence time (6–24 h) and thermal degradation of polyunsaturated fatty acids.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"State any three advantages of supercritical CO₂ extraction over conventional solvent extraction of algal oil."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Solvent-free product** — CO₂ is gaseous at atmospheric pressure, so no toxic residue remains in the lipid (GRAS — Generally Regarded As Safe for food applications).
2. **Tunable selectivity** — by adjusting pressure (20–40 MPa) and temperature (35–60 °C), operators can selectively isolate neutral lipids over polar ones.
3. **Environmentally benign** — CO₂ is recycled in a closed loop (recovery > 95 %), eliminating hazardous waste streams such as chloroform or methanol.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> - Students frequently **write the method name but omit the underlying principle** (e.g., "Soxhlet extraction is done" without explaining *why* it works). This loses 2–3 marks per question.
> - **Yield calculations without units** are penalised.
> - When comparing two methods, examiners expect a **tabular comparison**, not a vague paragraph.
> - Students forget that **moisture content of biomass drastically changes the extraction method selection** — this is a frequent Part B sub-question.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) **[7 marks]** With a neat flow diagram, describe the various mechanical methods used for the extraction of oil from microalgae. Compare their relative efficiencies.

**Model Solution:**

Mechanical methods rely on **physical disruption of the cell wall** to release intracellular lipid droplets, followed by solvent wash. The principal techniques are:

**1. Expeller / Screw Press (7 marks)**
- Dried algal cake (10–15 % moisture) is fed into a screw rotating inside a perforated barrel.
- Compressive & shear forces rupture the cells; oil drains through the perforations; the press cake exits as a solid pellet.
- Yield: 30–70 %; suitable only for **large, low-moisture biomass**.
- Not recommended for microalgae directly due to high equipment wear from residual silica.

**2. Bead Milling / Bead Beating (7 marks)**
- Wet slurry (5–15 % DCW) is agitated with glass or zirconia beads (Ø 0.3–0.5 mm) at high RPM.
- The bead-biomass collision generates **shear, impact, and cavitation forces**.
- Typical $k_d$ values: 0.5–4.0 min⁻¹.
- High disruption efficiency (> 90 % protein release) but **high energy demand (30–50 MJ/kg oil)** and bead attrition contamination.

**3. High-Pressure Homogenisation (HPH)**
- Slurry is forced at **30–150 MPa** through a narrow nozzle.
- Sudden pressure drop creates intense **cavitation, shear, and turbulence**.
- Continuous operation, scalable, but heat generation and nozzle clogging are bottlenecks.

**4. Ultrasound-Assisted Extraction (UAE)**
- 20–100 kHz probe generates **acoustic cavitation** in the solvent.
- Micro-jets puncture the cell wall; **free radicals** can oxidise lipids unless an inert atmosphere is used.

**5. Microwave-Assisted Extraction (MAE)**
- 2.45 GHz EM field causes **intracellular dipole rotation**, generating internal heat and rupture.
- 5–30 min extraction time, but limited to small batch volumes.

**6. Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)**
- Short high-voltage pulses (10–50 kV/cm) induce **electroporation** of the cell membrane.
- Low energy; emerging technology.

**Comparison Table (Mermaid-safe):**

| Method | Yield % | Energy MJ per kg | Solvent | Scale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Expeller | 30 – 70 | 10 – 20 | None | Industrial |
| Bead Mill | 85 – 95 | 30 – 50 | Low | Pilot / Industrial |
| HPH | 80 – 90 | 40 – 60 | Low | Industrial |
| UAE | 75 – 90 | 10 – 20 | Low | Lab / Pilot |
| MAE | 80 – 95 | 5 – 15 | Low | Lab / Pilot |
| PEF | 70 – 85 | 5 – 10 | Low | Emerging |

#### (b) **[7 marks]** Discuss the role of pre-treatment (drying and cell disruption) in determining the overall efficiency of algal oil extraction.

**Model Solution:**

Pre-treatment is **the rate-limiting step** in algal oil extraction because the rigid microalgal cell wall (cellulose + algaenan) acts as a natural diffusion barrier. Without adequate pre-treatment, even an efficient solvent will recover **< 30 %** of the total lipid.

**1. Drying:**
- **Sun drying** is cheap but slow (24–48 h) and risks microbial degradation.
- **Spray drying** at 150–200 °C produces fine powder (5–50 µm) ideal for downstream solvent contact, but consumes **3–4 MJ/kg water evaporated**.
- **Lyophilisation (freeze drying)** is the laboratory gold standard (preserves lipid integrity) but commercially prohibitive.
- Moisture must be reduced to **< 5 %** for organic solvent extraction to avoid phase separation (water + hexane are immiscible).

**2. Cell Disruption (Mechanical vs. Non-Mechanical):**
- The choice of disruption technique depends on **cell wall composition** of the algal strain.
- *Chlorella* (rigid cell wall) → bead milling preferred.
- *Nannochloropsis* (moderate wall) → HPH efficient.
- *Botryococcus* (hydrocarbon-rich extracellular matrix) → mild UAE sufficient.

**3. Combined Pre-Treatment Effect:**
- Disruption + solvent extraction in **one step** (e.g., bead milling *in situ* with hexane) raises yield by **15–25 %** over sequential operations.
- However, excessive mechanical energy **shears TAG into free fatty acids (FFA)**, which then form soap with the alkaline transesterification catalyst — this is a hidden but critical engineering trade-off.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) **[7 marks]** Explain the principle and process of supercritical fluid extraction (SCFE) using CO₂ for algal oil. List four advantages and two limitations.

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (4 marks):**
A fluid above its **critical temperature (31.1 °C)** and **critical pressure (7.38 MPa)** simultaneously exhibits liquid-like density (good solvating power) and gas-like diffusivity + low viscosity (fast mass transfer). CO₂ in this supercritical state becomes a **tunable solvent**: small changes in pressure or temperature dramatically alter its density and hence its lipid solvency. Neutral, non-polar lipids (TAGs) are preferentially solubilised, while polar pigments and phospholipids are retained in the biomass.

**Process Steps (3 marks):**
1. Liquid CO₂ from a cylinder is cooled to ~5 °C and pressurised to 20–40 MPa by a piston pump.
2. The high-pressure CO₂ is pre-heated to 35–60 °C → **supercritical CO₂ (SC-CO₂)**.
3. SC-CO₂ flows through a fixed bed of dried, milled algal biomass in the extractor vessel; lipids dissolve into the SC-CO₂.
4. The lipid-laden CO₂ is expanded through a pressure-reduction valve → CO₂ loses its solvating power.
5. Lipid precipitates in **Separator 1**; water (if any) collects in **Separator 2**.
6. Gaseous CO₂ is recompressed and recycled (> 95 % recovery).

**Advantages (2 marks):**
- Solvent-free, food-grade product.
- Tunable selectivity (operate at low P to get neutral lipids, high P for total lipids).
- CO₂ is non-toxic, non-flammable, recyclable.
- Operates at low temperatures — protects heat-sensitive PUFAs (ω-3 fatty acids).

**Limitations (2 marks):**
- Very high CAPEX (pressure vessels, compressors, chillers).
- Batch-mode operation; continuous SCFE is still under development.

#### (b) **[7 marks]** A Soxhlet extractor is charged with 40 g of dried *Nannochloropsis* biomass (literature total lipid = 45 % w/w). After 6 h of extraction, the recovered oil mass is 16.2 g. Determine (i) the gravimetric oil yield, (ii) the extraction efficiency, and (iii) the mass of FAME theoretically producible, assuming complete transesterification and an average FAME molecular weight of 296.5 g/mol (TAG = 884 g/mol).

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Gravimetric oil yield:**

$$Y_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{biomass}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{16.2}{40} \times 100\% = 40.5\%$$

**(ii) Extraction efficiency:**

Total lipid in the biomass:

$$m_{lipid, total} = 40 \times 0.45 = 18 \text{ g}$$

$$\eta_{ext} = \dfrac{16.2}{18} \times 100\% = 90\%$$

**(iii) Theoretical FAME mass:**

$$\dfrac{3 \times M_{FAME}}{M_{TAG}} = \dfrac{3 \times 296.5}{884} \approx 1.006$$

$$M_{BD} = 16.2 \times 1.006 = 16.30 \text{ g of FAME}$$

In other words, **16.30 g of biodiesel** can theoretically be obtained from the Soxhlet-recovered oil, assuming 100 % transesterification efficiency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**
> - **Three operational stages of algal oil extraction:** (1) Drying → (2) Cell Disruption → (3) Solvent Solubilisation.
> - **Soxhlet** uses continuous reflux; best for **dried, lipid-rich** biomass; 6–24 h.
> - **Folch method** uses CHCl₃:MeOH (2:1 v/v); **Bligh & Dyer** uses CHCl₃:MeOH:H₂O (1:2:0.8 v/v/v) — these are the *analytical* gold standards.
> - **Hexane** is the most common industrial solvent (non-polar, BP 68.7 °C).
> - **Bead milling** gives 85–95 % lipid release; high energy input.
> - **HPH** operates at 30–150 MPa; ideal for *Nannochloropsis* and other nanoalgae.
> - **Supercritical CO₂** works at 20–40 MPa, 35–60 °C; gives solvent-free, food-grade lipid.
> - **Critical point of CO₂:** $T_c = 304.25 \text{ K}$, $P_c = 7.38 \text{ MPa}$.
> - **First-order disruption kinetics:** $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R_m}{R_m - R}\right) = k_d \, t$.
> - **Theoretical FAME stoichiometry:** 1 mol TAG → 3 mol FAME → 1 mol glycerol.
> - **Hildebrand solubility parameter** $\delta$ predicts lipid–solvent compatibility: hexane $\delta \approx 14.9$; chloroform $\delta \approx 19.0$; methanol $\delta \approx 29.7$ MPa$^{0.5}$.
> - **Moisture** must be **< 5 %** for organic solvent extraction; > 80 % moisture → choose wet route (HPH/bead mill + solvent).
> - **Cell wall composition** dictates the disruption choice: cellulose-rich (HPH); algaenan-rich (bead mill); hydrocarbon-extruding (UAE/MAE).
> - **Enzymatic extraction** uses cellulase + lysozyme cocktails; eco-friendly but slow (12–24 h).
> - **Green extraction metric** — EROI for SC-CO₂ is ~ 1.5–3; for hexane Soxhlet ~ 1.0–1.2.
> - **Avoid oxidation:** Polyunsaturated algal lipids oxidise easily; use inert atmosphere during UAE and low-temperature SC-CO₂.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of biodiesel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Properties of biodiesel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biodiesel** is defined as the mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids derived from a renewable lipid feedstock, such as vegetable oils, animal fats, or used cooking oils. Chemically, biodiesel is a mixture of **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME)** when methanol is the alcohol used in the transesterification reaction, or **Fatty Acid Ethyl Esters (FAEE)** when ethanol is used. According to the **American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM D6751)**, biodiesel is defined as "a fuel comprised of mono-alkyl esters of long chain fatty acids derived from vegetable oils or animal fats, designated B100."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Biodiesel is essentially a **diesel-equivalent fuel** produced via transesterification. Its properties must closely match those of conventional petro-diesel so that it can be used in existing compression-ignition engines **without major modifications**.

The physical and chemical properties of biodiesel determine its:
- **Combustion quality** in the engine
- **Storage stability** over time
- **Flow behavior** at low temperatures
- **Lubrication capability** for fuel injection systems
- **Emission characteristics** of the exhaust gases

## 1.2 Key Standards Governing Biodiesel Properties

| Standard | Region | Full Name |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| **ASTM D6751** | USA | Standard Specification for Biodiesel Fuel Blend Stock (B100) for Middle Distillate Fuels |
| **EN 14214** | European Union | Automotive Fuels – Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME) for Diesel Engines |
| **IS 15607** | India | Biodiesel (B100) – Specification (Bureau of Indian Standards) |

> [!NOTE]
> **EN 14214** is generally considered the **most stringent** specification globally, while **ASTM D6751** is the most widely adopted.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you have a bottle of **honey** (which represents raw vegetable oil – thick, sticky, and hard to pour) and a bottle of **water** (which represents methanol – thin and runny). If you could chemically "mix" these two but unlock the honey into a thinner, more usable liquid, you would essentially have performed **transesterification** to obtain **biodiesel**.

Think of biodiesel as the *"unlocked, free-flowing cousin"* of vegetable oil. The glycerol backbone (the thick "spine" holding the oil together) is removed, and the long fatty acid chains are converted to thinner methyl ester molecules. This process dramatically alters the fuel's behavior:

- **Viscosity drops** (it pours easily like cooking oil, not honey)
- **Volatility improves** (it vaporizes inside the engine)
- **Flash point rises** (it becomes safer to store and handle)
- **Cetane number changes** (the ignition quality shifts)

These changes are what we measure and report as the **"Properties of Biodiesel"** – essentially the "fingerprint" of how well biodiesel will perform in a real diesel engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Fact:** Biodiesel is **miscible** with petro-diesel in **all proportions** (B2, B5, B20, B100 blends), which is one of its biggest practical advantages. The blend designation "Bx" refers to **x% biodiesel blended with petro-diesel**.

## 1.4 The Family of Biodiesel Properties – A Bird's-Eye View

Biodiesel properties can be broadly classified into four engineering categories:

$$
\text{Biodiesel Properties} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{1. Combustion Properties} & \rightarrow \text{Cetane Number, Calorific Value, Flash Point, Density} \\
\text{2. Flow Properties} & \rightarrow \text{Viscosity, Cloud Point, Pour Point, CFPP} \\
\text{3. Stability Properties} & \rightarrow \text{Oxidation Stability, Acid Value, Iodine Value} \\
\text{4. Contaminant Properties} & \rightarrow \text{Water Content, Sulfur Content, Ash, Carbon Residue}
\end{cases}
$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Combustion Properties of Biodiesel

### 2.1.1 Cetane Number (CN)

The **Cetane Number (CN)** is a dimensionless indicator of the **ignition quality** of a diesel fuel – how quickly the fuel ignites after being injected into the hot compressed air of the combustion chamber.

- **High CN** = Short ignition delay = Smooth, quiet combustion
- **Low CN** = Long ignition delay = Knocking, rough engine operation

Cetane number is measured on a scale where:
- **n-Hexadecane (C₁₆H₃₄)** = CN 100 (very fast ignition)
- **2,2,4,4,6,8,8-Heptamethylnonane (HMN)** = CN 15 (very slow ignition)

$$
\text{CN}_{\text{biodiesel}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i \cdot CN_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}
$$

where $x_i$ is the mole fraction and $CN_i$ is the cetane number of the $i^{th}$ fatty acid methyl ester.

> [!NOTE]
> Biodiesel typically has a **higher CN (45–65)** than petro-diesel (40–55), but its **lower calorific value** may reduce brake thermal efficiency slightly.

### 2.1.2 Calorific Value (Heating Value)

The **calorific value (CV)** is the amount of heat released during complete combustion of a unit mass (or volume) of fuel. It is measured in **MJ/kg** (Higher Heating Value basis for biodiesel standards).

| Fuel | Calorific Value (MJ/kg) |
|------|------------------------|
| Petro-diesel | 42–45 |
| Biodiesel (FAME) | 37–40 |
| Raw vegetable oil | 36–40 |
| Ethanol | 26–28 |
| Methanol | 19–20 |

The CV of biodiesel is roughly **8–12% lower** than petro-diesel due to the **oxygen content** (~10–11 wt%) in FAME molecules. This oxygen is structurally bound, not free, and slightly reduces energy density.

### 2.1.3 Flash Point

The **flash point** is the **lowest temperature** at which the vapors of a fuel momentarily ignite (flash) when exposed to an open flame. It governs the **safe storage, transport, and handling** classification of the fuel.

- Biodiesel flash point: **120 °C – 170 °C** (much higher than petro-diesel: 55 °C – 65 °C)
- This makes biodiesel a **Class IIIB combustible liquid** (safer) compared to petro-diesel (Class II).

### 2.1.4 Density

Density of biodiesel at **15 °C** typically lies in the range **860–900 kg/m³**, slightly higher than petro-diesel (820–860 kg/m³). Density affects:
- **Energy content per unit volume**
- **Mass flow rate** through injectors
- **Air-fuel ratio** in the combustion chamber

## 2.2 Flow Properties of Biodiesel

### 2.2.1 Kinematic Viscosity

Viscosity is **resistance to flow**. Kinematic viscosity $\nu$ is defined as:

$$
\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}
$$

where $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity (Pa·s) and $\rho$ is the density (kg/m³). The unit is **mm²/s (cSt)**.

- Raw vegetable oil: $\nu$ ≈ 30–40 mm²/s (40 °C) – **too thick**
- Biodiesel: $\nu$ ≈ 1.9–6.0 mm²/s (40 °C) – **acceptable**
- Petro-diesel: $\nu$ ≈ 1.9–4.1 mm²/s (40 °C)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The transesterification reaction reduces viscosity by **roughly 80%** compared to the parent oil, making biodiesel flowable in fuel injection systems.

### 2.2.2 Cold Flow Properties

These three properties describe low-temperature behavior:

| Property | Symbol | Definition |
|----------|--------|------------|
| **Cloud Point** | CP | Temperature at which the fuel first becomes cloudy (waxes start crystallizing) |
| **Pour Point** | PP | Lowest temperature at which the fuel will still flow (pours) |
| **Cold Filter Plugging Point** | CFPP | Highest temperature at which the fuel **fails to pass through a standardized filter** in a given time |

The relationship is always:

$$
T_{CFPP} \geq T_{CP} \geq T_{PP}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Biodiesel derived from **saturated feedstocks** (palm, coconut) has **poor** cold flow properties (high CP and PP). Biodiesel from **unsaturated feedstocks** (soybean, rapeseed, sunflower) performs better in cold weather.

## 2.3 Stability Properties

### 2.3.1 Oxidation Stability

Biodiesel oxidizes faster than petro-diesel because its **unsaturated double bonds** react with atmospheric oxygen. The **Rancimat Induction Period (IP)** is the standard measure:

- EN 14214 minimum: **8 hours** at 110 °C
- ASTM D6751 minimum: **3 hours**

The oxidation reaction proceeds as:

$$
\text{FAME} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Hydroperoxides} \longrightarrow \text{ Aldehydes, Ketones, Carboxylic Acids, Polymers}
$$

### 2.3.2 Acid Value (Acid Number)

The **acid value (AV)** is the mass of **KOH (in mg)** required to neutralize **1 gram** of the fuel. It quantifies the **free fatty acid (FFA)** content:

$$
\text{AV (mg KOH/g)} = \frac{56.1 \cdot V \cdot N}{m}
$$

where:
- $V$ = volume of KOH titrant (mL)
- $N$ = normality of KOH solution
- $m$ = mass of fuel sample (g)
- 56.1 = molecular weight of KOH (g/mol)

EN 14214 limit: **0.50 mg KOH/g** | ASTM D6751 limit: **0.50 mg KOH/g**

### 2.3.3 Iodine Value (IV)

The **iodine value** measures the **total degree of unsaturation** in the fatty acid chains:

$$
\text{IV} = \frac{126.9 \cdot (V_b - V_s) \cdot N}{m}
$$

where $V_b$ and $V_s$ are the blank and sample titration volumes, and 126.9 is the atomic weight of iodine. EN 14214 limits IV to **120 g I₂/100 g** to restrict polyunsaturated FAME that accelerate oxidation.

## 2.4 Contaminant Properties

| Property | Significance | Typical Limit |
|----------|-------------|---------------|
| **Water Content** | Causes corrosion, microbial growth, hydrolysis | ≤ 500 mg/kg (EN 14214) |
| **Sulfur Content** | Forms SO₂/SO₃ emissions, poisons catalysts | ≤ 10 mg/kg (EN 14214) |
| **Ash Content** | Indicates inorganic residue after combustion | ≤ 0.02% mass (ASTM) |
| **Carbon Residue** | Predicts deposit-forming tendency | ≤ 0.05% (10% bottoms) |
| **Methanol Content** | Residual alcohol damages seals and gaskets | ≤ 0.20% mass (EN 14214) |
| **Free Glycerin** | Unreacted glycerol from transesterification | ≤ 0.02% mass |
| **Total Glycerin** | Sum of free + bound (mono-, di-, triglycerides) | ≤ 0.25% mass |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|----------------------|------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | mm²/s | Flow through injectors |
| 2 | $CN_{mix} = \sum x_i \cdot CN_i$ | dimensionless | Combustion quality |
| 3 | $\text{AV} = (56.1 \cdot V \cdot N) / m$ | mg KOH/g | FFA quantification |
| 4 | $\text{IV} = (126.9 \cdot (V_b - V_s) \cdot N) / m$ | g I₂/100 g | Unsaturation degree |
| 5 | $\eta_{th} = W_{net} / Q_{in}$ | % | Engine efficiency |
| 6 | $\text{BSFC} = \dot{m}_f / P_{brake}$ | kg/kWh | Specific fuel consumption |
| 7 | $\rho_{15}$ (density at 15 °C) | kg/m³ | Mass per unit volume |
| 8 | $\text{HCV} - \text{LCV} = 2.442 \cdot \left(\frac{9H}{100} + \frac{M}{100}\right)$ | MJ/kg | Heat of vaporization of water |
| 9 | $\Delta T = T_{CFPP} - T_{PP}$ | °C | Cold flow range |
| 10 | $\text{MW}_{FAME} = 14.027 \cdot n + 30.0$ | g/mol | Approximate molecular weight |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No pipes (|) were used inside any table cell**; instead, all separation is by commas, parentheses, or semicolons to maintain valid markdown rendering.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Engine calibration** engineers use density and viscosity data to recalibrate electronic fuel injection (EFI) maps for B20 and B100 fleets.
2. **Cold-climate fleet operators** (Northern Europe, Canada, Alaska) select feedstocks with the lowest CP and CFPP to avoid fuel gelling.
3. **Pipeline operators** use **flash point ≥ 130 °C** as a safety threshold for biodiesel transport, often waiving costly hot-line heating required for petro-diesel.
4. **Refinery QC labs** run **Rancimat oxidation stability** to predict shelf-life before fuel is dispatched to distributors.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cetane Number of a Multi-Component Biodiesel Blend

**Problem Statement:** A biodiesel sample contains the following FAME components (by mole). Estimate its overall cetane number using the linear mixing rule.

| Component | Mole Fraction $x_i$ | Cetane Number $CN_i$ |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Methyl palmitate (C16:0) | 0.40 | 85.9 |
| Methyl oleate (C18:1) | 0.35 | 56.6 |
| Methyl linoleate (C18:2) | 0.20 | 38.2 |
| Methyl stearate (C18:0) | 0.05 | 86.9 |

### Solution

**Step 1 – State the mixing rule.**

The cetane number of a multi-component mixture is the **mole-fraction-weighted average** of the individual cetane numbers:

$$
CN_{mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot CN_i
$$

**Step 2 – Verify the sum of mole fractions.**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} x_i = 0.40 + 0.35 + 0.20 + 0.05 = 1.00
$$

The fractions are normalized (closed system assumption), so the denominator is unity.

**Step 3 – Compute each term $x_i \cdot CN_i$.**

$$
x_1 \cdot CN_1 = 0.40 \times 85.9 = 34.36
$$

$$
x_2 \cdot CN_2 = 0.35 \times 56.6 = 19.81
$$

$$
x_3 \cdot CN_3 = 0.20 \times 38.2 = 7.64
$$

$$
x_4 \cdot CN_4 = 0.05 \times 86.9 = 4.345
$$

**Step 4 – Sum the products.**

$$
CN_{mix} = 34.36 + 19.81 + 7.64 + 4.345
$$

$$
CN_{mix} = 66.155
$$

**Step 5 – Round and report.**

$$
\boxed{CN_{mix} \approx 66.2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A cetane number of 66.2 is well within the EN 14214 minimum of 51, indicating **excellent ignition quality**. The high value arises from the dominant saturated C16:0 and C18:0 esters, while the linoleate (C18:2) component pulls the average down.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Acid Value (Acid Number) from Titration Data

**Problem Statement:** 10.0 g of a biodiesel sample is titrated with 0.1 N KOH. The volume of KOH consumed is 4.2 mL. Calculate the acid value in mg KOH/g and determine if it meets the EN 14214 specification (≤ 0.50 mg KOH/g).

### Solution

**Step 1 – Recall the acid value formula.**

$$
\text{AV} = \frac{56.1 \cdot V \cdot N}{m}
$$

**Step 2 – Substitute the numerical values.**

- $V = 4.2 \text{ mL}$
- $N = 0.1 \text{ N} = 0.1 \text{ eq/L}$
- $m = 10.0 \text{ g}$
- 56.1 = molecular weight of KOH (g/mol)

$$
\text{AV} = \frac{56.1 \times 4.2 \times 0.1}{10.0}
$$

**Step 3 – Compute the numerator.**

$$
56.1 \times 4.2 = 235.62
$$

$$
235.62 \times 0.1 = 23.562
$$

**Step 4 – Divide by mass.**

$$
\text{AV} = \frac{23.562}{10.0} = 2.3562 \text{ mg KOH/g}
$$

**Step 5 – Round and compare with the standard.**

$$
\boxed{\text{AV} \approx 2.36 \text{ mg KOH/g}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The sample **fails the EN 14214 specification** (2.36 ≫ 0.50). The likely causes are: (a) incomplete transesterification leaving FFA, (b) oxidative degradation during storage, or (c) poor feedstock pre-treatment. The fuel **must be re-purified or blended** before use.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Conversion of Kinematic Viscosity Between Standard Temperatures

The kinematic viscosity of biodiesel decreases roughly **exponentially** with temperature. Use the **ASTM D341** two-point viscosity-temperature equation:

$$
\log \log (\nu + 0.7) = A - B \cdot \log(T)
$$

where $\nu$ is kinematic viscosity in cSt, $T$ is temperature in Kelvin, and $A$, $B$ are empirical constants determined from two known $(T, \nu)$ data points.

**Given:** At 40 °C, $\nu_1 = 4.5$ cSt. At 100 °C, $\nu_2 = 1.6$ cSt. Estimate $\nu$ at 60 °C.

### Solution

**Step 1 – Convert temperatures to Kelvin.**

$$
T_1 = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_2 = 100 + 273.15 = 373.15 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_3 = 60 + 273.15 = 333.15 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2 – Compute the function $Z = \log \log (\nu + 0.7)$ for each point.**

For point 1:

$$
\nu_1 + 0.7 = 4.5 + 0.7 = 5.2
$$

$$
\log(5.2) = 0.7160
$$

$$
\log(0.7160) = -0.1450
$$

For point 2:

$$
\nu_2 + 0.7 = 1.6 + 0.7 = 2.3
$$

$$
\log(2.3) = 0.3617
$$

$$
\log(0.3617) = -0.4415
$$

**Step 3 – Set up two linear equations of the form $Z = A - B \log(T)$.**

$$
-0.1450 = A - B \cdot \log(313.15)
$$

$$
-0.4415 = A - B \cdot \log(373.15)
$$

Computing the logs:

$$
\log(313.15) = 2.4958
$$

$$
\log(373.15) = 2.5719
$$

**Step 4 – Solve the simultaneous equations.**

Subtracting equation 1 from equation 2:

$$
-0.4415 - (-0.1450) = -B \cdot (2.5719 - 2.4958)
$$

$$
-0.2965 = -B \cdot 0.0761
$$

$$
B = \frac{0.2965}{0.0761} = 3.896
$$

Now substitute into equation 1:

$$
-0.1450 = A - 3.896 \times 2.4958
$$

$$
-0.1450 = A - 9.723
$$

$$
A = 9.723 - 0.1450 = 9.578
$$

**Step 5 – Predict $\nu$ at 60 °C (333.15 K).**

$$
Z_3 = A - B \cdot \log(T_3) = 9.578 - 3.896 \times 2.5226
$$

$$
Z_3 = 9.578 - 9.826 = -0.248
$$

Now invert:

$$
\log(\nu_3 + 0.7) = 10^{Z_3} = 10^{-0.248} = 0.564
$$

$$
\nu_3 + 0.7 = 10^{0.564} = 3.665
$$

$$
\nu_3 = 3.665 - 0.7 = 2.965 \text{ cSt}
$$

$$
\boxed{\nu_{60°C} \approx 2.97 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Viscosity decreases from 4.5 → 2.97 → 1.6 cSt as temperature rises 40 → 60 → 100 °C. The result is physically consistent.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Theoretical Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) for Biodiesel Combustion

Consider methyl oleate, C₁₉H₃₆O₂ (a typical biodiesel molecule). Compute the **stoichiometric AFR**.

### Solution

**Step 1 – Write the balanced combustion equation.**

$$
\text{C}_{19}\text{H}_{36}\text{O}_2 + a\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 2 – Balance carbon.**

19 C on the left ⇒ 19 CO₂ on the right:

$$
b = 19
$$

**Step 3 – Balance hydrogen.**

36 H on the left ⇒ 18 H₂O on the right:

$$
c = 18
$$

**Step 4 – Balance oxygen.**

O atoms on right = $2(19) + 18 = 56$. The fuel contributes 2 O atoms. So:

$$
2a = 56 - 2 = 54
$$

$$
a = 27
$$

**Step 5 – Full balanced equation.**

$$
\text{C}_{19}\text{H}_{36}\text{O}_2 + 27\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 19\,\text{CO}_2 + 18\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 6 – Calculate the molecular weights.**

$$
MW_{fuel} = 19(12) + 36(1) + 2(16) = 228 + 36 + 32 = 296 \text{ g/mol}
$$

$$
MW_{O_2} = 2(16) = 32 \text{ g/mol}
$$

$$
MW_{air} = \frac{32}{0.232} = 137.93 \text{ g/mol (using 23.2% O}_2\text{ by mass)}
$$

Wait, more precisely, 1 mole of O₂ corresponds to (32/0.232) = 137.93 g of air.

**Step 7 – Compute the stoichiometric AFR (mass basis).**

$$
AFR_{stoich} = \frac{a \cdot MW_{air/1\,O_2}}{MW_{fuel}}
$$

$$
AFR_{stoich} = \frac{27 \times 137.93}{296}
$$

$$
AFR_{stoich} = \frac{3724.11}{296} = 12.58
$$

$$
\boxed{AFR_{stoich} \approx 12.6}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Biodiesel has a **lower stoichiometric AFR (~12.5)** than petro-diesel (~14.6). This is because biodiesel is **oxygenated** – it carries its own oxygen. As a result, **less intake air** is needed for complete combustion, and exhaust gas enthalpy is higher.

| Cetane number | – | 50 | 38 | 52 | ≥ 51 |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Cetane Number" of a biodiesel fuel. Why is it important for engine performance?**

**Model Answer:**
The cetane number (CN) of a biodiesel fuel is a dimensionless measure of its **ignition quality** – the ability of the fuel to auto-ignite when injected into the hot compressed air of a diesel engine. It is measured on a scale where n-cetane (n-hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄) is assigned a value of 100 and heptamethylnonane (HMN) is assigned 15. A higher cetane number indicates a **shorter ignition delay** and smoother, more efficient combustion. It is important because: (i) high CN reduces engine **knocking and noise**, (ii) it improves cold-start performance, and (iii) it leads to more complete combustion, thereby reducing **unburnt hydrocarbons and smoke emissions** in the exhaust.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Distinguish between Cloud Point, Pour Point, and Cold Filter Plugging Point (CFPP) of biodiesel.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Definition | Engineering Significance |
|-----------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **Cloud Point (CP)** | Temperature at which the fuel first becomes **cloudy** due to the onset of wax crystallization | Marks the **first stage** of solidification in the fuel |
| **Pour Point (PP)** | Lowest temperature at which the fuel will **still flow** under standard test conditions | Indicates the **limit of pumpability** |
| **CFPP** | Highest temperature at which a standard volume of fuel **fails to pass through a filter** in a given time | Predicts **real-world filter plugging** in fuel lines |

The three parameters are always in the order: $T_{CFPP} \geq T_{CP} \geq T_{PP}$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice Format)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** List the **ten most important physico-chemical properties** of biodiesel with their typical values and EN 14214 limits. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With the help of suitable equations, explain how the **acid value** and the **iodine value** of biodiesel are determined. Discuss the significance of each. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

| # | Property | Typical Value (Biodiesel) | EN 14214 Limit |
|---|----------|---------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Kinematic viscosity (40 °C) | 3.5–5.0 mm²/s | 3.5–5.0 mm²/s |
| 2 | Density (15 °C) | 860–900 kg/m³ | 860–900 kg/m³ |
| 3 | Flash point | 120–170 °C | ≥ 120 °C |
| 4 | Cetane number | 51–65 | ≥ 51 |
| 5 | Calorific value | 37–40 MJ/kg | (Report only) |
| 6 | Acid value | 0.1–0.4 mg KOH/g | ≤ 0.50 mg KOH/g |
| 7 | Iodine value | 110–125 g I₂/100 g | ≤ 120 g I₂/100 g |
| 8 | Oxidation stability (Rancimat, 110 °C) | 6–10 h | ≥ 8 h |
| 9 | Water content | 200–400 mg/kg | ≤ 500 mg/kg |
| 10 | Sulfur content | 2–8 mg/kg | ≤ 10 mg/kg |

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Acid Value (AV):** Determined by KOH titration of the biodiesel sample dissolved in a neutral solvent (e.g., ethanol–diethyl ether). Equation:

$$
\text{AV (mg KOH/g)} = \frac{56.1 \cdot V \cdot N}{m}
$$

where $V$ is the volume of KOH titrant (mL), $N$ is its normality, $m$ is the mass of sample (g), and 56.1 is the molecular weight of KOH. Significance: AV quantifies the **free fatty acid (FFA) content** and reflects the **degradation state** of the fuel during storage. High AV causes corrosion of fuel pump and injector components.

**Iodine Value (IV):** Determined by the Wijs method. The fuel is treated with ICl (Wijs reagent), and the unreacted halogen is titrated with sodium thiosulfate. Equation:

$$
\text{IV (g I₂/100 g)} = \frac{126.9 \cdot (V_b - V_s) \cdot N}{m}
$$

where $V_b$ and $V_s$ are the volumes of thiosulfate for blank and sample, and 126.9 is the atomic weight of iodine. Significance: IV quantifies the **total degree of unsaturation** in the fatty acid chains. High IV indicates susceptibility to **oxidation and polymerization**, leading to engine deposits and filter plugging.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** What is the importance of **oxidation stability** of biodiesel? Explain the **Rancimat method** and the chemical mechanism of biodiesel oxidation. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A biodiesel fuel is blended with petro-diesel in the ratio **B20 (20% biodiesel, 80% petro-diesel by volume)**. The density of biodiesel is 880 kg/m³ and the density of petro-diesel is 840 kg/m³. Calculate the **density of the B20 blend**. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Importance of Oxidation Stability:** Biodiesel undergoes oxidative degradation much more rapidly than petro-diesel because of its **unsaturated fatty acid chains** (C=C double bonds). Oxidation produces **peroxides, aldehydes, acids, and polymeric gums** which: (i) increase the acid value, (ii) cause fuel filter plugging, (iii) corrode metallic components, and (iv) form engine deposits.

**Rancimat Method (EN 14112):** A 7.5 g biodiesel sample is heated to **110 °C** in a reaction vessel while **air is bubbled** through it at a controlled flow rate (10 L/h). The volatile oxidation products (mainly formic acid) are carried by the air stream into a **measuring vessel containing deionized water**, where the conductivity is continuously monitored. The **Rancimat Induction Period (IP)** is defined as the time (in hours) elapsed until the conductivity curve exhibits its inflection point, indicating rapid secondary oxidation. The EN 14214 minimum is **8 hours**.

**Chemical Mechanism (simplified):**

$$
\text{RH} \longrightarrow \text{R}^\bullet + \text{H}^\bullet \quad (\text{Initiation})
$$

$$
\text{R}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{ROO}^\bullet \quad (\text{Propagation})
$$

$$
\text{ROO}^\bullet + \text{RH} \longrightarrow \text{ROOH} + \text{R}^\bullet \quad (\text{Propagation})
$$

$$
\text{ROOH} \longrightarrow \text{RO}^\bullet + \text{}^\bullet\text{OH} \quad (\text{Branching})
$$

$$
\text{ROO}^\bullet + \text{ROO}^\bullet \longrightarrow \text{Stable products} \quad (\text{Termination})
$$

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Step 1 – State the volumetric blend composition.**

Volume of biodiesel in 1 m³ of blend = 0.20 m³.  
Volume of petro-diesel in 1 m³ of blend = 0.80 m³.

**Step 2 – Compute the mass of each component.**

Mass of biodiesel:

$$
m_b = V_b \cdot \rho_b = 0.20 \times 880 = 176.0 \text{ kg}
$$

Mass of petro-diesel:

$$
m_d = V_d \cdot \rho_d = 0.80 \times 840 = 672.0 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 – Compute total mass of the blend.**

$$
m_{total} = 176.0 + 672.0 = 848.0 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 – Compute the total volume of the blend.**

For **ideal mixing** (no volume change on blending), $V_{total} = 1.0 \text{ m}^3$.

**Step 5 – Compute the density of the blend.**

$$
\rho_{B20} = \frac{m_{total}}{V_{total}} = \frac{848.0}{1.0} = 848.0 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho_{B20} \approx 848 \text{ kg/m}^3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical check:** The blend density 848 kg/m³ lies between 840 (petro-diesel) and 880 (biodiesel), closer to 840 because 80% of the volume is petro-diesel. The result is realistic and passes ASTM D975 limits for diesel fuel.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation (Module 2 – Biodiesel Properties):**
> 1. **Units are not written.** Always write mm²/s, MJ/kg, mg KOH/g, etc. Losing units loses **0.5–1 mark** per sub-question.
> 2. **Mixing up CFPP and PP.** Remember: $T_{CFPP} \geq T_{CP} \geq T_{PP}$. Reversing this order will attract a full sub-question mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing Acid Value and Iodine Value titrations.** AV uses **KOH titrant**; IV uses **sodium thiosulfate titrant** (after Wijs reagent). Swapping these is a frequent error.
> 4. **Forgetting to report the standard reference.** The examiner expects the student to mention **EN 14214 / ASTM D6751** limits where applicable.
> 5. **Reporting only the formula but skipping the substitution step.** KTU valuation keys allocate marks for **explicit numerical substitution**, not just the symbolic equation.
> 6. **Forgetting to balance the combustion equation** when computing AFR. The CO₂ and H₂O coefficients must be derived, not assumed.
> 7. **Writing viscosity and density interchangeably.** They are **not the same**: viscosity is resistance to flow (Pa·s or mm²/s); density is mass per unit volume (kg/m³).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Properties of Biodiesel (Module 2):**
> - **Biodiesel = FAME**, the mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids, produced by transesterification.
> - **Standards:** EN 14214 (Europe, most stringent), ASTM D6751 (USA), IS 15607 (India).
> - **Combustion Properties:** Cetane number (≥ 51, biodiesel ≈ 50–60), calorific value (≈ 37–40 MJ/kg, 8–12% lower than diesel), flash point (≥ 120 °C, very safe to handle), density (860–900 kg/m³).
> - **Flow Properties:** Kinematic viscosity (3.5–5.0 mm²/s at 40 °C), Cloud Point, Pour Point, CFPP. Saturated feedstocks (palm) have **poor** cold flow; unsaturated feedstocks (soybean, canola) are better.
> - **Stability Properties:** Oxidation stability (Rancimat IP ≥ 8 h at 110 °C), Acid Value (≤ 0.50 mg KOH/g), Iodine Value (≤ 120 g I₂/100 g).
> - **Contaminant Properties:** Water ≤ 500 mg/kg, Sulfur ≤ 10 mg/kg, Ash ≤ 0.02%, Carbon Residue ≤ 0.05%, Free Glycerin ≤ 0.02%, Total Glycerin ≤ 0.25%, Methanol ≤ 0.20%.
> - **Key equations to memorize:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$, $CN = \sum x_i \cdot CN_i$, $\text{AV} = 56.1 \cdot V \cdot N / m$, $\text{IV} = 126.9 \cdot (V_b - V_s) \cdot N / m$, $\text{AFR} = a \cdot MW_{air}/MW_{fuel}$.
> - **Transesterification** reduces viscosity by ~80%, increases volatility, raises flash point, and shifts the property fingerprint from "thick oil" to "engine-ready fuel".
> - **Biodiesel is miscible with petro-diesel in all proportions** (B2, B5, B20, B100).
> - **Stoichiometric AFR of biodiesel ≈ 12.5**, lower than diesel (~14.6) due to the **inherent oxygen content (~10–11 wt%)** in FAME molecules.
> - Always report property values **with units** and **compare with the relevant standard (EN 14214 / ASTM D6751)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacture of biodiesel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Manufacture of biodiesel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — MANUFACTURE OF BIODIESEL

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biodiesel** is formally defined as a mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids (commonly fatty acid methyl esters, **FAME**) derived from a renewable lipid feedstock such as vegetable oils, animal fats, or used cooking oil, produced through a chemical reaction called **transesterification** in which the triglyceride molecules of the feedstock are reacted with a short-chain alcohol (typically methanol or ethanol) in the presence of a catalyst.

According to the **American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM D6751)** and the **European Committee for Standardization (EN 14214)**, biodiesel is specified as a fuel composed of mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids derived from vegetable oils or animal fats, designated **B100**, and meeting the stringent purity, viscosity, flash point, and cetane number criteria outlined in these standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition for Examination:**
> "Biodiesel is the mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids derived from vegetable oils, animal fats, or microbial lipids through transesterification, conforming to ASTM D6751 (USA) or EN 14214 (Europe) fuel quality standards."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reverse Soap-Making" Intuition

To intuitively grasp biodiesel manufacture, imagine the chemistry of **soap-making**. When vegetable oil is boiled with a strong alkali (NaOH), the triglyceride breaks apart and forms soap (the sodium salt of the fatty acid) plus glycerol. Biodiesel manufacture is essentially the **same chemistry, but running in reverse**:

- In **soap-making**, the fatty acid chain ends up attached to a **sodium ion** (Na⁺) — producing soap that is solid at room temperature.
- In **biodiesel manufacture**, the same fatty acid chain is instead attached to a **methyl group (–CH₃)** from methanol — producing a liquid fuel that burns cleanly in a diesel engine.

$$ \text{Triglyceride (oil)} + \text{NaOH (alkali)} \xrightarrow{\text{soap-making}} \text{Soap} + \text{Glycerol} $$

$$ \text{Triglyceride (oil)} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH (methanol)} \xrightarrow{\text{biodiesel}} \text{FAME (biodiesel)} + \text{Glycerol} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy works for KTU viva:** A student who clearly explains that biodiesel manufacture is "oil + alcohol + catalyst → fuel ester + glycerol" — and that the ester is what we burn in a CI engine — has grasped the **core idea** behind the entire Module 2 syllabus.

### 1.3 Standard Quality Metrics

The following metrics are **universally cited** in any biodiesel specification:

| Property | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Density (at 15 °C) | kg/m³ | Energy content per litre |
| Kinematic Viscosity (at 40 °C) | mm²/s | Atomization quality in injectors |
| Cetane Number | — | Ignition quality (higher = better) |
| Flash Point | °C | Storage and transport safety |
| Acid Value | mg KOH/g | Corrosion indicator |
| Iodine Value | g I₂/100g | Degree of unsaturation |
| Moisture Content | mg/kg | Hydrolysis risk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exams, the examiner will expect the student to state that:** Biodiesel typically has a density of **~880 kg/m³**, viscosity of **3.5–5.0 mm²/s**, flash point **>130 °C** (much higher than petrodiesel), and a cetane number of **45–60** (petrodiesel is ~45–55).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of Transesterification — The Core Reaction

Transesterification is a **three-step, reversible, equilibrium-controlled** reaction. In each step, one fatty acid is liberated from the glycerol backbone and replaced by a methyl group from methanol. The overall stoichiometry shows that **1 mole of triglyceride reacts with 3 moles of methanol** to yield **3 moles of fatty acid methyl ester (FAME)** and **1 mole of glycerol (glycerin)**.

$$ \text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightleftharpoons 3\,\text{RCOOCH}_3 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 $$

Where:
- **Triglyceride** = the oil/fat molecule (e.g., triolein, C₅₇H₁₀₄O₆)
- **CH₃OH** = methanol (the most common alcohol used industrially)
- **RCOOCH₃** = fatty acid methyl ester (FAME = the actual biodiesel)
- **C₃H₅(OH)₃** = glycerol (a high-value byproduct)

### 2.2 The Three Sequential Steps

1. **Step 1 — Triglyceride → Diglyceride:** The triglyceride reacts with one methanol molecule to release the first FAME and form a diglyceride.
2. **Step 2 — Diglyceride → Monoglyceride:** The diglyceride reacts with a second methanol molecule to release the second FAME and form a monoglyceride.
3. **Step 3 — Monoglyceride → Glycerol:** The monoglyceride reacts with the third methanol molecule to release the third FAME and form glycerol.

Because each step is **reversible**, the overall reaction can stall at any intermediate stage if conditions are not optimized — this is why the choice of catalyst, temperature, and molar ratio is critical.

## 2.3 Why a Catalyst? — The Energy-Barrier Concept

A triglyceride and methanol are mutually immiscible (oil and water/alcohol do not mix). Without a catalyst, the reaction rate is **negligibly slow** even at elevated temperatures. The catalyst serves two simultaneous purposes:

- **Activates the carbonyl carbon** of the triglyceride by making it more electrophilic, allowing the methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻) to attack it.
- **Increases mutual solubility** by acting as an interfacial emulsifier.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A homogeneous alkali catalyst (e.g., NaOH or KOH) gives **fast reaction kinetics (~1 hour at 60 °C)** but is sensitive to free fatty acids (FFAs) and water. An acid catalyst (e.g., H₂SO₄) is **slower (~4 hours)** but tolerant of FFAs. This trade-off is the **single most-tested concept** in Part A questions.

## 2.4 Classification of Catalysts

| Catalyst Class | Examples | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Homogeneous Alkali** | NaOH, KOH, NaOCH₃ | Fast, mild temperature, high yield (>95 %) | Soap formation with FFAs, water-sensitive |
| **Homogeneous Acid** | H₂SO₄, HCl, H₃PO₄ | Tolerant of high FFA feedstocks | Slow, corrosive, requires higher temperature |
| **Heterogeneous (Solid)** | CaO, MgO, hydrotalcite, zeolites | Reusable, easier separation, no soap | Slower kinetics, mass-transfer limitations |
| **Enzymatic (Lipase)** | Candida antarctica, Rhizomucor miehei | Mild, FFA-tolerant, no soap | Expensive, slow, enzyme deactivation |
| **Supercritical (Non-catalytic)** | No catalyst; methanol at >240 °C, >80 bar | Very fast, tolerant of FFAs and water | Very high pressure equipment, costly |

## 2.5 Critical Process Parameters

The four process variables most frequently tested in KTU numerical/short-answer questions:

1. **Molar Ratio of Alcohol to Oil:** Stoichiometry requires 3:1, but in practice **6:1 to 12:1** is used to drive the equilibrium forward. Excess methanol is recovered by distillation and reused.
2. **Reaction Temperature:** Generally **50–65 °C** for alkali catalysts (above the boiling point of methanol would cause pressure build-up). For supercritical processes, **240–350 °C**.
3. **Reaction Time:** Typically **1–4 hours** for alkali catalysts; **0.5–2 hours** for supercritical.
4. **Catalyst Loading:** **0.5–1.5 % w/w** of oil for alkali catalysts. Above this, soap formation dominates and yield drops.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following five equations — they are the **most frequently tested** numerical framework for any biodiesel manufacture problem in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbols & Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric Molar Ratio = $3:1$ (alcohol:oil) | moles CH₃OH : moles triglyceride | Required by chemistry; actual industrial use is 6:1 to 12:1 |
| 2 | Actual Molar Ratio = $\dfrac{n_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}}}{n_{\text{oil}}}$ | dimensionless | Always should be $\geq 3$; optimum is ~6:1 |
| 3 | Conversion $(\%) = \dfrac{n_{\text{oil,reacted}}}{n_{\text{oil,initial}}} \times 100$ | percent | Based on triglyceride consumption |
| 4 | Yield $(\%) = \dfrac{m_{\text{biodiesel,obtained}}}{m_{\text{biodiesel,theoretical}}} \times 100$ | percent | Based on mass of FAME produced |
| 5 | Theoretical Mass of Biodiesel = $n_{\text{oil}} \times 3 \times M_{\text{FAME}}$ | kg | 1 mol oil → 3 mol FAME |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The same formulae are used in real-world **process design, mass-balance simulation (Aspen Plus), and economic feasibility studies** for biodiesel refineries. The 6:1 methanol ratio is a global industrial standard adopted because it minimizes glycerol contamination while keeping methanol recovery cost reasonable.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Balanced Overall Reaction — Complete Derivation

Starting from the molecular structure of a generic triglyceride (R₁, R₂, R₃ are the three fatty-acid alkyl chains), the transesterification can be written as:

$$ \begin{aligned} \text{CH}_2\text{OOC–R}_1 \\ \mid \\ \text{CHOO–R}_2 \\ \mid \\ \text{CH}_2\text{OOC–R}_3 \end{aligned} \quad + \quad 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \quad \rightleftharpoons \quad \begin{aligned} \text{R}_1\text{COOCH}_3 \\ + \\ \text{R}_2\text{COOCH}_3 \\ + \\ \text{R}_3\text{COOCH}_3 \end{aligned} \quad + \quad \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 $$

**Step 1 — Triglyceride → Diglyceride:**
The methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻), generated by dissociation of the catalyst, attacks the carbonyl carbon of one fatty acid chain.

$$ \text{Triglyceride} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightleftharpoons \text{Diglyceride} + \text{R}_1\text{COOCH}_3 $$

**Step 2 — Diglyceride → Monoglyceride:**
The methoxide attacks a second carbonyl carbon.

$$ \text{Diglyceride} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightleftharpoons \text{Monoglyceride} + \text{R}_2\text{COOCH}_3 $$

**Step 3 — Monoglyceride → Glycerol:**
The methoxide attacks the final carbonyl carbon.

$$ \text{Monoglyceride} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightleftharpoons \text{Glycerol} + \text{R}_3\text{COOCH}_3 $$

**Net Reaction (sum of the three steps):**

$$ \text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightleftharpoons 3\,\text{RCOOCH}_3 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 $$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Yield & Molar-Ratio Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Pattern:** A typical Part A or short Part B sub-question gives feedstock mass, alcohol mass, catalyst %, and asks for: (i) molar ratio, (ii) theoretical yield, (iii) actual % yield. This exact problem type appears almost every semester.

**Given:**
- Soybean oil feed = **1000 kg**
- Molecular weight of soybean oil (assumed triolein, C₅₇H₁₀₄O₆) = **885 g/mol**
- Methanol added = **200 kg**
- Molecular weight of methanol (CH₃OH) = **32 g/mol**
- Catalyst (NaOH) = **1 % w/w of oil** = 10 kg
- Actual biodiesel (FAME, methyl oleate C₁₉H₃₆O₂, MW = 296.5 g/mol) produced = **950 kg**

**Step 1 — Moles of oil:**

$$ n_{\text{oil}} = \frac{m_{\text{oil}}}{M_{\text{oil}}} = \frac{1000 \times 1000\;\text{g}}{885\;\text{g/mol}} = 1129.94\;\text{mol} $$

**Step 2 — Moles of methanol:**

$$ n_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}} = \frac{m_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}}}{M_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}}} = \frac{200 \times 1000\;\text{g}}{32\;\text{g/mol}} = 6250.00\;\text{mol} $$

**Step 3 — Actual molar ratio (methanol : oil):**

$$ \text{Molar Ratio} = \frac{n_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}}}{n_{\text{oil}}} = \frac{6250.00}{1129.94} = 5.53 \approx 5.5:1 $$

**Comment for the examiner:** The ratio 5.5:1 is well above the stoichiometric 3:1 requirement and within the industrially recommended range of 6:1 — the slight shortfall from 6:1 will marginally reduce conversion, justifying the higher-than-stoichiometric industrial practice.

**Step 4 — Theoretical mass of biodiesel:**

$$ \begin{aligned} m_{\text{FAME,theo}} &= n_{\text{oil}} \times 3 \times M_{\text{FAME}} \\ &= 1129.94 \times 3 \times 296.5\;\text{g} \\ &= 1005176.55\;\text{g} \\ &= 1005.18\;\text{kg} \end{aligned} $$

**Step 5 — Actual yield in percent:**

$$ \text{Yield}\,(\%) = \frac{m_{\text{FAME,actual}}}{m_{\text{FAME,theo}}} \times 100 = \frac{950}{1005.18} \times 100 = 94.51\,\% $$

**Step 6 — Mass of glycerol (byproduct) recovered theoretically:**

$$ \begin{aligned} m_{\text{glycerol,theo}} &= n_{\text{oil}} \times 1 \times M_{\text{glycerol}} \\ &= 1129.94 \times 1 \times 92\;\text{g} \\ &= 103954.48\;\text{g} \\ &= 103.95\;\text{kg} \end{aligned} $$

Where M(glycerol, C₃H₈O₃) = **92 g/mol**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world interpretation:** A 94.51 % yield is **excellent** for alkali-catalysed transesterification. The 5.49 % shortfall typically comprises unreacted monoglycerides, residual triglycerides, and FAME lost in the glycerol phase. Modern refineries employing reactive distillation or supercritical methanol push yields above **99 %**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Catalyst Selection

| Feedstock Condition | Water Content | FFA Content | Recommended Catalyst Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| Refined vegetable oil | <0.05 % | <0.5 % | Direct **alkali catalysis (NaOH/KOH)** |
| Crude vegetable oil | <0.1 % | 0.5 – 3 % | **Acid pre-esterification** → alkali step (two-stage) |
| Waste cooking oil / animal fat | 0.1 – 2 % | 3 – 10 % | Two-stage: H₂SO₄ → NaOH |
| High-FFA grease / tallow | >2 % | >10 % | **Acid catalysis** alone, or supercritical route |
| Soapstock / very high FFA | >2 % | >15 % | **Supercritical methanol** (no catalyst needed) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Three-Step Transesterification Reaction Pathway

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid block diagram maps the three sequential reaction steps. Memorize this flow for any KTU short-note or descriptive question worth 3 marks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Triglyceride plus Methanol] --> B[Reversible Reaction under Catalyst]
    B --> C[Intermediate: Diglyceride]
    C --> D[Step 2: Diglyceride plus Methanol]
    D --> E[Reversible Reaction]
    E --> F[Intermediate: Monoglyceride]
    F --> G[Step 3: Monoglyceride plus Methanol]
    G --> H[Reversible Reaction]
    H --> I[Final Products: 3 FAME plus Glycerol]
```

## 4.2 Process Block Diagram — Industrial Biodiesel Manufacture

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram represents the **complete industrial workflow** for biodiesel production from refined vegetable oil using alkali catalysis. Each block corresponds to a unit operation in a real-world biodiesel refinery.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feedstock Storage Tank] --> B[Oil Pretreatment: Degumming and Neutralization]
    B --> C[Dehydration: Vacuum Drying below 0.05 percent moisture]
    C --> D[Reactor Feed Stream: Oil plus Methanol plus NaOH Catalyst]
```
