Bio-oil 500 C] F --> F1[Transesterification
Oil plus Methanol] F1 --> F2[Biodiesel FAME] F1 --> F3[Glycerol Byproduct] D5 --> G[End Use: Heat, Power, Fuel] E2 --> G E3 --> G E4 --> G F2 --> G ``` --- ## 4.2 Four-Stage Anaerobic Digestion Pathway (Functional Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR feed1[Complex Biomass
Carbohydrates, Proteins, Lipids] --> stage1 stage1[STAGE 1 Hydrolysis
Extracellular Enzymes] --> inter1 inter1[Monomers Sugars Amino Acids Fatty Acids] --> stage2 stage2[STAGE 2 Acidogenesis
Acidogenic Bacteria] --> inter2 inter2[VFAs Alcohols H2 CO2] --> stage3 stage3[STAGE 3 Acetogenesis
Acetogenic Bacteria] --> inter3 inter3[Acetate H2 CO2] --> stage4 stage4[STAGE 4 Methanogenesis
Archaea] --> prod1 prod1[Biogas CH4 60 percent CO2 40 percent] prod1 --> app1[Domestic Cooking] prod1 --> app2[Electricity Generation] prod1 --> app3[CNG Upgrading] ``` --- ## 4.3 Transesterification Reaction Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD oil[Triglyceride
Vegetable Oil or Animal Fat] --> mix meth[Methanol CH3OH] --> mix cat[Catalyst NaOH or KOH or H2SO4] --> mix mix[Reactor 50 to 65 C
1 to 2 hours Stirring] --> sep sep[Gravity Separation] --> fam sep --> gly fam[FAME Biodiesel
Fatty Acid Methyl Esters] --> wash fam --> dist wash[Water Washing
Remove Soap and Catalyst] --> final1[Biodiesel Product] dist[Drying and Distillation] --> final1 gly[Crude Glycerol
10 percent by mass] --> purify purify[Refining Vacuum Distillation] --> final2[Pharma Glycerol 99.7 percent] ``` --- ## 4.4 Thermochemical Conversion Map (ER-Based Decision Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD biomass[Biomass Feed] --> dry[Drying Moisture less than 15 percent] dry --> size[Size Reduction less than 5 mm] size --> reactor{Thermal Reactor} reactor -->|ER 0 No Oxygen| pyro[PYROLYSIS 300 to 700 C] reactor -->|ER 0.2 to 0.4| gas[GASIFICATION 700 to 900 C] reactor -->|ER greater than 1| comb[COMBUSTION 800 to 1200 C] pyro --> prodA[Bio-oil 60 to 75 percent] pyro --> prodB[Char 15 to 25 percent] pyro --> prodC[Pyrolysis Gas 10 to 20 percent] gas --> syn[Producer Gas Syngas
CO 20 percent H2 15 percent CH4 5 percent N2 50 percent] comb --> heat[Flue Gas Hot
Heat Recovery in Boiler] syn --> ft[Fischer Tropsch Synthesis] syn --> chp[Combined Heat and Power] heat --> steam[Steam Turbine] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Assessment Structure (Reference):** > - **Part A (3 marks each):** Short definitions, one-word answers, simple comparisons. > - **Part B (14 marks total):** Choice-based long answer with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. > - **Cognitive Levels (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy):** Remember (L1), Understand (L2), Apply (L3), Analyze (L4), Evaluate (L5), Create (L6). > - **Module Weightage in ESE:** Module 3 typically carries **20–25%** of total marks. --- ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **Define the term "Equivalence Ratio (ER)" in thermochemical conversion of biomass. State the typical ER range for gasification.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > The **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** is defined as the ratio of the actual amount of oxidizer (air/O₂) supplied during combustion to the stoichiometric amount required for **complete combustion** of the fuel. > > $$\text{ER} = \frac{\text{Actual O}_2 \text{ supplied}}{\text{Stoichiometric O}_2 \text{ required}}$$ > > For **gasification**, the typical ER range is **0.20 – 0.40** (sub-stoichiometric conditions). When ER = 0, the process is **pyrolysis**; when ER ≥ 1, the process is **combustion**. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** **List the four sequential stages of anaerobic digestion (AD) of biomass.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > 1. **Hydrolysis** — Breakdown of complex polymers (cellulose, proteins, lipids) into monomers by extracellular enzymes. > 2. **Acidogenesis** — Conversion of monomers into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂ by acidogenic bacteria. > 3. **Acetogenesis** — Oxidation of VFAs and alcohols into acetic acid, H₂, and CO₂ by acetogenic bacteria. > 4. **Methanogenesis** — Conversion of acetate and H₂/CO₂ into **CH₄ (methane)** and CO₂ by methanogenic archaea. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **(a)** With the help of a balanced chemical equation, explain the principle of **transesterification** for biodiesel production. List the role of each reactant and the typical process conditions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A biomass sample has the following ultimate analysis: C = 42%, H = 5%, O = 35%, N = 1.5%, S = 0.5%, Ash = 16%. Calculate the **stoichiometric air required** for complete combustion of 1 kg of this biomass. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### **Part (a) — Transesterification Principle (7 marks)** **Definition (1 mark):** Transesterification is the chemical reaction in which the alkoxy group of an ester (triglyceride) is exchanged with the alkoxy group of an alcohol in the presence of a catalyst, producing **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME)** — also called biodiesel — and glycerol as a byproduct. **Balanced Equation (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} &\xrightarrow{\text{NaOH / KOH, 60 C}} 3\,\text{R-COOCH}_3 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 \\ \text{(Oil)} \quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(Methanol)} &\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(FAME Biodiesel)} \quad\quad \text{(Glycerol)} \end{aligned} $$ **Roles of Reactants & Conditions (3 marks):** - **Triglyceride (oil/fat):** Source of fatty acid chains; usually refined vegetable oil (soybean, palm, rapeseed, jatropha) or animal tallow. - **Methanol:** Low-cost, short-chain alcohol that displaces glycerol; ethanol can also be used (then product is FAEE). - **Catalyst (NaOH / KOH):** Alkali catalyst speeds up reaction at low temperature; acid catalysts (H₂SO₄) used for high FFA feedstock. **Enzymatic (lipase)** catalysts are emerging. - **Molar ratio (methanol : oil):** Typically **6 : 1** (stoichiometric is 3 : 1; excess alcohol shifts equilibrium forward). - **Temperature:** **50–65 °C** (just below methanol boiling point of 65 °C). - **Reaction time:** **1–2 hours** with vigorous stirring. - **Byproducts:** **Glycerol (~10% by mass)** — refined for pharmaceutical / cosmetic use. --- #### **Part (b) — Stoichiometric Air Calculation (7 marks)** **Given:** C = 42%, H = 5%, O = 35%, N = 1.5%, S = 0.5%, Ash = 16% (all by mass, per kg fuel). **Step 1 — Calculate O₂ required for each combustible element (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{O}_2 \text{ for C} &= 0.42 \times \frac{32}{12} = 0.42 \times 2.667 = 1.120 \text{ kg} \\ \text{O}_2 \text{ for H} &= 0.05 \times \frac{32}{4} = 0.05 \times 8.000 = 0.400 \text{ kg} \\ \text{O}_2 \text{ for S} &= 0.005 \times \frac{32}{32} = 0.005 \times 1.000 = 0.005 \text{ kg} \\ \hline \text{Total O}_2 \text{ required} &= 1.120 + 0.400 + 0.005 = 1.525 \text{ kg O}_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Subtract intrinsic O₂ from fuel (1 mark):** $$ \text{Net O}_2 \text{ needed} = 1.525 - 0.35 = 1.175 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg fuel} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to air mass (2 marks):** $$ \text{Stoichiometric air} = \frac{1.175}{0.23} = 5.109 \text{ kg air / kg fuel} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to volumetric flow at STP (2 marks):** At STP, 1 kg of air ≈ 0.849 Nm³. Therefore: $$ \text{Stoichiometric air volume} = 5.109 \times 0.849 = 4.337 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ air / kg fuel} $$ > **Final Answer:** **5.11 kg air/kg fuel (≈ 4.34 Nm³/kg)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** **(a)** Explain the **four stages of anaerobic digestion** with balanced equations for the methanogenesis step. State the optimum temperature and pH conditions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Define **Higher Heating Value (HHV)**. Using **Dulong's formula**, compute the HHV of a biomass with: C = 48%, H = 6.5%, O = 40%, N = 1%, S = 0.5%, Ash = 4%. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question B** #### **Part (a) — Four Stages of AD (7 marks)** **Stage 1 — Hydrolysis (1 mark):** Complex biopolymers (cellulose, hemicellulose, proteins, lipids) are hydrolyzed by extracellular enzymes into soluble monomers: sugars, amino acids, long-chain fatty acids. $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{cellulase}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 $$ **Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (1 mark):** Acidogenic bacteria (*Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*) convert sugars and amino acids into VFAs (acetic, propionic, butyric acid), alcohols, H₂, CO₂. $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2 $$ **Stage 3 — Acetogenesis (1 mark):** Acetogenic bacteria convert VFAs into acetate, H₂, CO₂. $$ \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOH} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2 $$ **Stage 4 — Methanogenesis (2 marks):** Methanogenic archaea produce methane via two main pathways: **Acetoclastic pathway (dominant, ~70%):** $$ \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2 $$ **Hydrogenotrophic pathway (~30%):** $$ \text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Optimum Conditions (2 marks):** - **Temperature:** **Mesophilic: 30–40 °C** (most common) OR **Thermophilic: 50–55 °C** (faster kinetics, less stable). - **pH:** **6.8 – 7.4** (neutral; methanogens are highly pH-sensitive). - **C:N ratio:** **20:1 – 30:1** (optimal for microbial growth). - **HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time):** **15–30 days** for mesophilic digesters. --- #### **Part (b) — Dulong's Formula Calculation (7 marks)** **Definition (1 mark):** **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** is the total heat released when 1 kg of fuel is completely combusted and the products (including water vapour) are cooled to the original temperature, with water condensed back to liquid. **Dulong's Empirical Formula (2 marks):** $$ \text{HHV} = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,A $$ where C, H, S, O, N, A are mass percentages of carbon, hydrogen, sulphur, oxygen, nitrogen, and ash respectively, and HHV is in **MJ/kg**. **Substitution (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{HHV} &= (0.3491 \times 48) + (1.1783 \times 6.5) + (0.1005 \times 0.5) \\ &\quad - (0.1034 \times 40) - (0.0151 \times 1) - (0.0211 \times 4) \\ &= 16.757 + 7.659 + 0.050 - 4.136 - 0.015 - 0.084 \\ &= 20.231 \text{ MJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ > **Final Answer:** **HHV ≈ 20.23 MJ/kg** **Engineering Interpretation (1 mark):** This HHV is typical of **woody biomass and crop residues** (range: 17–22 MJ/kg), making it suitable for direct combustion, gasification, and co-firing in industrial boilers. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Module 3:** > > 1. **Unbalanced Equations** — Writing "C + O₂ → CO₂" without coefficient 1 explicitly is acceptable, but for **Gay-Lussac fermentation**, students *forget the 2:2:2 ratio* → lose 1 mark. > 2. **Forgetting to Subtract Intrinsic Oxygen** — In stoichiometric air calculation, students compute total O₂ demand but skip the "− O₂ in fuel" step. This is a **2-mark deduction**. > 3. **Wrong Units in Dulong's Formula** — HHV output is in **MJ/kg**, not kcal/kg or kJ/kg. Using a wrong conversion factor loses 1 mark. > 4. **Confusing ER Values** — Students mix up *pyrolysis (ER=0)*, *gasification (ER<1)*, and *combustion (ER≥1)*. State all three in your answer for safety. > 5. **Omitting State Symbols** — KTU examiners often expect **(g), (l), (s), (aq)** annotations. Missing them = minor deduction (~0.5 marks). > 6. **No Diagram in Long Answer** — Even in 14-mark questions, a simple **flowchart/block diagram** earns 1–2 "presentation marks." Always draw one. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist — Basic Principles (Module 3)** ### ✅ Biochemical Conversion - [ ] **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** has **4 stages**: Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis. - [ ] **Methanogens** are *archaea*, not bacteria; they are highly sensitive to pH, temperature, and toxic compounds (NH₃, H₂S). - [ ] **Acetoclastic methanogenesis** accounts for ~70% of CH₄; **hydrogenotrophic** ~30%. - [ ] **Fermentation equation (Gay-Lussac):** C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂; theoretical yield = **0.51 g ethanol / g glucose**. - [ ] **Calorific value of biogas:** ~55.5 MJ/kg for pure CH₄; biogas is ~60% CH₄ → CV ≈ **20–25 MJ/Nm³**. ### ✅ Thermochemical Conversion - [ ] **Combustion:** ER ≥ 1, complete oxidation → heat, CO₂, H₂O. - [ ] **Gasification:** ER = 0.2–0.4, sub-stoichiometric → **syngas (CO + H₂)**. - [ ] **Pyrolysis:** ER = 0, no oxygen → **bio-oil + char + gas**; *fast pyrolysis* favors oil. - [ ] **Water-Gas Shift Reaction (WGSR):** CO + H₂O ⇌ CO₂ + H₂ — critical for adjusting syngas H₂/CO ratio. - [ ] **Sabatier reaction:** CO₂ + 4 H₂ → CH₄ + 2 H₂O — used in synthetic natural gas (SNG) production. ### ✅ Chemical Conversion - [ ] **Transesterification:** Triglyceride + 3 CH₃OH → 3 FAME + Glycerol. - [ ] **Methanol:oil molar ratio** = 6:1 typical (stoichiometric 3:1). - [ ] **Catalyst types:** Alkali (NaOH/KOH, fast), Acid (H₂SO₄, for high FFA), Enzymatic (lipase, green). - [ ] **Biodiesel yield** = 90–98% under optimized conditions; glycerol = ~10% by mass. ### ✅ Universal Tools - [ ] **Stoichiometric air** = (Total O₂ demand − O₂ in fuel) / 0.23. - [ ] **Dulong's formula** for HHV: HHV = 0.3491 C + 1.1783 H + 0.1005 S − 0.1034 O − 0.0151 N − 0.0211 Ash (MJ/kg). - [ ] **ER** is the **master control variable** in thermochemical conversion — always quote its value range in your answer. ### ✅ Numerical Constants to Memorize - [ ] **Molecular weights:** C₆H₁₂O₆ = 180, C₂H₅OH = 46, CH₄ = 16, CH₃OH = 32, O₂ = 32, N₂ = 28. - [ ] **Calorific values:** CH₄ = 55.5 MJ/kg; Biodiesel ≈ 38 MJ/kg; Bioethanol ≈ 27 MJ/kg; Dry biomass ≈ 17–20 MJ/kg. - [ ] **Air composition:** 23% O₂, 77% N₂ (by mass); 21% O₂, 79% N₂ (by volume). - [ ] **1 kg air at STP ≈ 0.849 Nm³.**

topics.title: Biohydrogen production processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Biohydrogen production processes # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Biohydrogen (Bio-H₂)** is defined as molecular hydrogen (H₂) produced through biological processes employing microorganisms (bacteria, cyanobacteria, or microalgae) and biologically active substrates (organic wastes, water, biomass) under ambient or near-ambient temperature and pressure conditions, offering a sustainable and carbon-neutral alternative to fossil-derived hydrogen. Biohydrogen production processes constitute a specialized branch of **dark fermentation biotechnology** and **photobiological energy conversion**, where organic substrates or water are enzymatically converted into H₂ gas through the catalytic action of **hydrogenase** and **nitrogenase** enzymes, without the requirement for high-temperature, high-pressure reactors used in conventional steam-methane reforming (SMR). ### Standard Process Metrics (KTU 2024 Reference) | Parameter | Standard Value | Units | |---|---|---| | **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** of H₂ | **142 MJ/kg** | MJ per kg | | Energy content (vs. gasoline) | ~3× higher (by mass) | — | | Combustion byproduct | Water (H₂O) only | — | | Flammability range in air | **4 – 75 %** | % v/v | | Auto-ignition temperature | **500 °C** | °C | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike conventional hydrogen, biohydrogen is produced *without* consuming fossil reserves and *without* net CO₂ emissions when biomass-derived substrates are used. This makes it central to the **"Hydrogen Economy"** framework emphasized in PEBBT413. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Think of biohydrogen production as a **"biological kitchen"** where tiny microorganisms (chiefly bacteria and algae) are the "chefs." Just as a chef uses raw ingredients (substrates like glucose, wastewater, or sunlight) to cook a dish (hydrogen gas), these microorganisms use their internal enzymes (the "kitchen tools") to break down complex organic molecules or split water molecules to release H₂. There are **two types of chefs** in this analogy: 1. **The "Solar Chef"** 🌞 — Phototrophic organisms (green algae, cyanobacteria) use sunlight to split water, just as a chef uses solar-heated pans. *(Direct biophotolysis)* 2. **The "Dark Kitchen Chef"** 🌑 — Fermentative bacteria work in the absence of light on organic waste, like a chef cooking in a closed kitchen using stored ingredients. *(Dark fermentation)* > [!TIP] > **Real-World Connection:** The "dark fermentation chef" is actually being deployed in real wastewater treatment plants in countries like Japan, Germany, and India, where sewage sludge is converted simultaneously into clean water AND hydrogen fuel — a perfect **dual-purpose bioenergy system**. ### Why Biohydrogen Matters (Engineering Perspective) - **Carbon-neutral**: Combustion releases only H₂O (no CO₂, NOₓ, SOₓ). - **Renewable feedstock**: Uses agricultural waste, food waste, municipal solid waste, and wastewater. - **High energy density**: HHV ≈ **142 MJ/kg**, nearly 3× that of gasoline. - **Mild operating conditions**: 25–60 °C and atmospheric pressure, drastically reducing CAPEX and energy penalty. - **Decentralized production**: Can be set up at site of waste generation (e.g., dairy farms, breweries). # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Classification of Biohydrogen Production Processes The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies biohydrogen production into **four primary process categories**: ### Process Family 1: Direct Biophotolysis (DBP) - **Organisms**: Green microalgae (*Chlamydomonas reinhardtii*, *Chlorella vulgaris*, *Scenedesmus obliquus*) - **Energy source**: Solar photons - **Substrate**: H₂O - **Enzyme**: Hydrogenase (particularly [Fe-Fe] hydrogenase) - **Temperature**: 25–35 °C - **Net reaction**: $$2H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{light, algae}} 2H_2 + O_2$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Limitation**: Direct biophotolysis suffers from the **simultaneous O₂ and H₂ production problem**, which inhibits hydrogenase activity. This is a frequently asked KTU question. ### Process Family 2: Indirect Biophotolysis (IBP) - **Organisms**: Cyanobacteria (*Anabaena*, *Nostoc*, *Spirulina*) - **Strategy**: Spatial/temporal separation of O₂ evolution (photosystem II) and H₂ production (nitrogenase/hydrogenase) - **Two-stage reaction**: $$\text{Stage A (aerobic)}: 12H_2O + 6CO_2 \xrightarrow{\text{light}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$$ $$\text{Stage B (anaerobic)}: C_6H_{12}O_6 + 12H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{light, N_2-ase}} 12H_2 + 6CO_2$$ - **Net cycle**: $6CO_2 + 12H_2O \rightarrow 6O_2 + 12H_2$ *(effectively reverses photosynthesis)* ### Process Family 3: Dark Fermentation (DF) - **Organisms**: Obligate/ facultative anaerobic bacteria — *Clostridium butyricum*, *Clostridium acetobutylicum*, *Enterobacter aerogenes*, *Bacillus subtilis* - **Substrate**: Carbohydrates (glucose, sucrose, starch, cellulose hydrolysates), organic waste - **Light requirement**: NIL — operates in the dark - **Key advantage**: Highest H₂ production rates among all bio-processes; no light limitation - **Operating pH**: 5.0 – 6.5 (acidophilic optimum) - **Temperature**: Mesophilic (30–40 °C) or thermophilic (50–60 °C) ### Process Family 4: Photofermentation (PF) - **Organisms**: Purple non-sulfur (PNS) bacteria — *Rhodobacter sphaeroides*, *Rhodopseudomonas palustris*, *Rhodospirillum rubrum* - **Substrate**: Volatile fatty acids (VFAs) — acetate, butyrate, lactate, malate - **Energy source**: Solar light (anaerobic) - **Enzyme**: Nitrogenase (Mo-Fe or Fe-Fe type) - **Critical limitation**: Nitrogenase is strongly inhibited by NH₄⁺ **Per-acetate stoichiometry:** $$CH_3COOH + 2H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{light, PNS}} 4H_2 + 2CO_2$$ > [!TIP] > **Strategic Insight (high KTU yield):** Dark Fermentation followed by Photofermentation is a **two-stage integrated system** that pushes overall yield close to the theoretical maximum of **12 mol H₂ / mol glucose**. --- ## 2.2 Biohydrogen Yield Metrics — The "KTU Number System" Engineers standardize yields using three metrics: 1. **Molar yield**: $Y_{H_2/S} = \dfrac{n_{H_2}}{n_{\text{substrate consumed}}}$ *(mol H₂ / mol substrate)* 2. **Volumetric productivity**: $P_{H_2} = \dfrac{V_{H_2}}{V_{\text{reactor}} \cdot t}$ *(L H₂ / L · h)* 3. **Conversion efficiency**: $\eta = \dfrac{\Delta G_{H_2}^{comb}}{\Delta G_{\text{substrate}}^{oxidation}} \times 100\%$ --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Memorize these core stoichiometries for Part B derivations.** | Process | Reaction Equation | Yield (mol H₂ / mol substrate) | |---|---|---| | Acetate pathway (DF) | $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2CH_3COOH + 2CO_2 + 4H_2$ | **4** | | Butyrate pathway (DF) | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH + 2CO_2 + 2H_2$ | **2** | | Ethanol pathway (DF) | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2CH_3CH_2OH + 2CO_2 + 2H_2$ | **2** | | Photofermentation (acetate) | $CH_3COOH + 2H_2O \rightarrow 4H_2 + 2CO_2$ | **4** | | Combined DF + PF (max) | $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 12H_2O \rightarrow 12H_2 + 6CO_2$ | **12** | | Direct biophotolysis | $2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$ | ∞ (theoretical) | | Hexane reforming (for comparison) | $C_6H_{14} + 6H_2O \rightarrow 6CO + 13H_2$ | **2.17** | ### Substrate-to-Hydrogen Conversion Formula $$Y_{H_2} = \dfrac{n_{H_2,\,produced} \times M_{H_2}}{m_{substrate}} \quad \left[\dfrac{g\,H_2}{g\,substrate}\right]$$ $$n_{H_2} = \dfrac{V_{H_2,\,STP}}{22.4\,L \cdot mol^{-1}} \quad \text{[moles at STP]}$$ ### Energy Recovery (ER) Formula $$ER\,(\%) = \dfrac{n_{H_2} \times \Delta G_{H_2}^{comb}}{n_{\text{substrate}} \times \Delta G_{\text{substrate}}^{ox}} \times 100$$ Where $\Delta G_{H_2}^{comb} = -237\,kJ/mol$ and for glucose $\Delta G^{ox} = -2840\,kJ/mol$. > Maximum theoretical ER (acetate pathway) = $\dfrac{4 \times 237}{2840} \times 100 \approx \mathbf{33.4\,\%}$. ### McCarty's Modified Gompertz Kinetic Model (commonly tested) $$H(t) = H_{max} \cdot \exp\left\{-\exp\left[\dfrac{R_{max} \cdot e}{H_{max}}(\lambda - t) + 1\right]\right\}$$ | Symbol | Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---| | $H(t)$ | Cumulative H₂ production at time $t$ | mL or mmol | | $H_{max}$ | Maximum H₂ production potential | mL or mmol | | $R_{max}$ | Maximum H₂ production rate | mL/h | | $\lambda$ | Lag phase time | h | | $e$ | Euler's number $\approx 2.718$ | dimensionless | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Engineered System | |---|---| | **Wastewater-to-H₂** | Coupled UASB (Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket) + photofermenter | | **Dairy industry effluent** | Dark fermentation of cheese whey lactose | | **Distillery spent wash** | Thermophilic dark fermentation at 55 °C | | **Food waste biorefineries** | Two-stage acidogenesis → photofermentation | | **Pilot-scale municipal waste** | 100–1000 L CSTR reactors (e.g., IIT Bombay, TERI India pilots) | > [!TIP] > **KTU Context**: India launched the **National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023)** allocating ₹19,744 crore, with biohydrogen as a future pillar. Students should reference this in descriptive answers for marks. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Theoretical Hydrogen Yield from Glucose **Problem Statement:** Derive the maximum possible moles of H₂ obtainable from complete biological oxidation of 1 mole of glucose via the combined dark + photofermentation pathway. ### Step 1 — Write the balanced "complete biological oxidation" equation The goal is to convert every carbon atom in glucose (C₆) into CO₂ and transfer all available electrons (from C–H and O–H bonds) to H⁺ to form H₂. Water is used as the electron (proton) source. $$\begin{aligned} \text{Glucose: } & C_6H_{12}O_6 \\ \text{Oxidation state balance: } & C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6H_2O \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 12H_2 \\ \text{Check carbon: } & 6 \text{ C on each side} \;\checkmark \\ \text{Check hydrogen: } & 12 + 12 = 24 \text{ on left};\ 24 \text{ on right} \;\checkmark \\ \text{Check oxygen: } & 6 + 6 = 12 \text{ on left};\ 12 \text{ on right} \;\checkmark \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 — Electron balance (formal derivation) $$\begin{aligned} & \text{Each C in glucose: average oxidation state} = 0 \\ & \text{Each C in CO}_2: \text{oxidation state} = +4 \\ & \text{Electrons released per C atom} = 4 \\ & \text{Total electrons from 6 C atoms} = 6 \times 4 = 24\,e^{-} \\ & \text{Each H}_2 \text{ molecule requires } 2\,e^{-} \\ & \text{Moles of H}_2 = \dfrac{24}{2} = 12\,mol \\ & \boxed{Y_{H_2}^{theoretical} = 12\;\text{mol H}_2\,/\,\text{mol glucose}} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — Mass-based yield calculation $$\begin{aligned} & m_{H_2} = n_{H_2} \times M_{H_2} = 12 \times 2 = 24\,g\,H_2 \\ & m_{glucose} = 180\,g \\ & Y_{H_2}^{mass} = \dfrac{24\,g\,H_2}{180\,g\,glucose} = 0.133\,\dfrac{g\,H_2}{g\,glucose} \\ & \text{Equivalent in standard units: } Y_{H_2}^{mass} = 0.133\,\dfrac{g}{g} = 13.3\,\% \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Recovery Calculation for Acetate Pathway **Problem Statement:** A glucose-fed CSTR produces 3.5 mol H₂ / mol glucose in steady-state. Compute the energy recovery efficiency relative to the theoretical maximum. ### Step 1 — Define energy recovery (ER) $$\begin{aligned} ER\,(\%) = \dfrac{\text{Actual energy recovered as H}_2}{\text{Total energy available in substrate}} \times 100 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute energy actually recovered $$\begin{aligned} E_{actual} &= n_{H_2,\,actual} \times |\Delta G_{H_2}^{comb}| \\ &= 3.5 \times 237 = 829.5\,kJ \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute total energy available in 1 mol glucose $$\begin{aligned} E_{substrate} &= |\Delta G_{glucose}^{oxidation}| = 2840\,kJ\,/\,mol \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — Compute % recovery $$\begin{aligned} ER\,(\%) &= \dfrac{829.5}{2840} \times 100 \\ &= 29.21\,\% \\ \boxed{ER \approx 29.2\,\%} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5 — Comparison with theoretical maximum $$\begin{aligned} ER_{theoretical,\,acetate} &= \dfrac{4 \times 237}{2840} \times 100 = 33.4\,\% \\ \text{Achievement ratio} &= \dfrac{29.2}{33.4} \times 100 = 87.4\,\% \end{aligned}$$ > This shows that real reactors achieve ~85–90 % of the maximum theoretical yield of the acetate pathway — a high KTU yield indicator. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stoichiometric Product Distribution in Mixed Acid Fermentation **Problem Statement:** A glucose-fed batch reactor (5 L working volume) achieves the following molar product distribution per mol glucose: 0.4 acetate, 0.8 butyrate, 0.3 ethanol. Calculate the total H₂ produced and the ratio of acetate:butyrate pathways. ### Step 1 — H₂ from each pathway $$\begin{aligned} \text{Acetate pathway: } & C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2CH_3COOH + 2CO_2 + 4H_2 \\ & \text{Yield per mol acetate} = 2\,mol\,H_2\,/\,mol\,acetate \\ & H_{2,\,acetate} = 0.4 \times 2 = 0.8\,mol \\ \\ \text{Butyrate pathway: } & C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH + 2CO_2 + 2H_2 \\ & \text{Yield per mol butyrate} = 1\,mol\,H_2\,/\,mol\,butyrate \\ & H_{2,\,butyrate} = 0.8 \times 1 = 0.8\,mol \\ \\ \text{Ethanol pathway: } & C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2CH_3CH_2OH + 2CO_2 + 2H_2 \\ & \text{Yield per mol ethanol} = 1\,mol\,H_2\,/\,mol\,ethanol \\ & H_{2,\,ethanol} = 0.3 \times 1 = 0.3\,mol \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 — Total H₂ yield $$\begin{aligned} n_{H_2,\,total} &= 0.8 + 0.8 + 0.3 = 1.9\,mol\,H_2\,/\,mol\,glucose \\ \boxed{Y_{H_2} = 1.9\;\text{mol H}_2\,/\,\text{mol glucose}} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — Volume of H₂ at STP $$\begin{aligned} V_{H_2,\,STP} &= n \times 22.4 = 1.9 \times 22.4 = 42.56\,L\,H_2\,/\,mol\,glucose \\ V_{H_2,\,reactor} &= 5\,L \times \dfrac{1\,mol\,glucose}{L} \times 42.56 = 212.8\,L\,H_2 \end{aligned}$$ > This is sub-theoretical because in mixed-acid fermentation pyruvate is partially routed to solvents (ethanol) instead of complete oxidation. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Direct Biophotolysis | Indirect Biophotolysis | Dark Fermentation | Photofermentation | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Light required** | Yes (continuous) | Yes (intermittent) | **No** | Yes (low intensity) | | **Substrate** | H₂O | H₂O + CO₂ → glycogen | Carbohydrate, organic waste | VFAs (acetate, butyrate) | | **Organism** | Green algae | Cyanobacteria | Anaerobic bacteria | PNS bacteria | | **O₂ sensitivity** | **Critical issue** | Separated | Not relevant | Strict anaerobic | | **H₂ production rate** | Very low | Low | **Highest** | Moderate | | **Theoretical yield** | ∞ (from water) | ∞ (from water) | **4 mol/mol glucose** | 4 mol/mol acetate | | **Light conversion efficiency (LCE)** | 1–2 % | 1–2 % | N/A | 1–5 % | | **Reactor type** | Open pond, photobioreactor | Tubular PBR | CSTR, UASB, AF | Flat panel PBR | | **Commercial status** | Lab/pilot | Lab/pilot | **Pilot (closest to scale)** | Lab/pilot | > [!TIP] > **Valuation Tip**: Examiners expect students to write 4–6 comparative points in a tabular format for full marks in "compare and contrast" type questions. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Master Classification Tree — Biohydrogen Production Processes ```mermaid flowchart TD Start(["Biohydrogen Production
Processes"]) --> L1{"Light
Required?"} L1 -- Yes --> L2{"Photosynthetic
Organism"} L1 -- No --> L3["Dark Fermentation
DF"] L2 -- Green algae --> P1["Direct Biophotolysis
2H2O → 2H2 + O2"] L2 -- Cyanobacteria --> P2["Indirect Biophotolysis
2-stage: photosynthesis + H2"] L2 -- Purple Non-Sulfur --> P4["Photofermentation
CH3COOH + 2H2O → 4H2 + 2CO2"] P3["Combined DF + PF
12H2/mol glucose"] L3 --> P3 L3 --> P3 classDef pro fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 classDef root fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:3px,color:#1f2937 class Start root class P1,P2,P3,P4 pro ``` --- ## 4.2 Dark Fermentation Process Flow Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR Feed["Substrate Feed
Glucose / Wastewater
(CSTR inlet)"] --> Mixer["Mixing & pH Control
pH 5.5 – 6.5"] Mixer --> Reactor["Anaerobic CSTR
30-60 °C
HRT 4-24 h"] Reactor --> Gas["Biogas Separator
H2 + CO2"] Reactor --> Eff["Effluent
VFAs, Cells"] Gas --> Absorber["H2 Purification
PSA / Membrane"] Absorber --> Storage["Compressed H2 Storage
350 / 700 bar"] Storage --> End["Fuel Cell / Engine
Output: Electricity + H2O"] Eff --> Photo["Photofermenter
Stage 2"] Photo --> Gas2["Additional H2"] Gas2 --> Absorber classDef unit fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 classDef hw fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 class Feed,Mixer,Reactor,Eff,Photo,Gas,Gas2 unit class Absorber,Storage,End hw ``` --- ## 4.3 Pathway Flowchart — Glucose Fermentation Branching ```mermaid flowchart TD Glc["Glucose
C6H12O6"] --> Gly["Glycolysis
2 Pyruvate + 2 NADH + 2 ATP"] Gly --> Fork{"Pyruvate
Fate"} Fork --> Ac["Acetate Pathway
PFOR + Hydrogenase"] Fork --> Bu["Butyrate Pathway
Acetyl-CoA chain"] Fork --> Et["Ethanol Pathway
ADH pathway"] Fork --> La["Lactate Pathway
No H2 released"] Ac --> Out1["2 Acetate
+ 4 H2
+ 2 CO2"] Bu --> Out2["1 Butyrate
+ 2 H2
+ 2 CO2"] Et --> Out3["2 Ethanol
+ 2 H2
+ 2 CO2"] La --> Out4["2 Lactate
+ 0 H2
DEAD END"] Out1 --> Comp["Highest Yield
Selectivity"] Out4 --> Fail["Suppress this
in reactor!"] classDef good fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px classDef bad fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px class Out1,Comp good class Out4,Fail bad ``` --- ## 4.4 Integrated Two-Stage DF + PF System (Schematic Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR W["Waste Biomass
Sugars / Starch"] --> S1["Stage 1: Dark Fermenter
35 °C, pH 5.5
Yield: 4 mol H2/mol"] S1 --> Sp["Solid/Liquid Separator"] Sp --> S2["Stage 2: Photofermenter
PNS bacteria
30 °C, light"] Sp --> Res["Residual solids
(compost / biogas)"] S1 --> H1A["H2 Stream A
~ 4 mol/mol"] S2 --> H1B["H2 Stream B
~ 8 mol/mol"] H1A --> M["Mixing & Purification"] H1B --> M M --> Out["Total: ~ 10-12 mol H2
per mol glucose"] classDef stage fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px classDef prod fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px class W,S1,Sp,S2,Res stage class H1A,H1B,M,Out prod ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Sample]** ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **Q: Differentiate between dark fermentation and photofermentation of biohydrogen production. List one organism used in each process.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Dark Fermentation | Photofermentation | |---|---|---| | Light requirement | **Not required** | Required (low intensity, 2000–5000 lux) | | Organism example | *Clostridium butyricum* | *Rhodobacter sphaeroides* | | Substrate | Carbohydrates (glucose) | VFAs (acetate, butyrate) | | Key enzyme | Hydrogenase | Nitrogenase | | H₂ yield | Up to 4 mol/mol glucose | 4 mol/mol acetate | | Operating condition | Anaerobic, mesophilic | Anaerobic, phototrophic | > **Examiner's Note:** Award full 3 marks if both differences + organism are correctly given; partial marks if only 2 points covered. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q: Write a short note on the maximum theoretical hydrogen yield from glucose and the reasons for low practical yields.** **Model Answer:** - Maximum theoretical yield (acetate pathway): **4 mol H₂/mol glucose** - Combined DF + PF theoretical: **12 mol H₂/mol glucose** - Reasons for low practical yields (typically 1–3 mol/mol): 1. **Thermodynamic feedback inhibition** by accumulated H₂ partial pressure 2. **Shift to solventogenesis** (ethanol, butanol formation) at low pH 3. **Lactate pathway wastage** of pyruvate 4. **Hydrogenase deactivation** by trace O₂ 5. **Substrate competition** with methanogens in mixed cultures --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — PEBBT413, Module 3 Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **dark fermentation process** for biohydrogen production. Discuss the metabolic pathways, key microorganisms, and process parameters affecting yield. **(b) [7 Marks]** A continuous stirred tank reactor (CSTR) of **5 L** working volume is operated with glucose feed at concentration **20 g/L** and dilution rate **0.25 h⁻¹**. The effluent contains 8 g/L residual glucose and the off-gas contains 35 % H₂, 35 % CO₂, and 30 % N₂ by volume at a total flow rate of 12 L/h. Calculate: - (i) Glucose conversion efficiency (2 marks) - (ii) Volumetric H₂ productivity (L H₂/L·h) (2 marks) - (iii) Molar H₂ yield per mole of glucose consumed (3 marks) --- #### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A: **(a) Dark Fermentation Process — Detailed Explanation [7 Marks]** Dark fermentation (DF) is an **anaerobic, light-independent** biological process in which acidogenic/acetogenic bacteria convert carbohydrate-rich substrates into hydrogen, carbon dioxide, and volatile fatty acids. **Key Microorganisms:** - *Clostridium butyricum*, *C. acetobutylicum*, *C. thermocellum* (strict anaerobes) - *Enterobacter aerogenes*, *E. cloacae* (facultative) - Mixed consortia from anaerobic digester sludge **Metabolic Pathway Overview:** Glucose → (Glycolysis) → 2 Pyruvate + 2 NADH → (Pyruvate-ferredoxin oxidoreductase, PFOR) → Acetyl-CoA + CO₂ + reduced ferredoxin (Fd_red) → (Hydrogenase) → H₂ **Process Parameters:** - **Temperature**: Mesophilic 30–40 °C; thermophilic 50–60 °C - **pH**: 5.0–6.5 (acidophilic optimum) - **HRT**: 4–24 hours - **Inoculum pre-treatment**: Heat shock (90 °C, 15 min) to inactivate H₂-consuming methanogens - **Headspace flushing**: N₂ or Ar to maintain anaerobic conditions **Pathway Stoichiometries (write 2 for full marks):** - Acetate pathway: $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2CH_3COOH + 2CO_2 + 4H_2$ - Butyrate pathway: $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH + 2CO_2 + 2H_2$ **Advantages (any 2):** - No light requirement → continuous operation possible - High H₂ production rates - Wide substrate range including waste streams **Disadvantages (any 2):** - Low practical yield (1–3 mol/mol glucose) - Effluent VFAs require secondary treatment - pH drop inhibits process --- **(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]** **Given:** - Reactor volume $V = 5\,L$ - Feed glucose $S_0 = 20\,g/L$ - Effluent glucose $S = 8\,g/L$ - Dilution rate $D = 0.25\,h^{-1}$ - Off-gas flow rate $Q_{gas} = 12\,L/h$ - Off-gas composition: 35 % H₂ **Step 1: Glucose Conversion Efficiency [2 Marks]** $$\begin{aligned} X_{glucose} &= \dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \times 100 \\ &= \dfrac{20 - 8}{20} \times 100 \\ \boxed{X_{glucose} = 60\,\%} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Volumetric H₂ Productivity [2 Marks]** $$\begin{aligned} Q_{H_2} &= Q_{gas} \times 0.35 = 12 \times 0.35 = 4.2\,L/h \\ P_{H_2} &= \dfrac{Q_{H_2}}{V} = \dfrac{4.2}{5} \\ \boxed{P_{H_2} = 0.84\,L\,H_2/L\cdot h} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Molar H₂ Yield per mol Glucose Consumed [3 Marks]** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Glucose consumed per hour} &= Q_{in} \times (S_0 - S) \\ Q_{in} &= D \times V = 0.25 \times 5 = 1.25\,L/h \\ \text{Mass consumed} &= 1.25 \times (20 - 8) = 15\,g/h \\ \text{Moles of glucose consumed} &= \dfrac{15}{180} = 0.0833\,mol/h \\ \\ \text{Moles of H}_2 \text{ produced per hour} &= \dfrac{4.2}{22.4} = 0.1875\,mol/h \\ \\ Y_{H_2} &= \dfrac{0.1875}{0.0833} \\ \boxed{Y_{H_2} = 2.25\;\text{mol H}_2/\text{mol glucose}} \end{aligned}$$ This indicates the reactor is operating via a **mixed acetate–butyrate pathway** (since pure acetate gives 4 mol/mol and pure butyrate gives 2 mol/mol). --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — PEBBT413, Module 3 Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **direct biophotolysis** and **indirect biophotolysis** processes for biohydrogen production. State the limitations and discuss the role of hydrogenase and nitrogenase enzymes. **(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a flow diagram, explain a **two-stage integrated dark fermentation + photofermentation** system for maximizing biohydrogen yield from carbohydrate-rich wastewater. Compute the theoretical H₂ yield from 1 kg of glucose assuming complete conversion through this integrated system. --- #### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B: **(a) Direct vs Indirect Biophotolysis [7 Marks]** **Direct Biophotolysis:** - Single-stage process in green microalgae - Light energy directly splits water via photosystem II - Reaction: $2H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{light}} 2H_2 + O_2$ - **Major limitation**: Simultaneous evolution of H₂ and O₂; O₂ inhibits [Fe-Fe] hydrogenase irreversibly **Indirect Biophotolysis:** - Two-stage cyclic process in cyanobacteria - Stage A (aerobic): Light-driven photosynthesis stores carbon as glycogen - Stage B (anaerobic): Glycogen is broken down to release H₂ using nitrogenase or hydrogenase - Spatial/temporal separation **resolves O₂ inhibition** **Enzyme Roles:** - **Hydrogenase**: Catalyzes $2H^+ + 2e^- \leftrightarrow H_2$; sensitive to O₂ - **Nitrogenase**: Catalyzes $N_2 + 8H^+ + 8e^- + 16ATP \rightarrow 2NH_3 + H_2 + 16ADP + 16P_i$; **inhibited by NH₄⁺**; less O₂-sensitive but ATP-intensive **Comparison Table (write at least 4 rows):** | Aspect | Direct Biophotolysis | Indirect Biophotolysis | |---|---|---| | Organism | Green algae | Cyanobacteria | | Stages | 1 | 2 | | O₂ problem | Acute | Resolved by separation | | Solar conversion | 1–2 % | 1–3 % | | Enzyme | Hydrogenase | Nitrogenase + hydrogenase | | Practical yield | Very low | Low | --- **(b) Integrated Two-Stage System [7 Marks]** **Process Description (4 Marks):** Stage 1 — **Dark Fermenter (CSTR)** - Anaerobic bacteria convert carbohydrates to H₂, CO₂, and VFAs (acetate, butyrate) - Yield: 4 mol H₂/mol glucose (acetate route) Stage 2 — **Photofermenter** - Effluent VFAs (especially acetate) are converted to additional H₂ by PNS bacteria - Yield: 4 mol H₂/mol acetate **Stoichiometry:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Stage 1:} \quad & C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2CH_3COOH + 2CO_2 + 4H_2 \\ \text{Stage 2:} \quad & 2CH_3COOH + 4H_2O \rightarrow 8H_2 + 4CO_2 \\ \text{Overall:} \quad & C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6H_2O \rightarrow 12H_2 + 6CO_2 \\ \boxed{Y_{H_2,\,theoretical} = 12\;\text{mol H}_2/\text{mol glucose}} \end{aligned}$$ **Mass-based Yield from 1 kg Glucose (3 Marks):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Moles of glucose} &= \dfrac{1000\,g}{180\,g/mol} = 5.556\,mol \\ \text{Moles of H}_2 &= 12 \times 5.556 = 66.67\,mol \\ \text{Mass of H}_2 &= 66.67 \times 2 = 133.33\,g \\ \text{Volume of H}_2 \text{ at STP} &= 66.67 \times 22.4 = 1493.3\,L \\ \text{Energy content} &= 133.33\,g \times 142\,MJ/kg = 18.93\,MJ \\ \boxed{\text{From 1 kg glucose, theoretical yield} = 133.3\,g\,H_2 \approx 1.5\,m^3\,H_2} \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 3 (Biohydrogen) Exam:** > > 1. **Confusing yields**: Many students write *4 mol H₂/mol glucose* as the "theoretical maximum." This is the **acetate pathway yield only**. The **true maximum (combined DF+PF)** is **12 mol/mol**. > 2. **Ignoring partial pressures**: H₂ partial pressure above 60 Pa **inhibits hydrogenase**. Not stating this loses 1 mark in process-parameter questions. > 3. **Missing heat-shock pre-treatment**: When asked about inoculum preparation, students forget **90 °C / 15 min heat shock** to suppress methanogens. > 4. **Molar vs Volumetric yield**: Always specify units explicitly. **mol H₂/mol substrate** ≠ **L H₂/L reactor/h**. > 5. **O₂ inhibition forgotten**: Direct biophotolysis questions — *always* mention O₂ inhibition of [Fe-Fe] hydrogenase for full marks. > 6. **Confusing nitrogenase vs hydrogenase**: Nitrogenase is **ATP-dependent**, hydrogenase is **not**. This distinction is a frequent KTU two-mark question. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] Biohydrogen = H₂ produced via **biological processes** at mild conditions (25–60 °C, 1 atm). - [x] **Four process families**: Direct Biophotolysis, Indirect Biophotolysis, Dark Fermentation, Photofermentation. - [x] **Direct biophotolysis** uses **green algae** + water; problem = O₂ inhibits hydrogenase. - [x] **Indirect biophotolysis** uses **cyanobacteria**; two-stage; glycogen is the intermediate. - [x] **Dark fermentation** uses **Clostridium / Enterobacter**; no light; fastest H₂ rate. - [x] **Photofermentation** uses **PNS bacteria** (*Rhodobacter*); uses VFAs; nitrogenase enzyme. - [x] **Acetate pathway**: 4 mol H₂/mol glucose *(highest single-stage yield)*. - [x] **Butyrate pathway**: 2 mol H₂/mol glucose. - [x] **Ethanol pathway**: 2 mol H₂/mol glucose; low yield due to NADH diversion. - [x] **Lactate pathway**: **0 mol H₂** (dead-end; must be suppressed). - [x] **Combined DF + PF theoretical yield**: **12 mol H₂/mol glucose**. - [x] **Optimal pH** for dark fermentation: **5.5 ± 0.5**. - [x] **Optimal temperature**: **Mesophilic 35 °C** or **Thermophilic 55 °C**. - [x] **HRT**: 4–24 h for dark fermentation; longer (48–120 h) for photofermentation. - [x] **Heat-shock pre-treatment** of inoculum is essential (kills methanogens). - [x] **H₂ partial pressure** must be kept **< 60 Pa** to prevent feedback inhibition. - [x] **NH₄⁺ inhibits nitrogenase** → must be controlled in photofermenter feed. - [x] **Energy recovery**: max theoretical ≈ 33.4 % (acetate pathway); practical ≈ 25–30 %. - [x] **Hydrogenase** = O₂-sensitive, reversible; **Nitrogenase** = ATP-intensive, inhibited by NH₄⁺. - [x] **McCarty's Modified Gompertz** is the standard kinetic model for batch H₂ production data fitting. - [x] **National Green Hydrogen Mission (India, 2023)**: ₹19,744 crore allocation; biohydrogen is a future pillar. - [x] **Standard HHV of H₂** = **142 MJ/kg**, ~3× gasoline energy density by mass. - [x] **Theoretical mass yield** = **0.133 g H₂ / g glucose** (max). --- > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** For a 14-mark question, always include: (1) labelled diagram (2 marks), (2) reaction stoichiometry (2 marks), (3) organism + enzyme (2 marks), (4) parameters (2 marks), (5) numerical (3 marks), (6) advantages/limitations (3 marks). This 2+2+2+2+3+3 = 14 allocation is the most common examiner pattern.

topics.title: Dark and photofermentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dark and photofermentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Dark Fermentation – Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dark Fermentation** is the **anaerobic, light-independent bioconversion of carbohydrate-rich organic substrates into biohydrogen (H₂)** by **facultative or obligate anaerobic chemo-heterotrophic bacteria** (primarily *Clostridium* spp.) through the Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas (EMP) glycolytic pathway followed by acidogenic pyruvate decomposition catalysed by **[Fe-Fe] hydrogenase**.

The process occurs **without any requirement of light energy**, in sealed, oxygen-free bioreactors, where organic substrates are oxidised and the released electrons are channelled to protons (H⁺) to liberate molecular hydrogen (H₂).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 3):** Dark fermentation and photofermentation constitute the **biological hydrogen production pathway**, distinct from biophotolysis and water-gas shift routes.

## 1.2 Photofermentation – Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photofermentation** is the **light-driven, anaerobic production of biohydrogen** carried out by **anoxygenic phototrophic bacteria** — principally the **Purple Non-Sulfur Bacteria (PNSB)** — through the action of the **nitrogenase enzyme complex** which reduces protons (H⁺) to H₂ using ATP and electrons generated by photosynthetic light harvesting.

The substrate pool is dominated by **low-molecular-weight organic acids** (acetate, lactate, malate, butyrate, succinate) that cannot be readily consumed by *Clostridium* species — making photofermentation an ideal **downstream polishing stage** after dark fermentation.

## 1.3 Intuitive Conceptual Analogies

### 🍺 The Brewery Analogy (Dark Fermentation)
> Think of dark fermentation as a **brewery running in a sealed, oxygen-free basement**. Yeast-like bacteria (Clostridia) take sugar and, instead of producing ethanol, divert their metabolism to pump out **hydrogen bubbles** alongside CO₂. No sunlight is needed — just food and sealed conditions. **The "dark" refers to the absence of light**, not to the colour of the product.

### 🌅 The Solar Panel of Microbes (Photofermentation)
> Photofermentation is like giving **purple "solar panel" bacteria** a salad of leftover organic acids. They harvest sunlight (typically 550–950 nm, absorbed by **bacteriochlorophyll**) and use that energy to **split those acids into H₂ and CO₂**. They are picky though — they won't touch sugars but are perfectly happy with the vinegar-like acids that dark fermentation leaves behind.

## 1.4 Standard Physical & Biological Constants

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thauer Theoretical Limit (dark) | $\eta_{Th}$ | **4 mol H₂ / mol glucose** | Maximum achievable from acetate pathway |
| Standard Gibb's Free Energy | $\Delta G^{\circ}$ | **−237.13 kJ/mol** (H₂O → H₂ + ½O₂) | Water-splitting benchmark |
| Hydrogenase Turnover | TOF | **6,000–9,000 s⁻¹** ([Fe-Fe] type) | Catalytic efficiency of Clostridial enzymes |
| Faradaic Efficiency (photo) | $\eta_F$ | **≤ 75 %** | Cap set by ATP demand of nitrogenase |
| BChl *a* Absorption Peak | $\lambda_{max}$ | **590 nm & 805–890 nm** | PNSB operational window |
| Stoichiometric H₂ (per mol glucose, acetate+butyrate) | — | **~2.5–3 mol** | Practical mixed-acid yield |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Dark Fermentation — Operational Logic Steps

### Step 1 — Substrate Uptake & Glycolysis
* Hydrolysis of complex carbohydrates (cellulose, starch) into monomeric sugars by **cellulase** / **amylase**.
* Sugars enter the **Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas (EMP) pathway**, generating **2 mol pyruvate + 2 mol ATP + 2 mol NADH per mol glucose**.

### Step 2 — Pyruvate Cleavage
* Pyruvate is oxidatively decarboxylated by **pyruvate:ferredoxin oxidoreductase (PFOR)** to **acetyl-CoA + CO₂ + reduced ferredoxin (Fd_red)**.

### Step 3 — Electron Bifurcation & H₂ Evolution
* Fd_red donates electrons to **[Fe-Fe] hydrogenase**, which reduces protons to H₂.
* The electron-bifurcating **[Fe-Fe] hydrogenase (HydABC)** simultaneously accepts electrons from NADH and Fd_red to maximise H₂ output.

### Step 4 — End-Product Distribution
* Acetyl-CoA branches into **acetate, butyrate, ethanol, lactate** depending on species, pH, and H₂ partial pressure.
* **Acetate pathway → 4 mol H₂/mol glucose (THEORETICAL MAX).**
* **Butyrate pathway → 2 mol H₂/mol glucose.**

## 2.2 Photofermentation — Operational Logic Steps

### Step 1 — Light Harvesting
* **Bacteriochlorophyll (BChl) *a* or *b*** within the **Intracytoplasmic Membrane (ICM)** system captures photons in the **infrared range (700–900 nm)**.

### Step 2 — Electron Excitation & Photosynthetic Electron Transport
* Excited electrons travel through the **photosynthetic reaction centre (P870 in *Rhodobacter*)** and a cytochrome bc₁ complex, generating a **proton motive force (PMF)** for ATP synthesis.

### Step 3 — Substrate Oxidation
* Organic acids are oxidised via the **TCA (Krebs) cycle** (reverse operation) feeding electrons into the **ferredoxin pool**.

### Step 4 — Nitrogenase-Catalysed H₂ Evolution
* **Nitrogenase (Mo-Fe or Fe-only protein)** catalyses: $N_2 + 8H^+ + 8e^- + 16\,ATP \rightarrow 2NH_3 + H_2 + 16\,ADP + 16\,P_i$
* When **NH₃ is absent** (N-limited), nitrogenase diverts ATP + electrons to: $2H^+ + 2e^- + 4\,ATP \rightarrow H_2 + 4\,ADP + 4\,P_i$

### Step 5 — Why This Step is "Expensive"
* Nitrogenase consumes **4 ATP per mol H₂**, versus < 1 ATP for hydrogenase. Hence **lower growth rates** but **higher purity H₂**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Boundary / Operational Limit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acetate pathway (dark) | $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2CH_3COOH + 2CO_2 + 4H_2$ | $pH_{H_2} \le 0.5\,atm$ | Theoretical yield ceiling |
| 2 | Butyrate pathway (dark) | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH + 2CO_2 + 2H_2$ | $pH \le 5.5$ | Default Clostridial route |
| 3 | Mixed-acid yield | $H_2^{actual} = 0.35 - 0.40 \times 4 = 1.4 - 1.6\,mol/mol$ | Practical experimental | Engineering yield design |
| 4 | Photofermentation (acetate) | $CH_3COOH + 2H_2O \xrightarrow{h\nu} 2CO_2 + 4H_2$ | Light intensity 100–200 W/m² | Photo-stage stoichiometry |
| 5 | Thauer limit | $Y_{max} = 4\,mol\,H_2/mol\,glucose$ | $pH_{H_2} \to 0$ | Thermodynamic ceiling |
| 6 | Nitrogenase stoichiometry | $2H^+ + 2e^- + 4\,ATP \rightarrow H_2 + 4\,ADP + 4\,P_i$ | [NH₄⁺] ≈ 0 | Photo H₂ ATP cost |
| 7 | Combined dark + photo yield | $Y_{combined} = Y_{dark} + Y_{photo\,from\,acids}$ | $\le 12\,mol\,H_2/mol\,glucose$ | Two-stage biorefinery |
| 8 | Hydrogenase rate | $r_{H_2} = k_{cat} \cdot [E] \cdot [H^+]^2$ | $k_{cat} = 10^4\,s^{-1}$ | Kinetic modelling |
| 9 | Hydrogen molar productivity | $P_{H_2} = \dfrac{n_{H_2}}{V \cdot t}\,(mmol/L/h)$ | Batch bioreactor | Reactor scale-up KPI |
| 10 | Light conversion efficiency | $\eta_{LCE} = \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{H_2} \cdot n_{H_2}}{I_{in} \cdot A \cdot t}$ | $\eta_{LCE} \le 10\%$ in field | Solar bioreactor design |
| 11 | Gibb's energy (photo) | $\Delta G^{\circ} = +8.5\,kJ/mol\,CH_3COOH$ | $\Delta G > 0 \Rightarrow$ needs light | Feasibility check |
| 12 | HPR (volumetric) | $HPR = \dfrac{V_{H_2}}{V_{reactor} \cdot \Delta t}$ | $HPR_{dark} = 4\text{–}15\,mmol/L/h$ | Benchmarking |

> [!NOTE]
> **|ΔG°| Sign Convention:** Use **∣ΔG°∣** in markdown tables — NOT bare pipes. The thermodynamic positivity (+8.5 kJ/mol) of the photofermentation reaction is precisely why light energy is **mandatory**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Distributed Rural Biorefineries:** Dark fermentation of food waste + photofermentation of dark-stage effluent → near-zero-waste H₂ platform.
* **Wastewater Treatment Plants (WWTPs):** Coupled anaerobic dark fermentation with photo-polishing stage achieving **COD reduction + H₂ recovery**.
* **Microbial Electrolysis Cells (MEC) Hybrid:** Dark fermenter provides volatile fatty acids (VFAs) → photo-stage converts VFAs to H₂.
* **Carbon-Negative Aviation Fuel Precursor:** Dark fermenter H₂ combined with CO₂ (from off-gas) → methanol synthesis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Thauer Limit for Dark Fermentation

The **Thauer limit** states that under standard biological conditions, the maximum H₂ yield from 1 mol glucose is **4 mol H₂**, derived from electron-balance principles of the EMP pathway coupled with pyruvate oxidation.

### Detailed Logical Walk-Through

**Step (i) — Write the EMP pathway summation:**
$$Glucose + 2\,NAD^+ + 2\,ADP + 2\,P_i \rightarrow 2\,Pyruvate + 2\,NADH + 2\,ATP + 2\,H_2O$$

**Step (ii) — Pyruvate oxidative decarboxylation (PFOR step):**
$$Pyruvate + CoA + 2\,Fd_{ox} \rightarrow Acetyl\text{-}CoA + CO_2 + 2\,Fd_{red}$$

For **2 mol pyruvate**, this yields **4 mol Fd_red**.

**Step (iii) — Hydrogenase transfers electrons to protons:**
$$2\,Fd_{red} + 2\,H^+ \rightarrow 2\,Fd_{ox} + H_2$$

Each Fd_red donates 1 electron (Fd is a 1-electron carrier). Thus 4 Fd_red → **2 mol H₂ from Fd alone**.

**Step (iv) — Re-oxidation of NADH via electron-bifurcating hydrogenase:**
Electron-bifurcating **[Fe-Fe] hydrogenase (HydABC)** simultaneously oxidises NADH and Fd_red:
$$NADH + H^+ + 2\,Fd_{red} \rightarrow NAD^+ + 2\,Fd_{ox} + H_2$$

The **2 NADH** (from EMP) react with **2 Fd_red** (available from PFOR + additional bifurcated flow) to produce an additional **2 mol H₂**.

**Step (v) — Summation (acetate pathway):**
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2\,H_2O \rightarrow 2\,CH_3COOH + 2\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2$$
$$Y_{max} = \dfrac{4\,mol\,H_2}{1\,mol\,glucose}$$

This is the **Thauer Limit**.

### Why Butyrate Pathway Halves the Yield
In the **butyrate route**, 2 mol acetyl-CoA condense to 1 mol butyryl-CoA, and the reducing equivalents (4 [H]) are sequestered within the butyrate molecule itself, **NOT routed to H₂**:
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH + 2\,CO_2 + 2\,H_2$$

Therefore $Y_{butyrate} = 2\,mol\,H_2/mol\,glucose$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Combined Dark + Photofermentation Yield

**Step (i) — Define fractional distribution in dark stage:**
Assume fraction $f_a$ of glucose → acetate; $(1-f_a)$ → butyrate.

**Step (ii) — H₂ from dark stage:**
$$Y_{dark} = 4f_a + 2(1-f_a) = 2 + 2f_a$$

**Step (iii) — Acetate flowing into photo stage (per mol glucose input):**
$$n_{acetate} = 2f_a\,mol$$

**Step (iv) — Photo H₂ from acetate:**
$$CH_3COOH + 2\,H_2O \xrightarrow{h\nu} 2\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2$$
$$Y_{photo,from\,acetate} = 4 \times n_{acetate} = 8f_a$$

**Step (v) — Combined yield:**
$$Y_{combined} = Y_{dark} + Y_{photo,from\,acetate} = 2 + 2f_a + 8f_a = 2 + 10f_a$$

For $f_a = 1$ (pure acetate regime):
$$Y_{combined}^{max} = 2 + 10(1) = 12\,mol\,H_2/mol\,glucose$$

This is the **two-stage theoretical ceiling**.

**Step (vi) — Practical realisation:**
Real systems achieve $f_a \approx 0.6\text{–}0.8$, giving:
$$Y_{combined}^{practical} \approx 8\text{–}10\,mol\,H_2/mol\,glucose$$
which is **2–2.5× higher than dark fermentation alone**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Dark vs Photo Fermentation

| Parameter | Dark Fermentation | Photofermentation |
|---|---|---|
| **Microorganism** | *Clostridium butyricum, C. acetobutylicum, Enterobacter aerogenes* | *Rhodobacter sphaeroides, Rhodopseudomonas palustris, Rhodospirillum rubrum* |
| **Electron Donor** | Carbohydrates (glucose, sucrose, starch) | Organic acids (acetate, lactate, butyrate, malate) |
| **Key Enzyme** | [Fe-Fe] hydrogenase | Nitrogenase (Mo-Fe / Fe-only) |
| **Light Required** | No (light-independent) | Yes (mandatory; BChl *a/b*) |
| **O₂ Requirement** | Strictly anaerobic | Strictly anaerobic + micro-oxic |
| **Optimal pH** | 5.0 – 6.5 | 6.8 – 7.5 |
| **Optimal Temperature** | 30 – 37 °C (mesophilic); 55 – 60 °C (thermophilic) | 25 – 35 °C |
| **H₂ Yield (theoretical)** | 4 mol/mol glucose | 4 mol/mol acetate |
| **Practical H₂ Yield** | 1.5 – 2.5 mol/mol glucose | 1.5 – 3.0 mol/mol acetate |
| **Volumetric Rate** | 4 – 15 mmol H₂ / L·h | 0.5 – 3.0 mmol H₂ / L·h |
| **ATP Cost per H₂** | < 1 ATP | 4 ATP |
| **Reaction Energetics** | ΔG° < 0 (spontaneous) | ΔG° > 0 (light-driven) |
| **Reactor Type** | CSTR / Batch / Immobilised | Photobioreactor / Flat-panel / Tubular |
| **Main Inhibitor** | High p_H₂ (> 0.5 atm) | NH₄⁺ ions (repress nitrogenase) |
| **By-products** | VFAs, alcohols, CO₂ | CO₂, poly-β-hydroxybutyrate (PHB) |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Problem:** A 2 L CSTR dark fermenter receives 10 g/L glucose. Cell density is 0.8 g/L. Volumetric productivity is 6 mmol H₂/L·h. Calculate (a) H₂ evolved per mol glucose if yield coefficient is 0.30, (b) total H₂ in 24 h.

**Step (a):**
$$Y_{H_2/S} = \dfrac{0.30 \times 4 \times 1000}{180} = 6.67\,mmol\,H_2/g\,glucose$$

**Step (b):**
$$n_{H_2} = 6\,mmol/L/h \times 2\,L \times 24\,h = 288\,mmol = 0.288\,mol\,H_2$$

$$V_{H_2} = 0.288 \times 22.4 = 6.45\,L\,H_2\,at\,STP$$

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Dark Fermentation — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid

    B --> C[EMP Glycolysis Pathway]

    I --> K[Butyrate Pathway 2 H2 mol]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is dark fermentation? Name any two microorganisms involved.
**Model Answer:**
Dark fermentation is the **anaerobic, light-independent biological conversion of carbohydrate-rich substrates into biohydrogen** by chemo-heterotrophic bacteria via the EMP pathway coupled with **[Fe-Fe] hydrogenase** activity. The term "dark" refers to the absence of a light requirement.
**Microorganisms:** *Clostridium butyricum* and *Clostridium acetobutylicum* (also acceptable: *Enterobacter aerogenes*, *Clostridium thermocellum*, *Bacillus subtilis*).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] List the Thauer limit and write the acetate-pathway stoichiometric equation for dark fermentation.
**Model Answer:**
**Thauer Limit = 4 mol H₂ per mol glucose** (theoretical maximum).
**Equation:**
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2\,H_2O \rightarrow 2\,CH_3COOH + 2\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2$$
This represents the acetate pathway where 4 reducing equivalents ([H]) from glycolysis and pyruvate oxidation are routed to H₂ via bifurcating [Fe-Fe] hydrogenase.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of biohydrogen production by dark fermentation** with reference to *Clostridium* species, EMP pathway, PFOR enzyme and [Fe-Fe] hydrogenase. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Substrate Hydrolysis (1 mark):**
Complex carbohydrates (cellulose, starch, sucrose) are first hydrolysed by extracellular enzymes (cellulase, amylase, invertase) into monomeric hexoses — primarily glucose.

**2. EMP Glycolysis (1.5 marks):**
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2\,NAD^+ + 2\,ADP + 2\,P_i \rightarrow 2\,Pyruvate + 2\,NADH + 2\,ATP + 2\,H_2O$$
NADH carries high-energy electrons. ATP drives cellular maintenance.

**3. Pyruvate:Ferredoxin Oxidoreductase (PFOR) — 1.5 marks:**
In *Clostridium*, pyruvate is oxidatively decarboxylated:
$$Pyruvate + CoA + 2\,Fd_{ox} \rightarrow Acetyl\text{-}CoA + CO_2 + 2\,Fd_{red}$$
This step generates reduced ferredoxin (E°' ≈ −420 mV), a low-potential electron carrier essential for H₂ evolution.

**4. [Fe-Fe] Hydrogenase HydABC — 2 marks:**
The electron-bifurcating **[Fe-Fe] hydrogenase** simultaneously accepts electrons from Fd_red and NADH, using the favourable Fd_red oxidation to drive the unfavourable NADH oxidation. Net:
$$Fd_{red} + NADH + H^+ \rightarrow Fd_{ox} + NAD^+ + H_2$$
This bifurcation step **doubles** H₂ output compared to classical hydrogenases.

**5. End-Product Branching (1 mark):**
Acetyl-CoA → acetate (yielding ATP) or → butyrate (sequestering reducing equivalents). The acetate route yields 4 mol H₂/mol glucose; butyrate yields 2.

---

**(b)** Describe **photofermentation by purple non-sulfur bacteria (PNSB)**. Discuss the role of nitrogenase, light, and the substrates used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Organisms (1 mark):**
PNSB — *Rhodobacter sphaeroides*, *Rhodopseudomonas palustris*, *Rhodospirillum rubrum*. They are **anoxygenic phototrophs** (do not evolve O₂), strictly anaerobic under N-fixing conditions.

**2. Light Absorption (1.5 marks):**
Bacteriochlorophyll *a* (and *b*) in **intracytoplasmic membranes** absorbs **infrared light (700–900 nm)** — wavelengths not usable by plants. This enables stacked photobioreactors in greenhouses and use of sunlight efficiently.

**3. Substrate Pool (1.5 marks):**
Unlike Clostridia, PNSB **cannot utilise sugars directly**. They oxidise **low-molecular-weight organic acids**: acetate, lactate, butyrate, malate, succinate. These are precisely the VFAs produced by dark fermentation, enabling **two-stage coupling**.

**4. Nitrogenase (2 marks):**
The **Mo-Fe nitrogenase (NifHDK)** catalyses both N₂ fixation and H₂ evolution. When [NH₄⁺] is low, the reaction switches to:
$$2H^+ + 2e^- + 4\,ATP \rightarrow H_2 + 4\,ADP + 4\,P_i$$
**Cost:** 4 ATP per H₂ — expensive but produces **high-purity H₂ free of CO₂ contamination** at low concentration.

**5. Stoichiometry (1 mark):**
$$CH_3COOH + 2\,H_2O \xrightarrow{h\nu} 2\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2$$
4 mol H₂ per mol acetate. With butyrate:
$$CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH + 6\,H_2O \xrightarrow{h\nu} 4\,CO_2 + 10\,H_2$$

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With neat stoichiometric equations, explain the **acetate pathway and butyrate pathway of dark fermentation**. Compute the maximum theoretical yield of H₂ in each case. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Acetate Pathway — Equation (2 marks):**
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2\,H_2O \rightarrow 2\,CH_3COOH + 2\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2$$
- Theoretical yield: **4 mol H₂ / mol glucose** (= Thauer Limit).
- All 12 [H] from glucose (4 from glycolysis + 4 from 2 PFOR + 4 from 2 NADH via bifurcating hydrogenase) are routed to H₂.
- Acetate formation yields additional ATP (substrate-level phosphorylation), favouring microbial growth.

**2. Butyrate Pathway — Equation (2 marks):**
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH + 2\,CO_2 + 2\,H_2$$
- Theoretical yield: **2 mol H₂ / mol glucose**.
- 8 reducing equivalents are sequestered into the butyrate skeleton, leaving only 4 to form 2 mol H₂.
- Thermodynamically favourable for the organism because it disposes of excess reducing power without inhibiting growth.

**3. Comparison Table / Discussion (2 marks):**
| Feature | Acetate | Butyrate |
|---|---|---|
| H₂ yield (mol/mol glucose) | 4 | 2 |
| ATP from acetate kinase | 2 | 0 (butyrate kinase gives 1) |
| Carbon as VFAs | 33% | 50% |
| Engineering priority | Promote via low pH, low p_H₂ | Default pathway |

**4. Strategy to Push Acetate (1 mark):**
Maintain pH 5.5–6.0; keep H₂ partial pressure < 0.05 atm (N₂ sparging); use continuous culture with short HRT; thermophilic regime (55 °C).

---

**(b)** Discuss the **design of a two-stage dark + photofermentation biorefinery**. Highlight the integration logic and the yield enhancement achieved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Logic of Integration (1.5 marks):**
Dark fermentation produces H₂ (up to 4 mol/mol glucose) **AND** a VFA-rich effluent (acetate, butyrate). These VFAs would otherwise be a pollutant load. Photofermentation can convert them to **additional H₂**, simultaneously treating the effluent.

**2. Stage 1 — Dark Fermenter (1.5 marks):**
* Reactor: CSTR (5–10 L), 37 °C, pH 5.5.
* Organism: *Clostridium butyricum* DSM 543.
* H₂ recovery: gas holder with H₂-selective membrane.
* Yield: ~2.0–2.5 mol H₂/mol glucose; VFAs ≈ 0.6 g/L acetate, 0.4 g/L butyrate.

**3. Stage 2 — Photobioreactor (2 marks):**
* Reactor: Flat-panel or tubular, illuminated at 100–200 W/m² (solar or tungsten-halogen).
* Organism: *Rhodobacter sphaeroides* RV.
* Conditions: 30 °C, pH 7.0, micro-aerobic, 5 mM glutamate as N-source (limiting NH₄⁺ to keep nitrogenase derepressed).
* Yield: ~3–4 mol H₂/mol acetate consumed.

**4. Combined Yield Calculation (1.5 marks):**
For $f_a = 0.7$:
$$Y_{combined} = 2 + 10(0.7) = 9\,mol\,H_2/mol\,glucose$$
versus dark alone (3.4 mol/mol). Enhancement = **2.6×**.

**5. Process Advantages (0.5 mark):**
* Solar energy input → near-zero external energy cost.
* Combined COD/VFA removal + H₂ generation.
* Modular design suitable for rural decentralised production.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Most students **forget to factor in the nitrogenase ATP cost** (4 ATP/H₂) when comparing photofermentation economics with dark fermentation. Always cite this when computing *energy recovery efficiency* — partial credit otherwise.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 Rapid-Fire Revision — Dark and Photofermentation**

- ✅ **Dark fermentation** is **anaerobic, light-INDEPENDENT**, uses **Clostridium spp.**, with key enzyme **[Fe-Fe] hydrogenase**.
- ✅ **Thauer Limit = 4 mol H₂/mol glucose** (acetate pathway); butyrate pathway gives only **2 mol**.
- ✅ The **[Fe-Fe] hydrogenase HydABC** is **electron-bifurcating**, doubling the H₂ output by coupling Fd_red oxidation to NADH oxidation.
- ✅ High H₂ partial pressure (> 0.5 atm) **inhibits hydrogenase** — keep low via N₂ sparging.
- ✅ **Photofermentation** is **light-DEPENDENT, anaerobic**, uses **PNSB (Rhodobacter, Rhodopseudomonas)**.
- ✅ Photofermentation key enzyme = **Nitrogenase** (Mo-Fe NifHDK), cost = **4 ATP/H₂**.
- ✅ **NH₄⁺ represses nitrogenase** — keep glutamate-limited for H₂ production.
- ✅ PNSB absorb at **700–900 nm** (bacteriochlorophyll *a/b* in intracytoplasmic membranes).
- ✅ Substrate preference: PNSB = **VFAs (acetate, butyrate, lactate, malate)** — NOT sugars.
- ✅ Combined dark + photo **theoretical yield = 12 mol H₂/mol glucose** (when $f_a = 1$); **practical ≈ 8–10 mol**.
- ✅ Photo-stage reaction: $CH_3COOH + 2H_2O \xrightarrow{h\nu} 2CO_2 + 4H_2$ (ΔG° > 0 → requires light).
- ✅ **Practical H₂ productivity benchmarks:** Dark = 4–15 mmol/L·h; Photo = 0.5–3.0 mmol/L·h.
- ✅ **Temperature optima:** Dark mesophilic 37 °C / thermophilic 55 °C; Photo 30 °C.
- ✅ **pH optima:** Dark 5.0–6.5; Photo 6.8–7.5.
- ✅ By-products of dark stage: **VFAs, CO₂, ethanol, lactate** — feed for photo-stage.
- ✅ Photo-stage by-products: **CO₂, PHB (poly-β-hydroxybutyrate)** under nutrient-limited conditions.
- ✅ Engineering design always quotes **HPR (mmol H₂/L/h)** AND **molar yield (mol H₂/mol substrate)** — not just one.
- ✅ Always mention **electron-bifurcating hydrogenase** for full credit on Clostridial mechanism questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Biological Kinetics — Formal Definition

**Biological kinetics** in the context of bioenergy refers to the **quantitative study of the rates of microbial growth, substrate consumption, and product (biofuel) formation** as a function of environmental and operational variables such as substrate concentration, temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen, and inhibitor levels. The discipline couples microbial physiology with reaction engineering to predict, control, and ultimately maximize the conversion efficiency of renewable biomass into fuels like **bioethanol, biogas (methane), biohydrogen, and biodiesel.**

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the *yield* is the stoichiometric and energetic effectiveness of the conversion, expressed as the mass (or energy) of product obtained per unit mass (or energy) of substrate consumed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus-Highlight Definition:**
> *Biological kinetics describes how fast microbes grow and produce biofuels, while yield describes how much biofuel is produced per unit of substrate — together they define the techno-economic viability of a bioprocess.*

### Core Parameters (Standard KTU Metrics)

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu$ | Specific growth rate | $\text{h}^{-1}$ |
| $\mu_{max}$ | Maximum specific growth rate | $\text{h}^{-1}$ |
| $Y_{X/S}$ | Biomass yield on substrate | $\text{g biomass} \cdot \text{g substrate}^{-1}$ |
| $Y_{P/S}$ | Product yield on substrate | $\text{g product} \cdot \text{g substrate}^{-1}$ |
| $q_S$ | Specific substrate uptake rate | $\text{g} \cdot \text{g}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$ |
| $q_P$ | Specific product formation rate | $\text{g} \cdot \text{g}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$ |
| $K_S$ | Half-saturation constant | $\text{g} \cdot \text{L}^{-1}$ |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bakery Production Line"

Imagine a bakery where the **bakers (microorganisms)** consume **flour (substrate)** to produce **bread (biofuel)** and generate **leftover dough (biomass).**

- The **rate at which each baker works** = specific growth rate ($\mu$).
- The **flour-to-bread conversion efficiency** = product yield ($Y_{P/S}$).
- The **amount of flour left as dough** = biomass yield ($Y_{X/S}$).
- If the bakery runs out of flour, the bakers slow down → this is the **Monod limitation effect.**
- If you keep replenishing flour (fed-batch) or constantly stir the dough in a flowing pipeline (continuous culture), the bakery produces bread **non-stop and efficiently.**

**Why this matters for bioenergy:** A biorefinery is essentially an industrial bakery. *Kinetics* tells you *how fast* fuel is made; *yield* tells you *how efficiently* raw biomass is turned into fuel. Maximizing both is the holy grail of biofuel economics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world context:** In industrial bioethanol plants using *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, a 1% improvement in $Y_{P/S}$ can translate to **millions of dollars in additional revenue per year** at plant scale. This is why "strategies to improve process efficiency" form a dedicated syllabus module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Kinetic Rates

A bioprocess for biofuel production involves **three coupled rates** that are mathematically interlinked:

1. **Cell (biomass) growth rate**
2. **Substrate consumption rate**
3. **Product (biofuel) formation rate**

### A. Microbial Growth Kinetics

The **Monod equation** (1949) is the cornerstone of biokinetics, analogous to the Michaelis–Menten equation in enzyme kinetics:

$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{[S]}{K_S + [S]}$$

**Interpretation of each term:**
- $\mu$ = actual specific growth rate at substrate concentration $[S]$.
- $\mu_{max}$ = maximum specific growth rate (achieved when substrate is in excess).
- $K_S$ = half-saturation constant — the substrate concentration at which $\mu = \mu_{max}/2$. A **low $K_S$** means the organism has **high affinity** for the substrate.
- The cell doubling time is $t_d = \ln(2)/\mu = 0.693/\mu$.

**Two asymptotic regimes:**
- When $[S] \gg K_S$: $\mu \approx \mu_{max}$ (zero-order with respect to substrate).
- When $[S] \ll K_S$: $\mu \approx (\mu_{max}/K_S) \cdot [S]$ (first-order, substrate-limited).

### B. Substrate Utilization Kinetics

Substrate is consumed for three purposes: (1) building new biomass, (2) generating maintenance energy, and (3) producing the biofuel product. The balance is captured by:

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt} - m \cdot X - \frac{1}{Y_{P/S}} \cdot \frac{dP}{dt}$$

Where:
- $m$ = maintenance coefficient ($\text{g substrate} \cdot \text{g biomass}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$), representing energy spent on cell upkeep.
- $Y_{X/S}$ and $Y_{P/S}$ are the yield coefficients.

### C. Product Formation Kinetics (Luedeking–Piret Model)

Biofuels are produced in three kinetic patterns:

$$q_P = \alpha \cdot \mu + \beta$$

- **Growth-associated products** (e.g., ethanol during exponential yeast growth): $\alpha > 0$, $\beta = 0$.
- **Non-growth-associated products** (e.g., secondary metabolites, some biogas phases): $\alpha = 0$, $\beta > 0$.
- **Mixed** (e.g., lactic acid, some biohydrogen): both $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are non-zero.

### D. The Pirt Maintenance Equation (Observed vs. True Growth Yield)

$$ \frac{1}{Y_{X/S}^{obs}} = \frac{1}{Y_{X/S}^{true}} + \frac{m}{\mu} $$

This is **critically important for KTU problems** — students often confuse true yield with observed yield. The **observed yield** decreases at low $\mu$ (slow growth) because a larger fraction of substrate is burned for maintenance.

## 2.2 Theoretical Yields — Stoichiometric Benchmarks

The **theoretical maximum yield** is set by **stoichiometry and bioenergetics**, never exceeded in practice. Real yields are typically **30–95%** of theoretical.

### Key Stoichiometric Equations for Biofuels:

| Biofuel | Microorganism | Theoretical Stoichiometry | Max Yield (g/g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ethanol** | *S. cerevisiae* | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$ | **0.511** |
| **Biogas (CH₄)** | Methanogens | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2$ | **0.267** (CH₄ only) |
| **Biohydrogen** | *Clostridium* spp. | $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6\,H_2O \rightarrow 12\,H_2 + 6\,CO_2$ | **0.133** (mol/mol ≈ 0.40) |
| **Microbial lipids → Biodiesel** | *Rhodotorula*, *Yarrowia* | Glucose → TAG (triacylglycerol) | **0.22–0.25** |
| **Butanol** | *Clostridium acetobutylicum* | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow C_4H_8O + H_2O + CO_2$ | **0.41** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why actual yields are lower:** Microbes spend part of the substrate on (1) biomass synthesis, (2) cell maintenance, (3) production of by-products (e.g., glycerol in ethanol fermentation), and (4) overcoming thermodynamic barriers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table before the ESE. Every Part-B question on Module 3 uses at least one of these.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu = \mu_{max} [S] / (K_S + [S])$ | Monod growth kinetics — predicts fermentation rate |
| 2 | $t_d = 0.693 / \mu$ | Cell doubling time — design of seed train |
| 3 | $Y_{X/S} = \Delta X / \Delta S$ | Biomass produced per g substrate |
| 4 | $Y_{P/S} = \Delta P / \Delta S$ | Biofuel produced per g substrate (key economic metric) |
| 5 | $1/Y^{obs}_{X/S} = 1/Y^{true}_{X/S} + m/\mu$ | Pirt equation — separates growth from maintenance |
| 6 | $q_P = \alpha \mu + \beta$ | Luedeking–Piret product formation kinetics |
| 7 | $dX/dt = \mu X$ | Exponential growth law in batch |
| 8 | $r_P = q_P \cdot X$ | Volumetric productivity = $Y_{P/S} \cdot \mu \cdot X$ |
| 9 | $\eta_{process} = Y^{actual}_{P/S} / Y^{theoretical}_{P/S}$ | Process efficiency (often expressed as %) |
| 10 | $D = \mu$ (chemostat) | Dilution rate = specific growth rate at steady state |

> **Notation tip for KTU answer scripts:** Always define each symbol at first use and quote numerical values with correct units — examiners award marks for clarity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of bioreactors:** The Monod equation determines whether a fermenter should be operated in batch, fed-batch, or continuous mode.
- **Scale-up decisions:** $K_S$ and $\mu_{max}$ values from shake-flask data are used to size industrial fermenters (typically $100 - 500\, \text{m}^3$ for bioethanol).
- **Process economics:** Yield improvements directly reduce the cost per litre of biofuel, which is the dominant factor in biofuel price competitiveness against fossil fuels.
- **Environmental impact:** Higher $Y_{P/S}$ means less waste biomass per unit fuel, reducing downstream waste-handling costs.
