# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Functional Unit" in the context of Life Cycle Assessment. Why is it important to define a functional unit before conducting an LCA?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Functional Unit (FU)** is the quantified reference basis that normalizes all inputs and outputs of an LCA to a common denominator of useful product or service performance. For biofuels, a typical FU is "1 MJ of fuel delivered at the pump" or "1 km travelled by a passenger car using the fuel."

**Importance (3 key points):**
1. **Enables fair comparison** between two products that deliver the same service (e.g., ethanol vs. gasoline) but have different densities and energy contents.
2. **Anchors system boundaries and allocation** decisions — all flows are computed per FU.
3. **Ensures reproducibility** of the LCA study.

> [!WARNING]
> A common mark-loss pitfall: writing that FU is simply "1 kg of fuel." A kilogram of gasoline and a kilogram of ethanol are *not* equivalent in service. Always tie the FU to the **function** performed, not the mass.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the difference between "Well-to-Tank (WTT)" and "Tank-to-Wheel (TTW)" emissions in biofuel LCA?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Well-to-Tank (WTT)** comprises **all upstream emissions** from feedstock cultivation ("well" = agricultural field) to the fuel arriving at the vehicle tank. This includes fertilizer manufacture, farm machinery diesel, feedstock transport, biorefinery energy use, and fuel distribution. For biofuels, WTT is typically the dominant contributor (60–95%) to total lifecycle emissions.
- **Tank-to-Wheel (TTW)** comprises only the **tailpipe (combustion) emissions** released when the fuel is burned in the vehicle's engine. For biofuels, TTW biogenic CO₂ is conventionally treated as carbon-neutral because the feedstock absorbed an equivalent amount during growth. TTW still includes pollutants like NOₓ, CO, and PM.

The sum WTT + TTW = **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)**, the standard boundary for transport biofuel policy.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]

**(a)** Explain the four phases of LCA as defined by ISO 14040/14044. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A WTW LCA of corn ethanol reports the following data per 1 MJ of ethanol delivered:

- Fertilizer-related N₂O: 5 g N₂O (CF = 265)
- Farm diesel and grid electricity: 22 g CO₂-eq
- Biorefinery energy (natural gas): 18 g CO₂-eq
- Transport: 5 g CO₂-eq
- Tailpipe biogenic CO₂: cancelled per convention

The fossil gasoline reference emits **94 g CO₂-eq/MJ**. Calculate the **GHG saving (%)** and **comment on whether this ethanol pathway meets the RED II 60% saving threshold**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — The Four Phases of LCA

**Phase 1 — Goal and Scope Definition (2 marks)**
- State the **purpose** of the study (e.g., policy comparison, product declaration).
- Define the **functional unit**, **system boundary**, **allocation procedures**, **impact categories**, and **data quality requirements**.

**Phase 2 — Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) (2 marks)**
- Compile a detailed **quantitative table** of all inputs (energy, raw materials, water) and outputs (emissions to air, water, soil; co-products; waste) per functional unit.
- This is the most data-intensive phase, often using Ecoinvent or GREET databases.

**Phase 3 — Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) (2 marks)**
- **Classification:** Assign LCI flows to impact categories (e.g., CH₄ → Climate Change; SO₂ → Acidification).
- **Characterization:** Multiply each flow by its **characterization factor** to express it in a common unit (e.g., kg CO₂-eq).
- **Normalization and Weighting** (optional): Scale results against a reference and assign weights if a single score is required.

**Phase 4 — Interpretation (1 mark)**
- Identify **significant issues**, perform **sensitivity analysis** on key assumptions, check **completeness and consistency**, and formulate **conclusions and recommendations**.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical Calculation

**Step 1 — Compute N₂O contribution.**

$$
E_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = 5 \ \text{g} \times 265 = 1325 \ \text{g CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum all WTT emissions.**

$$
E_{\text{WTT}} = 1325 + 22 + 18 + 5 = 1370 \ \text{g CO}_2\text{-eq/MJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Add TTW (cancelled).**

$$
E_{\text{net}} = 1370 + 0 = 1370 \ \text{g CO}_2\text{-eq/MJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute GHG saving.**

$$
\text{GHG}_{\text{saving}} = \frac{94 - 1370}{94} \times 100 = \frac{-1276}{94} \times 100 \approx -1357\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

The result is **massively negative**, meaning the corn ethanol pathway as described emits ~14.6× more GHG than gasoline. This is because the N₂O emission factor (CF = 265) is extremely punishing. **The pathway fails the RED II 60% saving threshold** by a wide margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** The negative sign in the answer is *mathematically correct* but *practically alarming*. If your answer comes out negative, do not write it off as a calculation error — interpret it. Real corn ethanol typically achieves only 20–40% saving because of high N₂O from fertilizers, exactly as shown here.

**To pass RED II**, this pathway would need either:
- Switch to **cellulosic feedstocks** (no grain N₂O burden).
- Adopt **precision agriculture** to cut N₂O by >60%.
- Use **cover crops and nitrification inhibitors**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]

**(a)** Discuss the **environmental implications** of large-scale biofuel production under four categories: (i) greenhouse gas emissions, (ii) land use change, (iii) water footprint, and (iv) biodiversity. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Calculate the **Net Energy Ratio (NER)** of a biodiesel process given:
- Soybean oil input: 1 kg → 0.95 kg biodiesel
- Energy content of biodiesel: 37 MJ/kg
- Energy input for soybean farming: 8 MJ/kg oil
- Energy input for transesterification: 4 MJ/kg biodiesel
- Glycerine co-product energy credit: 5 MJ/kg biodiesel

Comment on whether NER > 1 indicates sustainability. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — Environmental Implications

**(i) Greenhouse Gas Emissions (2 marks)**
Biofuels typically reduce net GHG by 30–90% versus fossil fuels, *provided* land use change is excluded. However, **N₂O from fertilizer application** can dominate the carbon footprint — 1 kg of N₂O has 265× the warming effect of 1 kg of CO₂. **Methane slip** from biogas plants and **combustion of crop residues** also contribute. RED II mandates ≥60% saving.

**(ii) Land Use Change (2 marks)**
- **Direct LUC (dLUC):** Conversion of forest/peatland to oil palm or soy releases 25–60 tonnes CO₂/ha.
- **Indirect LUC (iLUC):** Displacing food crops pushes agriculture into new frontiers (e.g., Amazon clearing for soy after US corn → ethanol).
- This is the **single biggest controversy** in biofuel LCA.

**(iii) Water Footprint (1.5 marks)**
- Biodiesel from soybean: ~4000 L water/MJ (mostly rainfed, blue water is small).
- Ethanol from irrigated sugarcane: ~1500 L water/MJ, with significant **blue water** stress.
- Water-intensive biofuels in arid regions (jatropha in India, corn ethanol in dry US states) compete with drinking water.

**(iv) Biodiversity (1.5 marks)**
Monoculture plantations (palm, soy) reduce habitat heterogeneity. Peatland drainage releases ancient carbon and destroys orangutan habitat. Conversely, **second-generation (2G) biofuels from agricultural residues** have minimal biodiversity impact. **Perennial grasses (miscanthus, switchgrass)** can enhance biodiversity compared to annual row crops.

#### Model Solution for (b) — NER Calculation

**Step 1 — Determine the biodiesel output for 1 kg of oil input.**

$$
M_{\text{biodiesel}} = 0.95 \ \text{kg} \ (\text{from 1 kg oil})
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total energy output.**

$$
E_{\text{out}} = 0.95 \ \text{kg} \times 37 \ \text{MJ/kg} = 35.15 \ \text{MJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum upstream energy inputs.**

Energy input for farming is per kg of **oil** (not biodiesel), so it must be scaled:

$$
E_{\text{farm}} = 8 \ \text{MJ/kg oil} \times 1 \ \text{kg oil} = 8 \ \text{MJ}
$$

$$
E_{\text{trans}} = 4 \ \text{MJ/kg biodiesel} \times 0.95 \ \text{kg} = 3.8 \ \text{MJ}
$$

$$
E_{\text{glycerine credit}} = 5 \ \text{MJ/kg biodiesel} \times 0.95 \ \text{kg} = 4.75 \ \text{MJ (negative credit)}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute net energy input.**

$$
E_{\text{in, net}} = 8 + 3.8 - 4.75 = 7.05 \ \text{MJ}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute NER.**

$$
\text{NER} = \frac{35.15}{7.05} \approx 4.99
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

NER ≈ **5.0** means each unit of fossil energy invested yields 5 units of biodiesel energy. This is competitive with fossil diesel (typically EROI ≈ 15–20, but with non-renewable input).

> [!NOTE]
> **Does NER > 1 imply sustainability? No.** NER only quantifies **energy balance**, not environmental impact. A biofuel can have NER = 8 yet still:
> - Cause severe deforestation (palm oil),
> - Deplete aquifers (irrigated corn ethanol),
> - Trigger food-price inflation (food vs. fuel debate).
> 
> Sustainability requires the *joint* evaluation of NER, GHG saving, water footprint, land use change, and socio-economic indicators.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Two common errors in NER problems:
> 1. **Forgetting the glycerine credit** — this is a *negative* input (energy recovered as by-product) and must be subtracted.
> 2. **Mixing per-kg-oil and per-kg-biodiesel bases** — farming inputs are conventionally given per kg of oil, while transesterification inputs are per kg of biodiesel. Always normalize to a common basis (here, 1 kg of oil input) before summing.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
> - [ ] **LCA = Cradle-to-Grave environmental accounting** of a product, defined by ISO 14040/14044 in **4 phases**.
> - [ ] **Functional Unit (FU)** is the *anchor* of any LCA — never begin without defining it.
> - [ ] **WTW (Well-to-Wheel)** = WTT (upstream) + TTW (tailpipe) — the gold standard for transport biofuels.
> - [ ] **NER = E_out / E_in**; values >1 indicate net energy positive, but do **not** guarantee sustainability.
> - [ ] **GHG saving (%)** uses RED II fossil reference of **94 g CO₂-eq/MJ**; required minimum is **60%** (RED II).
> - [ ] **GWP₁₀₀** is the default; CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265 (IPCC AR5).
> - [ ] **Biogenic CO₂** is cancelled in RED II but contested; always state the accounting rule used.
> - [ ] **Land Use Change (LUC)** — both direct (dLUC) and indirect (iLUC) — is the *dominant* source of uncertainty in biofuel LCA.
> - [ ] **Co-product allocation** by mass, energy, or system expansion can swing results by ±30%.
> - [ ] **Hotspot stages** vary by feedstock: corn ethanol → N₂O; palm biodiesel → deforestation; cellulosic ethanol → pretreatment energy.
> - [ ] **Environmental implications** span GHG, LUC, water, biodiversity, and food security — all must be evaluated together.
> - [ ] **Unit traps:** kg vs. g; LHV vs. HHV; MJ vs. km — convert *before* computing.
> - [ ] **Mermaid/ASCII diagrams:** WTW flow chart, ISO 14040 four-phase loop, and impact category tree are the three most likely "draw and label" exam items.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Note:** When you see "calculate the GHG saving" or "compute the NER" in the KTU question paper, do **not** skip the **interpretation step**. Marks are split roughly 50:50 between arithmetic and *engineering interpretation*. A perfectly computed negative percentage with no comment loses 2–3 marks easily.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biomethane: Biogas and biomethane as high value renewable energy sources - properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Biomethane: Biogas and biomethane as high value renewable energy sources - properties # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Biogas — Formal Academic Definition **Biogas** is a combustible gaseous biofuel produced through the **anaerobic digestion (AD)** of organic biomass in the absence of oxygen, mediated by a synergistic consortium of hydrolytic, acidogenic, acetogenic, and methanogenic archaea. The resulting gas is a heterogeneous mixture dominated by **methane (CH₄: 50–70 %)** and **carbon dioxide (CO₂: 30–40 %)**, along with trace contaminants such as **hydrogen sulfide (H₂S)**, water vapor (H₂O), ammonia (NH₃), siloxanes, and volatile organic compounds. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Adaptable Form):** > Biogas is a renewable gaseous fuel generated by the biochemical breakdown of organic substrates (energy crops, animal manure, municipal solid waste, sewage, food waste) in oxygen-free environments, primarily comprising methane and carbon dioxide in variable proportions. ## 1.2 Biomethane — Formal Academic Definition **Biomethane** is the **upgraded, purified, and pipeline-quality** form of raw biogas, obtained by removing CO₂, H₂S, moisture, and other trace impurities to yield a gas with **CH₄ content ≥ 95 %** (typically 95–98 %), making its energy density and combustion properties comparable to **natural gas (PNG/CNG)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction for KTU Examinations:** > - **Biogas = Raw** (impure, multi-component, low-to-medium calorific value) > - **Biomethane = Purified Biogas** (CH₄ ≥ 95 %, grid/pipeline injectable, near-natural-gas equivalent) > Biomethane is what allows biogas to be a "high-value" renewable energy source rather than a localized waste-to-energy byproduct. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Coffee Bean"** > Think of **biogas** as **raw coffee beans** straight from the plant — they contain caffeine, oils, moisture, and impurities that limit direct utility. **Biomethane** is the **roasted, ground, and filtered espresso** — concentrated, refined, standardized, and ready for high-end use (grid injection, vehicle fuel). > - Biogas = mixed digestate gas (CH₄ + CO₂ + contaminants) > - Biomethane = CH₄-rich purified product suitable for **CNG vehicles, pipeline injection, and industrial boilers**. > > The "value-addition" step is the **upgrading process** (CO₂ scrubbing, H₂S removal, drying). ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Values (Bolded for Quick Recall):** > - **Methane Lower Heating Value (LHV) ≈ 50 MJ/kg** > - **Methane Higher Heating Value (HHV) ≈ 55.5 MJ/kg** > - **Standard Density of CH₄ at STP = 0.717 kg/m³** > - **Air-Fuel Stoichiometric Ratio (CH₄) = 17.2 : 1 (by mass)** > - **Methane Number ≈ 100** (high knock resistance) > - **Wobbe Index of Biomethane ≈ 47–52 MJ/Nm³** > - **Auto-ignition Temperature of CH₄ ≈ 540 °C** > - **Flammability Limits of CH₄ in air = 5–15 % (vol.)** --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Compositional Breakdown of Biogas vs Biomethane The compositional profile of biogas and biomethane governs every downstream engineering decision — from digester design to scrubber selection to engine calibration. The following stepwise logic breaks down the typical analytical hierarchy: ### Step 1 — Macroscopic Composition Raw biogas is dominated by two gases: - **CH₄ (Methane)** — the energy-bearing combustible component. - **CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide)** — an inert diluent that reduces calorific value. ### Step 2 — Trace Contaminants - **H₂S (Hydrogen Sulfide)** — corrosive (forms H₂SO₄ with condensate), toxic, odorous. - **H₂O (Water Vapor)** — causes pipeline corrosion and two-phase flow issues. - **NH₃ (Ammonia)** — toxic at high concentrations, contributes to NOx formation. - **Siloxanes** — form microcrystalline SiO₂ deposits in engines (engine-killer). - **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** — trace hydrocarbons from partial digestion. ### Step 3 — Upgrading Removes Diluents and Contaminants The upgrading process selectively separates CH₄ from the other components to yield biomethane. Common upgrading technologies: - **Water Scrubbing** (uses Henry's Law solubility differences). - **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** (uses zeolites/activated carbon). - **Membrane Separation** (polymer membranes selectively permeate CO₂). - **Chemical Absorption** (amines, e.g., MEA, MDEA). - **Cryogenic Separation** (liquefaction-based). ### Step 4 — The "Why" Behind Each Component - **Why remove CO₂?** It has zero heating value and lowers Wobbe Index, flame stability, and energy density. - **Why remove H₂S?** It forms sulfuric acid in engines, corrodes pipelines, poisons fuel cells. - **Why dry the gas?** Prevents hydrate formation, condensation, and pipeline corrosion. - **Why remove siloxanes?** They form glass-like deposits on engine pistons and catalyst beds. ## 2.2 Energy Content & Calorific Properties — Engineering Significance The **calorific value (CV)** of biogas depends on the CH₄:CO₂ ratio. The general relationship is: $$CV_{biogas} = x_{CH_4} \cdot CV_{CH_4} + x_{CO_2} \cdot CV_{CO_2} + \text{trace corrections}$$ where $x_i$ is the volume fraction of component $i$. Since $CV_{CO_2} = 0$, this simplifies to: $$CV_{biogas} \approx x_{CH_4} \cdot CV_{CH_4}$$ For a typical biogas with **60 % CH₄**: $$CV_{biogas} \approx 0.60 \times 35.8 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3 \approx 21.5 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$$ For biomethane with **97 % CH₄**: $$CV_{biomethane} \approx 0.97 \times 35.8 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3 \approx 34.7 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Implication:** The upgrading step **increases energy density by 60–70 %** by removing the inert CO₂ ballast. This is what makes biomethane a "high-value" fuel — it can directly substitute natural gas in **boilers, internal combustion engines, gas turbines, and fuel cells** without major modification. ## 2.3 Combustion & Performance Properties ### 2.3.1 Wobbe Index (W) — Interchangeability with Natural Gas The Wobbe Index determines whether two gases are interchangeable in combustion equipment at constant pressure: $$W = \frac{CV}{\sqrt{\rho_{rel}}}$$ where $\rho_{rel}$ is the relative density (gas density / air density). For biomethane, **W ≈ 47–52 MJ/Nm³**, very close to natural gas (**W ≈ 50 MJ/Nm³**). ### 2.3.2 Methane Number — Knock Resistance The **Methane Number (MN)** quantifies resistance to engine knock in CNG engines: - Pure CH₄ → MN = 100 - Biomethane (95–98 % CH₄) → **MN ≈ 95–99** - Compared to gasoline (RON 92–98) and CNG from natural gas (MN 75–85), biomethane offers **superior knock resistance**. ### 2.3.3 Flame Speed & Flammability Limits - **Laminar Burning Velocity of CH₄ ≈ 0.40 m/s** (vs gasoline vapor ~ 0.45 m/s). - **Flammability Limits in air: 5 % (LFL) – 15 % (UFL)** by volume. - **Auto-ignition temperature: ≈ 540 °C**. ### 2.3.4 Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio $$AFR_{stoich,CH_4} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{4 \times 32}{16} = 8 \text{ (minimum theoretical)}$$ However, the **standard accepted value used in KTU problems is AFR ≈ 17.2 : 1**, which accounts for the actual combustion of CH₄ with atmospheric air (which is only ~21 % O₂). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following table contains the master formula reference for KTU 2024 scheme examination questions on biogas/biomethane properties.** | **Property / Quantity** | **Formula / Definition** | **Typical Value (Biogas)** | **Typical Value (Biomethane)** | **Unit** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Composition (CH₄) | $x_{CH_4} = \frac{V_{CH_4}}{V_{total}}$ | 50–70 % | 95–98 % | % (vol) | | Composition (CO₂) | $x_{CO_2} = \frac{V_{CO_2}}{V_{total}}$ | 30–40 % | ≤ 2 % | % (vol) | | LHV (CH₄) | $LHV_{CH_4}$ | 50.0 | 50.0 | MJ/kg | | HHV (CH₄) | $HHV_{CH_4}$ | 55.5 | 55.5 | MJ/kg | | LHV (volumetric) | $LHV_{CH_4,vol} \approx 35.8$ | 18–25 | 33.7–34.7 | MJ/Nm³ | | Density (STP) | $\rho_{CH_4}$ | 0.717 | 0.717 | kg/m³ | | Relative Density | $\rho_{rel} = \rho_{gas} / \rho_{air}$ | 0.83–0.95 | ~ 0.55 | dimensionless | | Wobbe Index | $W = CV / \sqrt{\rho_{rel}}$ | 18–26 | 47–52 | MJ/Nm³ | | Methane Number | $MN \approx 1.5 \times x_{CH_4} + 5$ | 75–95 | 95–99 | dimensionless | | Auto-ignition Temp | $T_{ign}$ | 540 | 540 | °C | | Flammability (LFL/UFL) | Vol. % in air | 5 / 15 | 5 / 15 | % (vol) | | Stoichiometric AFR | $AFR = m_{air} / m_{fuel}$ | 17.2 : 1 | 17.2 : 1 | kg/kg | | Laminar Burning Velocity | $S_L$ | 0.40 | 0.40 | m/s | | Octane Number (RON) | Resistance to knock | 130+ | ~ 130 | dimensionless | | Energy Yield (per tonne waste) | $E = m_{CH_4} \times LHV$ | 200–500 kWh | — | kWh | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note on Units:** In KTU problems, **volumetric calorific values are typically reported per Nm³** (Normal cubic meter at 0 °C, 1 atm) or **per Sm³** (Standard cubic meter at 15 °C, 1 atm). Always check the temperature reference in the problem statement. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Calorific Value of Biogas from Compositional Analysis ### 3.1.1 Problem Context Given a biogas sample with volumetric composition: **CH₄ = 62 %, CO₂ = 35 %, H₂S = 0.5 %, N₂ = 1.5 %, H₂O = 1.0 %**, compute the **lower calorific value (LCV)** of the biogas in **MJ/Nm³**. ### 3.1.2 Given Data - LHV of pure CH₄ = **35.8 MJ/Nm³** - LHV of pure H₂S = **23.35 MJ/Nm³** (combustion: H₂S + 1.5 O₂ → SO₂ + H₂O) - LHV of CO₂ = **0 MJ/Nm³** (incombustible) - LHV of N₂ = **0 MJ/Nm³** (incombustible) - LHV of H₂O vapor = **0 MJ/Nm³** (incombustible) ### 3.1.3 Governing Principle The calorific value of a gas mixture is the **volumetric weighted average** of the calorific values of its combustible components only: $$LCV_{biogas} = \sum_{i} x_i \cdot LCV_i$$ where $x_i$ is the volume fraction of the $i^{th}$ component, and the sum runs over **combustible** species only. ### 3.1.4 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Identify combustible components** Only CH₄ and H₂S are combustible. CO₂, N₂, and H₂O are inert diluents. **Step 2 — Apply the weighted sum formula** $$LCV_{biogas} = x_{CH_4} \cdot LCV_{CH_4} + x_{H_2S} \cdot LCV_{H_2S}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute numerical values** $$LCV_{biogas} = (0.62)(35.8) + (0.005)(23.35)$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the CH₄ contribution** $$(0.62)(35.8) = 22.196 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the H₂S contribution** $$(0.005)(23.35) = 0.11675 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$$ **Step 6 — Sum the contributions** $$LCV_{biogas} = 22.196 + 0.11675 = 22.31275 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$$ **Step 7 — Round to engineering precision** $$\boxed{LCV_{biogas} \approx 22.31 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3}$$ ### 3.1.5 Interpretation This LCV is consistent with a "medium-grade" biogas (CH₄ ~ 60 %). Pure biomethane (97 % CH₄) would have LCV ≈ 34.7 MJ/Nm³, comparable to natural gas (LHV ≈ 35.0 MJ/Nm³). ## 3.2 Derivation: Wobbe Index & Interchangeability Analysis ### 3.2.1 Problem Context Compare the Wobbe Index of (a) raw biogas (CH₄ 60 %, CO₂ 40 %) and (b) upgraded biomethane (CH₄ 97 %, CO₂ 3 %), and assess interchangeability with natural gas (LHV = 35.0 MJ/Nm³, ρ_rel = 0.60). ### 3.2.2 Governing Formula $$W = \frac{LHV}{\sqrt{\rho_{rel}}}$$ ### 3.2.3 Step 1 — Compute LHV of Biogas Using the formula from §3.1: $$LHV_{biogas} = 0.60 \times 35.8 = 21.48 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$$ ### 3.2.4 Step 2 — Compute Relative Density of Biogas Molecular weights: M(CH₄) = 16 g/mol, M(CO₂) = 44 g/mol, M(air) = 28.97 g/mol. $$\rho_{rel,biogas} = \frac{(0.60)(16) + (0.40)(44)}{28.97} = \frac{9.6 + 17.6}{28.97} = \frac{27.2}{28.97} = 0.939$$ ### 3.2.5 Step 3 — Compute Wobbe Index of Biogas $$W_{biogas} = \frac{21.48}{\sqrt{0.939}} = \frac{21.48}{0.969} = 22.17 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$$ ### 3.2.6 Step 4 — Compute LHV of Biomethane $$LHV_{biomethane} = 0.97 \times 35.8 = 34.726 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$$ ### 3.2.7 Step 5 — Compute Relative Density of Biomethane $$\rho_{rel,biomethane} = \frac{(0.97)(16) + (0.03)(44)}{28.97} = \frac{15.52 + 1.32}{28.97} = \frac{16.84}{28.97} = 0.5813$$ ### 3.2.8 Step 6 — Compute Wobbe Index of Biomethane $$W_{biomethane} = \frac{34.726}{\sqrt{0.5813}} = \frac{34.726}{0.7624} = 45.55 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$$ ### 3.2.9 Step 7 — Wobbe Index of Natural Gas (Given) $$W_{NG} = \frac{35.0}{\sqrt{0.60}} = \frac{35.0}{0.7746} = 45.18 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$$ ### 3.2.10 Step 8 — Interchangeability Check For interchangeability, the Wobbe Index of the substitute gas must lie within **±10 %** of the reference gas: $$W_{NG} \pm 10\% = 40.66 \text{ to } 49.70 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$$ - **Biogas (W = 22.17):** **NOT interchangeable** (far below acceptable range). - **Biomethane (W = 45.55):** **INTERCHANGEABLE** (within ±10 % of natural gas). ### 3.2.11 Final Result Table | **Fuel** | **LHV (MJ/Nm³)** | **ρ_rel** | **Wobbe (MJ/Nm³)** | **Interchangeable with NG?** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Raw Biogas (60/40) | 21.48 | 0.939 | 22.17 | ❌ No | | Biomethane (97/3) | 34.73 | 0.581 | 45.55 | ✅ Yes | | Natural Gas (ref) | 35.00 | 0.600 | 45.18 | — (Reference) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **This numerical exercise is the classic KTU examination question pattern.** Students are often asked to justify the upgrading process mathematically using the Wobbe Index interchangeability criterion. ## 3.3 Derivation: Energy Yield from Anaerobic Digestion ### 3.3.1 Problem Context A dairy farm operates an anaerobic digester processing **50 tonnes/day** of cattle manure. The methane yield coefficient is **0.25 m³ CH₄ per kg VS** (volatile solids), and the VS content of manure is **80 %**. Compute the daily biogas energy output. ### 3.3.2 Given Data - Daily feed: 50,000 kg manure - VS fraction: 0.80 - Methane yield: 0.25 m³ CH₄/kg VS - LHV of CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/Nm³ ### 3.3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Compute mass of volatile solids** $$m_{VS} = 50{,}000 \times 0.80 = 40{,}000 \text{ kg VS/day}$$ **Step 2 — Compute volume of methane produced** $$V_{CH_4} = 40{,}000 \times 0.25 = 10{,}000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{day}$$ **Step 3 — Compute energy content of CH₄ (at 100 % CH₄)** $$E_{CH_4} = 10{,}000 \times 35.8 = 358{,}000 \text{ MJ/day}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ)** $$E_{CH_4} = \frac{358{,}000}{3.6} = 99{,}444 \text{ kWh/day} \approx 99.4 \text{ MWh/day}$$ **Step 5 — If biogas contains 60 % CH₄, compute total biogas volume** $$V_{biogas} = \frac{V_{CH_4}}{0.60} = \frac{10{,}000}{0.60} = 16{,}667 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{day}$$ **Step 6 — If CHP generator is 38 % electrical efficiency, compute electricity output** $$E_{electricity} = 99{,}444 \times 0.38 = 37{,}789 \text{ kWh/day} \approx 37.8 \text{ MWh/day}$$ **Step 7 — Annual electricity generation** $$E_{annual} = 37{,}789 \times 365 = 13{,}793{,}000 \text{ kWh/year} \approx 13.8 \text{ GWh/year}$$ ### 3.3.4 Final Answer $$\boxed{E_{electricity} \approx 37.8 \text{ MWh/day} \approx 13.8 \text{ GWh/year}}$$ This is sufficient to power approximately **2,500–3,000 Indian households annually**. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow: Biogas Production to Biomethane Upgrading The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete engineering pathway** from raw biomass to grid-injectable biomethane, highlighting the property transformations at each stage. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Biomass Feedstock
Manure / Food Waste / Energy Crops] B[Anaerobic Digester
Oxygen-free, 35-55 C, pH 6.8-7.5] C[Raw Biogas Output
CH4 50-70% + CO2 30-40% + H2S + H2O] D[Primary Treatment
H2S Removal FeCl3 or Iron Oxide] E[Secondary Treatment
Dehumidification Cooling and Adsorption] F[Upgrading Unit
PSA / Water Scrubbing / Membrane] G[Secondary Scrubbing
Polishing to CH4 over 97 percent] H[Biomethane Product
CH4 95-98% Grid Quality] I[End Use Applications
CNG Vehicles, Grid Injection, Industrial Boilers] J[Digestate Byproduct
Nutrient-rich Liquid Fertilizer] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I B --> J ``` ## 4.2 Property Comparison Matrix: Biogas vs Biomethane vs Natural Gas The following Mermaid graph renders a **comparative property matrix** as a block-level functional architecture, mapping the relative performance of each fuel across key engineering properties. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph P1[COMPOSITIONAL PROPERTY] A1[Biogas
CH4 60% CO2 40%] A2[Biomethane
CH4 97% CO2 less than 2%] A3[Natural Gas
CH4 90 to 98% CO2 less than 1%] end subgraph P2[ENERGY PROPERTY] B1[LHV Biogas
about 21.5 MJ per Nm3] B2[LHV Biomethane
about 34.7 MJ per Nm3] B3[LHV Natural Gas
about 35.0 MJ per Nm3] end subgraph P3[INTERCHANGEABILITY] C1[Wobbe Biogas
about 22 MJ per Nm3 NOT compatible] C2[Wobbe Biomethane
about 45.5 MJ per Nm3 compatible] C3[Wobbe Natural Gas
about 45 MJ per Nm3 reference] end subgraph P4[ENGINE PERFORMANCE] D1[Methane Number Biogas
around 80 to 90] D2[Methane Number Biomethane
about 95 to 99] D3[Octane RON Natural Gas
around 120 to 130] end A1 --- B1 --- C1 --- D1 A2 --- B2 --- C2 --- D2 A3 --- B3 --- C3 --- D3 ``` ## 4.3 Composition Pie-Chart Equivalent (Mermaid Pie Chart) The following Mermaid pie chart renders the **typical volumetric composition of raw biogas**, providing a visual reference for the CH₄/CO₂ balance. ```mermaid pie title Typical Raw Biogas Composition (Volume Percent) "Methane CH4" : 60 "Carbon Dioxide CO2" : 37 "Hydrogen Sulfide H2S" : 0.5 "Water Vapor H2O" : 1.5 "Nitrogen N2" : 0.5 "Ammonia NH3" : 0.5 ``` ## 4.4 Upgrading Technology Decision Tree The following Mermaid block diagram renders a **decision-making architecture** for selecting the appropriate biomethane upgrading technology based on feedstock and scale. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Biomethane Upgrading Decision] Q1{Large Scale
over 1000 Nm3 per hour} Q2{Small to Medium Scale
100 to 1000 Nm3 per hour} Q3{High Purity Required
over 99 percent CH4} R1[Water Scrubbing
High CH4 recovery 96 to 98 percent] R2[Pressure Swing Adsorption PSA
High purity 97 to 99 percent] R3[Membrane Separation
Modular and compact] R4[Cryogenic Separation
Highest purity and CH4 recovery] R5[Chemical Absorption Amine
Best for sour gas high H2S] Start --> Q1 Start --> Q2 Q1 --> Q3 Q3 -->|Yes| R4 Q3 -->|No| R1 Q1 -->|No| R2 Q2 --> R3 Start -->|Sour Gas Stream| R5 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Adapted]** **Q1.** Differentiate between **biogas** and **biomethane** in terms of composition, calorific value, and end-use applications. **Model Answer:** | **Parameter** | **Biogas** | **Biomethane** | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Raw gas from anaerobic digestion | Purified, upgraded biogas | | **CH₄ Content** | 50–70 % | 95–98 % | | **CO₂ Content** | 30–40 % | ≤ 2 % | | **H₂S Content** | 500–3000 ppm | < 5 ppm | | **Calorific Value (LHV)** | 18–25 MJ/Nm³ | 33–35 MJ/Nm³ | | **Wobbe Index** | 18–26 MJ/Nm³ | 47–52 MJ/Nm³ | | **End Use** | Direct boiler, CHP, cooking | Pipeline injection, CNG vehicles, grid export | | **Production Stage** | Digester outlet | After CO₂/H₂S removal | Biomethane is therefore a "high-value" renewable energy source because it can directly substitute natural gas in all existing infrastructure without modification. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Adapted]** **Q2.** Define the **Wobbe Index** and explain its significance in determining the interchangeability of biomethane with natural gas. **Model Answer:** The **Wobbe Index (W)** is defined as the ratio of the calorific value of a gas to the square root of its relative density (with respect to air): $$W = \frac{CV}{\sqrt{\rho_{rel}}}$$ **Significance:** - Wobbe Index determines whether two gases are **interchangeable** in combustion equipment (burners, boilers, engines) at the **same operating pressure** without requiring hardware modifications. - Two gases with **Wobbe Index within ±10 %** of each other are considered interchangeable. - For natural gas, $W_{NG} \approx 45 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$; the acceptable range is 40.5–49.5 MJ/Nm³. - **Raw biogas** has $W \approx 18–26 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$ — **NOT interchangeable** with natural gas. - **Upgraded biomethane** has $W \approx 45–52 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$ — **fully interchangeable** with natural gas. - This is the **primary engineering justification** for biogas upgrading. --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **Q(A).** (a) Discuss the **composition and properties of biogas** as a renewable energy source. Explain why biomethane is considered a "high-value" fuel compared to raw biogas. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A biogas plant produces a gas with the following composition by volume: **CH₄ = 65 %, CO₂ = 32 %, H₂S = 0.8 %, N₂ = 1.2 %, H₂O = 1.0 %**. Calculate: - (i) The lower calorific value of the biogas in **MJ/Nm³**. - (ii) The relative density and Wobbe Index of the biogas. - (iii) The **percentage increase in calorific value** when the biogas is upgraded to biomethane with 97 % CH₄. - (iv) Comment on the interchangeability of both gases with natural gas (given: LHV_NG = 35.0 MJ/Nm³, ρ_rel,NG = 0.60). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q(A)(a): **Biogas Composition (typical range):** - **CH₄**: 50–70 % (energy component) - **CO₂**: 30–40 % (inert diluent) - **H₂S**: 100–5000 ppm (corrosive, toxic) - **H₂O**: 1–5 % (saturated) - **NH₃**: < 1 % (toxic, NOx precursor) - **N₂, O₂, VOCs, Siloxanes**: trace levels **Key Properties:** - LHV: 18–25 MJ/Nm³ - Density: 1.0–1.3 kg/Nm³ - Auto-ignition: 540 °C - Flammability range: 5–15 % in air - Methane Number: 75–95 **Why biomethane is "high-value":** 1. **High calorific value** (~35 MJ/Nm³, comparable to natural gas). 2. **Grid injectability** — pipeline-ready quality. 3. **Vehicle fuel** — CNG replacement. 4. **Storage and transport** — can be liquefied (LBG) or compressed (CBG). 5. **Carbon credit eligibility** — counts as renewable natural gas (RNG) in many jurisdictions. 6. **Interchangeable** with natural gas (Wobbe Index compatibility). #### Model Solution to Q(A)(b): **Step 1 — Identify combustible components and their LHV:** - LHV (CH₄) = **35.8 MJ/Nm³** - LHV (H₂S) = **23.35 MJ/Nm³** - LHV (CO₂, N₂, H₂O) = **0** (inerts) **Step 2 — Compute LHV of biogas:** $$LCV_{biogas} = (0.65)(35.8) + (0.008)(23.35)$$ $$LCV_{biogas} = 23.27 + 0.187 = \boxed{23.46 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3}$$ **Step 3 — Compute relative density of biogas:** Using M(CH₄)=16, M(CO₂)=44, M(H₂S)=34, M(N₂)=28, M(H₂O)=18, M(air)=28.97. $$\rho_{rel} = \frac{(0.65)(16) + (0.32)(44) + (0.008)(34) + (0.012)(28) + (0.01)(18)}{28.97}$$ $$\rho_{rel} = \frac{10.4 + 14.08 + 0.272 + 0.336 + 0.18}{28.97} = \frac{25.268}{28.97} = \boxed{0.872}$$ **Step 4 — Compute Wobbe Index of biogas:** $$W_{biogas} = \frac{23.46}{\sqrt{0.872}} = \frac{23.46}{0.9338} = \boxed{25.13 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3}$$ **Step 5 — Compute LHV of biomethane (97 % CH₄):** $$LCV_{biomethane} = (0.97)(35.8) = \boxed{34.73 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3}$$ **Step 6 — Compute percentage increase in calorific value:** $$\% \text{ increase} = \frac{34.73 - 23.46}{23.46} \times 100 = \frac{11.27}{23.46} \times 100 = \boxed{48.04 \%}$$ **Step 7 — Interchangeability check with natural gas:** $$W_{NG} = \frac{35.0}{\sqrt{0.60}} = 45.18 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$$ Acceptable range: $W_{NG} \pm 10\% = 40.66 \text{ to } 49.70 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$ - **Biogas (W = 25.13):** **NOT interchangeable** (deviates by ~44 %). - **Biomethane (W = 45.55, using ρ_rel ≈ 0.581):** **INTERCHANGEABLE** (within ±10 %). **Conclusion:** The upgrading process yields a ~48 % increase in calorific value and brings the Wobbe Index into the natural gas interchangeability range, justifying the economic value of the upgrading investment. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** **Q(B).** (a) Describe the **physical and chemical properties of biomethane** that make it suitable as a **transportation fuel (CNG substitute)**. Compare its **methane number, octane rating, and Wobbe Index** with conventional gasoline and natural gas. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A municipal solid waste (MSW) processing plant generates **200 tonnes/day** of organic waste. The volatile solids (VS) content is **70 %**, and the methane yield coefficient is **0.30 m³ CH₄/kg VS**. The digester operates at **60 % CH₄ yield efficiency** (i.e., actual CH₄ / theoretical CH₄ = 0.60). Calculate: - (i) Daily **methane production** in Nm³/day. - (ii) Daily **biogas production** (assuming 60 % CH₄ content in biogas). - (iii) Daily **electricity generation** if the biogas is fed to a CHP system with **35 % electrical efficiency** and **45 % thermal efficiency**. - (iv) Daily **CO₂ emissions avoided** (assume natural gas emission factor = 0.20 kg CO₂/kWh thermal). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q(B)(a): **Physical Properties of Biomethane suitable for transport:** | **Property** | **Value** | **Significance for Transport** | |---|---|---| | Density (STP) | 0.717 kg/m³ | Low density → needs high-pressure compression (200 bar) | | LHV | 50.0 MJ/kg | High energy content per mass | | LHV (volumetric) | 35.8 MJ/Nm³ | Comparable to natural gas | | Methane Number | 95–99 | High knock resistance → high compression ratio engines | | Octane Rating (RON) | 130+ | Superior to gasoline (RON 92–98) | | Auto-ignition Temp | 540 °C | Higher than gasoline (300 °C) → safer handling | | Flammability Limits | 5–15 % | Narrower than gasoline (1.4–7.6 %) → safer | | Boiling Point | -161.5 °C | Liquefiable (LNG-like) for cryogenic storage | **Comparison Table — Biomethane vs Gasoline vs Natural Gas:** | **Property** | **Gasoline** | **Natural Gas (CNG)** | **Biomethane** | |---|---|---|---| | LHV (MJ/kg) | 44 | 50 | 50 | | LHV (MJ/L) | 32 | 25 (200 bar) | 25 (200 bar) | | Octane Number (RON) | 92–98 | 120–130 | 130+ | | Methane Number | N/A | 75–85 | 95–99 | | Wobbe Index (MJ/Nm³) | N/A | 45 | 45–52 | | CO₂ Emissions (g/MJ) | 73 | 56 | 25–35 (renewable) | | Flammability (% in air) | 1.4–7.6 | 5–15 | 5–15 | | Storage | Liquid (ambient) | Compressed (200 bar) | Compressed (200 bar) | **Why biomethane is a superior transport fuel:** 1. **High knock resistance** → enables **lean-burn and high-compression-ratio engines** → higher thermal efficiency. 2. **Lower CO₂ emissions** (life-cycle basis) → qualifies as **renewable fuel** under EU RED II, India SATAT, and US RFS. 3. **Lower particulate and NOx emissions** vs diesel/gasoline. 4. **Domestic production** → energy security and rural employment. 5. **Interchangeable with natural gas** → can use existing CNG infrastructure (refueling stations, pipelines, vehicle kits). #### Model Solution to Q(B)(b): **Given Data:** - Daily waste input: 200,000 kg/day - VS fraction: 0.70 - Theoretical CH₄ yield: 0.30 m³ CH₄/kg VS - Actual CH₄ efficiency: 60 % of theoretical - Biogas CH₄ content: 60 % - CHP electrical efficiency: 35 % - CHP thermal efficiency: 45 % - LHV of CH₄: 35.8 MJ/Nm³ - 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ - Natural gas CO₂ factor: 0.20 kg CO₂/kWh thermal **Step 1 — Compute VS mass:** $$m_{VS} = 200{,}000 \times 0.70 = 140{,}000 \text{ kg VS/day}$$ **Step 2 — Compute theoretical CH₄:** $$V_{CH_4,theo} = 140{,}000 \times 0.30 = 42{,}000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{day}$$ **Step 3 — Compute actual CH₄ (60 % efficiency):** $$V_{CH_4,actual} = 42{,}000 \times 0.60 = \boxed{25{,}200 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{day}}$$ **Step 4 — Compute daily biogas production (60 % CH₄):** $$V_{biogas} = \frac{25{,}200}{0.60} = \boxed{42{,}000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{day}}$$ **Step 5 — Compute energy content of biogas:** $$E_{biogas} = 25{,}200 \times 35.8 = 902{,}160 \text{ MJ/day}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to thermal kWh:** $$E_{thermal} = \frac{902{,}160}{3.6} = 250{,}600 \text{ kWh/day}$$ **Step 7 — Compute electrical output (35 % efficiency):** $$E_{electricity} = 250{,}600 \times 0.35 = \boxed{87{,}710 \text{ kWh/day} \approx 87.7 \text{ MWh/day}}$$ **Step 8 — Compute thermal output (45 % efficiency):** $$E_{heat} = 250{,}600 \times 0.45 = \boxed{112{,}770 \text{ kWh/day} \approx 112.8 \text{ MWh/day}}$$ **Step 9 — Compute avoided CO₂ emissions (replacing natural gas):** $$CO_2_{avoided} = 250{,}600 \times 0.20 = \boxed{50{,}120 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{day} \approx 50.1 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{day}}$$ **Step 10 — Annual avoidance (× 365):** $$CO_2_{avoided,annual} = 50{,}120 \times 365 = 18{,}293{,}800 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year} \approx 18{,}294 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}$$ #### Final Summary Table | **Quantity** | **Value** | |---|---| | CH₄ production | 25,200 Nm³/day | | Biogas production | 42,000 Nm³/day | | Electricity | 87.7 MWh/day | | Heat | 112.8 MWh/day | | Avoided CO₂ | 50.1 tonnes/day (≈ 18,294 tonnes/year) | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to include H₂S contribution** in LHV calculation (use weighted sum across **all combustible** species). > 2. **Using absolute density (kg/m³) instead of relative density (with respect to air = 28.97 g/mol)** in Wobbe Index formula — this is a frequent, mark-losing error. > 3. **Forgetting unit conversions** — kWh ↔ MJ (× 3.6), Nm³ ↔ Sm³ (check reference conditions). > 4. **Confusing Methane Number with Octane Number** — they are different scales (MN 0–100, RON 0–120+). > 5. **Not commenting on interchangeability** — KTU examiners specifically expect the ±10 % Wobbe Index rule to be invoked. > 6. **Skipping the "why biomethane is high-value" qualitative discussion** — even in numerical problems, the engineering interpretation carries 2–3 marks. > 7. **Forgetting to convert VS yield coefficient** (m³ CH₄/kg VS) consistently with the substrate moisture and ash content. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read Before Every Exam):** **🔹 Fundamental Definitions** - **Biogas** = Raw anaerobic digestion gas (CH₄ 50–70 %, CO₂ 30–40 %). - **Biomethane** = Upgraded biogas (CH₄ ≥ 95 %). - Biomethane is the "high-value" version due to grid/transport compatibility. **🔹 Critical Numerical Values to Memorize** - LHV of CH₄ = **35.8 MJ/Nm³** (volumetric) and **50.0 MJ/kg** (mass). - HHV of CH₄ = **55.5 MJ/kg**. - Density of CH₄ at STP = **0.717 kg/m³**. - Stoichiometric AFR = **17.2 : 1** (mass basis). - Auto-ignition temperature = **540 °C**. - Flammability limits = **5–15 % (vol.) in air**. - Methane Number of biomethane = **95–99**. - Wobbe Index of biomethane = **47–52 MJ/Nm³**. **🔹 Key Formulas (Must Memorize)** - LCV of mixture: $LCV_{mix} = \sum x_i \cdot LCV_i$ (combustibles only). - Wobbe Index: $W = LHV / \sqrt{\rho_{rel}}$. - Relative density: $\rho_{rel} = \sum x_i M_i / M_{air}$, where $M_{air} = 28.97$ g/mol. - Interchangeability: $|W_{new} - W_{ref}| / W_{ref} \leq 10\%$. **🔹 Critical Distinctions for KTU Exams** - **Biogas** is for **local use** (boilers, CHP, cooking stoves). - **Biomethane** is for **export** (grid, CNG, industrial, international). - **Methane Number** is for **CNG engines** (resistance to knock). - **Wobbe Index** is for **burner interchangeability** (gas appliances). **🔹 Upgrading Technologies (Memorize Names)** - **Water scrubbing** — most common, 96–98 % CH₄ recovery. - **PSA** — high purity, 97–99 % CH₄. - **Membrane separation** — modular, small-medium scale. - **Chemical absorption (amines)** — best for sour gas. - **Cryogenic** — highest purity, capital-intensive. **🔹 Why Biomethane is "High-Value" (Engineering Justifications)** 1. Comparable calorific value to natural gas. 2. Pipeline/grid injectability (Wobbe Index match). 3. CNG substitution (transport sector decarbonization). 4. Negative/neutral life-cycle CO₂ emissions. 5. Dual revenue stream (electricity + heat in CHP, or upgraded gas sale). 6. Carbon credit eligibility (Renewable Energy Certificates, RFS, RED II). **🔹 Common Units and Conversions** - 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ. - 1 Nm³ CH₄ = 0.717 kg = 35.8 MJ (LHV). - 1 Sm³ CH₄ ≈ 1.055 Nm³ (at 15 °C vs 0 °C). - 1 toe (tonne oil equivalent) ≈ 11,630 kWh ≈ 41.87 GJ. **🔹 Frequently Tested Numerical Pattern** - Given composition → Compute LCV, ρ_rel, Wobbe Index. - Given waste feed + VS yield → Compute CH₄ volume, electricity output, CO₂ avoided. - Compare biogas vs biomethane for interchangeability with natural gas. - Discuss upgrading technology selection based on scale and purity. ---

topics.title: Advantages and disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advantages and disadvantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biofuels and Bioenergy — Advantages and Disadvantages (KTU 2024 Definition Framework)**
> In the context of **PEBBT413 – Bioenergy and Biofuels (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the *advantages and disadvantages* of bioenergy systems refer to the comprehensive assessment of the **technical, economic, environmental, and socio-political merits and limitations** associated with the production, conversion, and utilization of biomass-derived solid, liquid, and gaseous fuels (such as biogas, biodiesel, bioethanol, biohydrogen, and producer gas) when compared to **conventional fossil-based energy carriers** (petroleum, natural gas, and coal).

The evaluation is performed across **four canonical pillars of sustainable energy assessment**, as standardized by the **International Energy Agency (IEA)** and adopted by the **MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy, Government of India)**:

1. **Energy Security Pillar** – domestic availability, import substitution.
2. **Environmental Pillar** – GHG emissions, life-cycle impact, land use.
3. **Economic Pillar** – cost of production, employment generation, rural development.
4. **Technical Pillar** – energy density, conversion efficiency, engine compatibility.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Medicine vs. Side-Effects" Analogy for Biofuels**
> Think of biofuels as a **herbal medicine** prescribed to a sick patient (the planet, suffering from fossil-fuel addiction and climate change). Just like a herbal remedy has **healing properties** (renewable, biodegradable, carbon-neutral) but also comes with **side-effects** (food-vs-fuel conflict, land-use change, high water footprint), biofuels are not a "perfect cure." A doctor weighs the **pros and cons** before prescribing. Similarly, engineers and policymakers must critically evaluate the **advantages and disadvantages** of each biofuel pathway before large-scale deployment.

## 1.3 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) Used in Evaluation

The following **standardized metrics** are universally applied for comparing biofuels against fossil fuels:

| KPI | Symbol | Standard Unit | Significance |
|-----|--------|---------------|--------------|
| Higher Heating Value | **HHV** | **MJ/kg** | Total energy released incl. latent heat of water vapor |
| Lower Heating Value | **LHV** | **MJ/kg** | Useful energy (water as vapor) |
| Cetane Number | **CN** | dimensionless | Diesel ignition quality (must be **≥ 40** for CI engines) |
| Octane Number | **RON/MON** | dimensionless | Petrol knock resistance (must be **≥ 95 RON** for gasoline) |
| Life-Cycle GHG Reduction | **LCA-CO₂e** | **%** vs. fossil benchmark (e.g., **petrol = 100%**) |
| Net Energy Ratio | **NER** | ratio (output/input) | Must be **> 1** for viability |
| Carbon Neutrality Factor | **CNF** | ratio | Closer to **1.0** = better |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEBBT413, Module 3):** Students must be able to critically analyze the **sustainability trade-offs** of first-generation (sugarcane ethanol, soybean biodiesel), second-generation (lignocellulosic ethanol, algae biodiesel), and third-generation (algae-based, microbial) biofuels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Pillar Classification Framework

The advantages and disadvantages of biofuels are best understood by **classifying them under four pillars**. This is the standard KTU examiner framework.

### **PILLAR 1 — Environmental Advantages**

- **Carbon Neutrality Principle:**
  The CO₂ released during biofuel combustion is **re-absorbed** by the next generation of biomass during photosynthesis, creating a **closed-loop carbon cycle**.

  $$\text{Net CO}_2 \text{ Emission} = \text{CO}_2 \text{ (combustion)} - \text{CO}_2 \text{ (photosynthesis uptake)}$$

  For an ideal biofuel, this tends to **zero** over a single growth-harvest cycle (typically **1 to 25 years**, depending on the feedstock).

- **Reduction in Particulate Matter (PM) and SO₂ Emissions:**
  Biofuels contain negligible sulfur (**< 10 ppm**) compared to diesel (**< 350 ppm**) and coal (**up to 30,000 ppm**). This eliminates SO₂-driven **acid rain** and PM-driven **respiratory diseases**.

- **Biodegradability:**
  Pure biodiesel (B100) is **> 90% biodegradable** within **28 days** (OECD 301B test), versus decades for petroleum diesel.

- **Lower Life-Cycle GHG Emissions:**
  - Bioethanol (sugarcane): **~ 60–90%** lower LCA-GHG vs. petrol.
  - Biodiesel (UCO – Used Cooking Oil): **~ 80–95%** lower LCA-GHG vs. diesel.
  - Biogas (from MSW): **> 90%** lower LCA-GHG vs. CNG.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Always state LCA-GHG values **with the correct benchmark** (e.g., "60–90% lower than petrol on a well-to-wheel basis"). Vague statements like "biofuels are green" fetch **0 marks**.

### **PILLAR 2 — Economic Advantages**

- **Energy Security & Import Substitution:**
  India imports **> 85%** of its crude oil (**≈ 4.5 million barrels/day**, 2023). The **Ethanol Blending Programme (EBP)** targets **E20 (20% ethanol)** by **2025–26**, potentially saving **₹ 30,000 crore/year** in forex.
- **Rural Employment & Decentralized Production:**
  Biogas plants and biodiesel presses are **decentralized**, generating **2.5 to 5 jobs per ML of biofuel produced**, mostly in rural India.
- **Agricultural Waste Monetization:**
  India generates **~ 500 million tonnes/year** of agricultural residue. Conversion to 2G ethanol or biogas converts a **pollution liability** (stubble burning) into a **revenue stream** (**₹ 2,000–4,000/tonne**).
- **By-Product Valorization:**
  - Biodiesel → **Glycerol** (10 wt%) → cosmetics, pharma (₹ 80–150/kg).
  - Biogas → **Digestate** (slurry) → organic fertilizer (₹ 5–8/kg N-equivalent).
  - Bioethanol → **DDGS** (Dried Distillers Grains with Solubles) → cattle feed.

### **PILLAR 3 — Energy & Technical Advantages**

- **Renewability:** Biomass regrows on a human timescale (months to decades), unlike fossil fuels (formed over **millions of years**).
- **High Octane/Cetane Properties:** Bioethanol has **RON = 108–110** (excellent anti-knock); biodiesel has **CN = 45–65** (better than diesel CN = 45–55).
- **Lubricity Enhancement:** Even **B2 (2% biodiesel blend)** restores lubricity to **ULSD (Ultra-Low Sulfur Diesel)**, extending engine life.
- **Dual-Use Engines:** Biogas (CNG-substitute) and ethanol (petrol-substitute) require **minimal retrofits**.
- **Modular & Scalable:** Plants range from **1 m³ household biogas** to **500,000 L/day industrial biodiesel**.

### **PILLAR 4 — Environmental Disadvantages**

- **Food-vs-Fuel Conflict:**
  1G biofuels use edible feedstocks (maize, sugarcane, soybean). India used **~ 1,200 million litres** of ethanol from sugarcane in **2022–23**, equivalent to feeding **~ 50 million people** for a year.
- **Indirect Land-Use Change (ILUC):**
  Deforestation to grow biofuel crops releases **"carbon debt"** of **50–100 tonnes CO₂/ha**. It can take **~ 100–300 years** of biofuel production to repay this debt.
- **High Water Footprint:**
  - 1G bioethanol: **~ 1,000–4,000 L water/L ethanol**.
  - Biodiesel (soybean): **~ 4,000–14,000 L water/L biodiesel**.
  - 2G biofuels (lignocellulose): reduced to **~ 200–1,000 L water/L**.
- **Biodiversity Loss:** Monoculture plantations (palm oil) reduce ecosystem diversity by **30–80%**.
- **Eutrophication & Pesticide Runoff:** Fertilizer and pesticide use for energy crops contaminates waterways.

### **PILLAR 5 — Economic Disadvantages**

- **Higher Production Cost:**
  Current biodiesel cost: **₹ 65–85/L** vs. diesel **₹ 65–95/L** (fossil diesel is subsidized; market is not level).
- **Feedstock Price Volatility:**
  Crude vegetable oil prices fluctuate **± 30–50%** per year, making biodiesel plant economics unstable.
- **High Capital Investment:**
  2G lignocellulosic ethanol plant: **₹ 100–150 crores for 50,000 L/day** capacity.
- **Distribution Infrastructure:**
  E20/E85 stations cost **₹ 1–3 crores** each to retrofit; pure ethanol is corrosive to existing pipelines.

### **PILLAR 6 — Technical Disadvantages**

- **Lower Energy Density:**
  - Ethanol: **LHV = 26.8 MJ/kg** (vs. petrol **43.4 MJ/kg**) → **~ 38% lower**.
  - Biodiesel: **LHV = 37.2 MJ/kg** (vs. diesel **42.5 MJ/kg**) → **~ 12% lower**.
  - Biogas: **LHV = 18–26 MJ/m³** (vs. CNG **35–38 MJ/m³**).
- **Hygroscopicity (Moisture Absorption):**
  Ethanol absorbs water → phase separation in fuel tanks, corrosion of metal components.
- **Cold-Flow Properties:**
  Biodiesel has a **Cloud Point (CP) of 0–15°C** for soy/palm → gels in winter (problematic in North India).
- **Engine Compatibility Issues:**
  - **NOₓ Increase:** Biodiesel can increase NOₓ by **5–15%** due to higher cetane and bound oxygen.
  - **Polymerization:** Pure biodiesel degrades to gums/polymers on long storage (> 6 months).
- **Lower Volatility:** Ethanol's high latent heat of vaporization (**841 kJ/kg**) causes **cold-start difficulties** in pure form.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Following Table is the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT REVISION TOOL for this topic. Memorize it.**

| Property | Petrol (Gasoline) | Diesel | Bioethanol (E100) | Biodiesel (B100) | Biogas (CH₄ 60%) |
|----------|------------------|--------|-------------------|------------------|------------------|
| **LHV (MJ/kg)** | **43.4** | **42.5** | **26.8** | **37.2** | **~ 22** (per m³: 18–26) |
| **Density (kg/L)** | 0.74 | 0.85 | 0.79 | 0.88 | ~ 1.16 kg/m³ |
| **Cetane Number** | 8–14 | 45–55 | 5–8 | **45–65** | N/A (CNG substitute) |
| **Octane Number (RON)** | 91–98 | N/A | **108–110** | 25–30 | 120–130 |
| **Flash Point (°C)** | –43 | 55–65 | **12** | **120–170** | < –188 (CH₄) |
| **Sulfur (ppm)** | < 50 | < 10 (ULSD) | < 5 | < 10 | < 10 |
| **Biodegradability (28 days)** | < 30% | < 30% | > 90% | **> 90%** | N/A |
| **LCA-GHG vs. fossil** | 100% (baseline) | 100% | **–60 to –90%** | **–40 to –80%** | **> –90%** |
| **Water Footprint (L/L)** | < 50 | < 30 | 1,000–4,000 | 4,000–14,000 | < 50 |
| **Food-vs-Fuel Conflict** | None | None | **HIGH** | **HIGH (1G)** | **NONE** (waste-based) |
| **Cost in India (₹/L, 2024)** | ~ 95–105 | ~ 90–95 | ~ 55–65 (bulk) | ~ 65–85 | ~ 25–40 (thermal) |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This comparative framework is used by:

- **Automotive OEMs** (Tata, Mahindra, Ashok Leyland) — to certify engines for **E20/B20** compliance.
- **MNRE & NITI Aayog** — to formulate the **National Biofuel Policy (2018, amended 2022)**.
- **Oil Marketing Companies (OMCs)** — IOCL, BPCL, HPCL — for ethanol procurement pricing (EBP).
- **International Bodies** — **IEA Bioenergy**, **IRENA** (International Renewable Energy Agency), **GHG Protocol** for carbon credit certification under **CORSIA** (aviation biofuels).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Net Energy Ratio (NER) for a Biofuel Pathway

The **Net Energy Ratio (NER)** is the **single most important metric** to assess whether a biofuel is energetically viable. The KTU 2024 examiner often tests this.

### **Step 1 — Define NER**

The Net Energy Ratio is defined as the ratio of the **energy output** of the biofuel to the **total fossil-energy input** consumed in its entire life cycle (agriculture + conversion + distribution).

$$\text{NER} = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in,fossil}}}$$

### **Step 2 — Express the Energy Terms**

- $E_{\text{out}}$ = Energy content of the biofuel = $M_{\text{fuel}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{fuel}}$ (in MJ).
- $E_{\text{in,fossil}}$ = sum of all fossil-derived energy inputs:

$$E_{\text{in,fossil}} = E_{\text{agri}} + E_{\text{conv}} + E_{\text{trans}}$$

where:
- $E_{\text{agri}}$ = energy for fertilizers, diesel (tractor), irrigation, harvesting.
- $E_{\text{conv}}$ = energy for steam, electricity, enzymes in the biorefinery.
- $E_{\text{trans}}$ = energy for feedstock transport and biofuel distribution.

### **Step 3 — Worked Example: Bioethanol from Sugarcane (Brazilian Model)**

**Given Data (1 hectare of sugarcane, 1 year):**
- Sugarcane yield: **$M_{\text{sc}} = 80 \text{ tonnes/ha/yr}$**
- Ethanol yield: **80 L ethanol per tonne sugarcane** → $M_{\text{EtOH}} = 6{,}400 \text{ L/ha/yr}$
- Density of ethanol: **$\rho_{\text{EtOH}} = 0.79 \text{ kg/L}$**
- LHV of ethanol: **$\text{LHV}_{\text{EtOH}} = 26.8 \text{ MJ/kg}$**
- Fossil energy input (well-established LCA literature): **$E_{\text{in,fossil}} = 12{,}000 \text{ MJ/ha/yr}$** (largely from fertilizers and boiler heat from bagasse, partially fossil-backstopped).

**Step 3a — Compute $E_{\text{out}}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{out}} &= M_{\text{EtOH}} \times \rho_{\text{EtOH}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{EtOH}} \\
&= 6{,}400 \text{ L} \times 0.79 \text{ kg/L} \times 26.8 \text{ MJ/kg} \\
&= 5{,}056 \text{ kg} \times 26.8 \text{ MJ/kg} \\
&= 135{,}500.8 \text{ MJ/ha/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3b — Compute NER:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NER} &= \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in,fossil}}} = \frac{135{,}500.8 \text{ MJ/ha/yr}}{12{,}000 \text{ MJ/ha/yr}} \\
&\approx 11.3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3c — Interpretation:**

> [!NOTE]
> **NER = 11.3 >> 1** ⇒ Sugarcane ethanol is **highly energetically favorable**. Each **1 MJ** of fossil input yields **11.3 MJ** of ethanol. This is the **gold standard** of 1G bioenergy. The bagasse (fibrous residue, **~ 270 kg/tonne cane**) provides process heat, making the system nearly self-sufficient in energy.

### **Step 4 — Worked Example: Biodiesel from Soybean**

**Given Data (1 hectare, 1 year):**
- Soybean yield: **$M_{\text{soy}} = 2.8 \text{ tonnes/ha/yr}$**
- Oil content: **18%** → Oil mass = $0.18 \times 2{,}800 = 504 \text{ kg/ha/yr}$
- Biodiesel yield (after transesterification, ~ 95%): **$M_{\text{B100}} = 504 \times 0.95 = 478.8 \text{ kg/ha/yr}$**
- LHV of biodiesel: **$\text{LHV}_{\text{B100}} = 37.2 \text{ MJ/kg}$**
- Fossil energy input: **$E_{\text{in,fossil}} = 22{,}000 \text{ MJ/ha/yr}$** (higher due to solvent recovery, methanol production, hexane extraction).

**Step 4a — Compute $E_{\text{out}}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{out}} &= M_{\text{B100}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{B100}} \\
&= 478.8 \text{ kg} \times 37.2 \text{ MJ/kg} \\
&= 17{,}811.4 \text{ MJ/ha/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4b — Compute NER:**

$$\text{NER} = \frac{17{,}811.4}{22{,}000} \approx 0.81$$

> [!WARNING]
> **NER = 0.81 < 1** ⇒ Soybean biodiesel is an **energy LOSER** at the well-to-wheel scale! More fossil energy goes in than comes out. This is why soybean biodiesel has poor NER and **should be discouraged** in favor of **UCO (Used Cooking Oil) biodiesel** or **algal biodiesel** (NER = 1.5–3.0).

## 3.2 Derivation: GHG Emission Reduction Percentage

### **Step 1 — Define LCA-GHG Reduction**

$$\text{GHG Reduction (\%)} = \frac{\text{GHG}_{\text{fossil}} - \text{GHG}_{\text{biofuel}}}{\text{GHG}_{\text{fossil}}} \times 100\%$$

### **Step 2 — Worked Example: UCO Biodiesel vs. Diesel**

**Given (GREET Model, well-to-wheel, India context):**
- Diesel well-to-wheel GHG: **$\text{GHG}_{\text{diesel}} = 87 \text{ g CO}_2\text{e/MJ}$**
- UCO Biodiesel (B100) well-to-wheel GHG: **$\text{GHG}_{\text{UCO}} = 14 \text{ g CO}_2\text{e/MJ}$** (low because feedstock has zero agricultural energy and no land-use change).

**Step 2a — Compute the Reduction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{GHG Reduction} &= \frac{87 - 14}{87} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{73}{87} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 83.9\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** UCO biodiesel delivers a **~ 84% reduction** in well-to-wheel GHG emissions vs. mineral diesel. This satisfies the **EU RED-II (Renewable Energy Directive)** threshold of **≥ 65%** for biofuels to qualify as "renewable transport fuel."

## 3.3 Comparison: First-Generation vs. Second-Generation Biofuels

| Parameter | 1G (Food-based) | 2G (Lignocellulosic) | 3G (Algal) |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|------------|
| **Feedstock** | Sugarcane, maize, soy, palm | Bagasse, straw, wood waste | Microalgae, macroalgae |
| **Food Conflict** | **SEVERE** | **NONE** | **NONE** |
| **LHV (MJ/kg)** | 26.8–37.2 | 26.8–37.2 | 36–40 |
| **NER** | 1.3–11 (sugarcane high) | 2–6 | 1.5–3.0 |
| **LCA-GHG reduction** | 40–90% | 60–110% | 50–90% |
| **Water Footprint** | HIGH | MODERATE | LOW (can use saline) |
| **Land Use (m² yr/GJ)** | ~ 200–500 | ~ 50–150 | ~ 10–50 |
| **Cost (₹/L)** | 55–85 | 70–110 | 100–200+ |
| **Technology Readiness Level (TRL)** | **9 (Commercial)** | **6–7 (Pilot/Demo)** | **4–5 (Lab)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This diagram is the **KTU board examiner's** preferred way to structure answers on "Advantages and Disadvantages of Biofuels."

flowchart LR

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**Define the term "Net Energy Ratio" (NER) of a biofuel. State the typical NER values for sugarcane bioethanol and soybean biodiesel. Comment on the energy viability of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Net Energy Ratio (NER)** of a biofuel is defined as the ratio of the energy output of the biofuel to the total fossil energy input consumed in its entire life-cycle (agriculture, conversion, and distribution).
>
> $$\text{NER} = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in,fossil}}}$$
>
> - **Sugarcane bioethanol (Brazilian model):** NER ≈ **8–11** (highly viable, energy-positive).
> - **Soybean biodiesel:** NER ≈ **0.8–1.2** (marginal to non-viable; energy-negative in some studies).
>
> A biofuel is energetically sustainable **only if NER > 1**. Sugarcane ethanol easily clears this benchmark, while soybean biodiesel is marginal and should be replaced by UCO or algal biodiesel. **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**List any three environmental advantages and three environmental disadvantages of biofuels.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **Carbon-neutrality** — CO₂ released on combustion is re-absorbed by the next biomass growth cycle.
2. **Low SO₂ and PM emissions** — sulfur content < 10 ppm, eliminating acid rain and respiratory particulates.
3. **High biodegradability** — > 90% degraded in 28 days (OECD 301B), preventing soil/water contamination.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Food-vs-Fuel conflict** — 1G crops compete with food security (e.g., maize ethanol in the USA).
2. **High water footprint** — up to 14,000 L water/L biodiesel for soybean.
3. **Indirect Land-Use Change (ILUC)** — deforestation releases a "carbon debt" of 50–100 t CO₂/ha, repayable only over **100–300 years**. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **environmental advantages** of biofuels with reference to (i) carbon-neutrality principle, (ii) reduction in GHG emissions with LCA data, and (iii) biodegradability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Critically evaluate the **disadvantages of first-generation biofuels** in terms of food-vs-fuel conflict, water footprint, and indirect land-use change (ILUC).

---

#### **Model Solution for (a): Environmental Advantages of Biofuels**

**(i) Carbon-Neutrality Principle (2 Marks):**
- Biofuels are produced from biomass, which absorbs atmospheric CO₂ during photosynthesis via the reaction:
  $$\begin{aligned} 6\text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} &\xrightarrow{\text{sunlight}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\text{O}_2 \end{aligned}$$
- The CO₂ released during combustion is, in principle, re-absorbed by the next generation of biomass, resulting in a **closed-loop carbon cycle** with theoretically **net-zero** atmospheric CO₂ impact. The growth-harvest cycle is short (**1 year** for sugarcane, **3–7 years** for short-rotation woody crops), enabling rapid carbon recycling.

**(ii) Reduction in GHG Emissions (LCA Data) (3 Marks):**
- **Bioethanol from sugarcane:** **60–90%** lower well-to-wheel GHG vs. petrol (GREET, EIO-LCA models).
- **Biodiesel from Used Cooking Oil (UCO):** **~ 80–95%** lower GHG vs. diesel.
- **Biogas from agricultural residue / MSW:** **> 90%** lower GHG vs. CNG.
- **2G cellulosic ethanol (e.g., from bagasse, rice straw):** **> 100%** potential (i.e., **carbon-negative** when co-products displace fossil equivalents like electricity or LPG).
- **Sulfur and particulate reductions:** Sulfur content < 10 ppm (vs. 350 ppm in diesel); PM 2.5 emissions reduced by **30–50%** for biodiesel blends (B20–B100).

**(iii) Biodegradability (2 Marks):**
- Pure biodiesel (B100) is **> 90% biodegradable** within **28 days** as per the **OECD 301B** standard test, while petroleum diesel requires **> 28 years** for equivalent degradation.
- This prevents long-term soil/water contamination in the event of spills (especially relevant for marine and inland waterway transport).
- Bioethanol is fully miscible with water and is rapidly metabolized by natural microbial populations in soils and aquifers.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b): Disadvantages of First-Generation Biofuels**

**(i) Food-vs-Fuel Conflict (3 Marks):**
- 1G biofuels use edible feedstocks: **sugarcane, maize, soybean, rapeseed, palm oil, jatropha**.
- Global ethanol production in **2022** used **~ 170 million tonnes of maize**, equivalent to the annual caloric intake of **~ 400 million people**.
- In India, the **EBP (Ethanol Blending Programme)** used **~ 1,200 million litres** of sugarcane ethanol in 2022–23, diverting feedstock from sugar mills and inflating domestic sugar prices.
- This creates **price volatility** in food markets and is socially regressive (hurts low-income consumers).

**(ii) Water Footprint (2 Marks):**
- 1G biofuels are **water-intensive**:
  - Sugarcane ethanol: **1,000–4,000 L water per L ethanol** (mostly blue water for irrigation).
  - Soybean biodiesel: **4,000–14,000 L water per L biodiesel**.
- India, being water-stressed (**per capita availability < 1,500 m³**), cannot sustainably scale 1G biofuels. **2G and 3G** pathways are preferred.

**(iii) Indirect Land-Use Change – ILUC (2 Marks):**
- Expanding 1G crop cultivation into forests/grasslands releases **"carbon debt"** through biomass burning and soil organic carbon loss.
- Estimated debt: **50–100 t CO₂/ha** for tropical deforestation (palm oil, soybean).
- **Repayment time** of this debt through biofuel displacement: **100–300 years**, negating climate benefits in the short term.
- Mitigation: **2G biofuels** from agricultural waste and **3G algae** on marginal land.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **technical properties** (LHV, cetane number, octane number, water content) of bioethanol and biodiesel with their fossil-fuel counterparts. Discuss the engineering implications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a Net Energy Ratio (NER) calculation, justify why **bagasse-based 2G bioethanol** is preferable to **soybean-based 1G biodiesel** for the Indian context.

---

#### **Model Solution for (a): Technical Property Comparison**

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Property | Petrol | Diesel | Bioethanol (E100) | Biodiesel (B100) |
|----------|--------|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 43.4 | 42.5 | **26.8** | **37.2** |
| Cetane Number | 8–14 | 45–55 | 5–8 | **45–65** |
| Octane (RON) | 91–98 | N/A | **108–110** | 25–30 |
| Flash Point (°C) | –43 | 55–65 | **12** | **120–170** |
| Density (kg/L) | 0.74 | 0.85 | 0.79 | 0.88 |
| Sulfur (ppm) | < 50 | < 10 | < 5 | < 10 |

**Engineering Implications (3 Marks):**
- **LHV deficit** → Larger fuel tanks needed for same range (for E100: ~ 1.6× tank volume; for B100: ~ 1.14×).
- **High cetane of biodiesel** → Smooth CI engine operation, lower emissions; high octane of ethanol → enables higher compression ratio engines (future-proofing).
- **High flash point of biodiesel** → Safer storage/transport, qualifies as a non-flammable liquid in many jurisdictions.
- **High latent heat of ethanol vaporization (841 kJ/kg)** → Cold-start issues → engine management systems with **flex-fuel sensors** needed.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b): NER-Based Justification**

**Bagasse-based 2G Ethanol (1 ha equivalent, bagasse from 80 t sugarcane = ~ 22 t bagasse):**
- Cellulose-to-glucose yield (after pretreatment): **~ 80%**
- Ethanol yield from bagasse: **~ 280 L/tonne bagasse** → $M_{\text{EtOH}} = 22{,}000 \times 0.28 / 0.79 = 7{,}797 \text{ kg/ha}$
- $E_{\text{out}} = 7{,}797 \times 26.8 = 208{,}960 \text{ MJ}$
- $E_{\text{in,fossil}} = 28{,}000 \text{ MJ}$ (pretreatment + enzymatic hydrolysis is energy-intensive)
- **NER = 208,960 / 28,000 ≈ 7.46** (very high; also avoids food conflict).

**Soybean 1G Biodiesel (Recap from Section 3.1):**
- $E_{\text{out}} = 17{,}811 \text{ MJ}$
- $E_{\text{in,fossil}} = 22{,}000 \text{ MJ}$
- **NER ≈ 0.81** (energy-negative).

**Justification (3 Marks):**
- **Bagasse 2G ethanol has NER ≈ 7.46 vs. soybean biodiesel NER ≈ 0.81** — nearly **9.2×** higher energy efficiency.
- Bagasse is an **agricultural waste** (no food conflict, no ILUC, no extra land use).
- India's **SATAT (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation)** scheme targets **5,000 crore litres/year of 2G ethanol by 2025–26**, leveraging surplus bagasse and rice straw.
- Hence, **bagasse-based 2G ethanol is decisively preferable** to soybean biodiesel for the Indian context on energy, food-security, and sustainability grounds.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never write vague answers** like "biofuels are renewable" or "biofuels are green" — these fetch **0 marks**. Always support with **quantified LCA data** (e.g., "**~ 84% GHG reduction**").
> 2. **Confusing cetane and octane** is a common error. **Cetane = diesel quality; Octane = petrol quality**. Ethanol has **high octane, low cetane**.
> 3. **Misapplying LHV vs. HHV** — for gas-phase fuels (biogas, CNG), use **LHV** (water leaves as vapor); for liquid biofuels, both are valid but specify which one.
> 4. **Ignoring the "**with respect to**"** clause in KTU questions — the examiner expects comparative statements (e.g., "60–90% **lower than petrol**", not just "60–90% lower").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before the exam)**

- **Definition:** Biofuels = fuels derived from biomass (recent biological origin); they are **renewable** and (ideally) **carbon-neutral**.
- **Four Pillars of Assessment:** Environmental, Economic, Technical/Energy, Socio-Political.
- **Carbon-Neutral Equation:** $\text{Net CO}_2 \approx \text{CO}_2^{\text{combustion}} - \text{CO}_2^{\text{uptake}} \to 0$ for closed-loop biomass.
- **Net Energy Ratio (NER):** $\text{NER} = E_{\text{out}} / E_{\text{in,fossil}}$. Must be **> 1** for sustainability.
- **Top Advantages:** Carbon-neutral, low SO₂/PM, biodegradable > 90% in 28 days, forex savings, rural jobs, high cetane (biodiesel) / high octane (ethanol).
- **Top Disadvantages:** Food-vs-fuel (1G), ILUC carbon debt (50–100 t CO₂/ha), high water footprint (1,000–14,000 L/L), lower LHV (26.8–37.2 MJ/kg), hygroscopicity (ethanol), cold-flow (biodiesel), NER < 1 (soybean biodiesel), high CAPEX (2G/3G).
- **Key Numerical Benchmarks to Memorize:**
  - LHV of petrol = **43.4 MJ/kg**; diesel = **42.5**; ethanol = **26.8**; biodiesel = **37.2**.
  - Cetane: diesel = **45–55**; biodiesel = **45–65**.
  - Octane: petrol = **91–98**; ethanol = **108–110**.
  - Flash point: petrol = **–43°C**; biodiesel = **120–170°C** (safer).
  - India's ethanol target = **E20 by 2025–26** (saves **~ ₹ 30,000 cr/year**).
  - India's target: **5,000 crore litres/year of 2G ethanol (SATAT)**.
- **Generation-Wise Trade-off:** 1G = Mature (TRL 9) but food conflict → 2G = No food conflict, TRL 6–7 → 3G = Best land efficiency, TRL 4–5.
- **Algal Biofuel Advantage:** Uses **non-arable land**, **saline/brackish water**, and has **> 30× higher oil yield per hectare** than soybean.
- **Glycerol By-Product:** Biodiesel produces **~ 10 wt% glycerol** → valorized into **cosmetics, pharma, and propylene glycol** (revenue stream).
- **DDGS By-Product:** Bioethanol produces **DDGS (Dried Distillers Grains with Solubles)** → high-protein **cattle feed** (~ 30% crude protein).
- **Policy Reference (India):** **National Policy on Biofuels (2018, amended 2022)** targets **20% ethanol blending** and **5% biodiesel blending (B5)** by 2030.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Advantages and Disadvantages | PEBBT413 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
