Continuous Digesters** Feed is added and effluent removed continuously, achieving steady-state operation. #### **B.1 Conventional / Traditional Digesters** | Type | Origin | Gas Storage | Key Feature | |---|---|---|---| | **Fixed Dome (Chinese)** | China | Built into the dome itself | No moving parts, low cost, constant gas pressure | | **Floating Drum (Indian / KVIC)** | India | Steel drum floats up/down | Constant gas pressure, expensive due to steel corrosion | #### **B.2 High-Rate / Modern Digesters** | Type | Configuration | Application | |---|---|---| | **CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor)** | Fully mixed | High-solids slurry, municipal waste | | **Plug Flow / Tubular** | Piston-like flow | Cattle manure (high fibre) | | **UASB (Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket)** | Upward flow through sludge blanket | Low-strength wastewater (sugarcane, distillery) | | **Anaerobic Filter (AF)** | Biomass attached to media | Industrial effluent | | **Anaerobic Baffled Reactor (ABR)** | Compartmentalised | Domestic wastewater | | **EGSB (Expanded Granular Sludge Bed)** | High upflow velocity | Cold effluents, low strength | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋 | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_{d} = Q \times \theta_{HRT}$ | Digester volume = daily feed × hydraulic retention time | m³ | | 2 | $\theta_{HRT} = \dfrac{V_{d}}{Q}$ | Hydraulic Retention Time | days | | 3 | $OLR = \dfrac{Q \times S_{0}}{V_{d}}$ | Organic Loading Rate | kg VS/m³·day | | 4 | $SRT = \dfrac{M_{biomass}}{Q \times X_{eff}}$ | Solids Retention Time (biomass inside / biomass washed out per day) | days | | 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{S_{0} - S_{e}}{S_{0}} \times 100$ | COD/VS destruction efficiency | % | | 6 | $Q_{CH_{4}} = 0.35 \times Q \times (S_{0} - S_{e})$ | Theoretical methane yield (Buswell equation constant = 0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD) | m³ CH₄/day | | 7 | $Q_{gas} = Q_{CH_{4}} / f_{CH_{4}}$ | Total biogas flow (CH₄ fraction $f_{CH_4} \approx 0.6$) | m³/day | | 8 | $E = Q_{gas} \times CV$ | Energy output (CV ≈ 22 MJ/m³ for biogas) | MJ/day | | 9 | $P = \dfrac{E}{t \times \eta_{gen}}$ | Electrical power output (η_gen ≈ 0.35) | kW | | 10 | $V_{gas,stored} = Q_{gas,daily} \times N_{days}$ | Required gas holder volume (N = storage days) | m³ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Conditions to remember:** > - $\theta_{HRT}$ must be **longer** than the doubling time of methanogens (≈ 5 days at 35 °C), otherwise washout occurs. > - $OLR$ must stay **below the toxic limit** (typically < 4 kg VS/m³·day for mesophilic CSTR). > - For **UASB**, OLR can be as high as **10–15 kg COD/m³·day** due to sludge retention. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 🏭 | Industry / Application | Digester Type | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Dairy farms | Plug flow / CSTR | Manure → electricity + bedding | | Municipal sewage | UASB / EGSB | Sludge stabilisation + wastewater treatment | | Distilleries (molasses-based) | UASB | High-strength effluent treatment (COD 50,000 mg/L) | | Food processing | CSTR / ABR | Organic waste valorisation | | Rural household | KVIC / Fixed dome | Cooking fuel from kitchen waste | --- # 📙 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sizing the Digester Volume A small dairy farm generates **500 kg/day** of cattle dung with a **Total Solids (TS) content of 16 %** and a **Volatile Solids (VS) fraction of 80 %** of TS. The farm wants to install a **mesophilic CSTR digester** with an HRT of **30 days** and produce biogas for cooking. **Given:** - Mass of dung, $M = 500 \text{ kg/day}$ - $TS = 16\%$, $VS/TS = 80\%$ - $\theta_{HRT} = 30 \text{ days}$ - Slurry water-to-dung ratio, $R_w = 1:1$ (typical) **Step 1 — Volatile Solids added per day** $$S_{0,in} = M \times TS \times \frac{VS}{TS}$$ $$S_{0,in} = 500 \times 0.16 \times 0.80 = 64 \text{ kg VS/day}$$ **Step 2 — Total slurry volume per day (assuming slurry density ≈ 1000 kg/m³)** $$Q = \dfrac{M + M_{water}}{\rho_{slurry}} = \dfrac{500 + 500}{1000} = 1.0 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}$$ **Step 3 — Required digester volume (using HRT definition)** $$V_{d} = Q \times \theta_{HRT}$$ $$V_{d} = 1.0 \times 30 = 30 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 4 — Organic Loading Rate check** $$OLR = \dfrac{Q \times S_{0,conc}}{V_{d}}$$ where $S_{0,conc} = \dfrac{64 \text{ kg VS/day}}{1.0 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}} = 64 \text{ kg VS/m}^3$ $$OLR = \dfrac{1.0 \times 64}{30} = 2.13 \text{ kg VS/m}^3\text{·day}$$ ✅ Within the safe mesophilic range (1.0–4.0 kg VS/m³·day). **Step 5 — Biogas production estimate** Assume methane yield $Y_{CH_4} = 0.25 \text{ m}^3\text{ CH}_4\text{/kg VS destroyed}$ and VS destruction efficiency $\eta = 60\%$. $$Q_{CH_4} = Y_{CH_4} \times S_{0,in} \times \eta$$ $$Q_{CH_4} = 0.25 \times 64 \times 0.60 = 9.6 \text{ m}^3\text{ CH}_4\text{/day}$$ Total biogas at $f_{CH_4} = 0.6$: $$Q_{gas} = \dfrac{9.6}{0.6} = 16.0 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}$$ **Step 6 — Cooking equivalence** One typical Indian household consumes ≈ 0.5 m³ of biogas/day for cooking. $$\text{Households served} = \dfrac{16.0}{0.5} = 32 \text{ households}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Detention Time & Microbial Washout Criterion For a CSTR, the steady-state biomass balance is: $$X \cdot V \cdot \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S} = Q \cdot X$$ At washout, the SRT equals the **inverse of the maximum specific growth rate**: $$\theta_{SRT,min} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{max}}$$ For methanogens at 35 °C, $\mu_{max} = 0.0167 \text{ h}^{-1}$ (doubling time ≈ 60 h ≈ 2.5 days): $$\theta_{SRT,min} = \dfrac{1}{0.0167} \approx 60 \text{ h} = 2.5 \text{ days}$$ **Step-by-step conversion logic:** 1. Convert μₘₐₓ from h⁻¹ to day⁻¹: $$\mu_{max} = 0.0167 \text{ h}^{-1} \times 24 \text{ h/day} = 0.4 \text{ day}^{-1}$$ 2. Apply the washout criterion: $$\theta_{SRT,min} = \dfrac{1}{0.4} = 2.5 \text{ days}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design rule of thumb:** Actual HRT must be **≥ 2 × θ_SRT,min** as a safety margin, so the design HRT for a mesophilic CSTR should be **≥ 5 days** for a methanogen-dominated system. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Fixed Dome vs. Floating Drum | Design Parameter | Fixed Dome (Chinese) | Floating Drum (KVIC) | |---|---|---| | Construction cost | **Low** (brick/masonry) | **High** (steel drum) | | Gas pressure | Variable (3–12 kPa) | **Constant** (≈ 4–8 kPa) | | Corrosion risk | **None** | High (H₂S attacks steel) | | Lifespan | **20–30 years** | 8–15 years (drum rusts) | | Gas leakage | Possible at joints | **Negligible** (sealed) | | Maintenance | Difficult inspection | Easy inspection | | Heat retention | **Better** (thick masonry) | Poor (thin metal) | | Common in KTU exam? | ✅ Frequently asked | ✅ Frequently asked | --- # 📕 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Working Principle — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a biogas digester — showing how the four biochemical stages, the physical subsystems (inlet, digester, gasholder, outlet), and the energy/material flows interact in a continuous, closed-loop system. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FEED["FEEDSTOCK INTAKE"] A1["BIOMASS INPUT
Cow dung, kitchen waste,
crop residue, sewage sludge"] end subgraph MIX["SLURRY PREPARATION TANK"] A2["WATER + BIOMASS MIXING
TS adjusted to 8-12 percent
C/N ratio checked"] end subgraph DIG["ANAEROBIC DIGESTION CHAMBER (SEALED, O2-FREE)"] direction TB S1["STAGE 1 HYDROLYSIS
Polymers to Monomers
Duration 1 to 3 days"] S2["STAGE 2 ACIDOGENESIS
Monomers to VFAs, H2, CO2
Duration 2 to 5 days"] S3["STAGE 3 ACETOGENESIS
VFAs to Acetate, H2
Duration 5 to 10 days"] S4["STAGE 4 METHANOGENESIS
Acetate and H2 to CH4
Duration 10 to 40 days"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 end subgraph GAS["BIOGAS COLLECTION AND STORAGE"] G1["GAS HOLDER / DOME
Stores CH4 50-70 percent
CO2 30-40 percent, H2S trace"] G2["PURIFICATION UNIT
H2S removal via iron oxide
CO2 removal via water scrubbing"] end subgraph USE["END USE APPLICATIONS"] U1["COOKING GAS
Domestic burners"] U2["ELECTRICITY
Gas engine / CHP"] U3["CNG UPGRADATION
Vehicle fuel after CO2 stripping"] end subgraph OUT["DIGESTATE OUTLET"] D1["LIQUID FRACTION
Nitrogen rich biofertilizer"] D2["SOLID FRACTION
Compost, soil conditioner"] end A1 --> A2 A2 -->|Feed slurry| S1 S4 -->|Biogas rises| G1 G1 --> G2 G2 --> U1 G2 --> U2 G2 --> U3 S4 -->|Effluent overflow| D1 D1 -->|Solid separation| D2 D2 -.->|Recycle to field| A1 ``` --- ## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Functional Schematic — KVIC Floating Drum Digester ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph KVIC["KVIC FLOATING DRUM DIGESTER (CROSS SECTION VIEW)"] direction TB IN["INLET CHAMBER
Daily charging of dung slurry
Connected to mixing tank"] DG["DIGESTION TANK
Underground masonry chamber
HRT 30 to 40 days
Working volume Vd"] DC["DISPLACEMENT / OUTLET CHAMBER
Effluent overflow point
Connected to slurry pit"] FH["FLOATING GAS HOLDER (STEEL DRUM)
Inverted drum rises with gas volume
Constant gas pressure maintained"] GS["GAS OUTLET PIPE
Carries biogas to burner / engine"] end IN -->|Slurry flows down| DG DG -->|Effluent rises by displacement| DC DG -.->|Biogas bubbles upward| FH FH -->|Pressurised gas| GS DC -->|Spent slurry| SP["SLURRY PIT
Storage for field application"] GS --> BURNER["COOKING BURNER / ENGINE"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — UASB Reactor ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph UASB["UASB REACTOR (UPFLOW ANAEROBIC SLUDGE BLANKET)"] direction TB INF["INFLUENT
Low to medium strength wastewater
COD 1000 to 50000 mg per L"] DIST["INFLOW DISTRIBUTOR
Ensures uniform upflow at base"] SB["SLUDGE BLANKET
Granular biomass
SRT 30 to 100 days
HRT 4 to 12 hours"] GSS["GAS SOLID SEPARATOR
Three phase separator at top"] EF["EFFLUENT WEIR
Treated water overflows"] end INF --> DIST --> SB --> GSS --> EF GSS -.->|Biogas captured| GAS["BIOGAS TO USE"] EF -.->|Treated water| DIS["DISCHARGE"] ``` --- # 📒 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) — Short Answer --- ### **Q1.** *Define Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT). Why must it be longer than the doubling time of methanogens in a biogas digester?* **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 mark):** Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) is the average time a unit volume of feedstock slurry spends inside the digester. $$\theta_{HRT} = \dfrac{V_{d}}{Q}$$ **Why it must exceed the methanogen doubling time (2 marks):** Methanogenic archaea grow very slowly (doubling time ≈ 2.5 days at 35 °C). If the HRT is shorter than this doubling time, the microbes are washed out of the digester with the effluent faster than they can reproduce, leading to **biomass washout** and collapse of methane production. Therefore, HRT is designed at **15–40 days** for cattle-dung-based digesters, providing a large safety margin over the minimum SRT of ~2.5 days. --- ### **Q2.** *Compare Fixed Dome (Chinese) and Floating Drum (KVIC) biogas digesters based on gas pressure variation and lifespan.* **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Fixed Dome (Chinese) | Floating Drum (KVIC) | |---|---|---| | **Gas pressure** | **Variable** — rises as gas accumulates, falls when used (3–12 kPa) | **Constant** — floating drum maintains steady pressure (~4–8 kPa) | | **Lifespan** | **20–30 years** (brick/masonry, no corrosion) | **8–15 years** (steel drum corrodes due to H₂S and moisture) | | **Cost** | Low | High (steel + anti-corrosion paint) | (1 mark per row of comparison, plus 1 mark for stating which is preferred and why.) --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### **Q.3 (A)** *With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of a biogas plant. Describe the four stages of anaerobic digestion and the role of microbes in each stage.* **[14 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Distribution:** (a) Schematic + working principle → 7 marks; (b) Four stages with microbial role → 7 marks ### **Model Solution:** #### **(a) Schematic & Working Principle (7 Marks)** A biogas plant is a sealed reactor comprising the following components: 1. **Inlet / Feeding tank** (1 mark) — receives and mixes dung with water to obtain a slurry of 8–12 % TS. 2. **Digester chamber** (2 marks) — an airtight underground tank where anaerobic digestion occurs at mesophilic (35 °C) or thermophilic (55 °C) temperatures. HRT is maintained at 15–40 days. 3. **Gas collection dome / holder** (2 marks) — biogas (CH₄ 50–70 %, CO₂ 30–40 %, traces of H₂S, NH₃) accumulates at the top, displacing slurry into the outlet chamber. 4. **Outlet / displacement chamber** (1 mark) — overflow point for the spent slurry (digestate), which is rich in N, P, K and is used as biofertiliser. 5. **Gas outlet pipe** (1 mark) — carries pressurised biogas to a burner, engine, or purification unit. Working principle: organic matter is converted by a **syntrophic microbial consortium** in the absence of oxygen into biogas and stabilised digestate. The four sequential stages are: #### **(b) Four Stages & Microbial Role (7 Marks)** | Stage | Substrate → Product | Duration | Key Microbes | |---|---|---|---| | **1. Hydrolysis** (2 marks) | Polymers (carbs, proteins, lipids) → Monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids) | Day 1–3 | *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides* (secrete extracellular enzymes) | | **2. Acidogenesis** (2 marks) | Monomers → VFAs (acetate, propionate, butyrate), alcohols, H₂, CO₂ | Day 2–5 | *Clostridium*, *Peptococcus* (fermentative bacteria) | | **3. Acetogenesis** (1.5 marks) | VFAs, alcohols → Acetate, H₂, CO₂ | Day 5–10 | *Syntrophobacter*, *Syntrophomonas* (syntrophic) | | **4. Methanogenesis** (1.5 marks) | Acetate + H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄ | Day 10–40 | *Methanosaeta*, *Methanosarcina* (acetoclastic); *Methanobacterium* (hydrogenotrophic) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. Students often **omit one of the four stages** (especially acetogenesis) — this loses **2 marks** outright. > 2. Writing only "bacteria do digestion" without naming the **genera** loses the **microbial specificity mark**. > 3. Failing to mention **syntrophy** between acetogens and methanogens (low H₂ partial pressure requirement) is a 1-mark loss. > 4. Not labelling the **schematic with HRT, temperature, and gas composition** loses up to 2 marks in part (a). --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) ### **Q.3 (B)** *(i) Explain the design features of a KVIC floating drum biogas plant with a neat sketch. (ii) A community requires 200 m³ of biogas per day for cooking. Assuming an HRT of 40 days and a slurry TS of 10 %, estimate the digester volume needed and the daily feed required.* **[7 + 7 = 14 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Solution:** #### **(i) Design Features of KVIC Floating Drum Plant (7 Marks)** | Component | Design Feature (mark split) | |---|---| | **Inlet pipe (1 mark)** | Diameter 100–150 mm, slope ≥ 1:6, with mixing tank above to homogenise dung-water slurry | | **Digester (2 marks)** | Underground cylindrical/brick masonry, depth 3–6 m; volume sized using $V_d = Q \cdot \theta_{HRT}$; masonry walls coated with waterproof sealant to prevent gas leakage | | **Floating gas holder (2 marks)** | Mild steel drum, 1.5–3 mm thick, inverted; floats on the slurry surface; rises/falls with gas volume → **constant gas pressure** of 4–8 kPa; central guide rod to prevent tilting; coated with anti-corrosion bitumen paint to resist H₂S | | **Outlet/Displacement chamber (1 mark)** | Connected to digester via baffle wall; effluent overflows by hydraulic displacement when new slurry is fed | | **Gas outlet pipe (1 mark)** | 12–25 mm GI pipe with valve, moisture trap, and pressure gauge; connects to burner or engine | #### **(ii) Numerical Sizing (7 Marks)** **Given:** $Q_{gas} = 200 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}$, $f_{CH_4} = 0.6$ (assume), $CV_{biogas} = 22 \text{ MJ/m}^3$, $\theta_{HRT} = 40 \text{ days}$, $TS = 10\%$, $\rho_{slurry} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **Step 1 — Methane requirement (1 mark)** $$Q_{CH_4} = 0.6 \times 200 = 120 \text{ m}^3\text{ CH}_4\text{/day}$$ **Step 2 — VS destroyed per day using $Y_{CH_4} = 0.25 \text{ m}^3\text{ CH}_4\text{/kg VS}$ (1 mark)** $$S_{0,in} \times \eta = \dfrac{Q_{CH_4}}{Y_{CH_4}} = \dfrac{120}{0.25} = 480 \text{ kg VS/day}$$ **Step 3 — Assume 60 % VS destruction efficiency (1 mark)** $$S_{0,in} = \dfrac{480}{0.60} = 800 \text{ kg VS/day}$$ **Step 4 — Total solids (TS) needed (1 mark)** $$TS_{required} = \dfrac{800}{0.80} = 1000 \text{ kg TS/day} \quad (VS/TS = 0.80)$$ **Step 5 — Wet dung mass (1 mark)** $$M_{dung} = \dfrac{1000}{0.10} = 10{,}000 \text{ kg dung/day} = 10 \text{ tonnes/day}$$ **Step 6 — Slurry volume (water : dung = 1:1) (1 mark)** $$Q = \dfrac{10{,}000 + 10{,}000}{1000} = 20 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}$$ **Step 7 — Digester volume (1 mark)** $$V_d = Q \times \theta_{HRT} = 20 \times 40 = 800 \text{ m}^3$$ ✅ **Answer:** Required digester volume = **800 m³**, daily feed = **10 tonnes of wet dung** (≈ 20 m³ slurry). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers for Numericals:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert** wet dung → dry solids → VS. Most students jump directly to volume — loses 2 marks. > 2. **Unit mismatches:** Writing HRT in hours instead of days (the formula is sensitive). > 3. **Assuming 100 % methane** in biogas instead of using $f_{CH_4} = 0.6$ — loses 1 mark in Step 1. > 4. Not specifying whether the answer is **wet mass or dry mass** of dung — vague answers lose the final mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡ - ✅ A **biogas digester** is an airtight, oxygen-free bioreactor for **anaerobic digestion** of biomass. - ✅ Biogas composition: **CH₄ (50–70 %) + CO₂ (30–40 %)**; calorific value ≈ **22 MJ/m³**. - ✅ The four stages **H-A-A-M** (Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis) are sequential and **syntrophic**. - ✅ Methanogenesis is the **rate-limiting** and most **temperature-sensitive** stage. - ✅ Optimum temperature: **Mesophilic 35 °C**, Thermophilic 55 °C. - ✅ Optimum pH: **6.5–7.5**; C : N ratio **20–30 : 1**. - ✅ **HRT** for cattle-dung digesters = **15–40 days**; minimum SRT for methanogens ≈ **2.5 days** (use 5+ day safety factor). - ✅ **OLR** for CSTR: **1–4 kg VS/m³·day**; for UASB: **10–15 kg COD/m³·day** (high biomass retention). - ✅ **Fixed Dome**: cheap, long life, variable pressure. **Floating Drum (KVIC)**: constant pressure, costly, shorter life. - ✅ **UASB** is preferred for **high-strength industrial wastewater** (distillery, sugar); CSTR/plug-flow for **solid-rich slurries** (cattle dung). - ✅ **Key formulas** to memorise: - $V_d = Q \times \theta_{HRT}$ - $OLR = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{V_d}$ - $\theta_{SRT,min} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{max}}$ - $Q_{CH_4} = Y \times S_0 \times \eta$ - ✅ **Design safety rule:** HRT ≥ 2 × methanogen doubling time, OLR ≤ toxic threshold, pH buffered above 6.5. - ✅ Common KTU exam keywords: *"syntrophy," "acetoclastic," "hydraulic retention time," "organic loading rate," "washout criterion," "UASB."* > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Strategy for KTU 2024:** > Memorise the **H-A-A-M table**, the **two formula sets** ($V_d$ and $Q_{CH_4}$), the **comparison between Fixed Dome and KVIC**, and **one labelled sketch** of a biogas plant — together they account for ≈ **70 %** of marks in any question on this topic. ---

topics.title: Microbial fuel cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Microbial fuel cells # ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Microbial Fuel Cell (MFC)** is a bio-electrochemical transducer (bioreactor) that converts the **chemical energy locked in biodegradable organic substrates** (e.g., wastewater, biomass, acetate, glucose) **directly into electrical energy** through the catalytic metabolic activity of living microorganisms functioning as **biocatalysts** under controlled (typically anaerobic) conditions at the anode. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition to Memorize:** > "A Microbial Fuel Cell (MFC) is a device that converts chemical energy to electrical energy by the action of microorganisms. It comprises an anode chamber (anaerobic) and a cathode chamber (aerobic) separated by a Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM), where microorganisms oxidize the substrate at the anode and release electrons that flow through an external circuit to the cathode." **Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary):** - **Exoelectrogens / Electrochemically Active Bacteria (EAB):** Bacteria capable of extracellular electron transfer (EET) to a solid electrode. Examples: *Geobacter sulfurreducens*, *Shewanella oneidensis*. - **Bioanode:** Anode colonized by electroactive biofilm. - **Biocathode:** Cathode where biological catalysts (e.g., microalgae, bacteria) assist oxygen reduction. - **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM):** A semi-permeable membrane (typically **Nafion 117**) that selectively transports **H⁺ ions** while blocking oxygen crossover. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview) > [!NOTE] > **Think of an MFC as a "Bacterial Battery":** > Imagine you have a sealed fish tank (anode chamber) filled with muddy wastewater. The mud contains tiny, microscopic workers (bacteria) that "eat" the sugars/waste in the water. While eating, they spit out electrons (like static electricity on a comb after combing hair) and protons (H⁺). The electrons want to escape, but they cannot cross a special wall (the membrane). Instead, they are forced to travel through a wire to the other side (cathode) — and this forced movement **IS electricity**, which can light a small LED bulb. Meanwhile, the protons sneak through the wall to meet the electrons on the other side, and together with oxygen, they form harmless water. So, an MFC is essentially a **"bio-battery"** that eats pollution and produces electricity while cleaning water! --- ## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold Constants/Parameters) | Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Value | Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) | $V_{oc}$ | **0.3 V – 0.8 V** | Theoretical max voltage with no load | | Cell Voltage under load | $V$ | **0.2 V – 0.5 V** | Practical operating voltage | | Power Density (volumetric) | $P_v$ | **10 – 1000 W/m³** | Power per unit reactor volume | | Power Density (areal) | $P_a$ | **0.1 – 10 W/m²** | Power per unit anode area | | Coulombic Efficiency | $C_E$ | **2 % – 80 %** | Fraction of electrons recovered as current | | Internal Resistance | $R_{int}$ | **10 – 1000 Ω** | Sum of all ohmic losses | | pH (anode) | – | **6.5 – 7.5** | Optimal for EAB | | Temperature | T | **20 °C – 40 °C** | Mesophilic operation range | | Faraday's Constant | F | **96,485 C/mol** | Charge per mole of electrons | | Substrate → Electrons (acetate) | n | **8 e⁻/acetate** | From CH₃COO⁻ + 4H₂O → 2HCO₃⁻ + 9H⁺ + 8e⁻ | --- # ⚡ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The operation of an MFC is governed by **anodic oxidation** (catalyzed by biofilm) coupled with **cathodic reduction**, separated spatially by a PEM to prevent direct substrate oxidation by oxygen. ### **STEP 1: Substrate Catabolism at the Bioanode (Anaerobic Chamber)** - The electroactive bacteria metabolize the organic substrate (e.g., acetate, glucose, lactate). - The oxidation half-reaction is mediated **intracellularly** by microbial enzymes (NADH, cytochromes) and the **electrons are exported** outside the cell via: - **Direct Electron Transfer (DET):** via outer-membrane c-type cytochromes or conductive pili ("**microbial nanowires**"). - **Mediated Electron Transfer (MET):** via self-produced or added **redox mediators** (e.g., methylene blue, neutral red, pyocyanin). **Generic Anodic Reaction (Acetate example):** $$CH_3COO^- + 4H_2O \longrightarrow 2HCO_3^- + 9H^+ + 8e^-$$ ### **STEP 2: Electron Flow Through External Circuit** - Electrons travel from the bioanode (low potential, e.g., **−0.3 V** vs. SHE) through the external load (wire) to the cathode. - This directional flow **is the current** ($I$). - A **potentiostat or data acquisition system** can measure voltage. ### **STEP 3: Proton Migration Through PEM** - Protons (H⁺) generated at the anode are **hydrated** and migrate through the **Nafion membrane** (or salt bridge in lab-scale dual-chamber designs) toward the cathode. - Protons maintain **electroneutrality** in the system. ### **STEP 4: Cathodic Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR)** - At the cathode, electrons, protons, and the terminal electron acceptor (O₂ from air) combine to form water. **Cathodic Reaction (in acidic/aqueous media):** $$O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \longrightarrow 2H_2O$$ ### **STEP 5: Net Cell Reaction & Theoretical Voltage** Adding the two half-reactions: $$CH_3COO^- + 2O_2 \longrightarrow 2HCO_3^- + H^+$$ - The standard **thermodynamic EMF** for this cell is **E°cell ≈ +0.877 V**, but the **practical OCV is only ~0.3 – 0.6 V** due to irreversible losses (overpotentials). --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **This table is the KTU exam formula master-list. Memorize every entry.** | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Unit | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Ohm's Law (MFC power)** | $P = V \times I$ | W (Watts) | Power delivered to external load | | 2 | **Power in terms of R** | $P = I^2 \times R_{ext}$ | W | Power maximized when $R_{ext} = R_{int}$ | | 3 | **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem** | $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^2}{4 \cdot R_{int}}$ | W | Peak power when load resistance equals internal resistance | | 4 | **Volumetric Power Density** | $P_v = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{V_{reactor}}$ | $\text{W/m}^3$ | Normalizes power to reactor size | | 5 | **Areal Power Density** | $P_a = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{A_{anode}}$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Normalizes power to electrode area | | 6 | **Coulombic Efficiency** | $C_E = \dfrac{M_{actual}}{M_{theoretical}} = \dfrac{\int_0^t I\, dt}{F \cdot n \cdot \Delta C \cdot V_{anode}}$ | % (dimensionless) | Fraction of substrate electrons recovered as current | | 7 | **Nernst Equation (Theoretical Cell Voltage)** | $E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} - \dfrac{R \cdot T}{n \cdot F}\ln Q$ | V (Volts) | Voltage depends on substrate/product concentrations | | 8 | **Activation Overpotential** | $\eta_{act}$ | V | Energy barrier at electrode-electrolyte interface | | 9 | **Ohmic (Concentration) Losses** | $\eta_{ohm} = I \cdot R_{int}$ | V | Resistance from electrolyte + membrane + electrodes | | 10 | **Total Polarization Loss** | $V_{cell} = V_{oc} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$ | V | Net operating voltage under load | | 11 | **Acetate Theoretical Electrons** | $n_{CH_3COO^-} = 8$ | e⁻/mol | Moles of e⁻ released per mole of acetate oxidized | | 12 | **Faraday's Constant** | $F = 96{,}485$ | C/mol | Charge carried by one mole of electrons | | 13 | **Current Density** | $j = \dfrac{I}{A_{anode}}$ | $\text{A/m}^2$ | Current per unit anode area | | 14 | **Energy Recovery** | $\eta_E = \dfrac{\int_0^t P\, dt}{\Delta H_{substrate}}$ | % | Ratio of electricity produced to chemical energy input | > [!NOTE] > **Important Boundary Conditions for KTU Numericals:** > - At $R_{ext} = R_{int}$, power is **maximum** (Impedance Matching Principle). > - For acetate at pH 7: $E^{\circ\prime}_{anode} = -0.28$ V; $E^{\circ\prime}_{cathode} = +0.805$ V → $E^{\circ\prime}_{cell} = 1.085$ V (biochemical standard). > - Nernst equation: $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T = 298.15$ K, so $RT/F \approx 0.02569$ V. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why MFCs Matter to Engineers:** > 1. **Simultaneous wastewater treatment + electricity generation** — a "two-in-one" process (COD removal + bioelectricity). > 2. **Biosensors** — MFC voltage is proportional to BOD/COD, enabling real-time biodegradable pollution monitoring. > 3. **Off-grid power in remote locations** (e.g., benthic MFCs powering marine sensors, weather stations). > 4. **Hydrogen production** (via Microbial Electrolysis Cells — MECs, an MFC variant). > 5. **Bioremediation** of heavy metals (Cr(VI) → Cr(III), U(VI) → U(IV) at the cathode). --- # ⚡ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Power Transfer in an MFC ### **Problem Setup** An MFC has an open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ and an internal resistance $R_{int}$. An external load resistor $R_{ext}$ is connected. Derive the condition for maximum power delivered to $R_{ext}$. ### **Step-by-Step Derivation** > [!NOTE] > We model the MFC as a **Thevenin equivalent circuit**: a voltage source $V_{oc}$ in series with $R_{int}$, connected to a variable load $R_{ext}$. **Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):** $$V_{oc} = I \cdot R_{int} + I \cdot R_{ext}$$ **Step 2 — Express current I using Ohm's Law:** $$I = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{int} + R_{ext}}$$ **Step 3 — Express power delivered to the load $R_{ext}$:** Power dissipated in $R_{ext}$: $$P = I^2 \cdot R_{ext} = \left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{int} + R_{ext}}\right)^2 \cdot R_{ext}$$ **Step 4 — Expand the expression:** $$P = \dfrac{V_{oc}^2 \cdot R_{ext}}{(R_{int} + R_{ext})^2}$$ **Step 5 — Differentiate P with respect to $R_{ext}$ and set dP/dR_ext = 0 (Maxima condition):** We use the **Quotient Rule** for $P = \dfrac{N}{D}$ where $N = V_{oc}^2 \cdot R_{ext}$ and $D = (R_{int} + R_{ext})^2$: $$\dfrac{dP}{dR_{ext}} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^2 \cdot (R_{int} + R_{ext})^2 - V_{oc}^2 \cdot R_{ext} \cdot 2(R_{int} + R_{ext})}{(R_{int} + R_{ext})^4}$$ **Step 6 — Simplify numerator (set it to zero):** $$V_{oc}^2 (R_{int} + R_{ext}) \left[(R_{int} + R_{ext}) - 2R_{ext}\right] = 0$$ Since $V_{oc} \neq 0$ and $R_{int} + R_{ext} \neq 0$: $$(R_{int} + R_{ext}) - 2R_{ext} = 0$$ **Step 7 — Solve for $R_{ext}$:** $$R_{ext} = R_{int} \quad \text{(Maximum Power Transfer Theorem)}$$ **Step 8 — Substitute back to find $P_{max}$:** $$P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^2 \cdot R_{int}}{(R_{int} + R_{int})^2} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^2 \cdot R_{int}}{4R_{int}^2} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{int}}$$ ### **Final Result Boxed:** $$\boxed{P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{int}} \quad \text{when} \quad R_{ext} = R_{int}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Coulombic Efficiency (C_E) ### **Problem Setup** An MFC of anode volume $V_{anode}$ = 0.5 L is fed with acetate (initial concentration $C_i$ = 1000 mg/L; final $C_f$ = 100 mg/L). Over 24 hours, the integrated current $\int_0^t I\,dt$ = 18,000 C. Molar mass of acetate = 59 g/mol. n = 8 e⁻ per mole. Calculate Coulombic efficiency. ### **Step-by-Step Solution** **Step 1 — Compute moles of acetate consumed:** $$\Delta C = C_i - C_f = 1000 - 100 = 900 \text{ mg/L} = 0.9 \text{ g/L}$$ $$\Delta m_{acetate} = 0.9 \text{ g/L} \times 0.5 \text{ L} = 0.45 \text{ g}$$ $$n_{moles} = \dfrac{0.45}{59} = 7.627 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}$$ **Step 2 — Theoretical electrons available (M_theoretical in moles of e⁻):** $$M_{theoretical} = n \cdot n_{moles} = 8 \times 7.627 \times 10^{-3} = 0.06102 \text{ mol e}^-$$ **Step 3 — Theoretical charge in Coulombs:** $$Q_{theoretical} = M_{theoretical} \times F = 0.06102 \times 96{,}485 = 5888 \text{ C}$$ **Step 4 — Actual charge (from current integration):** $$Q_{actual} = \int_0^t I\, dt = 18{,}000 \text{ C}$$ **Step 5 — Compute Coulombic Efficiency:** $$C_E = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{5888} = 3.057 \approx 3.06$$ ### **Final Result Boxed:** $$\boxed{C_E \approx 3.06 \quad (\text{or } 306\%)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **If C_E > 100%**, the assumption is **invalid** — likely reasons are: extra substrate was present (not measured), or oxygen crossover to the anode created non-MFC current, or measurement error. **Always validate mass balance in a real problem.** --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Nernst Cell Voltage for Acetate/O₂ MFC ### **Setup** For the cell reaction: $CH_3COO^- + 2O_2 \rightarrow 2HCO_3^- + H^+$ At T = 298 K, compute $E_{cell}$ when [acetate] = 1 mM, [HCO₃⁻] = 10 mM, [H⁺] = 10⁻⁷ M, pO₂ = 0.2 atm. Standard biochemical potentials: $E^{\circ\prime}_{anode} = -0.28$ V, $E^{\circ\prime}_{cathode} = +0.805$ V. ### **Step-by-Step Solution** **Step 1 — Compute standard cell voltage:** $$E^{\circ\prime}_{cell} = E^{\circ\prime}_{cathode} - E^{\circ\prime}_{anode} = 0.805 - (-0.28) = 1.085 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2 — Reaction quotient Q:** For $CH_3COO^- + 2O_2 \rightarrow 2HCO_3^- + H^+$: $$Q = \dfrac{[HCO_3^-]^2 \cdot [H^+]}{[CH_3COO^-] \cdot (pO_2)^2}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute values:** $$Q = \dfrac{(10 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 10^{-7}}{(1 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.2)^2} = \dfrac{10^{-4} \times 10^{-7}}{10^{-3} \times 0.04}$$ $$Q = \dfrac{10^{-11}}{4 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Nernst Equation (n = 8 e⁻ transferred):** $$E_{cell} = E^{\circ\prime}_{cell} - \dfrac{0.02569}{8} \ln Q$$ $$E_{cell} = 1.085 - 0.00321 \times \ln(2.5 \times 10^{-7})$$ $$\ln(2.5 \times 10^{-7}) = -15.20$$ $$E_{cell} = 1.085 - 0.00321 \times (-15.20) = 1.085 + 0.0488 = 1.134 \text{ V}$$ ### **Final Result Boxed:** $$\boxed{E_{cell} \approx 1.134 \text{ V (theoretical; practical OCV is much lower due to losses)}}$$ --- # ⚡ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 MFC Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A1[ANODE CHAMBER - ANAEROBIC] A1A[Organic Substrate
Acetate Wastewater] A1B[Electroactive Biofilm
Geobacter Shewanella] A1C[Anode Carbon Cloth
Graphite Felt] A1A --> A1B A1B --> A1C end subgraph A2[CATHODE CHAMBER - AEROBIC] A2A[Cathode Carbon Pt
or Biocathode] A2B[Oxygen from Air
Terminal Electron Acceptor] A2C[Water Product H2O] A2B --> A2A A2A --> A2C end PEM1[Proton Exchange Membrane
Nafion 117] R1[External Load Resistor
R ext] A1C -->|Electrons e-| R1 R1 -->|Electrons e-| A2A A1B -->|Protons H+| PEM1 PEM1 -->|Protons H+| A2A A1B -->|CO2 release| A1X[Biogas Vent] ``` --- ## 4.2 Electron Transfer Mechanisms in MFC ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Microbial Cell Membrane] S1[Direct Electron Transfer DET] S2[Mediated Electron Transfer MET] S3[Conductive Pili Nanowires] START --> S1 START --> S2 START --> S3 S1 --> S1A[Outer membrane c-Cytochromes
OmcA OmcB] S2 --> S2A[Self produced mediators
Pyocyanin Riboflavin] S2 --> S2B[Exogenous mediators
Methylene blue Neutral red] S3 --> S3A[Geobacter pili
Electrically conductive protein filaments] S1A --> ANODE[Anode Electrode] S2A --> ANODE S2B --> ANODE S3A --> ANODE ``` --- ## 4.3 Classification Tree of MFC Architectures ```mermaid flowchart TD MFC[Microbial Fuel Cell MFC] MFC --> M1[Single Chamber MFC] MFC --> M2[Dual Chamber H-Type MFC] MFC --> M3[Air Cathode MFC] MFC --> M4[Upflow MFC] MFC --> M5[Stacked MFC] MFC --> M6[Sediment MFC Benthic] MFC --> M7[Plant MFC] M1 --> M1A[No PEM possible, simple, low cost] M2 --> M2A[Lab scale research, easy control] M3 --> M3A[Most practical, direct air, high power] M4 --> M4A[Continuous flow wastewater treatment] M5 --> M5A[Scale up for higher voltage] M6 --> M6A[Marine sediment power] M7 --> M7A[Rhizodeposition organic fuel] ``` --- # ⚡ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Q1. Define Microbial Fuel Cell. List any two microorganisms used in MFC.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** A Microbial Fuel Cell (MFC) is a bio-electrochemical device that converts chemical energy of biodegradable substrates into electrical energy using **microorganisms as biocatalysts** at the anode under anaerobic conditions. The electrons released during substrate oxidation by bacteria flow through an external circuit to the cathode, producing current, while protons migrate through a Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) to complete the reaction by forming water with oxygen. *(1.5 marks)* **Two microorganisms used in MFC:** 1. ***Geobacter sulfurreducens*** — a model exoelectrogen forming thick conductive biofilms via pili (nanowires) and outer-membrane cytochromes. 2. ***Shewanella oneidensis*** — uses flavin-mediated extracellular electron transfer and outer-membrane c-type cytochromes (MtrC, OmcA). *(1.5 marks — 0.75 each)* --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q2. Differentiate between MFC and a chemical fuel cell.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** | Feature | Microbial Fuel Cell (MFC) | Chemical Fuel Cell | |---|---|---| | **Catalyst** | Living microorganisms (biocatalyst) | Noble metals (Pt, Pd) or inorganic catalysts | | **Operating Conditions** | Mild (room temperature, neutral pH) | High temperature / pressure often required | | **Fuel/Flexibility** | Wide range (wastewater, biomass, organic acids) | Pure H₂ or methanol (high purity needed) | | **Anode Reaction** | Biological oxidation (mediated by enzymes) | Catalytic oxidation (chemical) | | **Voltage Output** | Lower (~0.3–0.5 V) | Higher (~0.6–1.2 V) | | **Durability** | Limited by biofilm viability | High durability | | **Cost** | Low (no precious metals) | High (Pt catalyst) | | **Pollution Tolerance** | Can use pollutants as fuel | Cannot tolerate impurities | *(3 marks — 1.5 for each side, tabular form preferred for clarity)* --- ## 5.2 PART B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - ESE Model Paper 2024]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) Explain the construction and working principle of a dual-chamber Microbial Fuel Cell with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) in MFC performance.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **Construction:** A dual-chamber MFC (also called an "H-type" MFC because of its shape) consists of two glass/PVC chambers connected by a tube containing a **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM)** — typically **Nafion 117**. Each chamber is sealed with rubber gaskets and ports for sampling, gas sparging, and reference electrode insertion. - **Anode chamber:** Filled with anaerobic substrate (acetate, glucose, or real wastewater). Inoculated with anaerobic electroactive bacteria. Maintained under N₂ sparging to ensure strict anaerobic conditions. The **anode** is made of carbon cloth, graphite felt, or carbon brush. - **Cathode chamber:** Filled with aerated buffer (e.g., 50 mM phosphate buffer) or plain water. The **cathode** is often platinum-coated carbon or a biocathode. Continuously sparged with air/O₂ to maintain saturation. - **PEM (Nafion 117):** Sandwiched between the chambers using flanges. Pre-treated with dilute H₂SO₄ and H₂O₂ to activate proton conduction. - **External circuit:** Copper wires connect the anode and cathode through an external resistor (typically 1000 Ω for initial polarization studies). *(3 marks — diagram + labels)* **Working Principle:** 1. **Anodic oxidation:** Anaerobic bacteria (e.g., *Geobacter*) oxidize acetate: $$CH_3COO^- + 4H_2O \rightarrow 2HCO_3^- + 9H^+ + 8e^-$$ 2. **Electron transfer:** Electrons are transferred to the anode via outer-membrane cytochromes and conductive pili (Direct Electron Transfer) or via self-produced mediators. 3. **Proton migration:** H⁺ ions move through the hydrated PEM toward the cathode. 4. **Cathodic reduction:** At the cathode, electrons (from external circuit) + protons (from PEM) + dissolved O₂ form water: $$O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O$$ 5. **Net reaction:** $CH_3COO^- + 2O_2 \rightarrow 2HCO_3^- + H^+$; current flows in the external circuit, driving the load. *(2.5 marks)* **Role of PEM:** - **Selective proton transport:** Allows only H⁺ (cations) to pass, blocking O₂ crossover to the anode (which would otherwise waste electrons via aerobic respiration). - **Electrical separation:** Prevents direct contact between anode and cathode, forcing electrons through the external circuit (where useful work is done) rather than short-circuiting. - **Maintains pH gradient:** Prevents bulk mixing, preserving the thermodynamic driving force. - **Limits biofouling and substrate crossover** to the cathode. - **Drawbacks:** Adds internal resistance (ohmic loss), expensive (Nafion), and susceptible to biofouling over long-term operation. *(1.5 marks)* --- **(b) Discuss the various types of MFCs based on design. Compare their advantages and limitations. List the major factors affecting MFC power output.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **Types of MFCs:** 1. **Dual-Chamber (H-Type) MFC:** Two chambers joined by a tube/PEM. Easy for lab studies and reference electrode placement. Limitation: High internal resistance, low power density. 2. **Single-Chamber Air-Cathode MFC:** Cathode exposed directly to air; no aeration needed. Higher power density (up to 1000+ W/m³). Limitation: PEM dehydration, cathode flooding. 3. **Upflow MFC:** Continuous upward flow of wastewater through the anode chamber. Suitable for continuous wastewater treatment. 4. **Stacked MFC:** Multiple MFCs connected in series/parallel to increase voltage/current. Limitation: Voltage reversal in series stacks. 5. **Sediment/Benthic MFC:** Anode buried in sediment; cathode in overlaying water. Powers remote marine sensors. 6. **Plant-MFC (Plant Microbial Fuel Cell):** Uses rhizodeposition (organic compounds exuded by plant roots) as fuel in wetland plants. *(3 marks)* **Comparison Table:** | Type | Power Density | Cost | Complexity | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | Dual-Chamber H-Type | Low (0.1–10 W/m³) | Medium | High | Lab research | | Single-Chamber Air-Cathode | High (100–1000 W/m³) | Low | Low | Pilot wastewater | | Upflow | Medium | Medium | Medium | Continuous treatment | | Stacked | Very High (scaled) | High | High | Scale-up | | Sediment/Benthic | Very Low | Very Low | Low | Remote sensors | | Plant-MFC | Low | Very Low | Low | Constructed wetlands | *(1 mark)* **Major Factors Affecting Power Output:** 1. **Substrate type and concentration** — affects catabolic rate and electron yield. 2. **Microbial community** — pure cultures (Geobacter) vs. mixed consortia. 3. **Anode material and surface area** — high surface area + biocompatibility = higher biofilm density. 4. **Cathode catalyst** — Pt > activated carbon > biocathode (in performance order). 5. **PEM type and thickness** — affects proton flux and internal resistance. 6. **Electrode spacing** — smaller spacing reduces internal resistance. 7. **pH and temperature** — optimum 7.0 and 30 °C for most EAB. 8. **External load resistance** — power is maximum when $R_{ext} = R_{int}$. 9. **Dissolved oxygen at cathode** — affects ORR kinetics. 10. **Wastewater characteristics** — COD, salinity, presence of inhibitors. *(3 marks)* --- ### **Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)** **(a) Define Coulombic Efficiency. An MFC operates with an acetate feed. The anode volume is 1 L, initial acetate concentration is 1500 mg/L and final is 200 mg/L. The total charge collected over 48 hours is 25,000 C. Calculate the Coulombic Efficiency. (Molar mass of acetate = 59 g/mol, n = 8 electrons per mole of acetate, F = 96,485 C/mol).** *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Coulombic Efficiency ($C_E$) is the **ratio of the actual electric charge recovered as current** from the MFC to the **theoretical maximum charge** that could be produced from complete oxidation of the consumed substrate. It indicates how effectively the substrate is converted to electricity versus being lost to competing processes (e.g., methanogenesis, aerobic respiration, fermentation). $$C_E(\%) = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} \times 100$$ *(2 marks)* **Calculation:** **Step 1 — Moles of acetate consumed:** $$\Delta C = 1500 - 200 = 1300 \text{ mg/L} = 1.3 \text{ g/L}$$ $$\Delta m = 1.3 \text{ g/L} \times 1 \text{ L} = 1.3 \text{ g}$$ $$n_{moles} = \dfrac{1.3}{59} = 0.02203 \text{ mol}$$ **Step 2 — Theoretical moles of electrons:** $$M_{theoretical} = 8 \times 0.02203 = 0.1763 \text{ mol e}^-$$ **Step 3 — Theoretical charge:** $$Q_{theoretical} = 0.1763 \times 96{,}485 = 17{,}012 \text{ C}$$ **Step 4 — Coulombic Efficiency:** $$C_E = \dfrac{25{,}000}{17{,}012} \times 100 = 146.95\%$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** Since $C_E > 100\%$, the calculation indicates an **error in measurement or assumption**: - There may be **additional unmeasured substrates** (e.g., other organics in the wastewater) contributing to the current. - **Oxygen leakage** to the anode could have caused non-MFC current from spurious redox reactions. - Substrate adsorption onto the anode or biofilm matrix may have introduced errors. - Possible loss of acetate via volatilization (not measured) was not accounted for. *(5 marks — 2 for calculation, 1 for interpretation, 2 for explanation)* --- **(b) Discuss the materials used for anode, cathode, and PEM in MFC fabrication. Explain the criteria for selecting these materials. What is the role of exoelectrogens in MFCs?** *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **Anode Materials:** - **Carbon-based:** Graphite rods/plates, carbon cloth, carbon felt, carbon paper, reticulated vitreous carbon (RVC), carbon brush (most popular for high power). - **Metal-based:** Stainless steel, titanium — cheaper but susceptible to corrosion. - **Modified/Composite anodes:** Polyaniline-coated carbon, CNT-decorated anodes, graphene-oxide composites, Fe-N-C catalysts. *(1 mark)* **Selection criteria for anode:** 1. **High electrical conductivity.** 2. **High specific surface area** (to support dense biofilm). 3. **Biocompatibility** (non-toxic to EAB). 4. **Chemical stability** in anaerobic aqueous environments. 5. **Low cost and scalability.** 6. **Appropriate porosity** for substrate diffusion. *(1 mark)* **Cathode Materials:** - **Pt-coated carbon** (the gold standard, but expensive). - **Activated carbon / carbon black** with binders (cheaper alternative). - **Biocathode:** Microalgae (*Chlorella*) or bacteria (*Achromobacter*) catalyze ORR. - **Metal oxides:** MnO₂, Fe-N-C, Co-N-C (emerging non-Pt catalysts). - **Air-cathode with gas diffusion layer** (most practical for real-world applications). *(1 mark)* **Selection criteria for cathode:** 1. **High ORR catalytic activity.** 2. **Long-term stability in wet/aerobic conditions.** 3. **Low cost** (since cathode is often the most expensive component). 4. **Mechanical robustness.** *(0.5 mark)* **PEM Materials:** - **Nafion 117** (DuPont) — most common, perfluorosulfonic acid membrane. - **Ultrex CMI-7000** — alternative sulfonated styrene-divinylbenzene. - **SPEEK** (Sulfonated Polyether Ether Ketone) — cheaper, research-grade. - **Ceramic / glass fiber membranes** — used in some designs. - **Salt bridges** — low-cost alternative, but high resistance. *(0.5 mark)* **Selection criteria for PEM:** 1. **High proton conductivity** (low resistance). 2. **Low oxygen/crossover permeability.** 3. **Chemical and mechanical stability.** 4. **Biocompatibility** (must not poison biofilm). 5. **Reasonable cost and ease of fabrication.** *(0.5 mark)* **Role of Exoelectrogens (Electrochemically Active Bacteria — EAB):** Exoelectrogens are the **biological heart** of the MFC. They perform three critical functions: 1. **Substrate catabolism:** They oxidize organic substrates and channel the released electrons through their respiratory chain. 2. **Extracellular Electron Transfer (EET):** They export electrons across the outer cell membrane to the solid (insoluble) anode — either **directly** (via cytochromes and pili) or **indirectly** (via self-produced redox mediators). 3. **Biofilm formation:** They colonize the anode, forming a thick, conductive biofilm that continuously transfers electrons to the electrode. **Common exoelectrogens:** - *Geobacter sulfurreducens* (most studied, model organism). - *Shewanella oneidensis* MR-1. - *Rhodoferax ferrireducens*. - *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* (produces pyocyanin mediator). - Mixed cultures from wastewater/anaerobic sludge. *(2.5 marks)* --- > [!WARNING] > ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > **Common Mark-Deduction Errors Students Make in MFC Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the half-reactions** — Always write both anode and cathode half-reactions explicitly. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks just for balanced equations. > 2. **Confusing PEM with salt bridge** — PEM is a **proton-selective** polymer; a salt bridge is a generic ionic conductor used in lab prototypes. Do not interchange terminology. > 3. **Ignoring the role of biofilm vs. suspended cells** — Only biofilm-attached cells contribute significantly to current; mention "biofilm-mediated electron transfer." > 4. **Coulombic efficiency mistakes** — Always check mass balance. If $C_E > 100\%$, you have made a calculation or assumption error — explain it. > 5. **Forgetting the units in power density** — Distinguish between **W/m²** (areal) and **W/m³** (volumetric). Examiners WILL mark you down for missing units. > 6. **Skipping the Nernst equation derivation** — For derivations, show the logarithmic step clearly: $\ln Q = 2.303 \log Q$. > 7. **Forgetting temperature assumption (298 K)** — Always state "T = 298 K" before applying $RT/F = 0.02569$ V. > 8. **Missing the 8 e⁻ per acetate** — This is the most-commonly-missed stoichiometric fact in numerical problems. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These Bullet Points:** - **MFC:** Bio-electrochemical device; converts chemical energy of organics → electrical energy using **microorganisms** as anode catalysts. - **Anode chamber:** ANAEROBIC; biofilm of **exoelectrogens** (Geobacter, Shewanella). - **Cathode chamber:** AEROBIC; Pt catalyst OR biocathode; reduction of **O₂ to H₂O**. - **PEM:** Nafion 117; selectively transports **H⁺**; blocks O₂ crossover; main source of internal resistance. - **Anode half-reaction (acetate):** $CH_3COO^- + 4H_2O \rightarrow 2HCO_3^- + 9H^+ + 8e^-$ → **8 e⁻/acetate**. - **Cathode half-reaction:** $O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O$ → **4 e⁻/O₂**. - **Two EET mechanisms:** DET (cytochromes + pili) and MET (redox mediators like pyocyanin, riboflavin). - **Maximum Power Theorem:** $P_{max} = V_{oc}^2 / 4R_{int}$ when $R_{ext} = R_{int}$. - **Coulombic Efficiency:** $C_E = Q_{actual}/Q_{theoretical} \times 100\%$. - **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^\circ - (RT/nF)\ln Q$; $RT/F = 0.02569$ V at 298 K. - **Standard potentials (biochemical, pH 7):** Acetate/CO₂ anode = **−0.28 V**; O₂/H₂O cathode = **+0.805 V**; $E_{cell}^\circ = 1.085$ V theoretical. - **Practical OCV:** 0.3 – 0.6 V (much less than theoretical due to overpotentials). - **Power density range:** 0.1 – 10 W/m² (areal) or 10 – 1000 W/m³ (volumetric). - **Faraday's Constant:** F = **96,485 C/mol**. - **6 Types of MFC:** Dual-chamber, Single-chamber, Air-cathode, Upflow, Stacked, Sediment/Benthic, Plant-MFC. - **4 Polarization Losses:** Activation (η_act), Ohmic (η_ohm), Concentration (η_conc), and Internal Current Losses. - **Anode materials:** Carbon cloth, carbon brush, graphite felt (NOT platinum — too expensive for anode). - **Cathode materials:** Pt-coated carbon (best but costly); activated carbon, MnO₂, biocathode (cheaper). - **MFC Advantages:** Dual benefit (wastewater treatment + electricity), low operating cost, ambient conditions, no precious metals. - **MFC Limitations:** Low power density, scale-up challenges, membrane biofouling, voltage reversal in stacks. - **Applications:** Wastewater treatment, BOD biosensors, remote power (sediment MFCs), hydrogen production (MEC variant), bioremediation. - **MEC** (Microbial Electrolysis Cell) = MFC variant that produces **H₂** by applying a small external voltage (0.2–0.8 V). > 🎯 **Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4 questions on MFCs usually test: (1) construction + working (diagram compulsory), (2) half-reactions and net reaction, (3) Coulombic efficiency numericals, (4) types of MFC comparison, and (5) electrode materials selection criteria. **Always draw a labelled diagram** — it alone can fetch 2–3 marks.

topics.title: Microbial electrolysis cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Microbial electrolysis cells # Module 4 – Microbial Electrolysis Cells (MECs) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Microbial Electrolysis Cell (MEC)** is a bioelectrochemical system (BES) that couples the oxidative metabolism of electroactive (exoelectrogenic) microorganisms at an anode with a cathodic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), driven by a small externally applied voltage (typically **0.2 V – 1.0 V**) to produce **biohydrogen (H₂)**, methane, or other value-added chemicals from organic substrates. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** MECs are classified under **Module 4 – Bioelectrochemical Systems** and are conceptually positioned as the "electrolysis-assisted twin" of Microbial Fuel Cells (MFCs). The examiner expects students to clearly differentiate between MFCs (electricity generation) and MECs (fuel/chemical generation). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Assisted Water Wheel" Imagine a water wheel that wants to lift buckets of water uphill but is too weak on its own. You give it a small nudge from the side (the external power supply). It doesn't need a huge push—just a little. That's exactly how an MEC works: - The **microbes** are the water wheel, breaking down organic waste (acetic acid, glucose, wastewater) at the anode. - They release **electrons** (which are normally hard to extract as a useful fuel). - Normally, a Microbial Fuel Cell would just let these electrons flow to oxygen to make a small current (~0.3 V). - But to **force these electrons to make hydrogen gas** at the cathode, we must apply a tiny voltage (~0.6–0.8 V) — far less than the **1.23 V** needed for plain water electrolysis. - The microbes + their substrate provide the **remaining thermodynamic energy**, so the external supply only "tops up" the deficit. > [!NOTE] > **Why MECs Matter in Industry:** Conventional water electrolysis consumes **~4.5–5.0 kWh per Nm³ H₂**. MECs can reduce this by **over 50%** because the substrate (wastewater, biomass) supplies the bulk of the free energy. This makes MECs one of the most promising low-carbon hydrogen production pathways for the **National Green Hydrogen Mission (India, 2023)**. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants Used | Metric | Typical Range | Units | |---|---|---| | Applied Voltage ($E_{ap}$) | 0.2 – 1.0 | V | | Standard HER potential | **−0.414 V** vs SHE at pH 7 | V | | Standard acetate oxidation | −0.279 V vs SHE at pH 7 | V | | Coulombic Efficiency (CE) | 60 – 98 | % | | Cathodic Hydrogen Recovery (r_cat) | 30 – 95 | % | | Overall H₂ Recovery (R_H₂) | 20 – 90 | % | | Faraday Constant (F) | **96,485** | C/mol | | Universal Gas Constant (R) | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle – Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1: Substrate Oxidation at the Bio-Anode** Exoelectrogenic bacteria (e.g., *Geobacter sulfurreducens*, *Shewanella oneidensis*) oxidize the organic substrate. For acetate (the model compound): $$CH_3COO^- + 4H_2O \rightarrow 2HCO_3^- + 9H^+ + 8e^-$$ The electrons are transferred to the solid anode via **outer membrane c-type cytochromes** or **self-secreted mediators / nanowires (pili)**. **Step 2: Electron Migration Through External Circuit** Electrons travel through the wire to the cathode, generating a small "bioelectric" potential (similar to MFC). **Step 3: Proton Migration Through the Membrane** Protons (H⁺) produced at the anode diffuse through a **proton exchange membrane (PEM)**, typically **Nafion 117**, into the cathodic chamber. **Step 4: External Voltage Application** A DC power source provides a small voltage (E_ap) to overcome the thermodynamic barrier (because the cell's own EMF is too low to drive H₂ evolution spontaneously). **Step 5: Hydrogen Evolution Reaction (HER) at the Cathode** $$2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 \uparrow \quad (E^\circ = -0.414 \text{ V vs SHE at pH 7})$$ > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind the External Voltage:** Pure water electrolysis requires **1.23 V** theoretically. MEC requires only **~0.2–0.6 V** because the microbes already provide the energy from substrate oxidation — a savings of up to **80%** compared to conventional electrolysis. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{cell} = E_{cat} - E_{an}$ | Cell voltage (open circuit) | Determine spontaneity | | 2 | $E_{th,MEC} = 0.414 - (-0.279) = 0.693$ V | Theoretical MEC voltage | Minimum required input | | 3 | $\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | Activation overpotential | Butler-Volmer kinetics | | 4 | $\eta_{conc} = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(1 - \frac{i}{i_L}\right)$ | Concentration overpotential | Mass-transfer limitation | | 5 | $CE = \frac{Q_{rec}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{M \int I dt}{F b V_{An} \Delta COD}$ | Coulombic Efficiency | Electron capture | | 6 | $r_{cat} = \frac{n_{H_2}}{Q_{rec}/(2F)}$ | Cathodic H₂ recovery | Cathode performance | | 7 | $R_{H_2} = CE \times r_{cat}$ | Overall H₂ recovery | System performance | | 8 | $E_{ap} = E_{th} + \eta_{an} + \eta_{cat} + IR$ | Applied voltage equation | Design specification | | 9 | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | Gibbs free energy | Thermodynamic feasibility | | 10 | $HPR = \frac{V_{H_2}}{P_{in} \cdot t}$ | Hydrogen Production Rate | Design output | **Critical Note on Sign Conventions (KTU Pitfall):** - $E_{cat} = -0.414$ V (HER at pH 7) - $E_{an} = -0.279$ V (acetate oxidation at pH 7) - Both are REDUCTION potentials. When writing oxidation at anode, sign flips. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** MECs are deployed in **dark fermentation effluent polishing**, **municipal wastewater treatment plants** with simultaneous H₂ generation, and **pilot-scale biohydrogen production units** (e.g., Penn State University, USA; King Abdullah University, KSA). ### 2.3 Types of MEC Cathodes 1. **Chemical/Pt Cathode** — Platinum-based; highest HER activity but expensive. 2. **Biocathode** — Microorganisms (e.g., *Geobacter*) replace Pt; cheap, self-regenerating. 3. **Nickel-based Cathode** — Cost-effective alternative to Pt. 4. **Stainless Steel Cathode** — Used in large-scale MECs for durability. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Theoretical Voltage for MEC **Problem Statement:** Derive the minimum theoretical voltage needed to operate an MEC using acetate as substrate at pH 7 and 25 °C. **Step 1: Write Half-Reactions** **Anode (Oxidation of Acetate):** $$CH_3COO^- + 4H_2O \rightarrow 2HCO_3^- + 9H^+ + 8e^-$$ Standard reduction potential (lookup): $$E^\circ_{an,red} = -0.279 \text{ V vs SHE (pH 7)}$$ **Cathode (Reduction – HER):** $$2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$$ Standard reduction potential (Nernst-adjusted to pH 7): $$E^\circ_{cat,red} = -0.414 \text{ V vs SHE (pH 7)}$$ **Step 2: Apply Cell EMF Formula** $$E_{cell} = E^\circ_{cat,red} - E^\circ_{an,red}$$ **Step 3: Substitute Values** $$E_{cell} = (-0.414) - (-0.279)$$ $$E_{cell} = -0.414 + 0.279$$ $$\boxed{E_{cell} = -0.135 \text{ V}}$$ **Step 4: Interpret the Sign** The negative sign indicates that the reaction is **non-spontaneous**. Therefore, an external voltage of at least **0.135 V** must be applied for the reaction to proceed. **Step 5: Practical Adjustment** In practice, we also add activation, concentration, and ohmic overpotentials: $$E_{ap,min} = E_{th} + \eta_{an} + \eta_{cat} + IR_{ohmic}$$ $$E_{ap,min} = 0.135 + 0.1 + 0.2 + 0.1 \approx 0.5 \text{ – } 0.6 \text{ V}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Comparison:** Conventional water electrolysis needs **1.23 V** theoretically. MEC needs only **0.135 V** theoretically — saving **~89%** of input energy, which is the central engineering advantage of MECs. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Coulombic Efficiency (CE) **Problem Statement:** A single-chamber MEC with effective anode volume 50 mL and initial COD of 1000 mg/L is operated for 24 hours. Current profile: 5 mA for first 12 h, then 3 mA for next 12 h. Calculate the CE if 1 mole O₂ = 32 g, b = 4 (electrons per mole O₂). **Step 1: Total Charge Recovered** $$Q_{rec} = \int I \, dt$$ $$Q_{rec} = (0.005 \text{ A} \times 12 \times 3600) + (0.003 \text{ A} \times 12 \times 3600)$$ $$Q_{rec} = (0.005 \times 43200) + (0.003 \times 43200)$$ $$Q_{rec} = 216 + 129.6 = 345.6 \text{ C}$$ **Step 2: Charge in Substrate (COD)** $$\Delta COD = 1000 \text{ mg/L} \times 0.05 \text{ L} = 50 \text{ mg} = 0.05 \text{ g}$$ $$n_{O_2,eq} = \frac{0.05 \text{ g}}{32 \text{ g/mol}} = 1.5625 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}$$ **Step 3: Theoretical Charge** $$Q_{th} = b \cdot F \cdot n_{O_2,eq}$$ $$Q_{th} = 4 \times 96485 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$Q_{th} = 603.03 \text{ C}$$ **Step 4: Coulombic Efficiency** $$CE = \frac{Q_{rec}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{345.6}{603.03}$$ $$\boxed{CE = 0.5732 = 57.32\%}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Overall Hydrogen Recovery (R_H₂) **Given:** CE = 0.5732 and r_cat = 0.80 (assume 80% cathodic recovery). **Step 1: Apply the Master Recovery Formula** $$R_{H_2} = CE \times r_{cat}$$ $$R_{H_2} = 0.5732 \times 0.80$$ $$\boxed{R_{H_2} = 0.4586 = 45.86\%}$$ **Interpretation:** Of the total possible H₂ producible from substrate, **45.86%** is actually recovered as gas. Improvements require biofilm engineering and cathode catalysis. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: MEC vs MFC vs Water Electrolysis | Parameter | MEC | MFC | Water Electrolysis | |---|---|---|---| | External Voltage | 0.2 – 1.0 V | **0 V** | 1.8 – 2.5 V | | Main Product | **H₂** | Electricity | H₂ | | Microorganisms | Required | Required | **None** | | Cathode Reaction | HER | ORR (O₂ reduction) | HER | | Theoretical ΔE | 0.135 V | 0.693 V | 1.23 V | | Energy Recovery | 70 – 90% | 5 – 25% | 60 – 80% | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram – Functional Architecture of a Single-Chamber MEC ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Organic Substrate
Acetate Glucose Wastewater] --> B[Anode Chamber
Biofilm of Exoelectrogens] B --> C[Anode
Carbon Cloth Graphite] C --> D[External Circuit
Copper Wire Resistor] D --> E[DC Power Source
0.2 to 1.0 Volt] E --> F[Cathode
Pt Carbon Ni Stainless] F --> G[Proton Exchange Membrane
Nafion 117] G --> B F --> H[H2 Gas Collection
Bubble Trap Gasometer] H --> I[Biohydrogen Storage
Purification Use] J[Biofilm
Geobacter Shewanella] -.-> B K[CO2 Released] -.-> B style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff4e1 style F fill:#e8f5e9 style H fill:#fce4ec style I fill:#f3e5f5 ``` ### 4.2 Process Block Diagram – Electron and Proton Pathways ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph AnodeSide[ANODE COMPARTMENT] S[Substrate Oxidation] --> E1[Electrons e-] S --> P1[Protons H+] S --> CO2[CO2 Bicarbonate] end E1 -->|External Wire| DC[DC Power Supply
0.2 to 1.0 V] DC --> E2[Electrons to Cathode] P1 -->|Diffusion through PEM| E2 E2 --> HER[Hydrogen Evolution Reaction
2H+ + 2e- yields H2] HER --> OUT[Biohydrogen Output] style S fill:#fff9c4 style DC fill:#ffccbc style HER fill:#c8e6c9 style OUT fill:#b3e5fc ``` ### 4.3 Cathode Comparison Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR MEC[MEC System] --> C1[Platinum Cathode
High Cost High Activity] MEC --> C2[Biocathode
Low Cost Self Regenerating] MEC --> C3[Nickel Cathode
Moderate Cost Durable] MEC --> C4[Stainless Steel
Lowest Cost Industrial Scale] style MEC fill:#e3f2fd style C1 fill:#ffebee style C2 fill:#e8f5e9 style C3 fill:#fff8e1 style C4 fill:#f3e5f5 ``` ### 4.4 Performance Loss Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD IN[Substrate Energy Input] --> A1[Activation Loss Anode eta] A1 --> A2[Ohmic Loss Membrane Electrolyte] A2 --> A3[Activation Loss Cathode eta] A3 --> A4[Concentration Loss Mass Transfer] A4 --> A5[Methanogenesis Loss Side Reaction] A5 --> OUT[Final H2 Output Useful Energy] style IN fill:#c8e6c9 style OUT fill:#fff59d style A5 fill:#ffcdd2 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Microbial Electrolysis Cell (MEC). Mention any two advantages over conventional water electrolysis.** **Model Answer:** A Microbial Electrolysis Cell (MEC) is a bioelectrochemical device that uses **exoelectrogenic microorganisms** to oxidize organic substrates at the anode, producing **hydrogen gas at the cathode** with the help of a small externally applied voltage (0.2–1.0 V). **Two Advantages over Conventional Water Electrolysis:** 1. **Lower energy input:** MEC requires only ~0.6 V (vs 1.8–2.0 V for water electrolysis), giving **~70% energy savings**. 2. **Waste utilization:** MEC uses wastewater/biomass as feedstock, coupling **hydrogen production with pollutant removal**, unlike pure water electrolysis. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark each for two advantages)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Differentiate between an MFC and an MEC in terms of (a) external power, (b) main product, and (c) electron acceptor at cathode.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | MFC | MEC | |---|---|---| | (a) External Power | **Not required** | **Required (0.2–1.0 V)** | | (b) Main Product | **Electricity (Current)** | **Hydrogen gas (H₂)** | | (c) Cathode Electron Acceptor | **Oxygen (O₂)** | **Protons (H⁺)** | *(3 marks: 1 mark per correct row)* --- ### 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat schematic, explain the **working principle of a single-chamber Microbial Electrolysis Cell (MEC)**. Identify the electrode materials, membrane, and microorganisms used. **(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the **minimum theoretical voltage required** for an MEC operating with acetate oxidation at the anode and hydrogen evolution at the cathode (pH 7, 25 °C). State how this compares with conventional water electrolysis. --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Working Principle of Single-Chamber MEC:** A single-chamber MEC contains **no membrane** between the anode and cathode (improving H₂ recovery but allowing some methane formation). The components and functions are: | Component | Material/Type | Function | |---|---|---| | **Anode** | Carbon cloth, graphite brush, or carbon felt | Site of biofilm growth and electron release | | **Biofilm** | *Geobacter*, *Shewanella*, mixed consortia | Oxidize substrate, transfer electrons | | **Cathode** | Pt/C, Ni-based, biocathode, or stainless steel | HER catalyst (2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂) | | **Membrane (if dual-chamber)** | Nafion 117, Ultrex CMI-7000 | Allows H⁺ transport, separates chambers | | **External Supply** | DC source, 0.2–1.0 V | Overcomes thermodynamic barrier | | **Reference Electrode** | Ag/AgCl | For potential monitoring | **Process:** 1. Substrate (acetate) is fed into the anode chamber. 2. Microbes oxidize it, releasing CO₂, H⁺, and e⁻. 3. Electrons travel via the external circuit (through DC source) to the cathode. 4. Protons migrate (directly or via PEM). 5. At the cathode: $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2\uparrow$ *(7 marks: 3 marks schematic description + 2 marks components + 2 marks process explanation)* **(b) Derivation of Minimum Theoretical Voltage:** **Anode half-reaction (acetate oxidation):** $$CH_3COO^- + 4H_2O \rightarrow 2HCO_3^- + 9H^+ + 8e^- \quad E^\circ_{an} = -0.279 \text{ V}$$ **Cathode half-reaction (HER at pH 7):** $$2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 \quad E^\circ_{cat} = -0.414 \text{ V}$$ **Calculate cell EMF:** $$E_{cell} = E^\circ_{cat} - E^\circ_{an} = (-0.414) - (-0.279) = -0.135 \text{ V}$$ **Negative sign → non-spontaneous** → External voltage needed. **Minimum theoretical voltage to be applied:** $$E_{ap,min} = \vert E_{cell} \vert = 0.135 \text{ V}$$ **Comparison with conventional water electrolysis (1.23 V):** $$\text{Energy Savings} = \frac{1.23 - 0.135}{1.23} \times 100\% \approx 89\%$$ *(7 marks: 2 marks reactions + 2 marks EMF calculation + 2 marks interpretation + 1 mark comparison)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Define **Coulombic Efficiency (CE)** and **Cathodic Hydrogen Recovery (r_cat)**. An MEC delivers a current of 8 mA for 10 hours. The COD removed is 150 mg in a 100 mL reactor with b = 4. Calculate the CE. **(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **major factors affecting MEC performance** and explain how each can be optimized. --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Definitions and CE Calculation:** **Coulombic Efficiency (CE):** The ratio of actual charge recovered as current to the theoretical charge available from substrate oxidation. $$CE = \frac{Q_{rec}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{\int I \, dt}{b \cdot F \cdot V_{An} \cdot \Delta COD / M_{O_2}}$$ **Cathodic Hydrogen Recovery (r_cat):** The fraction of electrons reaching the cathode that actually produce H₂ gas (as opposed to being lost to side reactions like methanogenesis or oxygen reduction). **Given Data:** - I = 8 mA = 0.008 A - t = 10 h = 36,000 s - ΔCOD = 150 mg = 0.150 g - V_An = 100 mL = 0.1 L - b = 4, M_O₂ = 32 g/mol, F = 96,485 C/mol **Step 1: Charge Recovered** $$Q_{rec} = I \times t = 0.008 \times 36000 = 288 \text{ C}$$ **Step 2: Theoretical Charge** $$\Delta COD = 0.150 \text{ g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_{O_2} = \frac{0.150}{32} = 4.6875 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}$$ $$Q_{th} = b \cdot F \cdot n_{O_2} = 4 \times 96485 \times 4.6875 \times 10^{-3} = 1808.6 \text{ C}$$ **Step 3: Coulombic Efficiency** $$CE = \frac{288}{1808.6} = 0.1592 = 15.92\%$$ *(7 marks: 2 marks each for definitions + 3 marks for CE calculation)* **(b) Major Factors Affecting MEC Performance:** | Factor | Effect | Optimization Strategy | |---|---|---| | **Applied Voltage** | Higher voltage → faster kinetics but lower energy efficiency | Operate at **0.5–0.8 V** optimum | | **Anode Material** | Affects biofilm attachment and electron transfer | Use **graphite brush / carbon felt** | | **Cathode Catalyst** | Determines HER overpotential | Use **Pt or Ni-Mo cathodes** | | **Substrate Type** | Acetate best; complex substrates reduce CE | Prefer **simple fatty acids** | | **pH** | Affects microbial activity and thermodynamics | Maintain **pH 6.8–7.2** | | **Temperature** | Mesophilic optimum for biofilms | Operate at **30–35 °C** | | **Methanogens** | Compete with H₂-producing reactions | Use **methanogenic inhibitors** (2-bromoethanesulfonate) | | **Membrane** | Affects proton transfer and internal resistance | Use **Nafion 117** or anion exchange membrane | *(7 marks: 5 marks for 5–6 factors + 2 marks for synthesis/opinion)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout – Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign Error in E_cell:** Forgetting that both half-cell potentials are REDUCTION potentials and writing E_cell = E_an − E_cat (instead of E_cat − E_an) is a **2-mark loss**. Always: $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$. > 2. **Units Confusion:** Mixing COD units (mg/L) with volumes (mL) and not converting to consistent SI units before CE calculation — common **1.5-mark penalty**. > 3. **Confusing MFC and MEC:** Stating "MEC produces electricity" instead of hydrogen — **3-mark deduction** (entire concept wrong). > 4. **Forgetting 89% Savings:** Comparing MEC voltage (0.135 V) with water electrolysis (1.23 V) without calculating the percentage saving — loses the **1-mark "engineering utility"** component. > 5. **Ignoring Overpotentials:** Reporting only the theoretical 0.135 V and not adding activation/ohmic overpotentials (yielding practical 0.5–0.8 V) — **1–2 marks lost**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **MEC** = bioelectrochemical device producing **H₂** with **0.2–1.0 V** external input. - ✅ Theoretical MEC voltage = **0.135 V** (acetate + HER at pH 7). - ✅ Theoretical water electrolysis voltage = **1.23 V**. - ✅ MEC energy savings vs electrolysis ≈ **89%**. - ✅ Half-reactions to memorize: - Anode (acetate): $CH_3COO^- + 4H_2O \rightarrow 2HCO_3^- + 9H^+ + 8e^-$ - Cathode (HER): $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$ - ✅ Key microbes: **Geobacter, Shewanella** (exoelectrogens). - ✅ Key metrics: **CE, r_cat, R_H₂, η_E** — all in 0–1 or 0–100% range. - ✅ Coulombic Efficiency formula: $CE = \frac{\int I \, dt}{b \cdot F \cdot V_{An} \cdot \Delta COD / M_{O_2}}$ - ✅ Overall H₂ recovery: $R_{H_2} = CE \times r_{cat}$ - ✅ Practical voltage includes overpotentials: $E_{ap} = E_{th} + \eta_{an} + \eta_{cat} + IR$ - ✅ **Difference from MFC:** MEC needs power + makes H₂; MFC needs no power + makes electricity. - ✅ **Difference from Electrolysis:** MEC uses microbes + organic substrate; electrolysis uses Pt electrodes + pure water. - ✅ Cathodes ranked by cost: Pt (expensive) > Biocathode > Ni > Stainless Steel (cheapest). - ✅ Methanogenesis is the biggest parasitic loss — solved by **pure cultures** or **chemical inhibitors**.

topics.title: Other bioelectrical systems - basic principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Other bioelectrical systems - basic principles ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Bioelectrical systems** are a class of **bioelectrochemical devices (BES)** that utilize biological catalysts — whole microorganisms (bacteria, algae, photosynthetic consortia) or isolated enzymes — to drive **oxidation-reduction (redox) reactions** at electrode interfaces, thereby converting **chemical energy of organic substrates** into **electrical energy** (galvanic mode) or **utilizing electrical energy** to drive non-spontaneous biochemical reactions such as hydrogen evolution, desalination, or synthesis (electrolytic mode). In the context of Module 4, *"Other bioelectrical systems"* refers specifically to **bioelectrochemical architectures beyond the conventional microbial fuel cell (MFC)**. These include: | # | System | Abbreviation | Primary Output | |---|--------|--------------|----------------| | 1 | Microbial Electrolysis Cell | MEC | Hydrogen gas (H₂) | | 2 | Microbial Desalination Cell | MDC | Fresh water + electricity | | 3 | Microbial Solar Cell | MSC | Electricity (light-driven) | | 4 | Enzymatic Biofuel Cell | EBFC | Electricity (enzyme-based) | | 5 | Sediment Microbial Fuel Cell | SMFC | In-situ electricity | | 6 | Plant Microbial Fuel Cell | PMFC | Plant-sourced electricity | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The unifying "basic principle" across all these systems is the **exoelectrogenic biofilm** — a consortium of electroactive bacteria that transfer metabolically generated electrons to a solid **anode** (the *anodic electron transfer* step), which is the rate-limiting and defining step of every bioelectrical system. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Bacterial Power Grid"** > Imagine a battery. Inside, two metal plates (electrodes) sit in an electrolyte solution, and chemical reactions drive electrons from one plate to the other through a wire, lighting a bulb. In a **bioelectrical system**, the metal plates are replaced (or coated) with **carbon-based electrodes**, and the chemical reactants are replaced with **bacteria feeding on wastewater**. The bacteria literally "eat" organic molecules (glucose, acetate, lactate), and instead of storing the resulting energy inside their cells, they **dump electrons onto the anode** — like workers handing off packages onto a conveyor belt. From there, the electrons travel through an **external circuit** (lighting your LED, charging a capacitor), arrive at the **cathode**, and combine with protons and oxygen (or another acceptor) to form water or hydrogen. > > The difference between MFC, MEC, MDC, and MSC is essentially **what we put at the cathode end** and **what extra compartments or membranes we add**. An MEC is an MFC with a tiny external power supply that "pushes" electrons uphill to make H₂. An MDC adds a middle compartment with salt water. An MSC feeds the bacteria sunlight. An EBFC replaces whole bacteria with single enzymes. **The principle — electron transfer at an electrode–biology interface — is identical.** ### 1.3 Core Bioelectrochemical Principle Every bioelectrical system rests on three coupled phenomena: 1. **Substrate oxidation at the bio-anode** (loss of electrons): $$\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 7\text{H}^+ + 8e^-$$ 2. **Electron transport through an external circuit** (the *useful* work). 3. **Terminal electron acceptor reaction at the cathode** (gain of electrons), e.g., oxygen reduction: $$\text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Physical Standard:** > - **Faraday's constant:** $F = \mathbf{96{,}485\ C\ mol^{-1}}$ (charge per mole of electrons) > - **Standard hydrogen electrode (SHE) potential:** $E^\circ = \mathbf{0.000\ V}$ at pH 0, 25 °C, 1 atm. > - **Anode potential (acetate oxidation):** $E^\circ_{anode} \approx \mathbf{-0.290\ V}$ vs. SHE > - **Cathode potential (O₂ reduction):** $E^\circ_{cathode} = \mathbf{+0.820\ V}$ vs. SHE at pH 7 > - **Theoretical open-circuit voltage of an MFC:** $E^\circ_{cell} = E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode} \approx \mathbf{1.11\ V}$ ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic of Bioelectrical Systems — Step-by-Step Every bioelectrical system — regardless of whether it produces electricity, hydrogen, or desalinated water — follows a **six-step thermodynamic and kinetic pathway**: 1. **Substrate Diffusion into the Anodic Chamber** - Organic substrate (acetate, glucose, lactate, or complex wastewater organics) enters the anode compartment, typically by convection or diffusion across a separator membrane. 2. **Bacterial Metabolism and Biofilm Formation** - Electroactive bacteria (e.g., *Geobacter sulfurreducens*, *Shewanella oneidensis*, mixed consortia from wastewater) attach to the anode surface, forming a **biofilm** of thickness ~10–100 µm. - Substrate is oxidized inside the cell cytoplasm via the TCA cycle, generating NADH and reduced electron carriers. 3. **Extracellular Electron Transfer (EET) — The Defining Step** - Electrons must cross the bacterial outer membrane to reach the insoluble anode. Two mechanisms: - **Direct Electron Transfer (DET):** via outer-membrane *c-type cytochromes* (e.g., OmcZ, OmcS in *Geobacter*) physically contacting the electrode. - **Mediated Electron Transfer (MET):** via self-produced or added soluble redox shuttles (e.g., flavins, humic acids, methylene blue, neutral red). 4. **Electron Migration Through the External Circuit** - Electrons flow from anode → wire → load (resistor, LED, capacitor) → cathode, generating a measurable **current (I)** and **voltage (V)**. 5. **Proton / Ion Migration Across the Separator / Membrane** - To maintain electroneutrality, cations (mainly H⁺) migrate from anode to cathode through a proton exchange membrane (PEM, e.g., Nafion 117), anion exchange membrane (AEM), or salt bridge. 6. **Cathodic Reduction Reaction — Defines System Type** - The terminal electron-acceptor reaction **determines the system identity**: - **O₂ reduction → MFC** (power generation) - **H⁺ reduction → MEC** (H₂ production, requires external ~0.2–0.8 V bias) - **Na⁺ migration through middle chamber → MDC** (desalination) - **Photo-excited dye molecule reduction → MSC** (light energy capture) ### 2.2 Why and How of Each Step - **Why EET is rate-limiting:** The anode overpotential (η_anode) typically exceeds 0.2–0.4 V, accounting for >50% of total voltage losses in most BES. -
