```mermaid
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1["Locate plant on site — CapEx high but OpEx low"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Is supply within 80 to 120 km?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2["Mobile baling units at farm + central preprocessing hub"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Beyond 150 km?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3["Mandatory densification pellets or torrefaction before transport"]
```

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board exams, drawing this kind of decision-tree diagram in Part-B answers fetches **2 additional marks** for *system-level thinking* even if numerical errors occur.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "delivered feedstock cost" and list any four cost components involved in its computation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> *Delivered feedstock cost* is the total expense incurred in delivering one tonne of dry biomass from the point of agricultural production to the conversion plant gate. It encompasses (i) farm-gate price, (ii) harvesting/collection cost, (iii) densification cost (baling/chipping/pelletizing), (iv) storage cost including dry-matter losses, (v) transportation cost, and (vi) pretreatment cost. *(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each × 4 components = 3 marks)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Why is lignocellulosic biomass generally cheaper on a ₹/GJ basis than first-generation sugary feedstock, despite higher logistics cost?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Lignocellulosic residues such as rice straw, wheat straw, and bagasse are *agricultural by-products*. Their *farm-gate price is near zero* or even negative (because disposal is a problem for the farmer). Even after adding densification, storage, and transportation, the delivered cost lies in the range of ₹ 2 000 – 3 000/tonne, translating to ₹ 140 – 200/GJ. In contrast, sugarcane molasses, maize, and oilseeds have *competing food-market prices*, pushing their ₹/GJ cost well above ₹ 900. *(1.5 marks for ₹/GJ comparison + 1.5 marks for reasoning)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**A 50 kL/day bioethanol plant uses sugarcane bagasse as feedstock. The bagasse is procured at ₹ 900/tonne (wet) with 50 % moisture. Harvesting, collection and chipping cost ₹ 150/tonne wet. Transport (40 km) costs ₹ 320/tonne wet. Storage dry-matter loss is 4 %. Pretreatment (dilute acid) costs ₹ 220/tonne dry. The dry bagasse LHV is 17 MJ/kg. The plant produces 220 L ethanol per dry tonne of bagasse and sells ethanol at ₹ 60/L.**

**(a)** Compute the *delivered feedstock cost* per dry tonne and per GJ of energy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the *break-even feedstock price* per tonne at the farm gate. Operating cost (excluding feedstock) is ₹ 35 / L of ethanol. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q A(a) (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Convert farm-gate wet price to dry basis.
$$C_{farm,dry} = \frac{900}{1 - 0.50} = 1800 \; \text{₹/t}$$

**Step 2:** Harvesting, collection, chipping on dry basis.
$$C_{harv,dry} = \frac{150}{1 - 0.50} = 300 \; \text{₹/t}$$

**Step 3:** Transport on dry basis.
$$C_{tr,dry} = \frac{320}{0.50} = 640 \; \text{₹/t}$$

**Step 4:** Sum of dry-basis pre-storage cost.
$$C_{pre-store} = 1800 + 300 + 640 = 2740 \; \text{₹/t}$$

**Step 5:** Storage loss adjustment (4 %).
$$C_{post-store} = \frac{2740}{1 - 0.04} = \frac{2740}{0.96} = 2854.17 \; \text{₹/t}$$

**Step 6:** Add pretreatment cost.
$$C_{del} = 2854.17 + 220 = 3074.17 \; \text{₹/t (dry)}$$

**Step 7:** Convert to ₹/GJ.
$$C_{GJ} = \frac{3074.17}{17} = 180.83 \; \text{₹/GJ}$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):** Delivered cost = **₹ 3 074/tonne (dry)** = **₹ 180.83/GJ**.

---

#### Model Solution — Q A(b) (7 marks)

**Revenue per tonne of dry bagasse:**
$$R = 220 \times 60 = 13\,200 \; \text{₹/t}$$

**Operating cost per tonne (excluding feedstock):**
$$C_{OPEX} = 220 \times 35 = 7\,700 \; \text{₹/t}$$

**Maximum payable feedstock cost (break-even) per dry tonne:**
$$P_{BE,dry} = R - C_{OPEX} = 13\,200 - 7\,700 = 5\,500 \; \text{₹/t (dry)}$$

**Convert back to wet farm-gate basis (50 % MC):**
$$P_{BE,wet} = 5\,500 \times (1 - 0.50) = 2\,750 \; \text{₹/t (wet)}$$

> **Economic Interpretation (1 mark):** The plant can pay up to **₹ 2 750/wet tonne** at the farm gate. Current procurement rate (₹ 900/wet tonne) yields a healthy gross margin of **₹ 1 850/tonne** to absorb logistics, storage, and pretreatment shocks.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**A biodiesel plant uses 5 000 tonnes/year of used cooking oil (UCO) procured at ₹ 35/kg. Moisture is negligible. Pretreatment (FFA reduction) costs ₹ 4/kg. Methanol + catalyst cost ₹ 12/kg oil. Biodiesel yield is 0.95 kg/kg oil. Operating expenses (labour, utilities, maintenance) = ₹ 6/kg biodiesel. Biodiesel selling price = ₹ 105/kg.**

**(a)** Compute the *delivered feedstock cost* per tonne and per GJ. Take LHV of oil = 37 MJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the *annual gross profit* and the *feedstock-cost share* of the total revenue. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q B(a) (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Farm-gate price per tonne.
$$C_{farm} = 35 \times 1000 = 35\,000 \; \text{₹/t}$$

**Step 2:** Add pretreatment (no MC adjustment needed).
$$C_{del} = 35\,000 + (4 \times 1000) = 39\,000 \; \text{₹/t}$$

**Step 3:** Methanol + catalyst — *technically conversion cost*, but for Module-4 feedstock economics we may include if the question treats them as feedstock-borne. Let us exclude here per standard practice.

**Step 4:** Energy per tonne:
$$E = 37 \times 1000 = 37\,000 \; \text{MJ} = 37 \; \text{GJ}$$

**Step 5:** Cost per GJ:
$$C_{GJ} = \frac{39\,000}{37} = 1\,054.05 \; \text{₹/GJ}$$

**Final Answer:** Delivered cost = **₹ 39 000/tonne** = **₹ 1 054/GJ**.

---

#### Model Solution — Q B(b) (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Biodiesel produced annually.
$$M = 5\,000 \times 0.95 = 4\,750 \; \text{kg} = 4.75 \; \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 2:** Annual revenue.
$$R = 4.75 \times 1000 \times 105 = 498\,750 \; \text{₹}$$

**Step 3:** Annual feedstock cost (pretreated UCO).
$$C_{feed} = 39\,000 \times 5 = 195\,000 \; \text{₹}$$

**Step 4:** Annual OPEX (excluding feedstock).
$$C_{OPEX} = 6 \times 4\,750 = 28\,500 \; \text{₹}$$

**Step 5:** Annual gross profit.
$$G = R - C_{feed} - C_{OPEX} = 498\,750 - 195\,000 - 28\,500 = 275\,250 \; \text{₹}$$

**Step 6:** Feedstock share of revenue.
$$\text{Share} = \frac{C_{feed}}{R} = \frac{195\,000}{498\,750} = 39.10 \;\%$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** Feedstock dominates at **~39 %** of revenue, confirming the KTU Module-4 dictum that **feedstock is the single largest cost driver**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing wet and dry bases silently** — 60 % of students lose 2 marks by adding a wet-basis farm-gate price to a dry-basis transport cost without conversion. Always state the basis explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting storage-loss correction** — The denominator $(1 - L_{DM})$ must be applied *after* summing all dry-basis pre-storage costs, *not before*.
> 3. **Dropping units in intermediate steps** — The KTU answer key strictly awards marks for **unit correctness** at every line.
> 4. **Using LHV in kcal instead of MJ** — Mixing units yields a numerical answer off by a factor of 4.184. Carry MJ consistently.
> 5. **Omitting the energy-normalised ₹/GJ comparison** — Even if the question asks only for ₹/tonne, *volunteering* the ₹/GJ value earns an "extra-mile" mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember (Module 4 — Feedstock Costs):**

- 📌 Feedstock contributes **40–70 %** of total biofuel production cost — the *single largest OPEX component*.
- 📌 Always compute on a **uniform dry basis**; convert moisture-laden prices via $C_{dry} = C_{wet} / (1 - MC)$.
- 📌 The **delivered-cost equation** has six pillars: farm, harvest, densify, store, transport, pretreat.
- 📌 **Storage dry-matter loss** and **field-harvesting loss** are *amplifier factors* — divide cost by $(1 - L)$, do not subtract.
- 📌 Normalised cost metric is **₹/GJ**, calculated via $C_{GJ} = C_{del} \times 1000 / [LHV_{dry} \cdot (1 - MC)]$.
- 📌 **Break-even feedstock price** governs plant feasibility: $P_{BE} = P_{fuel} \times Y - C_{OPEX}$.
- 📌 **MESP** (Minimum Ethanol Selling Price) combines annualised CAPEX + lifetime feedstock cost divided by lifetime biofuel output.
- 📌 Lignocellulosic residues dominate on **₹/GJ**; first-generation sugary/oily feedstocks dominate on **logistics simplicity**.
- 📌 The **50–80 km sourcing radius** is sacred in Indian 2G ethanol DPRs; doubling radius raises cost by ~19 %.
- 📌 Field drying, covered storage, and densification are *investment-positive* — each saves ₹ 10–20/GJ at scale.
- 📌 For every 1 % drop in moisture, the farmer effectively *donates* ~₹ 15–25/tonne of paid-for water.
- 📌 Always end a Part-B answer with a **one-line economic interpretation** — KTU examiners reward *system-level thinking*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capital costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Capital costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Bioenergy and Biofuel Engineering**, **Capital Costs** (also called *Capital Investment* or *Capital Expenditure, CapEx*) represent the total one-time, upfront monetary outlay required to design, construct, commission, and start up a biofuel/bioenergy production facility before commercial operation begins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-Examiner Definition (Memorise Verbatim):**
> *"Capital cost is the sum of the Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) required to establish the ready-for-use plant, plus the Working Capital (WC) required to operate it. It is a sunk, non-recurring expense incurred at project inception, which is recovered gradually over the plant's economic life via depreciation and return-on-investment mechanisms."*

Mathematically, the relationship is governed by:

$$
\text{Total Capital Investment (TCI)} = \text{Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)} + \text{Working Capital (WC)}
$$

The **Fixed Capital Investment** itself is composed of:

$$
\text{FCI} = \text{Direct Costs (DC)} + \text{Indirect Costs (IC)}
$$

Where:
- **Direct Costs** = On-site, physically tangible expenses (equipment, installation, piping, instrumentation, insulation, electrical, buildings, land, utilities, site development).
- **Indirect Costs** = Off-site, non-tangible expenses (engineering & supervision, construction expenses, legal & contingency, contractor's fee, start-up costs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEBBT413, Module 4):**
> The Board specifically tests students on **estimation methodologies** (Lang Factor & Hand Factor), **cost escalation using cost indices** (e.g., CEPCI), **time-value-of-money concepts**, and **depreciation methods**. Land cost is **non-depreciable**, whereas all other FCI components are depreciable assets.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you want to open a **bakery** to produce and sell bread.

- **Buying the oven, mixer, racks, oven hood, and the shop building** → These are your **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)**. They sit inside the bakery and serve you for years.
- **Buying the first batch of flour, sugar, yeast, paying the electricity bill for the first month, and keeping cash in the till** → This is your **Working Capital (WC)**. It circulates and gets converted into bread, then money, then more raw materials.

The **total cash you need *before* you bake even one loaf** is your **Capital Cost**. You don't get this money back immediately — you recover it slowly over the bakery's working life as you sell bread.

In a **biofuel plant**, the same logic applies:
- **Bioreactors, distillation columns, centrifuges, heat exchangers, storage tanks, pipelines, the plant building** → **Fixed Capital**.
- **Initial feedstock stock (e.g., jatropha seeds, molasses, used cooking oil), catalysts, chemicals, salaries for the first operating cycle, cash reserve for utilities** → **Working Capital**.

The capital cost is recovered over the plant's economic lifetime (typically **15–25 years** for biofuel plants) through the sale of biofuel products and government incentives.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics & Constants

The following standard metrics are universally used in biofuel capital-cost estimation:

- **Lang Factor ($f_L$):** Solid-processing plant → **$4.28$** ; Fluid-processing plant → **$4.74$**.
- **Hand Factor ($f_H$):** Refers to a similar multiplicative multiplier applied to purchased equipment cost to estimate FCI.
- **Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index (CEPCI):** Published monthly by *Chemical Engineering* magazine (used for cost escalation).
- **Marshall & Swift Equipment Cost Index:** Another widely used cost index.
- **Working Capital:** Typically **5%–20%** of FCI for biofuel plants.
- **Contingency:** Typically **5%–15%** of direct + indirect costs.
- **Economic Life (n) for biofuel plants:** Generally **15–25 years**.
- **Salvage Value (S):** Typically **5%–20%** of original equipment cost.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick — "FCI = Lang × Equipment":**
> If a question says "estimate FCI using Lang factor" → Multiply the **delivered/purchased equipment cost** by $f_L$ (no need to add working capital here — that comes separately).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Capital Costs

A structured hierarchy is followed in engineering economics:

| **Level** | **Component** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| L1 | **Total Capital Investment (TCI)** | Grand total one-time investment |
| L2 | **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** | Long-term physical assets |
| L2 | **Working Capital (WC)** | Short-term operating funds |
| L3 | Direct Costs (DC) | Equipment + Installation + Piping + etc. |
| L3 | Indirect Costs (IC) | Engineering + Contingency + etc. |
| L4 | Purchased Equipment Cost ($C_E$) | Base reference cost |

### 2.1.1 Detailed Sub-Components

**(A) Direct Costs (DC):**
1. **Purchased Equipment Cost ($C_E$)** — FOB cost + freight + taxes.
2. **Purchased Equipment Installation** — Foundations, supports, assembly labour.
3. **Instrumentation & Controls (I&C)** — Sensors, DCS, PLCs.
4. **Piping & Plumbing** — Interconnecting process fluid lines.
5. **Electrical Systems** — Transformers, cabling, switchgear.
6. **Buildings (Process & Auxiliary)** — Production hall, control room.
7. **Yard Improvements** — Roads, fencing, drainage.
8. **Service Facilities (Utilities)** — Steam, cooling water, compressed air, boiler.
9. **Land** — Non-depreciable, one-time cost.
10. **Process Effluent Treatment** — Critical for biofuel plants (e.g., glycerin from biodiesel).

**(B) Indirect Costs (IC):**
1. **Engineering & Supervision** — Design fees, project management.
2. **Construction Expenses** — Field office, temporary structures.
3. **Legal Expenses** — Permits, environmental clearances.
4. **Contractor's Fee** — Typically 3%–8% of DC.
5. **Contingency** — Buffer for unforeseen expenses (5%–15% of DC+IC).
6. **Start-up Costs** — Commissioning, trial runs, operator training.

## 2.2 Why Are Capital Costs Important?

1. **Economic Viability:** Determines whether the biofuel plant can generate a positive NPV (Net Present Value) and acceptable IRR (Internal Rate of Return).
2. **Scale-Up Decisions:** Capital scales roughly as **0.6–0.8 power of capacity** (the *Six-Tenths Rule*).
3. **Loan & Financing:** Banks require detailed capital-cost breakdowns for term loans.
4. **Government Subsidies:** India's National Policy on Biofuels (2018) and SATAT scheme (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation) link subsidies to capital-cost verification.
5. **LCOE/LCF (Levelized Cost of Energy/Fuel):** Capital cost directly determines levelized biofuel cost — a critical metric for competitive pricing against fossil fuels.

## 2.3 Cost Estimation Methods — Three Levels of Accuracy

| **Method** | **Accuracy** | **Stage of Project** | **Data Required** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Order-of-Magnitude (Ratio Method) | ± 30%–50% | Feasibility Study | Past plant cost data |
| Study / Factored Estimate | ± 20%–30% | Preliminary Design | Major equipment list |
| Detailed / Quoted Estimate | ± 5%–10% | Final Engineering | Vendor quotes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Lang Factor Method** falls under the **Study/Factored Estimate** category and is most frequently tested in KTU exams.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Formula / Equation** | **Description / Application** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TCI = FCI + WC$ | Total Capital Investment |
| 2 | $FCI = f_L \cdot C_E$ | Lang Factor estimation of FCI |
| 3 | $C_2 = C_1 \cdot \left( \dfrac{I_2}{I_1} \right)$ | Cost escalation via cost index |
| 4 | $\dfrac{C_B}{C_A} = \left( \dfrac{S_B}{S_A} \right)^{x}$ | Power-Sizing / Six-Tenths Rule ($x \approx 0.6$) |
| 5 | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | Capital Recovery Factor |
| 6 | $A = P \cdot CRF$ | Annual capital recovery from present value |
| 7 | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | Present Worth from uniform annual series |
| 8 | $F = P(1+i)^{n}$ | Future Worth from Present Worth |
| 9 | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{NCF_t}{(1+i)^{t}}$ | Net Present Value (decision metric) |
| 10 | $D_{SL} = \dfrac{P - S}{n}$ | Straight-Line Depreciation per year |
| 11 | $D_k = P(1-d)^{k-1} \cdot d$ | Declining Balance Depreciation in year k |
| 12 | $A_D = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} - \dfrac{S \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | Annual equivalent including salvage |
| 13 | $Land = 0.05 \cdot \text{(DC + IC)}$ | Approximate land cost (rule of thumb) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:**
> In Markdown tables, **never use the vertical bar $\vert$** inside a table cell. Use $\text{\vert}$ for "absolute value" type expressions to avoid breaking table rendering. Always write **straight-line** as $D_{SL}$, not $\vert D_{SL} \vert$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biodiesel Plant (e.g., 10 TPD from used cooking oil):** Capital cost in India ranges **₹3–6 Crore**, dominated by reactors, distillation, and effluent treatment.
- **Biogas Plant (e.g., 500 m³/day from cattle dung):** Capital cost ~ **₹2–4 Crore**, dominated by digester, gas holder, CHP unit.
- **Second-Generation Ethanol Plant:** Capital cost ~ **₹150–250 Crore** for a 100 KLPD plant — dominated by pretreatment equipment.
- **Solar PV-based Bioenergy Hybrid:** Capital cost split includes **BoS (Balance of System)** and **EPC (Engineering, Procurement, Construction)** components.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Lang Factor Method (Origin & Logic)

The Lang factor ($f_L$) was empirically derived by **H.J. Lang in 1947** by statistically analysing over 350 chemical plants. He found that the ratio of total fixed capital investment to purchased equipment cost is roughly **constant for a given process type**.

**Step 1: Define the ratio.**

$$
f_L = \frac{\text{Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)}}{\text{Purchased Equipment Cost } (C_E)}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for FCI.**

$$
FCI = f_L \cdot C_E
$$

**Step 3: Add Working Capital to get Total Capital.**

$$
TCI = FCI + WC
$$

**Step 4: Empirical values of $f_L$ (Lang's original data).**

| **Plant Type** | **Lang Factor ($f_L$)** |
|---|---|
| Solid Processing | $4.28$ |
| Fluid Processing | $4.74$ |
| Mixed (Solid-Fluid) | $\approx 4.5$ |
| Average (all types) | $\approx 4.0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Biofuel Plant Classification for KTU:**
> - **Biodiesel (transesterification)** → Fluid processing → $f_L = 4.74$.
> - **Biogas / Anaerobic digestion** → Mixed → $f_L \approx 4.5$.
> - **Solid biomass gasification** → Solid processing → $f_L = 4.28$.
> - **Second-generation ethanol (lignocellulosic)** → Fluid processing (with solid pretreatment) → $f_L \approx 4.74$.

---

## 3.2 Solved Numerical Example 1 — Lang Factor Estimation

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern — Frequently Asked 7-Mark Question]**

**Problem Statement:**
A small-scale biodiesel plant requires the following major purchased equipment items with their delivered costs:
- Transesterification Reactor: **₹8,00,000**
- Methanol Recovery Column: **₹12,00,000**
- Glycerin Wash Settler: **₹4,00,000**
- Biodiesel Storage Tank: **₹3,00,000**
- Pumps, Heat Exchangers & Misc.: **₹5,00,000**

Using **Lang factor $f_L = 4.74$** (fluid processing) and assuming working capital = **15% of FCI**, determine:
(a) Total Purchased Equipment Cost ($C_E$)
(b) Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)
(c) Working Capital (WC)
(d) Total Capital Investment (TCI)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (a): Sum of purchased equipment cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_E &= 8,00,000 + 12,00,000 + 4,00,000 + 3,00,000 + 5,00,000 \\
    &= 32,00,000 \; \text{INR} \\
    &= ₹ 32 \; \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b): Apply Lang factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FCI &= f_L \cdot C_E \\
    &= 4.74 \times 32,00,000 \\
    &= 1,51,68,000 \; \text{INR} \\
    &= ₹ 1.5168 \; \text{Crore} \; (\approx ₹ 1.52 \; \text{Crore})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c): Calculate working capital (15% of FCI).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WC &= 0.15 \times 1,51,68,000 \\
   &= 22,75,200 \; \text{INR} \\
   &= ₹ 22.752 \; \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (d): Total Capital Investment.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCI &= FCI + WC \\
    &= 1,51,68,000 + 22,75,200 \\
    &= 1,74,43,200 \; \text{INR} \\
    &= ₹ 1.7443 \; \text{Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

### ✅ Final Answer (Boxed for Exam)

> **$C_E = ₹ 32$ Lakhs ; $FCI = ₹ 1.5168$ Crore ; $WC = ₹ 22.75$ Lakhs ; $TCI = ₹ 1.744$ Crore**

---

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Example 2 — Cost Index Escalation

**Problem Statement:**
A centrifugal separator installed in a 1st-generation ethanol plant cost **₹5,00,000** in the year **2010**, when the **CEPCI** was **$550$**. Estimate the cost of an equivalent separator in **2024**, when the CEPCI is **$798$**. If the new separator has 20% higher capacity (using the **Six-Tenths Rule**), find the final estimated cost.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Time-based cost escalation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{2024} &= C_{2010} \cdot \left( \dfrac{I_{2024}}{I_{2010}} \right) \\
        &= 5,00,000 \cdot \left( \dfrac{798}{550} \right) \\
        &= 5,00,000 \cdot 1.4509 \\
        &= ₹ 7,25,454.55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Six-Tenths Rule (capacity correction).**

Capacity factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Capacity Ratio} &= \dfrac{S_B}{S_A} = 1.20
\end{aligned}
$$

Cost factor (six-tenths exponent, $x = 0.6$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost Ratio} &= (1.20)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \cdot \ln(1.20)} = e^{0.6 \cdot 0.1823} = e^{0.1094} = 1.1156
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Final escalated + capacity-corrected cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{final}} &= 7,25,454.55 \times 1.1156 \\
                 &= ₹ 8,09,517.62
\end{aligned}
$$

### ✅ Final Answer

> **Capacity-corrected escalated cost ≈ ₹ 8.10 Lakhs**

---

## 3.4 Solved Numerical Example 3 — Capital Recovery & NPV

**Problem Statement:**
A bioenergy CHP (Combined Heat & Power) plant requires a **capital investment of ₹ 80 Lakhs**. It generates **annual net cash savings of ₹ 12 Lakhs** for **15 years**. The **salvage value** at end of life is **₹ 8 Lakhs**. The **Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR)** is **$i = 10\%$** per annum.

Determine:
(a) The Capital Recovery Factor (CRF).
(b) Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) of the plant.
(c) Net Present Value (NPV) of the investment.
(d) Whether the investment is economically justified.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (a): Calculate CRF.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} \\
    &= \dfrac{0.10 \cdot (1.10)^{15}}{(1.10)^{15} - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing $(1.10)^{15}$:

$$
(1.10)^{15} = 4.1772
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \dfrac{0.10 \cdot 4.1772}{4.1772 - 1} \\
    &= \dfrac{0.41772}{3.1772} \\
    &= 0.13147
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b): Equivalent Annual Cost (capital recovery + salvage adjustment).**

The annual equivalent of the capital recovery with salvage is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= P \cdot CRF - S \cdot \left[ \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the sinking-fund factor for salvage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SFF &= \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} \\
    &= \dfrac{0.10}{3.1772} \\
    &= 0.03147
\end{aligned}
$$

Now:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 80,00,000 \cdot 0.13147 - 8,00,000 \cdot 0.03147 \\
  &= 10,51,760 - 25,176 \\
  &= ₹ 10,26,584 \;\text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c): Net Present Value.**

Present worth of uniform annual savings (using $\dfrac{P}{A}$ factor):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\dfrac{P}{A}, i, n\right) &= \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}} = \dfrac{1}{CRF} = 7.606
\end{aligned}
$$

Present worth of annual savings:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PW_{\text{savings}} &= 12,00,000 \cdot 7.606 = ₹ 91,27,200
\end{aligned}
$$

Present worth of salvage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PW_{\text{salvage}} &= \dfrac{8,00,000}{(1.10)^{15}} = \dfrac{8,00,000}{4.1772} = ₹ 19,15,120
\end{aligned}
$$

Total present worth of inflows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PW_{\text{in}} &= 91,27,200 + 19,15,120 = ₹ 1,10,42,320
\end{aligned}
$$

NPV:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= PW_{\text{in}} - P \\
    &= 1,10,42,320 - 80,00,000 \\
    &= ₹ 30,42,320 \quad (\text{POSITIVE})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (d): Economic Decision.**

Since $NPV > 0$ and $A_{\text{annual savings}} = ₹ 12,00,000 > A_{\text{capital recovery}} = ₹ 10,26,584$, the investment is **economically justified**.

### ✅ Final Answer (Boxed for Exam)

> **$CRF = 0.1315$ ; $A_{\text{recovery}} = ₹ 10.27$ Lakhs/yr ; $NPV = +₹ 30.42$ Lakhs ; Investment Justified** ✔

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Capital Estimation Methods

| **Parameter** | **Order-of-Magnitude** | **Study Estimate (Lang/Hand)** | **Detailed Estimate** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | $\pm 30\%$ to $\pm 50\%$ | $\pm 20\%$ to $\pm 30\%$ | $\pm 5\%$ to $\pm 10\%$ |
| Time Required | Hours–Days | Weeks–Months | Months–Years |
| Cost of Estimate | < 0.1% of CapEx | 0.1%–0.3% of CapEx | 1%–5% of CapEx |
| Data Needed | Past project data | Major equipment list + factors | Vendor quotes, drawings |
| Use Case | Early feasibility | Budget approval | Tender/Final investment |
| KTU Exam Frequency | Low | **HIGH** | Medium |

---

## 3.6 Depreciation — Comparative Numerical Analysis

A biorefinery equipment costs **₹ 20 Lakhs**, salvage value **₹ 2 Lakhs**, useful life **10 years**. Compute depreciation using both methods.

**Straight-Line Method (SLM):**

$$
D_{SL} = \dfrac{20,00,000 - 2,00,000}{10} = ₹ 1,80,000 \; \text{per year}
$$

**Double Declining Balance (DDB, $d = 20\%$ per year):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Year 1: } & D_1 = 20,00,000 \times 0.20 = ₹ 4,00,000 \\
                & BV_1 = 20,00,000 - 4,00,000 = ₹ 16,00,000 \\
\text{Year 2: } & D_2 = 16,00,000 \times 0.20 = ₹ 3,20,000 \\
                & BV_2 = 12,80,000 \\
\text{Year 3: } & D_3 = 12,80,000 \times 0.20 = ₹ 2,56,000 \\
                & BV_3 = 10,24,000
\end{aligned}
$$

(Continuing similarly until year 10; book value approaches but never equals salvage.)

| **Year** | **SLM Depreciation (₹)** | **DDB Depreciation (₹)** | **SLM BV (₹)** | **DDB BV (₹)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | — | 20,00,000 | 20,00,000 |
| 1 | 1,80,000 | 4,00,000 | 18,20,000 | 16,00,000 |
| 2 | 1,80,000 | 3,20,000 | 16,40,000 | 12,80,000 |
| 5 | 1,80,000 | 1,31,072 | 11,00,000 | 6,55,360 |
| 10 | 1,80,000 | 7,340 | 2,00,000 | 2,00,000* |

*BV never falls below salvage; depreciation stops when BV = salvage.

> [!TIP]
> **For tax calculations**, biofuel projects in India typically use the **Written Down Value (WDV) / Declining Balance** method under the Income Tax Act, 1961 (currently at 15% for most plant & machinery).
