# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Define the term "Fixed Capital Investment" in the context of a bioenergy plant. List any four direct costs included in it.**

**Model Answer (Examiner-Approved Word Count ~80–100 words):**

> *Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) is the total one-time expenditure required to construct, install, and commission a bioenergy plant to a ready-for-use state. It includes all depreciable physical assets and the indirect expenses associated with their establishment. The four main direct cost components are:*
> 1. *Purchased Equipment Cost (delivered at site).*
> 2. *Equipment Installation (foundations, supports, labour).*
> 3. *Piping & Instrumentation (process and control).*
> 4. *Buildings (process and auxiliary structures).*
>
> *FCI excludes working capital and is gradually recovered over the plant's economic life through depreciation.*

**[Marks Distribution: 1 (Definition) + 2 (Four Direct Costs)]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**Distinguish between "Fixed Capital Investment" and "Working Capital" with one example relevant to a biodiesel plant.**

**Model Answer (Tabular Format Preferred):**

> *Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) is a one-time, long-term capital outlay for physical assets that remain in the plant for many years, such as the **transesterification reactor** in a biodiesel plant. In contrast, Working Capital (WC) is the short-term, circulating funds needed to keep the plant operating, such as the **initial stock of methanol, NaOH catalyst, and feedstock oil** that get consumed and replenished each operating cycle. FCI is depreciable; WC is fully recoverable at project end.*

**[Marks Distribution: 1.5 (Distinction) + 1.5 (Biodiesel Example)]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Standard:** Within Module 4, students answer **one full 14-mark question** from a choice of two. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Slot 2]**

A small-scale **biodiesel plant** (fluid processing) has the following delivered equipment costs:

| **Equipment** | **Cost (₹ Lakhs)** |
|---|---|
| Transesterification Reactor | $12.0$ |
| Methanol Recovery Column | $18.0$ |
| Glycerin Wash Settler | $5.0$ |
| Biodiesel Storage Tank | $4.0$ |
| Pumps, Heat Exchangers & Misc. | $6.0$ |

**(a)** Using the **Lang Factor Method** ($f_L = 4.74$), determine the **Total Capital Investment (TCI)** if working capital is **18% of FCI**. State all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the plant is commissioned in **2024** (CEPCI = $798$) and we wish to scale the plant to **1.6 times** its original capacity using the **Six-Tenths Rule**, determine the revised capital investment. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (Question A)

**Part (a) — Lang Factor Method (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Total Purchased Equipment Cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_E &= 12.0 + 18.0 + 5.0 + 4.0 + 6.0 \\
    &= ₹ 45.0 \; \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Lang factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FCI &= 4.74 \times 45.0 = ₹ 213.3 \; \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Working Capital.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WC &= 0.18 \times 213.3 = ₹ 38.394 \; \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Total Capital Investment.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCI &= 213.3 + 38.394 = ₹ 251.694 \; \text{Lakhs} \approx ₹ 2.517 \; \text{Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assumptions Stated:**
- All costs are in INR (Lakhs).
- $f_L = 4.74$ is appropriate for fluid-processing plants.
- Working capital fraction (18%) is reasonable for the plant size.
- Land cost is included indirectly via Lang factor.

**Part (b) — Capacity Scaling (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Capacity scaling ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Capacity Ratio} &= \dfrac{S_B}{S_A} = 1.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Cost scaling factor (Six-Tenths Rule, $x = 0.6$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost Ratio} &= (1.6)^{0.6}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute via logarithm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.6)^{0.6} &= e^{0.6 \ln(1.6)} \\
             &= e^{0.6 \times 0.4700} \\
             &= e^{0.2820} \\
             &= 1.3258
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Revised Total Capital Investment.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCI_{\text{revised}} &= 251.694 \times 1.3258 \\
                     &= ₹ 333.66 \; \text{Lakhs} \\
                     &\approx ₹ 3.34 \; \text{Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** Cost indices (CEPCI) are not used here because no base year is specified — the question only asks for capacity scaling. If base year were given, escalation would be applied **before** capacity scaling.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Slot 1]**

A farmer-cooperative **biogas plant** (mixed solid-fluid processing) requires a **capital investment of ₹ 1.2 Crore**. The plant generates annual net savings of **₹ 18 Lakhs** for **20 years** with a **salvage value of ₹ 10 Lakhs** at the end of life. The **MARR** is **$i = 12\%$**.

**(a)** Calculate the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** and the **equivalent annual cost** of the capital. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the **NPV criterion**, determine whether the investment is **economically justified**. Comment briefly. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (Question B)

**Part (a) — CRF & Annual Cost (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute $(1.12)^{20}$.**

$$
(1.12)^{20} = 9.6463
$$

**Step 2: CRF calculation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \dfrac{0.12 \times 9.6463}{9.6463 - 1} \\
    &= \dfrac{1.1576}{8.6463} \\
    &= 0.13388
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Capital Recovery (annual cost excluding salvage adjustment).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{capital}} &= P \cdot CRF \\
                   &= 1,20,00,000 \times 0.13388 \\
                   &= ₹ 16,06,560 \; \text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Adjust for salvage value (Annual equivalent salvage credit).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{salvage}} &= S \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} \\
                  &= 10,00,000 \times \dfrac{0.12}{8.6463} \\
                  &= 10,00,000 \times 0.01388 \\
                  &= ₹ 1,38,800 \; \text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Net Equivalent Annual Cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{net}} &= A_{\text{capital}} - A_{\text{salvage}} \\
              &= 16,06,560 - 1,38,800 \\
              &= ₹ 14,67,760 \; \text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — NPV Criterion (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Present Worth of Annual Savings.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PW_{\text{savings}} &= A \cdot \left(\dfrac{P}{A}, 12\%, 20\right) \\
                    &= 18,00,000 \times \dfrac{9.6463 - 1}{0.12 \times 9.6463} \\
                    &= 18,00,000 \times 7.4694 \\
                    &= ₹ 1,34,44,920
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Present Worth of Salvage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PW_{\text{salvage}} &= \dfrac{10,00,000}{9.6463} = ₹ 1,03,667
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — let me recompute carefully. Salvage occurs at the end of year 20, so it must be discounted by $(1.12)^{20} = 9.6463$.

$$
PW_{\text{salvage}} = \dfrac{10,00,000}{9.6463} \approx ₹ 1,03,667
$$

**Step 3: Total Present Worth of Inflows.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PW_{\text{in}} &= 1,34,44,920 + 1,03,667 = ₹ 1,35,48,587
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Net Present Value.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= PW_{\text{in}} - P \\
    &= 1,35,48,587 - 1,20,00,000 \\
    &= +₹ 15,48,587 \; (\text{POSITIVE})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $NPV > 0$, the **investment is economically justified** at MARR = 12%.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Capital-Cost Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Working Capital:** Many students compute only FCI and forget to add WC. Board examiners specifically check for $TCI = FCI + WC$ — losing **2–3 marks** is common.
>
> 2. **Wrong Lang Factor:** Using $f_L = 4.28$ for a fluid-processing biodiesel plant (or vice versa). **Memorise: Solid = 4.28, Fluid = 4.74, Mixed = ~4.5.**
>
> 3. **Confusing CEPCI Application:** Cost index escalation applies to **time-based changes**, not capacity changes. If both are involved, apply **escalation first, then Six-Tenths Rule**.
>
> 4. **Sign Convention for NPV:** Forgetting the negative sign on initial investment leads to wrong NPV. Always: $NPV = PW_{\text{inflows}} - P_{\text{initial}}$.
>
> 5. **Missing Salvage Value Adjustment:** In $A = P \cdot CRF$ formula, if salvage exists, students forget the second term. Use the full formula $A = P \cdot CRF - S \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
>
> 6. **Units Inconsistency:** Mixing Lakhs and Crores. Stick to **one unit throughout** a numerical solution and convert only at the final answer.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Capital Cost = FCI + WC** — always state this equation first.
- **FCI includes Direct + Indirect costs**; **Land is non-depreciable**.
- **Lang Factor ($f_L$):** Solid = **4.28**, Fluid = **4.74**, Mixed ≈ **4.5**.
- **FCI = $f_L \times C_E$** (delivered equipment cost).
- **Cost Index Escalation:** $C_2 = C_1 \times (I_2 / I_1)$ — applies to **time**, not capacity.
- **Six-Tenths Rule:** Cost $\propto$ Capacity$^{0.6}$ — applies to **capacity**, not time.
- **Working Capital** is typically **5%–20%** of FCI for biofuel plants.
- **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF):** $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
- **Annual Equivalent:** $A = P \cdot CRF - S \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
- **NPV > 0** → Investment justified.
- **Straight-Line Depreciation:** $D_{SL} = (P - S) / n$.
- **Declining Balance:** $D_k = P \cdot (1-d)^{k-1} \cdot d$; book value never drops below salvage.
- **Order of Magnitude → Study Estimate (Lang/Hand) → Detailed Estimate** — accuracy increases from ±30% to ±5%.
- **For KTU exam:** Always write assumptions; show all formulas before substitution; box the final answer with units.

> **🎯 Final Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question in Module 4, structure your answer as **(a) Given Data → (b) Formula → (c) Step-by-step Substitution → (d) Final Answer with units → (e) Interpretation/Comment.** Board examiners award **methodology marks** even when arithmetic slips occur — but only if your reasoning is clearly written.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operating costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Cost (OPEX)**: In the context of bioenergy and biofuel process engineering, **Operating Cost** is defined as the total recurring expenditure incurred over the operational lifetime of a biofuel/bioenergy plant to convert raw biomass feedstocks into finished fuels (bioethanol, biodiesel, biogas, etc.), expressed per unit of product (₹/L, \$/GJ, ₹/kg) or as an annualized figure.

Operating cost is a critical engineering economic parameter that combines **Variable Manufacturing Costs (VMC)**, **Fixed Manufacturing Costs (FMC)**, and **General Expenses (GE)**. It is the principal determinant of a biofuel plant's profitability and is distinct from the one-time Capital Expenditure (CAPEX).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Daily Petrol Pump" Analogy**: Think of owning a petrol pump. The **land and building (CAPEX)** is a one-time cost, but every day you need to spend money on **buying fuel from the refinery, paying the pump attendant, electricity for the lights, cleaning supplies, and routine servicing**. These daily, recurring expenses are your *Operating Costs*. If your daily expenses (OPEX) per litre of fuel sold exceed the selling price, you'll go bankrupt — no matter how beautiful your pump station is (CAPEX). In biofuel plants, the same logic applies: feedstock cost alone can be **60–80%** of OPEX, making it the single largest battleground for economic viability.

### 1.3 Classification of Operating Costs (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Category | Description | Example in Biofuel Plant |
|----------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Variable Costs** | Costs that scale with production volume | Sugarcane molasses, enzyme doses, process water |
| **Fixed Costs** | Costs independent of production volume | Plant manager salary, insurance premiums |
| **Direct Manufacturing Costs** | Traceable to specific product | Feedstock, direct labor, utilities for reactor |
| **Indirect Manufacturing Costs** | Shared across plant operations | Maintenance overhead, QC lab expenses |
| **General Expenses** | Non-manufacturing recurring costs | R&D, sales, administrative overheads |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics**: A 1 megawatt (MW) biogas plant typically incurs **OPEX of ₹1.5–3.0 crore/year** (≈ \$180k–360k/year), while a 100 KLPD biodiesel plant has OPEX dominated by oil feedstock (~**70–85%** of total variable cost).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Decomposition of Operating Cost

The **Total Operating Cost (TOC)** is built bottom-up, layer by layer, following the rigorous Lang Factor methodology used in chemical process economics.

**Step 1 — Raw Material Cost ($C_{RM}$)**
This is the *biggest contributor* in any biofuel plant. Calculated as:
$$C_{RM} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i \times P_i \times t)$$
where $Q_i$ is the consumption rate of material $i$ (kg/hr), $P_i$ is its unit price (₹/kg), and $t$ is the annual operating hours.

**Step 2 — Utility Cost ($C_U$)**
Covers steam, cooling water, process water, electricity, and compressed air:
$$C_U = \sum_{j} (u_j \times p_j \times t)$$
where $u_j$ is the utility consumption rate and $p_j$ is its unit price.

**Step 3 — Direct Labor & Supervision ($C_{DL}$)**
$$C_{DL} = N_{workers} \times \text{Monthly Salary} \times 12$$
Include **operators, shift engineers, supervisors, QC technicians**.

**Step 4 — Maintenance & Repairs ($C_{MR}$)**
Typically estimated as **3–6% of Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** per year:
$$C_{MR} = f_{MR} \times FCI \quad \text{where } f_{MR} \in [0.03,\, 0.06]$$

**Step 5 — Operating Supplies ($C_{OS}$)**
Estimated as **10–20% of $C_{MR}$**:
$$C_{OS} = 0.15 \times C_{MR} \;(\text{typical})$$

**Step 6 — Laboratory / QC Cost ($C_{QC}$)**
Estimated as **10–15% of $C_{DL}$**.

**Step 7 — Patents & Royalties ($C_{PR}$)**
Estimated as **~3% of TOC** (for licensed technologies like enzymatic hydrolysis).

**Step 8 — Insurance & Local Taxes ($C_{IT}$)**
Estimated as **1–2% of FCI**.

**Step 9 — Plant Overheads ($C_{PO}$)**
**50–70% of $C_{DL} + C_{MR} + C_{OS}$** (covers security, cafeteria, safety equipment, etc.).

**Step 10 — Administrative Expenses ($C_{AE}$)**
**15–25% of $C_{DL}$** (covers HR, accounts, management).

**Step 11 — Distribution & Selling ($C_{DS}$)**
**2–10% of TOC** (for retail biofuel marketing, logistics).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Unit | Typical Value |
|---|-----------|---------------------|------|---------------|
| 1 | **Raw Material Cost** | $C_{RM} = \sum Q_i P_i t$ | ₹/yr | 60–80% of VMC |
| 2 | **Utility Cost** | $C_U = \sum u_j p_j t$ | ₹/yr | 5–15% of TOC |
| 3 | **Direct Labor** | $C_{DL} = N \times s \times 12$ | ₹/yr | 5–10% of TOC |
| 4 | **Maintenance** | $C_{MR} = (0.03–0.06) \times FCI$ | ₹/yr | — |
| 5 | **Operating Supplies** | $C_{OS} = 0.10–0.20 \times C_{MR}$ | ₹/yr | — |
| 6 | **QC/QA Cost** | $C_{QC} = 0.10–0.15 \times C_{DL}$ | ₹/yr | — |
| 7 | **Insurance & Tax** | $C_{IT} = 0.01–0.02 \times FCI$ | ₹/yr | — |
| 8 | **Plant Overhead** | $C_{PO} = 0.50–0.70 \times (C_{DL}+C_{MR}+C_{OS})$ | ₹/yr | — |
| 9 | **Admin Cost** | $C_{AE} = 0.15–0.25 \times C_{DL}$ | ₹/yr | — |
| 10 | **Distribution & Selling** | $C_{DS} = 0.02–0.10 \times TOC$ | ₹/yr | — |
| 11 | **Total Production Cost** | $TPC = C_{RM} + C_U + C_{DL} + C_{MR} + C_{OS} + C_{QC} + C_{PR} + C_{IT} + C_{PO} + C_{AE} + C_{DS}$ | ₹/yr | — |
| 12 | **Unit Operating Cost** | $UOC = TPC / (P_{annual})$ | ₹/kg or ₹/L | — |
| 13 | **Levelized Cost of Fuel** | $LCOF = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} (CAPEX_t + OPEX_t)/(1+r)^t}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} E_t/(1+r)^t}$ | ₹/GJ | — |
| 14 | **Break-Even Price** | $BEP = \frac{FCI \times CRF + TOC}{Q_{annual}}$ | ₹/L | — |
| 15 | **Capital Recovery Factor** | $CRF = \dfrac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$ | dimensionless | 0.10–0.15 |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plant Sizing Decisions**: Engineers use OPEX projections to choose between a 50 KLPD vs 100 KLPD biodiesel plant. The unit OPEX typically **decreases with scale** (economy of scale).
- **Feedstock Switching Decisions**: When palm oil prices rise, OPEX of biodiesel spikes — engineers compare switching to waste cooking oil or jatropha to maintain OPEX within target margins.
- **Government Policy Inputs**: India's **SATAT (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation)** scheme and the **National Biofuel Policy 2018** are anchored on OPEX thresholds (e.g., ₹55–60/L for 2G ethanol).
- **Comparative LCA**: A 2024 IEA Bioenergy report showed that LCOF of **2G ethanol from bagasse is ~\$0.55/L** versus 1G from sugarcane at **~\$0.45/L**, primarily due to higher enzyme OPEX in 2G.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Levelized Cost of Fuel (LCOF)

The LCOF is the constant price at which fuel must be sold over the project life to recover all CAPEX and OPEX, including the time value of money.

**Starting Point — Net Present Value of Costs**

The total discounted cost over project life $n$ years:

$$
NPV_{cost} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CAPEX_t + OPEX_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

For a typical biofuel plant, CAPEX is incurred in year 0 and OPEX is uniform for years 1 to $n$:

$$
NPV_{cost} = CAPEX_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{OPEX_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Apply Uniform Series Present Worth Factor**

If $OPEX_t = OPEX$ (constant annual operating cost), the summation becomes a uniform series:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+r)^t} = \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n} = \frac{1}{CRF}
$$

Therefore:

$$
NPV_{cost} = CAPEX_0 + \frac{OPEX}{CRF}
$$

**Discount the Energy Produced**

Similarly for annual production $E$ (in GJ or litres):

$$
NPV_{energy} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t} = \frac{E}{CRF}
$$

**Form the LCOF Ratio**

$$
LCOF = \frac{NPV_{cost}}{NPV_{energy}} = \frac{CAPEX_0 \times CRF + OPEX}{E}
$$

This is the canonical engineering economic formula appearing in KTU exam problems.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

> **Problem**: A proposed biodiesel plant has the following data. Compute the **Total Operating Cost (TOC)**, **Unit Operating Cost (UOC in ₹/L)**, and the **Minimum Selling Price (Break-Even Price)** for a 10% internal rate of return over 15 years.

**Given Data**
- Production capacity: 30,000 litres/day of biodiesel
- Operating days per year: 330
- Annual production: $Q = 30{,}000 \times 330 = 9{,}900{,}000$ L/yr $\approx 9.9 \times 10^6$ L/yr
- Fixed Capital Investment (FCI): ₹12 crore = ₹1.2 × 10⁸
- Feedstock consumption: 32,000 kg/day waste cooking oil @ ₹45/kg
- Methanol: 4,500 L/day @ ₹28/L
- NaOH catalyst: 300 kg/day @ ₹140/kg
- Phosphoric acid: 200 kg/day @ ₹90/kg
- Steam: 8,000 kg/day @ ₹0.5/kg
- Electricity: 1,200 kWh/day @ ₹7/kWh
- Direct Labor: 12 workers @ ₹18,000/month + 2 supervisors @ ₹35,000/month
- Annual maintenance rate: 4% of FCI

**Step 1 — Compute Annual Raw Material Cost**

Feedstock: $32{,}000 \times 45 \times 330 = 4.752 \times 10^8$ ₹/yr
Methanol: $4{,}500 \times 28 \times 330 = 4.158 \times 10^7$ ₹/yr
NaOH: $300 \times 140 \times 330 = 1.386 \times 10^7$ ₹/yr
H₃PO₄: $200 \times 90 \times 330 = 5.94 \times 10^6$ ₹/yr

$$
C_{RM} = 4.752 \times 10^8 + 4.158 \times 10^7 + 1.386 \times 10^7 + 5.94 \times 10^6
$$

$$
C_{RM} = 4.752 \times 10^8 + 0.4158 \times 10^8 + 0.1386 \times 10^8 + 0.0594 \times 10^8 = 5.3658 \times 10^8 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Utility Cost**

Steam: $8{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 330 = 1.32 \times 10^6$ ₹/yr
Electricity: $1{,}200 \times 7 \times 330 = 2.772 \times 10^6$ ₹/yr

$$
C_U = 1.32 \times 10^6 + 2.772 \times 10^6 = 4.092 \times 10^6 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Direct Labor**

Workers: $12 \times 18{,}000 \times 12 = 2.592 \times 10^6$ ₹/yr
Supervisors: $2 \times 35{,}000 \times 12 = 8.40 \times 10^5$ ₹/yr

$$
C_{DL} = 2.592 \times 10^6 + 0.840 \times 10^6 = 3.432 \times 10^6 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Maintenance & Repairs**

$$
C_{MR} = 0.04 \times 1.2 \times 10^8 = 4.80 \times 10^6 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

**Step 5 — Operating Supplies** (15% of $C_{MR}$)

$$
C_{OS} = 0.15 \times 4.80 \times 10^6 = 7.20 \times 10^5 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

**Step 6 — QC/QA** (12% of $C_{DL}$)

$$
C_{QC} = 0.12 \times 3.432 \times 10^6 = 4.118 \times 10^5 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

**Step 7 — Insurance & Tax** (1.5% of FCI)

$$
C_{IT} = 0.015 \times 1.2 \times 10^8 = 1.80 \times 10^6 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

**Step 8 — Plant Overhead** (60% of $C_{DL} + C_{MR} + C_{OS}$)

$$
C_{PO} = 0.60 \times (3.432 + 4.80 + 0.72) \times 10^6 = 0.60 \times 8.952 \times 10^6 = 5.371 \times 10^6 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

**Step 9 — Administrative** (20% of $C_{DL}$)

$$
C_{AE} = 0.20 \times 3.432 \times 10^6 = 6.864 \times 10^5 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

**Step 10 — Distribution & Selling** (5% of TOC, assume TOC ≈ 5.45 × 10⁸ for first pass)

$$
C_{DS} = 0.05 \times 5.45 \times 10^8 = 2.725 \times 10^7 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

(Refining via iteration: $TOC = 5.45 \times 10^8$ gives $C_{DS} \approx 2.72 \times 10^7$ — converges in 1 iteration.)

**Step 11 — Total Operating Cost**

$$
TOC = C_{RM} + C_U + C_{DL} + C_{MR} + C_{OS} + C_{QC} + C_{IT} + C_{PO} + C_{AE} + C_{DS}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TOC = &\ 536.58 \times 10^6 + 4.09 \times 10^6 + 3.43 \times 10^6 + 4.80 \times 10^6 + 0.72 \times 10^6 \\
& + 0.41 \times 10^6 + 1.80 \times 10^6 + 5.37 \times 10^6 + 0.69 \times 10^6 + 27.25 \times 10^6
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
TOC = 585.14 \times 10^6 \text{ ₹/yr} \approx \text{₹ 5.85 crore/yr}
$$

**Step 12 — Unit Operating Cost**

$$
UOC = \frac{TOC}{Q} = \frac{5.8514 \times 10^8}{9.9 \times 10^6} = 59.10 \text{ ₹/L}
$$

**Step 13 — Capital Recovery Factor**

$$
CRF = \frac{0.10(1.10)^{15}}{(1.10)^{15} - 1} = \frac{0.10 \times 4.1772}{3.1772} = 0.1315
$$

**Step 14 — Break-Even Price (BEP)**

$$
BEP = \frac{FCI \times CRF + TOC}{Q} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^8 \times 0.1315 + 5.8514 \times 10^8}{9.9 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
BEP = \frac{1.578 \times 10^7 + 5.8514 \times 10^8}{9.9 \times 10^6} = \frac{6.009 \times 10^8}{9.9 \times 10^6} = 60.70 \text{ ₹/L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation**: To recover CAPEX and OPEX at 10% IRR, biodiesel must sell above **₹60.70/L**. Current market price in India is **₹90–105/L**, indicating a healthy margin of **₹30–44/L**, provided feedstock supply remains stable.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Analysis

A 1% change in feedstock cost causes a **0.78% change in TOC** (highest leverage). A KTU viva favourite is the "sensitivity table":

| Parameter Varied | % Change in TOC (per +10% in parameter) | Vulnerability Rank |
|------------------|---------------------------------------|--------------------|
| Feedstock Price | +7.8% | 1 (highest) |
| Maintenance Rate | +0.08% | 2 |
| Labor | +0.06% | 3 |
| Steam Cost | +0.022% | 4 |
| Electricity | +0.05% | 5 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Total Operating Cost

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TOTAL OPERATING COST TOC] --> B[Variable Manufacturing Costs VMC]
    A --> C[Fixed Manufacturing Costs FMC]
    A --> D[General Expenses GE]

    B --> B1[Raw Materials C_RM]
    B --> B2[Utilities C_U]
    B --> B3[Packaging C_PK]

    C --> C1[Direct Labor C_DL]
    C --> C2[Maintenance C_MR]
    C --> C3[Operating Supplies C_OS]
    C --> C4[Quality Control C_QC]
    C --> C5[Insurance and Tax C_IT]
    C --> C6[Plant Overheads C_PO]

    D --> D1[Administrative C_AE]
    D --> D2[Distribution and Selling C_DS]
    D --> D3[Royalties C_PR]
    D --> D4[R and D C_RD]

    B1 --> B1a[Feedstock 60 to 80 percent]
    B1 --> B1b[Catalysts and Reagents]
    B1 --> B1c[Enzymes for 2G]
    B2 --> B2a[Steam and Hot Oil]
    B2 --> B2b[Electricity]
    B2 --> B2c[Process and Cooling Water]
    C2 --> C2a[Scheduled Turnaround]
    C2 --> C2b[Spare Parts]
    C2 --> C2c[Contractor Services]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: From OPEX Inputs to Break-Even Selling Price

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[OPERATING COST INPUTS]
        I1[Feedstock Q and P]
        I2[Utility Rates]
        I3[Labor Headcount]
        I4[FCI from CAPEX]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[CALCULATION ENGINE]
        K1[Compute C_RM]
        K2[Compute C_U]
        K3[Compute C_DL]
        K4[Compute C_MR as 4 percent of FCI]
        K5[Compute Overheads and Admin]
        K6[Sum to TOC]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS[FINANCIAL METRICS]
        O1[Unit Operating Cost UOC]
        O2[CRF and Annualized CAPEX]
        O3[Break Even Price BEP]
        O4[Net Profit Margin]
    end

    I1 --> K1
    I2 --> K2
    I3 --> K3
    I4 --> K4
    K1 --> K6
    K2 --> K6
    K3 --> K6
    K4 --> K5
    K5 --> K6
    K6 --> O1
    K4 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> O4
```

### 4.3 Cost Composition Pie-View (Biodiesel Plant)

```mermaid
pie title OPEX Composition of 30 KLPD Biodiesel Plant
    "Feedstock Oil" : 78.0
    "Methanol" : 6.5
    "Catalyst NaOH" : 2.2
    "Utilities" : 0.6
    "Labor" : 0.5
    "Maintenance" : 0.7
    "Overheads and Admin" : 0.8
    "Insurance" : 0.3
    "Distribution and Selling" : 4.4
    "Other" : 6.0
```

### 4.4 Cash Flow Timeline Over Project Life

```mermaid
gantt
    title Typical Biofuel Plant OPEX-CAPEX Cash Flow
    dateFormat YYYY
    axisFormat Y
    section Capital Phase
    Engineering and Construction :a1, 2024, 2y
    section Operating Phase
    Year 1 OPEX :a2, 2026, 1y
    Year 2 OPEX :a3, 2027, 1y
    Year 3 OPEX :a4, 2028, 1y
    Year 4 OPEX :a5, 2029, 1y
    Year 5 OPEX :a6, 2030, 1y
    Year 6 to 15 OPEX :a7, 2031, 9y
    section Shutdown
    Decommissioning :a8, 2040, 1y
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`
**Distinguish between Fixed Manufacturing Cost (FMC) and Variable Manufacturing Cost (VMC) in a biodiesel plant. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer**:
- **FMC** remains constant regardless of production volume within design capacity. **Examples**: Plant manager salary, annual insurance premium, depreciation-linked maintenance contracts.
- **VMC** varies directly with production volume. **Examples**: Waste cooking oil feedstock, methanol reagent, enzyme dosage, process steam.
- **Key test**: If the plant shuts down for a month, FMC continues; VMC goes to zero.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is feedstock cost often the single largest contributor to operating cost in a biofuel plant? Illustrate with a numerical statement.**

**Model Answer**:
Feedstock (biomass, oil, sugar, starch) is converted stoichiometrically into fuel with a typical mass yield of only **20–40%**. Hence, for every litre of fuel produced, ~**2.5–5 kg of feedstock** must be procured. Since biofuel plants consume **tons of feedstock daily**, even small unit-price variations cause large OPEX swings. For a 30 KLPD biodiesel plant, feedstock constitutes **70–80% of TOC**, making it the dominant economic lever.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Model Paper, Module 4]`

**(a)** Define Total Operating Cost (TOC) for a biofuel plant. Enumerate and briefly explain **any five components** of operating cost, stating the typical range or empirical formula used to estimate each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50,000 m³/yr biogas plant is proposed. The feedstock is 30,000 tonnes/yr of press mud @ ₹800/tonne. Electricity is consumed at 1.8 million kWh/yr @ ₹6/kWh. Labor cost is ₹45 lakh/yr. Maintenance is 5% of FCI = ₹8 crore. Plant overheads = 50% of labor. Admin = 20% of labor. Selling = 5% of TOC. Compute the **TOC** and **Unit Operating Cost (₹/m³)**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**(a)** Definition and components as given in Sections 1.1 and 2.1 above. Any 5 of: $C_{RM}, C_U, C_{DL}, C_{MR}, C_{OS}, C_{QC}, C_{IT}, C_{PO}, C_{AE}, C_{DS}$.

**(b)** Step-by-step:

$$
C_{RM} = 30{,}000 \times 800 = 2.4 \times 10^7 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

$$
C_U = 1.8 \times 10^6 \times 6 = 1.08 \times 10^7 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

$$
C_{DL} = 4.5 \times 10^6 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

$$
C_{MR} = 0.05 \times 8 \times 10^7 = 4.0 \times 10^6 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

$$
C_{PO} = 0.50 \times 4.5 \times 10^6 = 2.25 \times 10^6 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

$$
C_{AE} = 0.20 \times 4.5 \times 10^6 = 9.0 \times 10^5 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

Sub-total of explicit terms: $24.0 + 10.8 + 4.5 + 4.0 + 2.25 + 0.9 = 46.45 \times 10^6$ ₹/yr

Assuming $C_{DS} = 5\%$ of TOC: $TOC = 46.45 \times 10^6 + 0.05 \cdot TOC$

$$
0.95 \cdot TOC = 46.45 \times 10^6 \Rightarrow TOC = 48.89 \times 10^6 \text{ ₹/yr} \approx \text{₹ 4.89 crore/yr}
$$

$$
UOC = \frac{4.889 \times 10^7}{50{,}000} = 977.8 \text{ ₹/m}^3 \approx \text{₹ 978/m}^3
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall**: Students often forget the **self-consistent $C_{DS}$** calculation (5% of TOC includes $C_{DS}$ itself, so iterative or algebraic solution is required). Marks are lost for ignoring this.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - 2023 ESE Supplementary]`

**(a)** What is the Levelized Cost of Fuel (LCOF)? Derive its expression from fundamental discounted cash flow principles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2G ethanol plant requires CAPEX of ₹250 crore and OPEX of ₹35 crore/yr. Annual production = 30 million litres. Plant life = 20 years, discount rate = 12%. Compute the **LCOF in ₹/L** and comment on its competitiveness with petrol at ₹103/L. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**(a)** Derivation as in Section 3.1:

$$
LCOF = \frac{CAPEX_0 \times CRF + OPEX}{E}
$$

with

$$
CRF = \frac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}
$$

**(b)** Step-by-step:

Compute CRF:

$$
CRF = \frac{0.12(1.12)^{20}}{(1.12)^{20} - 1}
$$

$$
(1.12)^{20} = 9.646 \Rightarrow CRF = \frac{0.12 \times 9.646}{8.646} = \frac{1.1575}{8.646} = 0.1339
$$

Annualized CAPEX:

$$
A_{CAPEX} = 250 \times 10^7 \times 0.1339 = 33.47 \times 10^7 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

Total annualized cost:

$$
A_{total} = 33.47 \times 10^7 + 35.0 \times 10^7 = 68.47 \times 10^7 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

LCOF:

$$
LCOF = \frac{68.47 \times 10^7}{30 \times 10^6} = \frac{6.847 \times 10^8}{3.0 \times 10^7} = 22.82 \text{ ₹/L}
$$

**Comment**: At ₹22.82/L, 2G ethanol is **~22% cheaper** than petrol, indicating strong economic competitiveness. However, this excludes **blending margins, transport, and excise** which add ₹5–8/L.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall**: 
> 1. Forgetting to multiply CAPEX by CRF (students often divide OPEX by production, then *add* CAPEX directly per litre — wrong dimensionally and economically). 
> 2. Using the wrong number of years ($n=20$ vs 25 — read carefully). 
> 3. Mixing units: ₹/L vs ₹/kL is a frequent silent error. Always state the unit explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Viva & ESE**

- [ ] **Operating Cost (OPEX) ≠ Capital Cost (CAPEX)**. OPEX is recurring; CAPEX is one-time.
- [ ] **Feedstock is king** — typically 60–80% of TOC in biodiesel/bioethanol plants. The single biggest lever for cost reduction is feedstock strategy.
- [ ] **FMC vs VMC**: FMC continues even if plant is shut; VMC scales with throughput.
- [ ] **Maintenance = 3–6% of FCI/yr**. Don't use 10% or 1% — both are wrong.
- [ ] **Plant overhead = 50–70% of (Labor + Maintenance + Operating Supplies)**. Memorize the "50–70%" range.
- [ ] **Admin = 15–25% of Labor**. Used to compute $C_{AE}$.
- [ ] **Selling/Distribution is iterative**: $C_{DS} = 5\%$ of $TOC$ creates a self-reference — solve algebraically.
- [ ] **CRF** is the *annualized* version of CAPEX: $CRF = r(1+r)^n / [(1+r)^n - 1]$. Used to compare plants of different lifetimes.
- [ ] **LCOF = (CAPEX × CRF + OPEX) / Annual Production**. Always gives ₹/L or ₹/GJ.
- [ ] **Break-Even Price = (CAPEX × CRF + OPEX) / Annual Production**. The minimum selling price for NPV = 0.
- [ ] **Sensitivity**: A 10% increase in feedstock price causes a **~7.8% increase in TOC** in a typical biodiesel plant — the most sensitive parameter.
- [ ] **Unit consistency**: When mixing ₹/kg, ₹/L, ₹/kWh, always convert to a common base (₹/yr) before summing.
- [ ] **India-specific anchor**: The SATAT scheme targets CBG (Compressed Biogas) at **₹46/kg**; National Policy on Biofuels 2018 mandates 20% ethanol blending by 2025 (ESY-20).
- [ ] **International benchmark**: 2G ethanol LCOF in 2024 ≈ **\$0.50–0.65/L globally**; biodiesel LCOF ≈ **\$0.80–1.10/L**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food versus fuel debate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT413 - BIOENERGY AND BIOFUELS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Food versus fuel debate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food vs. Fuel Debate (KTU 2024 Definition):** The *Food versus Fuel Debate* is the socio-economic, ethical, and environmental contention surrounding the diversion of agricultural feedstocks (food crops, arable land, freshwater, and fertilizers) from human and livestock consumption toward the production of **first-generation biofuels** such as bioethanol and biodiesel. The debate evaluates whether the energy security benefits of biofuels outweigh the consequences on global food security, commodity prices, and arable land availability.

The debate formally emerged after the **Energy Policy Act of 2005 (USA)** and the **EU Renewable Energy Directive (RED) 2009**, which mandated minimum biofuel blending targets. The redirection of maize, soybean, sugarcane, rapeseed, and palm oil into the energy market triggered what economists term the **"Agrofuel Paradox"** — a scenario where renewable fuel production inadvertently threatens the very food systems it intends to sustain.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Single Plate Problem":**
> Imagine a family of **four** with a single large plate of rice. Now suppose the head of the family decides to set aside **one-third of the rice** to brew beer (for income), arguing it earns more money. While economically rational, the family now has less rice to eat. If the rice price rises globally, the family may even sell *more* of its share, pushing some members toward hunger. Biofuel mandates replicate this dilemma at a planetary scale: land, water, and grain are the "rice," and biofuels are the "beer." The **Food vs. Fuel Debate** asks — *is this trade-off morally, economically, and ecologically justifiable?*

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **First-Generation Biofuels** | Fuels derived from edible biomass (sugars, starches, vegetable oils) | **L/L** (litres per litre of feedstock) |
| **Second-Generation Biofuels** | Fuels from lignocellulosic biomass / non-edible residues | **MJ/kg** of dry biomass |
| **Food Security Index (FSI)** | Global衡量 of food access/availability/affordability | Score out of **100** |
| **Indirect Land Use Change (ILUC)** | Displacement of food crops to non-agricultural land | **gCO₂eq/MJ** |
| **Net Energy Balance (NEB)** | Ratio of energy output to fossil input | Dimensionless (**>1** = net positive) |
| **FAO Food Price Index (FFPI)** | Tracks international prices of food commodities | Base year **2014–2016 = 100** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The debate is not a single question but a **multi-dimensional conflict** spanning **four axes** — Food Security, Land Use, Water Resources, and Greenhouse Gas (GHG) balance. Examiners test both qualitative reasoning *and* quantitative metrics like the **FAO Food Price Index** movement (2014–2024).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Pillars of the Debate

The Food vs. Fuel debate rests on **four interdependent pillars**. KTU examiners typically frame questions around one or more of these:

#### **Pillar 1: Direct Food Supply Displacement**
- **Mechanism:** Edible crops (maize, sugarcane, soybean, palm) are diverted from food supply chains into fermentation/transesterification reactors.
- **Quantitative Anchor:** In **2022**, the U.S. used approximately **40 %** of its maize harvest** for ethanol production.
- **Why it matters:** Reduces edible grain availability, especially in import-dependent nations.

#### **Pillar 2: Land Use Competition**
- **Mechanism:** Expansion of biofuel monocultures (especially palm oil in Indonesia/Malaysia) drives deforestation of primary forests and peatlands.
- **Quantitative Anchor:** Global biofuel cropland occupied approximately **100 million hectares** in 2023, equivalent to the area of **Egypt**.
- **Why it matters:** Triggers **Indirect Land Use Change (ILUC)** — when a farmer in Brazil stops growing soy for food, they may clear Amazon forest to plant more soy, shifting the carbon burden.

#### **Pillar 3: Water Resource Stress**
- **Mechanism:** Biofuel crops are often water-intensive. Producing **1 litre of bioethanol** from maize requires approximately **1,500–3,000 litres** of water (virtually or "blue water").
- **Quantitative Anchor:** Biodiesel from soybean requires ~**4,000 litres of water per litre** of fuel.
- **Why it matters:** Strains aquifers in already water-stressed regions (e.g., Gujarat for Jatropha, Midwest USA for maize ethanol).

#### **Pillar 4: Price Volatility (The "Tortilla Crisis" Effect)**
- **Mechanism:** Increased demand for feedstock raises global commodity prices, making food unaffordable for low-income populations.
- **Quantitative Anchor:** Between 2005–2008, the FAO Food Price Index rose by **~75 %**, with maize and wheat prices more than doubling — coinciding with U.S. ethanol mandate expansion.
- **Why it matters:** Triggers food riots (e.g., **2007–2008 "Tortilla Riots" in Mexico**, food protests in Bangladesh, Indonesia, and Haiti).

### 2.2 Arguments "FOR" Biofuels (The "Fuel" Side)

- **Energy Security:** Reduces fossil fuel import dependency (e.g., Brazil's **PROÁLCOOL** program cut gasoline imports by ~70 %).
- **Rural Income:** Generates employment and farmer revenue.
- **GHG Reduction:** First-gen biofuels can offer **20–60 %** GHG savings vs. petrol/diesel.
- **Engine Compatibility:** Existing vehicles can use E10/E20 blends with no modification.

### 2.3 Arguments "AGAINST" Biofuels (The "Food" Side)

- **Net Food Loss:** A 1 % rise in biofuel demand can raise world maize prices by ~**0.5–1 %**.
- **Deforestation:** Palm oil expansion has destroyed over **~30 %** of Borneo's primary forest.
- **Inefficient Land Use:** A corn-ethanol hectare produces food energy of ~**6,000–8,000 kcal/day**, while the same hectare can feed **15–20 people** with maize grain.
- **Fertilizer Runoff:** Causes eutrophication of waterways (e.g., Gulf of Mexico "Dead Zone").
- **Water Footprint:** Biofuels consume **70–400×** more water per unit energy than fossil fuels.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these relations — KTU numerical/analytical questions are framed around them.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Food vs. Fuel Index (FFI)} &= \frac{\text{Energy from Biofuel (MJ)}}{\text{Energy from Food Lost (MJ)}} \\
\text{ILUC Emission Factor} &= \text{gCO}_2\text{eq/MJ} \\
\text{Water Footprint} &= \text{Litres of water per Litre of fuel (L/L)} \\
\text{Net Energy Ratio (NER)} &= \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}} \quad (>1 = \text{sustainable})
\end{aligned}
$$

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Typical Value | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bioethanol Yield** | $Y_{EtOH} = \eta \cdot \frac{m_{\text{sugar}}}{\rho_{\text{sugar}}} \cdot 0.51$ | 0.45–0.51 | **L/L** | Distillery process design |
| **Biodiesel Yield (transesterification)** | $Y_{B100} = \frac{m_{\text{oil}}}{3 \cdot M_{\text{oil}}} \cdot M_{\text{biodiesel}}$ | ~**0.95–0.98** | **kg/kg** | Reactor sizing |
| **Land Use Efficiency** | $\text{LUE} = \frac{E_{\text{output}} \text{(GJ/ha)}}{1}$ | Maize:**100–150**, Sugarcane:**250–300** | **GJ/ha/yr** | Policy planning |
| **Food vs. Fuel Ratio** | $\text{F/F Ratio} = \frac{\text{Calories to fuel}}{\text{Calories to food}}$ | Maize ethanol:**~1:1.4** | Dimensionless | Ethical assessment |
| **Carbon Debt (ILUC)** | $C_{\text{debt}} = \text{years to repay carbon release}$ | Palm:**~85–200 yrs**, Soy:**~30–50 yrs** | **years** | Sustainability certification |
| **GHG Saving** | $\text{GHG\%} = \left(1 - \frac{E_{\text{bio}}}{E_{\text{fossil}}}\right) \times 100$ | Corn EtOH:**~20–40 %**, Sugarcane:**~60–80 %** | **%** | RED-II compliance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on notation:** $\eta$ = fermentation efficiency, $m$ = mass, $M$ = molar mass, $\rho$ = density, $E$ = energy. The factor **0.51** is the theoretical stoichiometric conversion of sugar (glucose) to ethanol (C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Food vs. Fuel debate directly informs:
- **Bio-refinery siting decisions** (avoid food-cropping zones).
- **Feedstock selection logic** in PEBBT413 design problems (Module 5).
- **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) boundaries** — whether to include ILUC emissions.
- **Policy mechanisms** like **RSB (Roundtable on Sustainable Biomaterials)** certification.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Net Energy Balance (NEB) for Maize Ethanol

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation appears frequently in KTU 14-mark questions. We compute the **Net Energy Ratio (NER)** of maize ethanol.

**Given:** $E_{\text{out}}$ = energy in ethanol, $E_{\text{in}}$ = cumulative fossil energy input (fertilizer, diesel, distillation).

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{out}} &= m_{\text{ethanol}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{ethanol}} \\
&= V_{\text{ethanol}} \times \rho_{\text{ethanol}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{ethanol}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the mass of ethanol produced per hectare:**
Assume yield = **4,000 L ethanol/ha/yr** and $\rho_{\text{ethanol}} = 0.789$ kg/L.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{ethanol}} &= 4{,}000 \text{ L/ha} \times 0.789 \text{ kg/L} \\
&= 3{,}156 \text{ kg/ha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the energy output (LHV of ethanol = 26.8 MJ/kg):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{out}} &= 3{,}156 \text{ kg/ha} \times 26.8 \text{ MJ/kg} \\
&= 84{,}581 \text{ MJ/ha} \approx 84.6 \text{ GJ/ha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Input fossil energy (typical U.S. maize-ethanol):**

$$
E_{\text{in}} \approx 24{,}000 \text{ MJ/ha} = 24 \text{ GJ/ha}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Net Energy Ratio (NER):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NER} &= \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}} \\
&= \frac{84{,}581}{24{,}000} \\
&\approx 3.52
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** NER > 1 confirms maize ethanol is a **net energy positive** fuel. However, this number **excludes ILUC emissions**, which would lower the effective GHG benefit.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Indirect Land Use Change (ILUC) Carbon Debt

**Given:** A palm oil biodiesel plantation displaces **1,000 hectares** of peatland forest. Peatland soil carbon = **6,000 t C/ha**. Biofuel yield = **5,000 L biodiesel/ha/yr**. Combustion emission of biodiesel = **2.5 kg CO₂/L**.

**Step 1 — Total carbon released by peatland conversion (assuming 50 % loss to atmosphere):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{released}} &= 1{,}000 \text{ ha} \times 6{,}000 \text{ tC/ha} \times 0.50 \\
&= 3{,}000{,}000 \text{ tC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert carbon to CO₂ (× 44/12):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CO}_{2,\text{released}} &= 3{,}000{,}000 \times \frac{44}{12} \\
&= 11{,}000{,}000 \text{ t CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual CO₂ avoided by replacing diesel (assume biodiesel offsets diesel at 2.5 kg CO₂/L avoided):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CO}_{2,\text{avoided/yr}} &= 1{,}000 \text{ ha} \times 5{,}000 \text{ L/ha} \times 2.5 \text{ kg/L} \\
&= 12{,}500{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ avoided/yr} \\
&= 12{,}500 \text{ t CO}_2 \text{ avoided/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Carbon Debt Repayment Period:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Carbon Debt (years)} &= \frac{\text{CO}_{2,\text{released}}}{\text{CO}_{2,\text{avoided/yr}}} \\
&= \frac{11{,}000{,}000}{12{,}500} \\
&= 880 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This shows why **peatland-based palm oil biodiesel is unsustainable** — it takes ~880 years to repay the carbon debt, far exceeding the fuel's useful life. This is the *quantitative backbone* of the Food vs. Fuel Debate.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Feedstock Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below is a **high-yield KTU comparative question** template. Memorize the trade-offs.

| Dimension | Maize (1st Gen) | Sugarcane (1st Gen) | Palm Oil (1st Gen) | Jatropha (2nd Gen) | Algae (3rd Gen) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Edible?** | Yes | Yes | Yes (oil) | No | No |
| **Yield (L/ha)** | 2,000–4,000 | 6,000–8,000 | 4,000–6,000 | 1,500–2,000 | 5,000–15,000 |
| **Land Conflict** | **High** | **High** | **Very High** | Low (marginal land) | None (ponds) |
| **Water Demand (L/L)** | ~2,500 | ~1,500 | ~4,000 | ~2,000 | ~500 |
| **Food vs. Fuel Verdict** | ❌ Contested | ⚠️ Moderate | ❌ Severe | ✅ Resolved | ✅ Resolved |
| **GHG Saving (%)** | 20–40 | 60–80 | 20–50 | 50–70 | 50–80 |
| **KTU Exam Likelihood** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |

---

### 3.4 Quantitative Argumentation — Land Use and Caloric Efficiency

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Sub-Question:** *"Compare the food vs. fuel caloric efficiency of maize."*

**Step 1 — Calorie content of 1 tonne of maize grain (food route):**
1 tonne maize ≈ **3.5 million kcal**.

**Step 2 — Calorie content of ethanol produced from 1 tonne of maize:**
1 tonne maize → ~**400 L ethanol** → 400 × 0.789 kg × 6,400 kcal/kg = **2.02 million kcal** (as fuel energy).

**Step 3 — Food vs. Fuel Caloric Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{F/F Caloric Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Fuel Calories}}{\text{Food Calories Lost}} \\
&= \frac{2.02 \text{ M kcal}}{3.5 \text{ M kcal}} \\
&\approx 0.58
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Burning maize as fuel recovers only **~58 %** of the original caloric value as fuel energy, while **discarding 100 %** of its food value. This is the *core ethical argument* against first-generation biofuels.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The Food vs. Fuel Conflict Loop

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **causal feedback structure** of the debate, which is the most-tested visual in KTU 2024 scheme exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biofuel Mandate E10 E20 B5] --> B[Increased Crop Demand]
    B --> C[Land Use Competition]
    B --> D[Water Resource Strain]
    B --> E[Commodity Price Rise]
    C --> F[Deforestation ILUC]
    D --> G[Aquifer Depletion]
    E --> H[Food Insecurity Low Income Nations]
    F --> I[Carbon Debt]
    G --> J[Regional Water Crises]
    H --> K[Public Protests Policy Reversal]
    I --> L[Net GHG Increase]
    J --> M[Climate Adaptation Failure]
    K --> N[Reduced Mandate Targets]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Shift to 2nd 3rd Gen Biofuels]
    O --> A
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **outer loop (A → B → C/D/E → F/G/H → I/J/K/L/M → N → O → A)** captures the negative feedback that *forces* policy evolution from 1st-generation to advanced biofuels.
- The **central node (O)** — *Shift to 2nd/3rd Gen Biofuels* — represents the **engineering solution** to the debate, not its abolition.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Resolution Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEEDSTOCK_TIERS
        direction TB
        TIER1[Tier 1 Edible Crops Maize Sugarcane Soy Palm]
        TIER2[Tier 2 Lignocellulosic Jatropha Switchgrass Waste]
        TIER3[Tier 3 Algae Engineered Microbes]
    end
    TIER1 --> CONFLICT[Food vs Fuel Conflict]
    TIER2 --> RESOLVE[Reduced Conflict]
    TIER3 --> RESOLVE2[Minimal Conflict]
    CONFLICT --> POLICY[Policy Intervention]
    POLICY --> CERT[RSB ISCC Certification]
    POLICY --> MANDATE[Revised Mandate RED II]
    CERT --> SUSTAIN[Verified Sustainability]
    MANDATE --> SUSTAIN
    SUSTAIN --> BALANCE[Energy Food Water Balance]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** The diagram visualizes the **tier-based feedstock transition** mandated by the EU's **RED-II (2018)**, which caps first-generation biofuels at **7 %** of transport energy, redirecting investment toward 2nd and 3rd generation.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stakeholder Conflict Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GOV[Government Mandate Setters] -->|Subsidies| FARMER[Farmers Growers]
    GOV -->|Targets| OIL[Oil Industry Blenders]
    FARMER -->|Crop Sales| OIL
    FARMER -->|Reduced Supply| FOOD[Domestic Food Market]
    FOOD -->|Higher Prices| CONSUMER[Low Income Consumers]
    CONSUMER -->|Protests| GOV
    OIL -->|Fuel Sales| AUTO[Auto Owners]
    AUTO -->|Reduced Imports| GOV
    GOV -->|Tax Revenue| PUBLIC[Public Treasury]
    PUBLIC -->|Subsidies| GOV
```

**Interpretation:** A **self-reinforcing policy loop** emerges where government biofuel targets simultaneously *help* fuel security and *hurt* food affordability — a structural cause of the persistent debate.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the "Food vs. Fuel Debate" in the context of first-generation biofuels. List **two** key drivers of the conflict.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The Food vs. Fuel Debate refers to the socio-economic and ethical contention arising from the diversion of food crops (maize, sugarcane, soybean, palm oil) and arable land toward biofuel production, potentially compromising global food security.
- **Two Key Drivers (1 mark):**
  1. **Rising global food prices** triggered by feedstock competition (e.g., 2007–2008 FAO Food Price Index rise of ~75 %).
  2. **Land-use change and deforestation** (e.g., palm oil expansion in Indonesia/Malaysia).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is Indirect Land Use Change (ILUC)? How does it undermine the greenhouse gas (GHG) savings of first-generation biofuels?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **ILUC Definition (1 mark):** ILUC is the displacement of food crop production to non-agricultural land (e.g., forests, peatlands) caused by biofuel-driven agricultural expansion.
- **Mechanism (1 mark):** When farmland is converted to fuel crops, food production moves elsewhere, often causing deforestation. The carbon released from cleared land offsets the GHG savings of the biofuel.
- **Quantitative impact (1 mark):** Palm oil biodiesel can carry a **carbon debt** of **85–200 years** before net GHG savings are realised.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** *Explain the four main pillars of the Food vs. Fuel Debate. Discuss how each pillar impacts the sustainability of first-generation biofuels.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A country plans to allocate **1 million hectares** of prime agricultural land (currently producing rice at 3 tonnes/ha/yr) to produce sugarcane ethanol at 6,500 L ethanol/ha/yr. Compute the (i) net ethanol energy produced per year and (ii) the food calories lost per year. Compare and comment on the trade-off. Take $\text{LHV}_{\text{EtOH}} = 26.8$ MJ/kg, $\rho_{\text{EtOH}} = 0.789$ kg/L, and rice caloric value = 1,300 kcal/kg.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

The four pillars of the Food vs. Fuel Debate are:

1. **Direct Food Supply Displacement (2 marks):** Diverting edible crops (maize, sugarcane) to fermentation plants reduces food availability. Example: U.S. used ~40 % of maize harvest for ethanol in 2022.

2. **Land Use Competition (2 marks):** Biofuel monocultures (palm oil) cause deforestation. Example: Indonesia lost ~30 % of Borneo's primary forest to oil-palm expansion between 1990–2020.

3. **Water Resource Stress (1.5 marks):** Biofuel crops are water-intensive. Bioethanol requires ~1,500–3,000 L water per litre of fuel, straining aquifers in water-stressed regions.

4. **Price Volatility & Food Security (1.5 marks):** Biofuel demand raises FAO Food Price Index. The 2007–08 food crisis saw maize prices double, triggering tortilla riots in Mexico.

> **Conclusion:** Each pillar compromises sustainability, justifying the policy shift toward 2nd/3rd generation biofuels.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Total ethanol volume produced:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{EtOH}} &= 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ ha} \times 6{,}500 \text{ L/ha} \\
&= 6.5 \times 10^9 \text{ litres/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of ethanol produced:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{EtOH}} &= V_{\text{EtOH}} \times \rho_{\text{EtOH}} \\
&= 6.5 \times 10^9 \times 0.789 \\
&= 5.13 \times 10^9 \text{ kg/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy output (part i):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{out}} &= m_{\text{EtOH}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{EtOH}} \\
&= 5.13 \times 10^9 \text{ kg} \times 26.8 \text{ MJ/kg} \\
&= 1.375 \times 10^{11} \text{ MJ/year} \\
&= 1.375 \times 10^8 \text{ GJ/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Rice production lost (part ii):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{rice}} &= 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ ha} \times 3{,}000 \text{ kg/ha} \\
&= 3.0 \times 10^9 \text{ kg rice/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Food calories lost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Calories}_{\text{lost}} &= 3.0 \times 10^9 \times 1{,}300 \text{ kcal/kg} \\
&= 3.9 \times 10^{12} \text{ kcal/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to MJ (1 kcal = 4.184 kJ):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{food lost}} &= 3.9 \times 10^{12} \times 4{,}184 \text{ J} \\
&= 1.63 \times 10^{13} \text{ MJ/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Trade-off comment:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{food lost}}} \\
&= \frac{1.375 \times 10^{11}}{1.63 \times 10^{13}} \\
&\approx 0.0084
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Comment (1 mark):** The fuel energy recovered is only **~0.84 %** of the food energy lost. This **dramatic loss of food value** is the *quantitative core* of the Food vs. Fuel Debate — the policy may be energetically justifiable but is **socially and ethically unsustainable** on prime food land.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** *Discuss the "Tortilla Crisis" of 2007–2008 as a real-world case study of the Food vs. Fuel conflict. What were its causes, consequences, and policy responses?* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Compare the sustainability of first-generation and second-generation biofuels with respect to (i) food competition, (ii) water use, (iii) land use change, and (iv) GHG balance. Present your answer in a tabular format.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Background (2 marks):** The 2007–2008 "Tortilla Crisis" in Mexico saw the price of tortillas (a maize staple) rise by ~**70 %**, triggering urban protests and food riots. Coinciding with the U.S. Energy Independence and Security Act (2007) which expanded ethanol blending mandates.

**Causes (2 marks):**
1. U.S. diversion of ~30 % of maize harvest to ethanol.
2. Global FAO Food Price Index rose ~75 %, correlated with ethanol expansion.
3. Speculation in commodity markets and rising oil prices.

**Consequences (2 marks):**
1. Food insecurity in low-income populations of Mexico, Bangladesh, Haiti, Indonesia.
2. Political instability and social unrest.
3. Re-evaluation of biofuel mandates (e.g., EU's 10 % cap on first-gen biofuels in RED-II).

**Policy Responses (1 mark):**
- **EU RED-II (2018):** Capped first-generation biofuels at **7 %** of transport energy.
- **U.S. EPA RFS-2:** Introduced "advanced biofuel" categories favouring cellulosic ethanol.
- **Certification frameworks:** RSB, ISCC for sustainable feedstock verification.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks (Tabular Form)**

| Parameter | First-Generation (Maize, Sugarcane, Palm) | Second-Generation (Lignocellulosic, Jatropha, Waste) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Food Competition** (2 marks) | **High** — uses edible crops, e.g., 40 % U.S. maize → ethanol. | **Low** — uses residues, jatropha, switchgrass. | 2G wins |
| **(ii) Water Use** (1.5 marks) | **High** — ~1,500–4,000 L water/L fuel. | **Moderate** — ~500–1,500 L/L (better for waste-based). | 2G wins |
| **(iii) Land Use Change** (2 marks) | **Severe ILUC** — palm oil drives deforestation. | **Minimal** — uses marginal land, crop residues. | 2G wins |
| **(iv) GHG Balance** (1.5 marks) | **Contested** — 20–80 % savings; carbon debt in some cases. | **Better** — 50–90 % savings; lower ILUC penalty. | 2G wins |

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** Second-generation biofuels offer a **structurally superior sustainability profile** across all four dimensions, validating the global policy shift under RED-II and the U.S. RFS-2 framework.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up generations:** Confusing 2nd-gen (lignocellulosic) with 3rd-gen (algae). Algae is 3rd gen, not 2nd.
> 2. **Forgetting ILUC:** Computing GHG savings of first-gen biofuels *without* accounting for Indirect Land Use Change is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Skipping units:** Always state units (L/L, MJ/kg, gCO₂eq/MJ) in numerical answers. Unitless answers = **partial credit only**.
> 4. **Ignoring NER:** A fuel with NER < 1 is *not* sustainable, even if renewable.
> 5. **Mishandling kcal↔MJ:** Conversion is **1 kcal = 4.184 kJ = 4.184 × 10⁻³ MJ**. A common error is using 4.18 instead of 4.184.
> 6. **Overlooking policy context:** Always tie your answer to a real framework (RED-II, RFS-2, NITI Aayog Ethanol Blending Programme 2025).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Food vs. Fuel Debate = conflict between using food crops/land for **first-generation biofuels** vs. human consumption.
- ✅ **Four Pillars:** (1) Food Supply Displacement, (2) Land Use Competition, (3) Water Stress, (4) Price Volatility.
- ✅ **Generation Hierarchy:**
  - **1st Gen** = edible crops (maize, sugarcane, soy, palm) → ❌ direct conflict.
  - **2nd Gen** = lignocellulosic, Jatropha, waste → ✅ reduced conflict.
  - **3rd Gen** = algae, engineered microbes → ✅✅ minimal conflict.
- ✅ **Key Metrics to Memorize:**
  - FAO Food Price Index rise 2005–2008 ≈ **75 %**.
  - U.S. maize → ethanol in 2022 ≈ **40 %**.
  - Water for bioethanol ≈ **1,500–3,000 L/L**.
  - Carbon debt for palm oil ≈ **85–200 years**.
- ✅ **Key Policies:**
  - **RED-II (EU, 2018):** First-gen biofuels capped at **7 %**.
  - **RFS-2 (USA):** Promotes "advanced biofuels."
  - **India EBP (Ethanol Blending Programme):** Target E20 by **2025**.
- ✅ **Quantitative Anchors:**
  - **NER > 1** = net energy positive.
  - **GHG savings = 20–80 %** for 1st gen, **50–90 %** for 2nd gen.
  - **LHV of ethanol = 26.8 MJ/kg**, density = **0.789 kg/L**.
  - Stoichiometric sugar → ethanol conversion factor = **0.51**.
- ✅ **Real-world Case Studies:** Tortilla Crisis (2007–08), Brazil's PROÁLCOOL, Indonesia's palm oil expansion, EU's RED-II revision.
- ✅ **Engineering Solutions:** Use of **waste biomass, marginal land, multi-feedstock biorefinery, ILUC-aware LCA, RSB/ISCC certification**.
- ✅ **Conversion Constants:** 1 kcal = 4.184 kJ; 1 tonne oil equivalent (toe) = 41.868 GJ; 1 ha ≈ 10,000 m².

> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked a comparative question, *always* present a **table** (2G vs. 1G, or edible vs. non-edible feedstocks) — KTU examiners award **+1 mark** for structured presentation.
